EDCS-00380927.text_edition: \n Apollini Auxilius utus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-00380927.translation: Auxilius fulfilled his vow to Apollo willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-00380927.summary: An offering by Auxilius to the god Apollo, likely a votive inscription.

EDCS-00380931.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Domitio Aurelianus sic Pio Felici Invicto Augusto tribunicia potestate patri patriae sub cura Salli Aristaeneti legati Augusti p<r=A>(o) praetore mille passuum \n
EDCS-00380931.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, under the supervision of Sallius Aristaenetus, Augustan legate with praetorian power, a milestone.
EDCS-00380931.summary: A milestone inscription recording the actions of Emperor Aurelian and his legate Sallius Aristaenetus, dating to Aurelian's reign (270-275 AD).

EDCS-03000789.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae pro salute Favorini et Secundiae vernarum Titi Iuli Saturnini conductoris publici portorii Illyrici sub Iuliis Capitone et Euphoro circitoribus publici portorii Asclepiades servus vilicus et Lucensia parentes\n
EDCS-03000789.translation: For Diana Regina, for the health of Favorinus and Secundia, slaves of Titus Iulius Saturninus, public collector of customs duties in Illyricum, under the watchmen Iulius Capito and Iuphorus, Asclepiades, slave and steward, and Lucensia, parents, [dedicated this].
EDCS-03000789.summary: A dedication to Diana Regina for the health of slaves, set up by their steward and parents, associated with customs collectors in Illyricum.

EDCS-03000790.text_edition: \n Dianae pro salute Quinti Sabini Verani II VPR Macrinus arcarius posuit curante Hilaro Vitelius dedit votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-03000790.translation: For Diana, for the health of Quintus Sabinus Veranus, Macrinus the accountant erected [this]; Hilarus Vitelius, having taken care of it, gave it; he fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-03000790.summary: A dedication to Diana for the health of Quintus Sabinus Veranus, set up by Macrinus the accountant and Hilarus Vitelius.

EDCS-03000792.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXII consul IIII pater patriae Iulio Severo legato Augusti pro praetore III\n
EDCS-03000792.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, supreme pontiff, holder of tribunician power for the 22nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, [this was inscribed] by Julius Severus, Augustan legate with praetorian power, for the third time.
EDCS-03000792.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his titles and the legation of Julius Severus, likely associated with a public building or milestone.

EDCS-03300820.text_edition: \n Sabidia Tertulla Publi Sabidi filia vixit annos XX Publius Veianiuscus coniugi bene merenti faciendum curavit hic sita est\n
EDCS-03300820.translation: Sabidia Tertulla, daughter of Publius Sabidius, lived for 20 years. Publius Veianiuscus had this made for his well-deserving wife. She is buried here.
EDCS-03300820.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sabidia Tertulla, erected by her husband Publius Veianiuscus, recording her age at death.

EDCS-03300821.translation: To the departed spirits of the riverside dwellers. PAT [died?].
EDCS-03300821.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' (departed spirits), possibly referring to people living near a river.

EDCS-03300822.translation: To the departed spirits of Lucius Laelius. TIS [died?].
EDCS-03300822.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' (departed spirits) of Lucius Laelius.

EDCS-03300840.text_edition: \n Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Lucius Oppius Maximus et Lucius Oppius Ianuarius sacerdotes Matris Deum et Liberi Patris vexillo Novesium Oppianorum aram de suo posuerunt\n
EDCS-03300840.translation: Sacred to Augustus, for the health of the Emperor. Lucius Oppius Maximus and Lucius Oppius Ianuarius, priests of the Mother of the Gods and Liber Pater of the Novesium Oppianorum vexillum, set up an altar from their own resources.
EDCS-03300840.summary: A dedication to Augustus and for the Emperor's health, made by priests of the Mother of the Gods and Liber Pater from Novesium.

EDCS-03300841.text_edition: \n Marci Aurelii Sam iter qui conducens vitam <per> peregit cum Paulina filia sua et in hoc monumento ambo recepti sunt\n
EDCS-03300841.translation: Marcus Aurelius Samus, who lived his life with his daughter Paulina, and both were received into this monument.
EDCS-03300841.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius Samus and his daughter Paulina, indicating they are buried together in this monument.

EDCS-03700712.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Stratoclia Tomitana coniunx Flavi Maximini beneficarii consularis vixit nnos XX hic sita est\n
EDCS-03700712.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelia Stratoclia of Tomis, wife of Flavius Maximinus, a *beneficiarius consularis*, lived 20 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-03700712.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Stratoclia, wife of a *beneficiarius consularis*, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700713.text_edition: \n Deae Eponae Reginae pro salute domini nostri Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Valerius Rufus beneficiarius consularis legionis XI Claudiae Antoninianae votum solvit libens merito Laeto II et Ceriale consulibus\n
EDCS-03700713.translation: To the goddess Epona Regina, for the safety of our lord Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Valerius Rufus, *beneficiarius consularis* of the XI Claudia Antoniniana legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, under the consulship of Laetus II and Cerialis.
EDCS-03700713.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Epona, made by Valerius Rufus, a *beneficiarius consularis* of a Roman legion, during the consulship of Laetus II and Cerialis.

EDCS-03700714.text_edition: \n Aurelius Maximus cornicularius eques Aurelius Mucianus eques singularis Antonius Aurelius miles legionis I Antoninianae ex voto\n
EDCS-03700714.translation: Aurelius Maximus, *cornicularius*; Aurelius Mucianus, *eques singularis*; Antonius Aurelius, soldier of the I Antoniniana legion, out of fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-03700714.summary: This inscription records three soldiers, a *cornicularius*, an *eques singularis*, and a legionary, dedicating something in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-03700715.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XI pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate II et [Publius Septimius Geta] Caesar Augustus restituerunt per Caium Ovinium Tertullum legatum pro praetore\n
EDCS-03700715.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, emperor for the 11th time, father of the country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, and [Publius Septimius Geta] Caesar Augustus restored this, through Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, legate pro praetore.
EDCS-03700715.summary: This is a building inscription recording the restoration of something by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, overseen by the legate Gaius Ovinius Tertullus.

EDCS-03700716.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus restituit per C[] [[]] [[]] legatum pro praetore milia passuum\n
EDCS-03700716.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus restored this, through C[] [legatus pro praetore] [for] [the milestones] Roman miles.
EDCS-03700716.summary: This is a building inscription recording the restoration of milestones by Emperor Gordian III, through an unnamed legate.

EDCS-03700718.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Claudio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto agente praeside provinciae Titio Saturnino metropolis Tomitanorum mille passuum I\n
EDCS-03700718.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, while Titus Saturninus was presiding as governor of the province, the metropolis of Tomis, [restored] one Roman mile.
EDCS-03700718.summary: This is a building inscription recording the restoration of one Roman mile in the metropolis of Tomis, dedicated to Emperor Claudius Gothicus during the governorship of Titus Saturninus.

EDCS-04900922.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci Caius Candius Germanus trierarchus\n
EDCS-04900922.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the place, Gaius Candius Germanus, *trierarchus*.
EDCS-04900922.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius loci by Gaius Candius Germanus, a *trierarchus*.

EDCS-04900924.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Herculi Gaius Iulius Valens optio legionis I Italicae ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-04900924.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, and Hercules, Gaius Iulius Valens, *optio* of the I Italica legion, set this up out of fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-04900924.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and Hercules by Gaius Iulius Valens, an *optio* in a Roman legion.

EDCS-05000714.text_edition: \n Libero Patri dao et Aelius Secundus\n
EDCS-05000714.translation: To Liber Pater, [gift] and Aelius Secundus.
EDCS-05000714.summary: This is a dedication to Liber Pater, likely set up by Aelius Secundus.

EDCS-05000715.text_edition: \n atianus beneficiarius consularis us ex voto I\n
EDCS-05000715.summary: This fragmented inscription records a *beneficiarius consularis* namedatianus, who made a votive dedication.

EDCS-05000716.text_edition: \n Antoniae Gnesiae sanctissimae pientissimae Lucius Porcius Ambibius coniunx aedilicius bis magistratus sacerdos designatus civitatis Ulpiae Nicopolis ad Histrum uxori bene merenti et sibi posuit\n
EDCS-05000716.translation: To Antonia Gnesia, most holy and most dutiful, Lucius Porcius Ambibius, her husband, who twice held the office of aedile, designated priest of the city of Ulpia Nicopolis ad Istrum, erected this for his well-deserving wife and for himself.
EDCS-05000716.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Lucius Porcius Ambibius for his wife Antonia Gnesia, mentioning his own civic offices and his wife's epithet.

EDCS-05000717.text_edition: \n Rufae vixit annos IIII Valeria Rufa filiae et Coelius Phil eius heres faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-05000717.translation: Valeria Rufa and her heir Coelius Phileius had this made for Rufa, who lived for 4 years, their daughter.
EDCS-05000717.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Rufa, erected by her mother Valeria Rufa and her heir Coelius Phileius.

EDCS-05000718.text_edition: \n legionis I Italicae [Alexandrianae] militavit beneficiarius consularis et cornicularius procuratoris quot sic tiro proficiscens in bello Bosporano voverat et adiuvante numen sic eius multis periculis in barbarico liberatus sit merito votum posuit\n
EDCS-05000718.translation: He served as a *beneficiarius consularis* and *cornicularius* to the procurator of the I Italica Legion (Alexandriana), and as a new recruit setting out for war, he had vowed in the Bosporan War, and by the aid of his divinity, since he was freed from many dangers in foreign territory, he justly set up this vow.
EDCS-05000718.summary: This inscription records the service of a soldier of the I Italica Legion as a *beneficiarius* and *cornicularius* to a procurator, and his vow fulfillment after surviving the Bosporan War.

EDCS-05000719.text_edition: \n Puplius sic Aelius Venustinus strator consularis legionis XI Claudiae p<r=P>o salutem sic suam sic voto posuit \n
EDCS-05000719.translation: Publius Aelius Venustinus, *strator* to the consul of the XI Claudia Legion, to his own health, set this up by vow.
EDCS-05000719.summary: This is a votive inscription by Publius Aelius Venustinus, a *strator* of the XI Claudia Legion, dedicated for his own health.

EDCS-05000720.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae Titus Flavius Secundus vixit annis L Aelia Bend<is=SI> marito suo pientissimo et sibi vivo sic suo sic posuit \n
EDCS-05000720.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Titus Flavius Secundus, who lived 50 years. Aelia Bensis set this up for her most dutiful husband and for herself while alive.
EDCS-05000720.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Secundus, who lived 50 years, erected by his wife Aelia Bensis for him and for herself.

EDCS-05000721.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudius Martinus vixit nns XX Claudius Clemens pater filio pientissimo \n
EDCS-05000721.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudius Martinus, who lived 20 years. Claudius Clemens, his father, for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-05000721.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudius Martinus, who lived 20 years, erected by his father Claudius Clemens.

EDCS-05000722.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Valens vixit annos XLV et Aelius Sabinus filius e[t] vixit annos VIII titulum posuit Aelius Valens veteranus \n
EDCS-05000722.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelius Valens, who lived 45 years, and his son Aelius Sabinus, who lived 8 years. Aelius Valens the veteran erected this monument.
EDCS-05000722.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelius Valens and his son Aelius Sabinus, erected by Aelius Valens the veteran.

EDCS-05000723.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Pontico et Valeriae Nene parentibus bene merentibus Valerius Valens filius miles legionis I Italicae posuit\n
EDCS-05000723.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Valens, a soldier of the I Italica Legion, erected this for his well-deserving parents, Valerius Ponticus and Valeria Nene.
EDCS-05000723.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Valerius Valens, a soldier of the I Italica Legion, for his parents Valerius Ponticus and Valeria Nene.

EDCS-05000724.text_edition: \n ------ vi]xit annis XXXII Caius Valerius Longinus veteranus legionis V Macedonicae ex beneficiario COXI \n
EDCS-05000724.translation: [...] who lived 32 years. Caius Valerius Longinus, a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion, formerly a *beneficiarius* COXI [details missing].
EDCS-05000724.summary: This is a partial funerary epitaph for Caius Valerius Longinus, a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion, who lived 32 years and previously served as a *beneficiarius*.

EDCS-05000725.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Memmia Marcia vixit annis XVIIII Memmius Marcus pater eius et Flavius Martinus maritus eius et Flavius Secundus d eius coniugi pientissimae posuerunt\n
EDCS-05000725.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Memmia Marcia, who lived 17 years. Her father Memmius Marcus, and her husband Flavius Martinus, and Flavius Secundus erected this for their most dutiful wife/daughter.
EDCS-05000725.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Memmia Marcia, who lived 17 years, erected by her father, her husband, and another relative named Flavius Secundus.

EDCS-05000726.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Titiae matri posuit Aelius Titianus filius bene merenti vixit annos LX\n
EDCS-05000726.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelius Titianus erected this for his deserving mother Flavia Titia, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-05000726.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a son to his mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05000727.text_edition: \n LIIrius vixit annos LXX Flavia Primitiva coniux bene merito coniugi posuit L\n
EDCS-05000727.translation: Flavia Primitiva erected this for her most deserving husband, who lived 70 years.
EDCS-05000727.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200611.text_edition: \n Lucius Freius Luci libertus Faustus lixa legionis V vixit annos L\n
EDCS-05200611.translation: Lucius Freius Faustus, freedman of Lucius, a soldier of the Vth Legion, lived 50 years.
EDCS-05200611.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier of the Vth Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200612.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus bene merentibus Castae quae vixit annum I dies V et Reginae quae vixit nnos VII menses V dies XVIII filiabus dulcissimis Valerius Castus centurio ordinarius legionis I Ioviae Scythicae una cum Valerio Valeriano centenario socero suo posuit\n
EDCS-05200612.translation: To the Divine Shades. Valerius Castus, ordinary centurion of the Ist Jovian Scythian Legion, together with his father-in-law Valerius Valerian, a centenarian, erected this for their most beloved daughters Castra, who lived 1 year and 5 days, and Regina, who lived 7 years, 5 months, and 18 days.
EDCS-05200612.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a centurion and his father-in-law for their two young daughters, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200613.translation: To Aulus for his quaestorship in the municipium of Noviodunum.
EDCS-05200613.summary: A dedication honoring an individual's quaestorship in the Roman town of Noviodunum, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000849.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cives Romani consistentes vico classicorum curam agente Publio Pompeio magistro Severo et Herenniano consulibus\n
EDCS-07000849.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. The Roman citizens residing in the Classicorum district, with Publius Pompeius Severus acting as master and Herennianus as consul.
EDCS-07000849.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Roman citizens of the 'vicus classicorum' during the consulship of Severus and Herennianus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07000851.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. The Roman citizens residing in the Classicorum district, with Marcius Victorinus (?) and Severus serving as consuls.
EDCS-07000851.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Roman citizens of the 'vicus classicorum' during the consulship of Victorinus and Severus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07000852.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cives Romani consistentes vico classicorum curam agente Sossio Sossi magistro\n
EDCS-07000852.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. The Roman citizens residing in the Classicorum district, with Sossius as master.
EDCS-07000852.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Roman citizens of the 'vicus classicorum' under the magistracy of Sossius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000857.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Luci Septumi sic Severi Pertenacis sic Flavius Ianuarius magister vici Clementianensis de suo posuit Dextro II et Thrasea Prisco consulibus\n
EDCS-07000857.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax. Flavius Ianuarius, master of the Clementianensis district, dedicated this from his own funds. Dextrus and Thrasea Priscus serving as consuls.
EDCS-07000857.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the emperor's well-being, made by the master of the 'vicus Clementianensis' during the consulship of Dextrus and Thrasea Priscus, dating to the reign of Septimius Severus (193-211 CE).

EDCS-07400638.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Scantilio Marco militi legionis I Italicae Africanus pater posuit\n
EDCS-07400638.translation: To the Divine Shades. Africanus erected this for Quintus Scantilius Marcus, a soldier of the I Italica Legion.
EDCS-07400638.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a father for his son, a soldier in the I Italica Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400643.text_edition: \n Dianae Tiberius Claudius Ulpianus tribunus cohortis I Cilicum cum vexillationibus legionum I Italicae XI Claudiae classis Flaviae Moesicae ob venationem Caesarianam iniunctam a Claudio Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore ursis et visontibus prospere captis aram consecravit Largo et Messallino consulibus\n
EDCS-07400643.translation: To Diana, Tiberius Claudius Ulpianus, tribune of the I Cohort of Cilicians, with detachments from the Legions I Italica, XI Claudia, and the Flavian Moesian fleet, dedicated this altar for the successful capture of bears and wild boars during a Caesarian hunt, ordered by Claudius Saturninus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, during the consulship of Largo and Messallinus.
EDCS-07400643.summary: This inscription records an altar dedicated to Diana by a military tribune for a successful hunt ordered by a governor, dating to the consulship of Largo and Messallinus.

EDCS-07400645.text_edition: \n Dianae pro salute Marci Ponti Laeliani legati Auggustorum pro praetore Quintus Caecilius Larensis |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae\n
EDCS-07400645.translation: To Diana, for the health of Marcus Pontius Laelianus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, Quintus Caecilius Larensis, centurion of the XI Claudia Legion, offered this.
EDCS-07400645.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Diana by a centurion for the health of an imperial legate, likely dating to the period when Laelianus held office.

EDCS-07400647.text_edition: \n Deanae Augustae Caius Aemilius Donatus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae ex voto libens animo bassidem sic cum sigillo posuit\n
EDCS-07400647.translation: To Diana Augusta, Gaius Aemilius Donatus, centurion of the XI Claudia Legion, willingly set up this basin with a statuette, fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-07400647.summary: This inscription records a private offering of a basin and statuette to Diana Augusta by a centurion of the XI Claudia Legion, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-07400648.text_edition: \n Deanae Augustae aram cum sigillo Publius Aelius Clemens beneficiarius consularis legionis I Italicae et Aurelia Rufina cum suis ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-07400648.translation: Publius Aelius Clemens, a beneficiarius consularis of the I Italica Legion, and Aurelia Rufina, with their family, set up this altar with a statuette to Diana Augusta, fulfilling their vow.
EDCS-07400648.summary: This inscription records a joint vow fulfillment offering of an altar and statuette to Diana Augusta by a soldier and his family.

EDCS-07400649.text_edition: \n Aram cum sigillo Publius Aelius Clemens beneficiarius consularis legionis I Italicae et Aurelia Rufina cum suis ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-07400649.translation: Publius Aelius Clemens, a beneficiarius consularis of the I Italica Legion, and Aurelia Rufina, with their family, set up this altar with a statuette, fulfilling their vow.
EDCS-07400649.summary: This inscription records a joint vow fulfillment offering of an altar and statuette by a soldier and his family, dedicating it to an unspecified deity (likely Diana Augusta from the preceding inscription).

EDCS-07400650.translation: To Diana the Holy, Valerius PGR [offers this].
EDCS-07400650.summary: This very brief inscription records a personal offering to Diana the Holy by a person identified by the initials Valerius PGR.

EDCS-07400652.text_edition: \n Deanae aram et sigillum Caius Marius Victorinus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae ex voto posuit Dedicatum XV Kalendas Iunias Plautiano II et Publio Septimio Geta II consulibus\n
EDCS-07400652.translation: To Diana, Gaius Marius Victorinus, centurion of the I Italica Legion, set up this altar and statuette, fulfilling his vow. Dedicated on the 15th day before the Kalends of June, during the second consulship of Plautianus and the second consulship of Publius Septimius Geta.
EDCS-07400652.summary: This inscription records a private offering of an altar and statuette to Diana by a centurion of the I Italica Legion, dated to the consulship of Plautianus and Geta.

EDCS-07400653.text_edition: \n Aram cum sigillo Deanae Hispanius Victor hastatus legionis I Parthicae Severianae ex voto\n
EDCS-07400653.translation: To Diana, Hispanius Victor, hastatus of the I Parthica Severiana Legion, set up this altar with a statuette, fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-07400653.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfillment offering of an altar and statuette to Diana by a soldier of the I Parthica Severiana Legion.

EDCS-07400654.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae et Apollini pro salute Luci Iuli Statili Severi consularis et liberorum eius Calvisius Flavinus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae\n
EDCS-07400654.translation: To Diana the Queen and Apollo, Calvisius Flavinus, centurion of the I Italica Legion, for the health of Lucius Iulius Statius Severus, governor, and his children, offered this.
EDCS-07400654.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Diana Regina and Apollo by a centurion of the I Italica Legion for the health of a governor and his family.

EDCS-07400655.text_edition: \n Feliciter pro salute Imperatoris Augusti nostri Dianae et Apollini Anicetus Augusti nostri servus\n
EDCS-07400655.translation: May it be fortunate! For the health of our Emperor Augustus, Anicetus, our Emperor's servant, [dedicated this] to Diana and Apollo.
EDCS-07400655.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Diana and Apollo by a freedman of the emperor for the imperial family's health.

EDCS-07400656.text_edition: \n Diis Sanctis Dianae Reginae et Apollini Phoebo Iulius Mucazenus beneficiarius consularis legionis I Italicae agens territorio Montanensium pro salute sua et Iuliae coniugis eius gratias agens votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07400656.translation: To the Holy Gods, Queen Diana and Apollo Phoebus, Julius Mucazenus, a staff-officer of a consular legate of the I Italica legion, serving in the territory of the Montanenses, for his own health and that of his wife Julia, giving thanks, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07400656.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Diana Regina and Apollo Phoebus by Julius Mucazenus, a military officer, for his own health and that of his wife, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07400658.text_edition: \n Apollini Sancto Urbanus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-07400658.translation: To Holy Apollo, Urbanus, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07400658.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo by Urbanus, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07400659.text_edition: \n Feliciter Apollini Sancto Titus Flavius Iulius |(centurio) legionis I Italicae praepositus numeri civium Romanorum cum suis ex voto solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07400659.translation: May it be fortunate! To Holy Apollo, Titus Flavius Iulius, a centurion of the I Italica legion and commander of a detachment of Roman citizens, with his family, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07400659.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo by Titus Flavius Iulius, a centurion and commander of a Roman citizen detachment of the I Italica legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07400661.text_edition: \n Apollini Publius Aelius Fronto |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae votum solvit\n
EDCS-07400661.translation: To Apollo, Publius Aelius Fronto, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07400661.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo by Publius Aelius Fronto, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07400662.text_edition: \n Apollini pro salute felicissimorum Impperatorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini [et] [Publi Septimi Getae] Augggustorum et Iuliae Domnae Augustae matris castrorum Marcus Cocceius Silvanus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae ex voto\n
EDCS-07400662.translation: To Apollo, for the health of the most fortunate Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus [and Publius Septimius Geta] Augusti, and Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp, Marcus Cocceius Silvanus, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, [dedicated this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-07400662.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo by Marcus Cocceius Silvanus, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, for the health of Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, Geta, and Julia Domna, dating to their joint reign (c. 198-211/212 CE).

EDCS-07400663.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Publius Aelius Fronto |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae votum solvit\n
EDCS-07400663.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia, Publius Aelius Fronto, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07400663.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Aesculapius and Hygieia by Publius Aelius Fronto, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08300497.text_edition: \n pro salute Cnaei Suelli Rufi Cosconi Gentiani legati Titus Caelius Catus beneficiarius eius ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-08300497.translation: For the health of Gnaeus Suellius Rufus Cosconianus Gentianus, Titus Caelius Catus, his staff-officer, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08300497.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by Titus Caelius Catus, a military officer, for the health of his superior, Gnaeus Suellius Rufus Cosconianus Gentianus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08300498.text_edition: \n Divinibus Romae aeternae Genio provinciae Moesiae inferioris Domitius Antigonus vir clarissimus legatus Augusti pro praetore cum Pompeia Apa clarissima femina coniuge et Domitiis Antigono et Antigona\n
EDCS-08300498.translation: To the divine Eternal Rome, to the Genius of the province of Lower Moesia, Domitius Antigonus, vir clarissimus (most distinguished man), governor, with Pompeia Apa, clarissima femina (most distinguished woman), his wife, and their children Domitius Antigonus and Antigona [set this up].
EDCS-08300498.summary: This inscription records the dedication to the divine Eternal Rome and the Genius of Lower Moesia by the governor Domitius Antigonus, his wife, and children, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08300500.text_edition: \n Pro salute Antoni Marciani et Timothei et sua Caius Antonius Olympus ex voto\n
EDCS-08300500.translation: For the health of Antonius Marcianus and Timotheus and his own, Caius Antonius Olympus, [dedicated this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08300500.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Antonius Olympus for the health of Antonius Marcianus, Timotheus, and himself, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08300501.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris divi Traiani Parthici fili divi Nervae nepotis Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis XX consulis III Antius Rufinus inter Moesos et Thraces fines posuit\n
EDCS-08300501.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time, Antius Rufinus established the boundary between the Moesians and the Thracians.
EDCS-08300501.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary between the Moesians and Thracians by Antius Rufinus, likely a governor, under the authority of Emperor Hadrian and dating to his reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-08300502.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris divi Traiani Parthici fili divi Nervae nepotis Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis XX consulis III Antius Rufinus inter Moesos et Thraces fines posuit\n
EDCS-08300502.translation: By the authority of Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, father of the fatherland, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time, Antius Rufinus established this boundary between the Moesians and Thracians.
EDCS-08300502.summary: This is a boundary stone set up by Antius Rufinus under imperial authority, likely in the 2nd century AD, to mark a territorial division.

EDCS-08300507.summary: This inscription is a short dedication or marker associated with the First Italic Legion, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300512.text_edition: \n Deanae Luciferae Augustae Sergilianus vilicus ex voto pro se et suis posuit\n
EDCS-08300512.translation: To Deana Lucifera Augusta, Sergilianus, the steward, set this up in fulfillment of a vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-08300512.summary: This is a votive offering to the goddess Deana Lucifera, dedicated by a steward named Sergilianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300513.text_edition: \n Apollini sancto Caius Iulius Saturninus |(centurio) regionarius ex voto\n
EDCS-08300513.translation: To holy Apollo, Gaius Julius Saturninus, a regional centurion, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08300513.summary: This is a votive dedication to Apollo, made by a regional centurion, Gaius Julius Saturninus, probably during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300515.text_edition: \n Aram cum sigillo Apollini Ulpius Pris[cus ------\n
EDCS-08300515.translation: To Apollo Ulpius, Priscus [---] erected an altar with a statuette.
EDCS-08300515.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the dedication of an altar with a statuette to Apollo Ulpius by someone named Ulpius Priscus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300516.text_edition: \n Heroni deo sancto cum suis posuit\n
EDCS-08300516.translation: To the holy god Heron, he set this up with his family.
EDCS-08300516.summary: This is a dedication to a deity identified as Heron, set up by an individual and their family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300518.text_edition: \n Numini sancto deo aram cum sigillo Lucius Attienus Iulianus pro salute sua et suorum posuit et templum di<l=I>apsum ex suo restituit \n
EDCS-08300518.translation: To the holy divine spirit, Lucius Attienus Iulianus erected an altar with a statuette for his own safety and that of his family, and restored the dilapidated temple at his own expense.
EDCS-08300518.summary: This inscription records a dedication to a divine numen, the restoration of a temple, and is dedicated by Lucius Attienus Iulianus for his well-being, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300520.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Dometianus sic miles legionis IIII Flaviae militavit annis VII vixit annis XXVII Ulpius Longinianus fratri bene merenti et filio posuit\n
EDCS-08300520.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Ulpius Dometianus served as a soldier in the Fourth Flavian Legion for 7 years and lived for 27 years. Ulpius Longinianus set this up for his well-deserving brother and son.
EDCS-08300520.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and military service of Ulpius Dometianus and was dedicated by his brother and son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300521.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Lucius Cocceius Marcus |(centurio) legionis XI? Cl(audiae) praepositus numeri civium Romanorum simulacra libens pos(uit) cum ara invictae Dianae ex voto\n
EDCS-08300521.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, Lucius Cocceius Marcus, a centurion of the (possibly) XI Claudia legion, commander of a unit of Roman citizens, willingly set up statues together with an altar to the unconquered Diana, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08300521.summary: This inscription records the dedication of statues and an altar to Jupiter, Juno, and Diana, made by Lucius Cocceius Marcus, a centurion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300526.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae et Apollini sacrum pro salute Vitrasi Pollionis viri clarissimi legati Augusti pro praetore Caius Iulius Africanus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-08300526.translation: Sacred to Queen Diana and Apollo, Caius Julius Africanus, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for the safety of Vitrasius Pollio, a most distinguished man, the Emperor's legate with proconsular authority.
EDCS-08300526.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Diana and Apollo made by a centurion for the well-being of the distinguished governor Vitrasius Pollio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300527.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo patri patriae Caius Quintus Decius legatus Augusti pro praetore provinciarum Moesiae itemque Germaniae inferiorum candidatus Augusti devotissimus dicatissimusque numini maiestatique eorum\n
EDCS-08300527.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, father of the country, Caius Quintus Decius, imperial legate with praetorian power over the provinces of Moesia and Lower Germany, candidate of Augustus, most devoted and dedicated to their divine will and majesty.
EDCS-08300527.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Alexander Severus by Caius Quintus Decius, governor of Moesia and Lower Germany, likely dating to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-08300534.text_edition: \n Invicto Tiberius Claudios sic Z<e=\xce\x97>nodotos sic si<g=C>(nifer) |(centuriae) Iuni Pacati legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-08300534.translation: To the Unconquered god, Tiberius Claudius Zenodotus, standard-bearer of the first century of Iunius Pacatus from the I Italica legion, paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-08300534.summary: This is a votive inscription by Tiberius Claudius Zenodotus, a soldier of the I Italica legion, to an Unconquered god, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08300535.text_edition: \n Numini Invicti Ulpius Apollinaris ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-08300535.translation: Ulpius Apollinaris erected this in fulfillment of a vow to the Unconquered divine power.
EDCS-08300535.summary: This is a votive inscription by Ulpius Apollinaris to an Unconquered deity, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08300536.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Filici sic Invicto Augusto et Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesares sic per territorium Abritanorum mille passuum I\n
EDCS-08300536.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and to Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, along the territory of the Abritani, one mile.
EDCS-08300536.summary: This inscription records a milestone marking one mile along the territory of the Abritani, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, likely dating to their reign (244-249 CE).

EDCS-08500597.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Cai Valeri filio colonia Ulpia Zermizegetusa sic Iuliano primo pilo legionis XI Claudiae III piae fidelis qui vixit nnos LXXXVIII Pompeia Aquilina coniux et Valerius Aquilinus et Pompeia Iuliane sic et Valerii Iulianus et Vibianus et Pompeius Iulianus fili et heredes patri pientissimo et merentissimo faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-08500597.translation: To the Spirits of the departed: Caius Valerius Iulianus, first centurion of the XI Claudia legion, thrice pious and faithful, who lived 88 years, his wife Pompeia Aquilina, and his sons and heirs Valerius Aquilinus, Pompeia Iuliane, Valerius Iulianus, and Valerius Vibianus, and Pompeius Iulianus, took care to have this monument made for their most devoted and deserving father.
EDCS-08500597.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Caius Valerius Iulianus, a senior centurion from the legion XI Claudia, set up by his family in the colonia Ulpia Zermizegetusa, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600632.translation: Martinianus lies here, the day before the Kalends of March.
EDCS-08600632.summary: This is a very brief tomb inscription recording the burial of Martinianus on February 28th (or 29th in a leap year), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600634.summary: This is a dedication by Marcus Domitius Valens, likely a private monument, possibly a tomb or a small shrine, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600635.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vitales sic miles? legionis V Macedonicae Caius Iulius Festus frater et heres titulum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-08600635.translation: To the Spirits of the departed: Vitales, a soldier of the V Macedonica legion, his brother and heir Caius Iulius Festus, had this monument made.
EDCS-08600635.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Vitales, a soldier of the V Macedonica legion, set up by his brother and heir Caius Iulius Festus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08900590.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Salutari Iunoni Reginae ceterisque diis inmortalibus Silvius Silvanus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Moesiae inferioris pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-08900590.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest the Healer, to Juno Regina, and to all the immortal gods, Silvius Silvanus, a most distinguished man, governor of Lower Moesia, paid his vow for his safety and that of his family.
EDCS-08900590.summary: This is a votive inscription by Silvius Silvanus, governor of Lower Moesia, to Jupiter, Juno, and other gods for the safety of himself and his family, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08900591.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Aurelius Dizzo vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae pro salute sua suorumque omnium votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-08900591.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Dizzo, a most distinguished man, governor of the province, willingly set this up as a vow for his safety and that of all his family.
EDCS-08900591.summary: This is a sacred dedication by Aurelius Dizzo, governor of the province, to Jupiter Best and Greatest, in fulfillment of a vow for his safety and that of his family, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08900605.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Titus Flavius Severus posuit de suo libens merito\n
EDCS-08900605.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno Regina, Titus Flavius Severus willingly placed this from his own property, as was deserved.
EDCS-08900605.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter and Juno, likely from the imperial period, placed by Titus Flavius Severus.

EDCS-08900612.text_edition: \n Ex voto Ulpius Maximus poisuit dit sic\n
EDCS-08900612.translation: Ulpius Maximus set this up in fulfillment of a vow, as is fitting.
EDCS-08900612.summary: This is a votive dedication set up by Ulpius Maximus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-08900622.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Julius Silvanus.
EDCS-08900622.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the dead) for Julius Silvanus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-08901158.text_edition: \n Latonae Quintus Granius Romanus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-08901158.translation: To Latona, Quintus Granius Romanus, a centurion of the I Italica legion, willingly fulfilled his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-08901158.summary: This records a military man, Quintus Granius Romanus, a centurion of the I Italica legion, fulfilling a vow to Latona, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-08901159.text_edition: \n Maximianus Volsinis |(centurio) legionis II Augustae praepositus numeri civium Romanorum votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-08901159.translation: Maximianus, a centurion of the II Augusta legion and commander of a unit of Roman citizens, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-08901159.summary: This records Maximianus, a centurion and commander of a unit of Roman citizens in the II Augusta legion, fulfilling a vow, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-08901161.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto Gaius Mettius Sabinus miles cohortis I Aeliae Athoitarum et Berecorum luco Herculis votum et signum posuit\n
EDCS-08901161.translation: To Hercules the Unconquered, Gaius Mettius Sabinus, a soldier of the I Aelia cohort of Athoitae and Berecori, placed this vow and statue in the grove of Hercules.
EDCS-08901161.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Mettius Sabinus, a soldier in the I Aelia cohort, dedicating a statue to Hercules, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09100877.text_edition: \n Capitonius Priscus praefectus cohortis I Aquitanorum et tribunus cohortis I Cilicum\n
EDCS-09100877.translation: Capitonius Priscus, prefect of the I Aquitanorum cohort and tribune of the I Cilicum cohort.
EDCS-09100877.summary: This inscription records the military career of Capitonius Priscus, listing his commands as prefect and tribune of auxiliary cohorts, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09100878.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Gaiae coniugi suae vivae petenti Iulius Iulianus summus curator cohortis I Cilicum memoriam fecit\n
EDCS-09100878.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Julius Julianus, chief administrator of the I Cilicum cohort, made this memorial for his wife, Valeria Gaia, while he was still alive, at her request.
EDCS-09100878.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Julius Julianus for his wife Valeria Gaia, recording his own military position as chief administrator of the I Cilicum cohort, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09100880.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saturininus sic serbus sic Iuli Faustini tribuni\n
EDCS-09100880.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Saturninus, slave and son of the tribune Julius Faustinus.
EDCS-09100880.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Saturninus, a slave and son of a tribune named Julius Faustinus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09100882.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate X imperator VI pater patriae consul V fecit [sub] Lucio Fabio Iusto legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-09100882.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 10th time, imperator for the 6th time, father of the country, consul for the 5th time, made this, under Lucius Fabius Justus, his legate with proconsular authority.
EDCS-09100882.summary: This inscription records a public work commissioned by Emperor Trajan, likely during his reign (98-117 AD), mentioning his titles and a provincial governor.

EDCS-09100883.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XI et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate II<I> et Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus restituerunt per Caium Ovinium Tertullum legatum Auggustorum pro praetore V\n
EDCS-09100883.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 11th time, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus restored this through Gaius Ovinivius Tertullus, Legate of the Augusti, Pro Praetore, for the 5th time.
EDCS-09100883.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, executed by Legate Gaius Ovinivius Tertullus, likely during their joint rule in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09100884.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XI pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate II<I> et Lucius [Septimius Geta] Caesar Augustus restituerunt per Gaium Ovinium Tertullum legatum pro praetore II\n
EDCS-09100884.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 11th time, Father of the Country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus restored this through Gaius Ovinivius Tertullus, Legate, Pro Praetore, for the 2nd time.
EDCS-09100884.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, executed by Legate Gaius Ovinivius Tertullus, likely during their joint rule in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09100886.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Caesaribus Publio Licinnio sic Valeriano et Publio Licinnio sic Gallieniano Piis Felicibus Invictis Augustis PP\n
EDCS-09100886.translation: To our Emperors Caesars, Publius Licinius Valerianus and Publius Licinius Gallienus, the Pious, Happy, Unconquered Augusti, Fathers of the Country.
EDCS-09100886.summary: This is a dedication to Emperors Valerian and Gallienus, acknowledging their titles and divine favor, dating to their joint rule in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09100887.text_edition: \n ddominis nnnostris Flavio Valerio Constantino et Valerio Liciniano Licinio PPiis FFelicibus Auggustis et Flavio Valerio Crispo et Liciniano Licinio iuniori et Flavio Claudio Constantino nobilissimis Caessaribus milia passuum II\n
EDCS-09100887.translation: To our Lords, Flavius Valerius Constantinus and Valerius Licinianus Licinius, the Pious, Happy Augusti, and Flavius Valerius Crispus and Licinianus Licinius the Younger and Flavius Claudius Constantinus, most noble Caesars, for 2 miles.
EDCS-09100887.summary: This inscription marks a distance of two miles dedicated to Emperors Constantine the Great, Licinius, and their sons Crispus and Constantine II, dating to their period of co-rule in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-09100889.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Villanus ex decurione alae votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-09100889.translation: Publius Aelius Villanus, formerly a decurion of the cavalry, willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-09100889.summary: This is a votive inscription by Publius Aelius Villanus, a former cavalry officer, who dedicated it willingly, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09200008.text_edition: \n Marcus Aretio fecit Alexander Domitiani fecit\n
EDCS-09200008.translation: Marcus Aretius made this. Alexander Domitiani made this.
EDCS-09200008.summary: This inscription records that two individuals, Marcus Aretius and Alexander Domitiani, each created something, probably a monument or object, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09300032.text_edition: \n Iulius Val ex burgariis veteranus Herculenti sic Ripensi ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-09300032.translation: Julius Val, a veteran from the burgarii (a type of soldier or local guard) of Hercules Ripensis, set this up according to his vow.
EDCS-09300032.summary: This is a votive inscription by Julius Val, a veteran associated with Hercules Ripensis, likely set up in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09300034.text_edition: \n QVAERENlegionum adiutricum obitus Valeria digne locutus militiam coluit legionis II Herculiae Valerius Onesimus centurio qui vixit annos quinquaginta et menses septem Valeria Marcellina coniux pientissima superstans cum filibus sic suuos sic hunc titulum posuerunt avete viatores\n
EDCS-09300034.translation: Valerius Onesimus, a centurion of the II Herculian Legion, who lived fifty years and seven months, and whose wife Valeria Marcellina, most devoted, along with her sons, erected this tombstone, spoke worthily of the fallen soldiers of the legions, and diligently pursued military service. Greetings, travelers!
EDCS-09300034.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Valerius Onesimus, a centurion of the II Herculian Legion, detailing his age and military service, erected by his wife and sons, dating from the late Roman period.

EDCS-09300035.text_edition: \n Pro caritatem sic func sum egi vitam sene sic crimine gessi opservavi sic famam podore sic sapientia mea Flavius Tatianus vixi nnos LXI menses V dies sex horas VI et qui missus annis XV et post mortem decoravistis honore et tu Claudia coniux\n
EDCS-09300035.translation: For the affection, I have lived this life without crime, I have observed my reputation with honor and my wisdom. Flavius Tatianus lived 61 years, 5 months, 6 days, and 6 hours, and after death you honored me with distinction, and you, Claudia, my wife.
EDCS-09300035.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavius Tatianus, detailing his lifespan and virtues, and mentioning the honor bestowed upon him after death and his devoted wife Claudia, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-09300036.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Victorinus biarchus qui militavit in sacro palatio nnos VII vixit nnos XL qui in proelio Roamnorum sic Calchedonia contra adversarios decessit honoris gratia sanxit ut perpetuo se honorari sive honesta reliquiarum sepultura consecrari videatur hu<h>ic Matrona coniux pientissima viva se sibi et bene merenti conpari suo memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-09300036.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Valerius Victorinus, a biarchus (an officer) who served in the sacred palace for 7 years and lived 40 years, who died in battle against the Romans against the enemies at Chalcedon, was honored so that he might be honored perpetually or seem to be consecrated by honorable burial of his remains. Matrona, his most devoted wife, while alive, set up this memorial for herself and for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-09300036.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Valerius Victorinus, a biarchus who served in the imperial palace and died in battle, erected by his devoted wife Matrona, likely dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-09300037.text_edition: \n Valerius Valens et Saturnina Saturnini Decio Valenti qui vixit annis XIIII et Saturnino Valentis qui vixit annos VIIII et Varronni sic Valentis qui vixit annos V natis carissimis et sibi memoriam posuerunt qui defuncti sunt nati supra scripti intra menses VII have viator\n
EDCS-09300037.translation: Valerius Valens and Saturnina, daughter of Saturninus, set up this memorial for Decius Valentis, who lived 14 years, and Saturninus Valens, who lived 9 years, and Varro Valens, who lived 5 years, their dearest children, and for themselves; the above-named died within 7 months. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-09300037.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Valerius Valens and Saturnina for their three young sons and themselves, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300038.text_edition: \n Valeates sic vos qui superis setis sic et coletes sic Manes tures sic quia vos ad nos venituri sic setis sic Aurelius Sisinus paganus vixit annis super LXX et superis de suo et conugi \n
EDCS-09300038.translation: Farewell, you who are living, and pay respects to the gods below, as you too will come to us. Aurelius Sisinus, a villager, lived more than 70 years and [left something] for the gods above and for his spouse.
EDCS-09300038.summary: This epitaph records the death of Aurelius Sisinus, a villager over 70 years old, and his contributions, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09300039.text_edition: \n Deo sancto aeterno providentia cuius locus et aniorum Valens MINs veteranus I vixillationis! equitum scutariorumV M et ex voto pro salute suam sic filiorum suorum R suorum et pro solida tutela Riae popularium gratias refert deo\n
EDCS-09300039.translation: To the holy, eternal God, by whose providence this place [stands]. Valens, former soldier of the MINs vexillation of scutarii cavalry, gives thanks to God for his own health, for that of his sons, and for the complete protection of the populace, fulfilling a vow.
EDCS-09300039.summary: This dedication is made by Valens, a former cavalry soldier, to an eternal God, expressing gratitude for health and protection and fulfilling a vow, likely from the 3rd-4th century AD.

EDCS-09300663.text_edition: \n Quintus Philippicus Quinti filius Maecia Edessa signifer legionis V vixit annos XXXX hic situs est ex testamento fieri iussit heres faciendum curavit arbitratu Antoni architecti et Titi coriarii militavit annos XX\n
EDCS-09300663.translation: Quintus Philippicus, son of Quintus, of the Maecia tribe, standard-bearer of the 5th legion, lived 40 years. He is buried here. He ordered by his will that this be made, and his heir had it done under the supervision of Antonius, architect, and Titus, a leatherworker. He served 20 years.
EDCS-09300663.summary: This tombstone records the life and death of Quintus Philippicus, a standard-bearer in the 5th legion, who served for 20 years and lived to 40, with his heir fulfilling his will, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300664.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Veturio Vero militi legionis V Macedonicae vixit nnos XIX menses VIII dies XXIIII militavit annum I Caius Veturius filio \n
EDCS-09300664.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Veturius Verus, soldier of the 5th Macedonian legion, lived 19 years, 8 months, and 24 days. He served for 1 year. Caius Veturius [erected this] for his son.
EDCS-09300664.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Caius Veturius Verus, a soldier in the 5th Macedonian legion who died at the age of 19 and served for only one year, erected by his father, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300665.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Respectae coniugi bene merenti annos XXXV Marcus Corienius Colon[us ------\n
EDCS-09300665.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Ulpia Respecta, a most deserving wife, [who lived] 35 years. Marcus Corienius Colon[us] [erected this].
EDCS-09300665.summary: This tombstone honors Ulpia Respecta, who died at 35, and was erected by her husband, Marcus Corienius Colonus, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300666.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ius XX Aurelia Marcia coniugi pientissimo una cum filiis suis bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-09300666.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Marcia placed [this] for her most devoted wife, who lived 20 years, together with her well-deserving children.
EDCS-09300666.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Aurelia Marcia for her devoted wife and their well-deserving children, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300667.text_edition: \n ------ vi]xit annos V Titius Macianus coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09300667.translation: [She] lived 5 years. Titius Macianus made [this] for his deserving wife.
EDCS-09300667.summary: This epitaph records the death of a wife at the age of 5 and was made by her husband, Titius Macianus, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300668.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus PIASPI qui burgarius annos L\n
EDCS-09300668.translation: To the spirits of the departed. PIASPI, who was a burgarius, [lived] 50 years.
EDCS-09300668.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of PIASPI, identified as a 'burgarius', at the age of 50, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300669.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Septimius veteranus vixit nnos LXIII menses VI dies XVI Aelia Domnaa coniux et Valerii Valerianus et Vitalis et Martialis patri dignissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-09300669.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Septimius, veteran, lived 63 years, 6 months, and 16 days. His wife Aelia Domna and his sons Valerius Valerianus, Vitalis, and Martialis erected this for their most worthy father.
EDCS-09300669.summary: This tombstone honors Valerius Septimius, a veteran who lived 63 years, erected by his wife Aelia Domna and their sons, likely from the 2nd-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300670.text_edition: \n DIVA Claudia coniux et Marcus Sev<e=I>rus et Salvius et Severa et Claudia patri bene meriteo sic posuerunt fecit vale lector sit tibi terra levis \n
EDCS-09300670.translation: Diva Claudia, his wife, and Marcus Severus, Salvius, Severa, and Claudia erected this for their deserving father. Farewell, reader, may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-09300670.summary: A funerary epitaph for a father, erected by his wife and children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09300671.translation: Ulpius and others erected this for their deserving mother.
EDCS-09300671.summary: A funerary epitaph for a mother, erected by her children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09300678.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaius Valerius Herculanus veteranus ex statore praefecti alae II Aravacorum vixit nnos LXX natus vico Rami militavit annos XXVI Gaia Valeria C coniux vixit annos LX nata loco eodem quem habeo in conubio annos Gaius Valerius et Gaius Valerius Valentinus QVA VS STD maemoriam!? aeternam titulum in posuerunt\n
EDCS-09300678.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Valerius Herculanus, veteran, formerly a scout for the prefect of the II Aravacorum cavalry wing, lived 70 years. Gaia Valeria, his wife, lived 60 years, born in the same place, whom I had in marriage for many years. Gaius Valerius and Gaius Valerius Valentinus erected this eternal memorial.
EDCS-09300678.summary: A funerary epitaph for a veteran and his wife, detailing their lives and the duration of their marriage, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09401191.text_edition: \n Quattuor Ventis et Bono Evento per quibus sic constat ani male genus bene gubernantes sanctis sacrum Caius Antonius Herculanus veteranus legionis XI Claudiae ex custode armorum vicanis donum dedit quia tunc sacrum conveniunt Valerius Villanus sacerdos Publicius Priscus Iulius Crispinus veteranus Iulius Iulianus veteranus Aurelius Asclas veteranus Cocceius Herculanus veteranus Valerius Valerianus veteranus Cornelius Lucius veteranus Aelius Aelianus veteranus Flavius Gaius CVI Flavius Valerius Valerius Quintus Valerius Apollinaris Cocceius Vale Sestius Senpronius Valerius Valentin Valerius Pudi Pompeiano et Avito consulibus Pompeiano et Avito consulibus\n
EDCS-09401191.translation: To the Four Winds and Good Fortune, who thus ensure the safe guidance of souls. Sacred offering by Caius Antonius Herculanus, veteran of the XI Claudia legion, former keeper of arms, to the villagers who gather here. Valerius Villanus, priest; Publicius Priscus, veteran; Iulius Crispinus, veteran; Iulius Iulianus, veteran; Aurelius Asclas, veteran; Cocceius Herculanus, veteran; Valerius Valerianus, veteran; Cornelius Lucius, veteran; Aelius Aelianus, veteran; Flavius Gaius; Flavius Valerius; Valerius Quintus; Valerius Apollinaris; Cocceius Vale; Sestius Senpronius; Valerius Valentin; Valerius Pudi; consuls Pompeianus and Avitus.
EDCS-09401191.summary: A dedication to various deities by a veteran of the XI Claudia legion, listing several other veterans and local officials, dated to the consulship of Pompeianus and Avitus (likely late 3rd century AD).

EDCS-09401192.text_edition: \n Mercurio Sancto sacrum pro salute Auggustorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini et Publi Septimi Getae Caesaris Gaius Antonius Herculanus veteranus legionis XI Claudiae ex custode armorum veteranis consistentibus huiius loci donum dedit\n
EDCS-09401192.translation: Sacred to Mercury the Holy, for the health of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and Publius Septimius Geta Caesar. Caius Antonius Herculanus, veteran of the XI Claudia legion, former keeper of arms, dedicated this to the veterans residing in this place.
EDCS-09401192.summary: A dedication to Mercury by a veteran of the XI Claudia legion, invoking the health of Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09401193.text_edition: \n Caesar Germanicus maximus Dacicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus filius eius m<i>lliaria nova poni iusseru[nt ------\n
EDCS-09401193.translation: Caesar Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, and his son ordered new mile markers to be erected [---].
EDCS-09401193.summary: A fragmentary inscription indicating an order by an emperor and his son to erect new mile markers, likely referring to Emperor Trajan and his adopted son Hadrian.

EDCS-09401194.text_edition: \n irius legatus Augusti pro praetore templum Dianae Plestrensis vetustate conlapsum restituit per Iulium [] |(centurionem) legionis XI Claudiae regionarium\n
EDCS-09401194.translation: [...]rius, imperial legate with praetorian powers, restored the temple of Diana of Plestra, which had collapsed from age, through Iulius [], centurion of the XI Claudia legion, a regional official.
EDCS-09401194.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the restoration of a temple to Diana by a Roman governor, mentioning a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09401195.text_edition: \n ius M et Aureli- et Ulpi- \n
EDCS-09401195.summary: A very fragmentary inscription containing names, possibly part of a larger dedication or epitaph, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09401566.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto patri patriae proconsuli a Sacicidave sic milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-09401566.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, father of the fatherland, proconsul, four miles.
EDCS-09401566.summary: An inscription recording the distance of four miles to an imperial authority, likely Emperor Decius, from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09700321.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Minervae Matri Patri urbi Romae aeternae ceterisquae sic diis immortalibus Lucius Flavius Luci filius Palatina Victor Ostia primus pilus legionis XI Claudiae Alexandrianae cum Lucio Flavio Italo filio equite Romano votum solvit\n
EDCS-09700321.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina, Minerva Mother Fatherland, and the eternal city of Rome, and to all other immortal gods, Lucius Flavius Victor, son of Lucius Flavius, from the Palatine tribe, primus pilus (first spear) of the XI Claudia Alexandriana legion, together with his son Lucius Flavius Italus, a Roman knight, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09700321.summary: A dedication to various gods by a high-ranking officer (primus pilus) of the XI Claudia legion and his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09700329.text_edition: \n Apollini sac(rum) Iulius Favor |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae sacravit pro se et suis ex voto fecit\n
EDCS-09700329.translation: Sacred to Apollo. Iulius Favor, centurion of the eleventh Claudian legion, dedicated this from his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-09700329.summary: This is a dedication to Apollo by a centurion of the eleventh Claudian legion, made in fulfillment of a vow. Dated likely to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09700330.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Victor Herculani vixit annos XXII- Marcia ucxor sic Hercu<la>nus Scorinis ucxori sic filio pientissimis posuit\n
EDCS-09700330.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Victor Herculani lived 22 years. Marcia, his wife, and Herculanus Scorinis, his son, erected this for their most devoted wife and son.
EDCS-09700330.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Victor Herculani at age 22, with the monument erected by his wife and son. Dated likely to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09700331.text_edition: \n I vixit annis LX Iulia Antonia coniux bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-09700331.translation: I lived 60 years. Iulia Antonia, his wife, erected this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-09700331.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of an unnamed man at age 60, with the monument erected by his wife, Iulia Antonia. Dated likely to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09700340.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus II imperator Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis XVI<I> consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Armeniacus Augustus tribuniciae potestatis \n
EDCS-09700340.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus II Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time; and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Armeniacus Augustus, with tribunician power.
EDCS-09700340.summary: This inscription records the titles and honors of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Commodus) and Lucius Aurelius Verus, indicating a date around 178 CE based on their tribunician powers.

EDCS-09700744.text_edition: \n Dianae sacrum pro salute Marci Catoni Vindicis legati Augusti pro praetore Quintus Valerius Atinianus beneficiarius consularis ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-09700744.translation: Sacred to Diana. Quintus Valerius Atinianus, a beneficiarius of the consular governor, dedicated this from his vow for the health of Marcus Catonius Vindex, governor with the rank of legate Augusti pro praetore.
EDCS-09700744.summary: This is a votive offering to Diana made by a military subordinate (beneficiarius) for the health of his superior, the governor Marcus Catonius Vindex. Dated likely to the late 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701190.text_edition: \n Iulius Ferox primus pilus legionis XI Claudiae iussu viri clarissimi Servili Fabiani consularis nostri terminum posui in locum Subiati secundum sententias praeceden[tes ------\n
EDCS-09701190.translation: I, Iulius Ferox, primus pilus of the eleventh Claudian legion, by order of the most distinguished Servilius Fabianus, our consular governor, set up this boundary marker in the place of Subiatus, according to preceding judgments [---
EDCS-09701190.summary: This is a boundary marker set up by Iulius Ferox, a senior centurion, under the authority of the consular governor Servilius Fabianus, to delineate a specific area. Dated likely to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701191.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Rufus veteranus ex vico Vorovo Minore vivo suo sibi et coniugi suae Aureliae Zuraturmeni memoriam fecit insuper solum suum qui est annorum LXV valeat viator vibat sic qui leget\n
EDCS-09701191.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Valerius Rufus, a veteran from the village of Vorovo Minor, made this memorial for himself and his wife Aurelia Zuraturmeni while still alive; moreover, [he made it for] his own property. He is 65 years old. Farewell, traveler, live well, as will he who reads this.
EDCS-09701191.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a living veteran, Valerius Rufus, for himself, his wife Aurelia Zuraturmeni, and his property, dated to when he was 65 years old. Dated likely to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701195.text_edition: \n Dianae et Apollini sacrum Gaius Valerius Valens optio legionis XI Claudiae agens regione Montanensium votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09701195.translation: Sacred to Diana and Apollo. Gaius Valerius Valens, optio of the eleventh Claudian legion, serving in the Montanensian region, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09701195.summary: This is a votive dedication to Diana and Apollo by Gaius Valerius Valens, an optio in the eleventh Claudian legion, from the Montanensian region. Dated likely to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701196.text_edition: \n Deae Dianae domino nostro Co[mmodo] quod ex donariis in templo Ennius Repertus simulacrum argenteum N\n
EDCS-09701196.translation: To the goddess Diana, our lord Commodus, because from the donations in the temple, Ennius Repertus [dedicated?] a silver statue.
EDCS-09701196.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a silver statue to the goddess Diana and Emperor Commodus, likely funded by temple donations by Ennius Repertus. Dated to the reign of Commodus (177-192 CE).

EDCS-10701882.translation: Aufidia Avita erected this for herself while alive.
EDCS-10701882.summary: A self-funded funerary monument erected by Aufidia Avita during her lifetime. Dated likely to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201132.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli II designato patri patriae proconsuli reparatori disciplinae militaris fundatori sacrae urbis firmatori SPES FO \n
EDCS-11201132.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul designate for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, restorer of military discipline, founder of the sacred city, strengthener of hope.
EDCS-11201132.summary: This is a titulature inscription of Emperor Decius, likely from a public monument, praising his titles and achievements.

EDCS-11300004.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Sambatis habens ius liberorum vixit nnos XXV menses V dies XII animam rededit sic cui gemens Victorinus maritus ave vale viator\n
EDCS-11300004.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Sambatis, who had the ius liberorum, lived 25 years, 5 months, 12 days, and gave up her soul. Thus, her grieving husband Victorinus says: Hail and farewell, traveler.
EDCS-11300004.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Sambatis, recording her age and expressing her husband Victorinus's grief.

EDCS-11300105.text_edition: \n equitis vixillationis sic Aegissensis sic Valerius Proclus miles legionis I Ioviae pro parentibus ex prae<c=L>epto Deci praepositi \n
EDCS-11300105.translation: Valerius Proclus, a soldier of the I Iovian Legion, from the vexillation of Aegyssensis, for his parents, by order of Decius, the prefect.
EDCS-11300105.summary: This is an inscription recording a soldier of the I Iovian Legion, Valerius Proclus, acting on behalf of his parents under the command of Prefect Decius.

EDCS-11300172.text_edition: \n vixit annis XXV Z V SAVS ERB\n
EDCS-11300172.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the lifespan of an individual.

EDCS-11300666.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXIII consuli IIII patri patriae Iulio Statilio Severo legato Augusti pro praetore ab Axiupoli milia passuum VI\n
EDCS-11300666.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 23rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, by Julius Statilius Severus, governor with the rank of Augustal legate. Six miles from Axiupoli.
EDCS-11300666.summary: This inscription records a road marker, likely indicating a distance of six miles from Axiupoli, dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius and commissioned by his governor Julius Statilius Severus.

EDCS-11300667.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XI pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate II<I> et [Publius] [Septimius Geta Caesar] [Augustus] restituerunt per Caium Ovinium Tertullum legatum pro praetore \n
EDCS-11300667.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 11th time, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, and [Publius] [Septimius Geta Caesar] [Augustus] restored this through Gaius Ovinnius Tertullus, governor with the rank of Augustal legate.
EDCS-11300667.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, overseen by the governor Gaius Ovinnius Tertullus.

EDCS-11300668.text_edition: \n Imperante domino nostro Severo Alexandro consule III et Cassio Dione II consule Iulius Vitalis |(centurio) legionis ex praecepto viri clarissimi Mantenni consularis nostri terminos <f=E>ixit \n
EDCS-11300668.translation: While our Lord Severus Alexander was consul for the 3rd time and Cassius Dio was consul for the 2nd time, Julius Vitalis, a centurion of the legion, by order of the most distinguished vir clarissimus Mantennius, our governor, set these boundary markers.
EDCS-11300668.summary: This is a boundary marker inscription set by a centurion, Julius Vitalis, under the governorship of Mantennius and during the consulship of Severus Alexander and Cassius Dio.

EDCS-11300670.text_edition: \n Titus Iulius Titi filius Fabia Saturninus praefectus vehiculorum tribunus militum legionis I Italicae praefectus cohortis I Claudiae equitatae scriba tribunicius apparitor Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aelii Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii patris patriae conductor Illyrici utriusque et Ripae Thracicae\n
EDCS-11300670.translation: Titus Julius Saturninus, son of Titus, of the Fabia tribe, prefect of the transport services, military tribune of the I Italica Legion, prefect of the I Claudia Equitata Cohort, a clerk of the tribunes, attendant of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, contractor of both Illyricums and the Thracian bank.
EDCS-11300670.summary: This is a lengthy career inscription detailing the extensive public service of Titus Julius Saturninus under Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-11300673.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Tonanti et Iunoni Reginae et pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Antonino sic Augusti Pii et Veri Aurelii Caesare sic posuit Aelius Cara\n
EDCS-11300673.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Thunderer, and to Juno Regina, and for the safety of the Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and Verus Caesar, Aelius Cara erected this.
EDCS-11300673.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter and Juno, also praying for the safety of Emperors Antoninus Pius and Verus Caesar.

EDCS-11300674.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Tonanti et Iunoni Reginae et pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Antonini Pii Augusti Marci Aureli Veri Caesarais posu\n
EDCS-11300674.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Thunderer, and to Juno Regina, and for the safety of the Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar, he erected this.
EDCS-11300674.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter and Juno, also praying for the safety of Emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar.

EDCS-11300675.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae <e=F>t pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Veri et Luci Veri posuit Eftacentus sic Biti magister Aproniano II et Paullo II consulibus \n
EDCS-11300675.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Juno Regina, Eftacentus, as master of the neighborhood quarter, set this up for the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Verus and Lucius Verus, in the consulship of Apronianus II and Paullus II.
EDCS-11300675.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno made by a neighborhood official for the safety of emperors Marcus Aurelius Verus and Lucius Verus, dated to their second consulship (166 AD).

EDCS-11300676.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Fortunae Reduci signis cohortis I Germanorum Lucius Atilius Luci filius Quirina \n
EDCS-11300676.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Fortuna Redux, Lucius Atilius, son of Lucius, of the Voltinian tribe, [dedicated this] for the standards of the First Cohort of Germans.
EDCS-11300676.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Fortuna Redux by Lucius Atilius for the standards of the First Cohort of Germans, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11300677.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae pro salute Imperatoris Aureli Veri Commodi Genium Vici PSV \n
EDCS-11300677.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Juno Regina, for the safety of Emperor Aurelius Verus Commodus, [this dedication to] the Genius of the Neighborhood Quarter.
