EDCS-11300677.summary: A dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and the Genius of the Neighborhood Quarter for the safety of Emperor Commodus, likely dating to his reign (177-192 AD).

EDCS-11300679.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae et pro salute Imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Augusti et Luci Septimi Antonini posuit Claudius Cocceius de suo X Kalendas Iulias Victorino et Severo consulibus\n
EDCS-11300679.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Juno Regina, and for the safety of Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Augustus and Lucius Septimius Antoninus, Claudius Cocceius set this up at his own expense, ten days before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Victorinus and Severus.
EDCS-11300679.summary: A private dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the safety of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, dated by consuls to 195 AD.

EDCS-11300682.text_edition: \n magister vici Scenopesis de suo posuit Pisone et Iuliano consulibus\n
EDCS-11300682.translation: The master of the neighborhood quarter set this up at his own expense, in the consulship of Piso and Julianus.
EDCS-11300682.summary: A record of an expenditure by the master of the neighborhood quarter, dated by consuls to sometime between 148 and 161 AD.

EDCS-11300683.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae et pro salut<e=I> Imperanttor<is=ERO> Augusti Antonini et Luci Veri peosuit Aelius Longinus veteranus alae II Aravacorum pro se NVSV libens \n
EDCS-11300683.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Juno Regina, and for the safety of Emperor Augustus Antoninus and Lucius Verus, Aelius Longinus, a veteran of the Second Ala of the Aravaci, willingly set this up for himself.
EDCS-11300683.summary: A private dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the safety of emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, made by a veteran of the Second Ala of Aravaci, likely dating to their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-11300684.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Cocceius Vitulus veteranus cohortis I Ubiorum signifer vixit nnos LXX militavit nnos XXVIII Claudia coniunx et Cocceius Veturius et Cocceius Nardus filii heredes faciendum curaverunt Marcus Cocceius Titio piissimo parenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11300684.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Cocceius Vitulus, a veteran of the First Cohort of Ubii, a standard-bearer, lived 70 years and served 28 years. Claudia, his wife, and his sons Cocceius Veturius and Cocceius Nardus, the heirs, saw to the erection of this monument. Marcus Cocceius Titio saw to the erection of this monument for his most devoted parent.
EDCS-11300684.summary: A tombstone recording the life and service of a veteran, Marcus Cocceius Vitulus, with his family erecting the monument, dated likely to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300686.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Basso patri et Tsinnae fratri Zura et Tsiru posuerunt vixit annos LXX Tsinna vixit annos XV et Martiae coniugi votum libens merito\n
EDCS-11300686.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Zura and Tsiru set up [this monument] for Basso, their father, and Tsinna, their brother. He lived 70 years. Tsinna lived 15 years. And for Martia, wife [of Basso]. [They set this up] willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300686.summary: A funerary inscription for Basso, his son Tsinna, and wife Martia, set up by other children, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300687.text_edition: \n Marcus Cocceius Vitalis Marcus Cocceius Albucius Marcus Cocceius Valerius Marcus Cocceius Vitalis Marcus Cocceius us cI \n
EDCS-11300687.translation: Marcus Cocceius Vitalis, Marcus Cocceius Albucius, Marcus Cocceius Valerius, Marcus Cocceius Vitalis, Marcus Cocceius [son of] us cI.
EDCS-11300687.summary: A list of names, possibly of family members or patrons, with the last entry being incomplete, date uncertain.

EDCS-11300688.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelius Pisio sibi et Batsini coniugi suae posuit titulum vibus sic vixit annis item coniux annis avete\n
EDCS-11300688.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cornelius Pisio set up this monument for himself and his wife Batsina. He lived 70 years, likewise his wife. Hail!
EDCS-11300688.summary: A tombstone set up by Cornelius Pisio for himself and his wife Batsina, recording their ages, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300689.text_edition: \n vixit annos Iulius Lucius coniugi bene merenti posuit vale viator\n
EDCS-11300689.translation: Julius Lucius placed this for his well-deserving wife. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-11300689.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Julius Lucius for his wife, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300690.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus have domina Mammusa sic vixit nnos XXX Caius Veturius Tertius bene meritae coniugi Iuliae Veneriae fecit sibique vivo et Veturiae Furniae matri carissimae pientissimae posuit\n
EDCS-11300690.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Greetings, mistress Mammusa, who lived 30 years. Caius Veturius Tertius made this for his well-deserving wife, Julia Veneria, and also set it up for himself while alive and for his dearest, most devoted mother, Veturia Furnia.
EDCS-11300690.summary: A funerary monument erected by Caius Veturius Tertius for his wife Julia Veneria and his mother Veturia Furnia, also reserving a place for himself.

EDCS-11300691.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ave domina Veturia vixit nnos XXIIII menses II Marcus Ulpius Piso Veturiae sibique vivo et Veturiae Ulpiae et Marco Ulpio filiis carissimis vivis posuit\n
EDCS-11300691.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Greetings, mistress Veturia, who lived 22 years and 2 months. Marcus Ulpius Piso placed this for Veturia, and also for himself while alive, and for his dearest children Veturia Ulpia and Marcus Ulpius, who are living.
EDCS-11300691.summary: A funerary epitaph by Marcus Ulpius Piso for his wife Veturia and their living children, Veturia Ulpia and Marcus Ulpius.

EDCS-11300692.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fabricia Quinti filia Saturnina pia vixit nnos XXI Caius Munatius Venustus praefectus cohortis I Germanorum coniugi piissimae posuit\n
EDCS-11300692.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Fabricia Saturnina, daughter of Quintus, pious, who lived 21 years. Caius Munatius Venustus, prefect of the First Cohort of Germans, placed this for his most pious wife.
EDCS-11300692.summary: A funerary inscription by Caius Munatius Venustus, a military officer, for his pious wife Fabricia Saturnina.

EDCS-11300693.text_edition: \n ISEI Onisis Vnis Valerius Tertullus Valerius Campanus patri pientissimo posuerunt de suo ave viator\n
EDCS-11300693.translation: To Isis. Onisis. Valerius Tertullus and Valerius Campanus placed this from their own funds for their most devoted father. Greetings, traveler.
EDCS-11300693.summary: A dedication to the goddess Isis, funded by Valerius Tertullus and Valerius Campanus for their father.

EDCS-11300694.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fe magister dedicante coniuge Valeria Casta et nati et fratres posuerunt opto sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-11300694.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fe magister dedicated this, with his wife Valeria Casta, his children, and his brothers also dedicating it. I hope the earth is light upon you.
EDCS-11300694.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Fe magister, his wife, children, and brothers.

EDCS-11300695.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Flaviae filiae quae vixit annis XVIII menses sex Ulpius Flavius pater et Arria Cassia mater posuerunt sibique vivis\n
EDCS-11300695.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Ulpia Flavia, daughter, who lived 18 years and six months. Ulpius Flavius, father, and Arria Cassia, mother, placed this, and for themselves while living.
EDCS-11300695.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Ulpius Flavius and Arria Cassia for their deceased daughter Ulpia Flavia, also reserving space for themselves.

EDCS-11300697.text_edition: \n Tunc vixi bene vixi sine nulla sic crimina sic vixi resta viator Agrilia Trygitiani vixit con sic virginio nnos XII et moritur nnorum XXXV et demisit natos III Aurelius Gaius posuit memoria coniugi suae resta viator lege titolo sic\n
EDCS-11300697.translation: I lived well, I lived without any accusations. So I lived. Rest, traveler. Agrilia Trygitiani, who lived 12 years with Virginio, died at the age of 35 and left 3 children. Aurelius Gaius placed this in memory of his wife. Rest, traveler, read the inscription.
EDCS-11300697.summary: A funerary inscription by Aurelius Gaius for his wife Agrilia Trygitiani, detailing her life and death, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-11300699.summary: A fragment of a funerary inscription mentioning the name Euticus.

EDCS-11300711.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Cereri Frugiferae Veturius Tertius magister vici de suo posuit\n
EDCS-11300711.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to Juno, Ceres the Fruit-Bearer. Veturius Tertius, master of the district, placed this from his own funds.
EDCS-11300711.summary: A religious dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and Ceres by Veturius Tertius, likely a local official or priest, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300712.text_edition: \n Imperante domino nostro Severo Alexandro consule III T- Cassio Dione II consule Iulius Vitales sic |(centurio) legionis ex praecepto viri clarissimi Mantenni Sabini consularis nostri terminos ficxit sic\n
EDCS-11300712.translation: Under the reign of our lord Severus Alexander, in his third consulship, and of T. Cassius Dio, in his first consulship, Julius Vitales, a centurion of the legion, by order of the most distinguished man, Mannius Sabinus, our consular governor, erected these boundary markers.
EDCS-11300712.summary: A boundary marker erected by Julius Vitales, a legionary centurion, under the consuls Severus Alexander and Cassius Dio, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300714.text_edition: \n Fines pertinentes ad Tiberium Claudium Firminum\n
EDCS-11300714.translation: Boundaries belonging to Tiberius Claudius Firminus.
EDCS-11300714.summary: An inscription marking the property boundaries of Tiberius Claudius Firminus, likely Roman period.

EDCS-11300717.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salutem sic Marci Aureli Veri et Luci Veri Augusti et sua civis sic Romanis et Bessis consistentibus vico Ulmeto Flavius Germanus magister vici posuit de suo VII Kalendas Iulias Pontio Laeliano Caesennio Sospite consulibus\n
EDCS-11300717.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the safety of Marcus Aurelius Verus and Lucius Verus Augustus and his own, Flavius Germanus, priest of the village, with Roman citizens and the Bessians residing in the village of Ulmetum, placed this at his own expense on the seventh day before the Kalends of July, when Pontius Laelianus Caesennius Sospes was consul.
EDCS-11300717.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno by Flavius Germanus, a village priest, for the safety of the emperors and the community of Ulmetum, dated to 22 June, 169 CE.

EDCS-11300718.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum et Iunoni Reginae pro salutem sic Imperatoris Aureli Antonini Martius Philo magister vici Ulmet<e=I>nsium posuit de suo et Bessis sic VI Nonas Iulias Orfito et Maximo consulibus \n
EDCS-11300718.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the safety of the emperor Aurelius Antoninus, Martius Philo, priest of the village of Ulmetum, placed this at his own expense and that of the Bessians, on the sixth day before the Nones of July, when Orfitus and Maximus were consuls.
EDCS-11300718.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno by Martius Philo, priest of Ulmetum, for the emperor's safety, dated to 2 July, 149 CE.

EDCS-11300720.text_edition: \n Consacrani Silvani Satoris OVICCAIIAIIACAPINII posuerunt aram Silvano nomine suo per Valerio sic Valeriano sic quaestorem Dometium sic Consulem Kalandis sic Iunis Orfito Rufo\n
EDCS-11300720.translation: The fellow devotees of Silvanus Sator, the Oviccaiiaiiacapinii, erected this altar to Silvanus in their own name, through Valerius Valerianus, quaestor, and Dometius, consul, on the Kalends of June, with Orfitus Rufus.
EDCS-11300720.summary: An altar dedicated to Silvanus by a group of devotees, mentioning officials Valerius Valerianus and Dometius, and dated to 1 June, possibly in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11300721.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Sancto Silvano pro salute Inperatoris sic et salute consacranorum Flavius Augustales sic de suo posuit aram et tabulam memoria sua Aproniano et Bradua consulibus die Nonarum Iuniarom sic\n
EDCS-11300721.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Holy Silvanus, for the safety of the Emperor and the safety of the fellow devotees, Flavius Augustales placed this altar and tablet at his own expense for his memory, when Apronianus and Bradua were consuls, on the Nones of June.
EDCS-11300721.summary: A dedication by Flavius Augustales to Jupiter, Silvanus, and the emperor, including a memorial tablet, dated to 5 June, 184 CE.

EDCS-11300723.text_edition: \n Iovi et Iunoni Iulius Teres magistrans vico Ulmeto aram posuit de suo pro salute suam sic et filiorum sui et vicanorum anno suo fecit\n
EDCS-11300723.translation: To Jupiter and Juno, Julius Teres, a magistrate of the village of Ulmetum, placed this altar at his own expense for his own safety, and that of his sons, and of the villagers, in his own year.
EDCS-11300723.summary: An altar dedicated by Julius Teres, a village magistrate, to Jupiter and Juno for the safety of himself, his family, and the villagers of Ulmetum, dated by the inscription to his 'own year'.

EDCS-11300724.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Herculi pro salute sua et suorum Calventius Constans posuit\n
EDCS-11300724.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Hercules, for the safety of himself and his family, Calventius Constans erected this.
EDCS-11300724.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Hercules by Calventius Constans for the safety of his household, likely Roman period.

EDCS-11300726.text_edition: \n pro salute Luci Valeri Victorini et Ulpiae Nicandras et filiorum eorum Luci Valeri Victorini et Luci Valeri Turbonis et Luci Valeri Soteri Valerius Nilus actor ex votu<m=N> sic posuit \n
EDCS-11300726.translation: For the safety of Lucius Valerius Victorinus and Ulpia Nicandra and their sons Lucius Valerius Victorinus, Lucius Valerius Turbon, and Lucius Valerius Soter, Valerius Nilus, administrator, erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11300726.summary: A votive inscription by Valerius Nilus, an administrator, for the safety of the family of Lucius Valerius Victorinus and Ulpia Nicandra, likely Roman period.

EDCS-11300727.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Valerius Felicx sic pro salutem sic suorum et suam sic aram posuit Cocceius Helius scripsit\n
EDCS-11300727.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Valerius Felix placed this altar for the safety of his family and himself. Cocceius Helius wrote it.
EDCS-11300727.summary: An altar dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Valerius Felix for his family's safety, with the inscription written by Cocceius Helius, likely Roman period.

EDCS-11300729.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ithazis Dada titu<l>um sibi se vivivo et Ziftiae uxori suae defunctae posuit\n
EDCS-11300729.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ithazis Dada set up this monument to himself while still alive, and to his deceased wife, Ziftia.
EDCS-11300729.summary: A funerary monument set up by Ithazis Dada for himself and his late wife, Ziftia.

EDCS-11300730.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemili Postumini vixit annos XXXX Theodote coniux marito bene merenti et Barbarus et Theodorus et Iuliana patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11300730.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aemilius Postuminus lived 40 years. Theodote, his wife, and Barbarus, Theodorus, and Iuliana, his children, set this up for their deserving husband and father.
EDCS-11300730.summary: A funerary inscription for Aemilius Postuminus, set up by his wife and children, indicating his age at death.

EDCS-11300737.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Luci Septumnio sic Severi Aelius Aelianus magistratus vici Clementianesces sic aram de suo posuit Tertullo et Clement<e=I> consulibus \n
EDCS-11300737.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. For the health of the Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus. Aelius Aelianus, the magistrate of the Clementian neighborhood, set up this altar at his own expense, with Tertullus and Clementes as consuls.
EDCS-11300737.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the emperor's health, erected by a local magistrate during the consulship of Tertullus and Clementes.

EDCS-11300742.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Ulpius Deme<t>rius duplicarius singularis consularis alae II Aravacorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300742.translation: To the Unconquered God. Ulpius Demetrius, a duplicarius (a soldier receiving double pay) and singularis (an elite soldier attached to a commander) of the consular rank of the II Aravacorum cavalry ala, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300742.summary: A votive inscription to the Unconquered God by Ulpius Demetrius, a soldier of the II Aravacorum cavalry ala.

EDCS-11300743.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Eutyches vixit nnis LX et Valeria Iulia vixit annis XXV et Valeria Matrona vixit nno uno mensibus XI filias sic Valeriae Bonae et Valerius Bonus vixit annis V \n
EDCS-11300743.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Julius Eutyches lived 60 years, and Valeria Iulia lived 25 years, and Valeria Matrona lived 1 year and 11 months. [Daughters?] of Valeria Bona, and Valerius Bonus lived 5 years.
EDCS-11300743.summary: A funerary inscription listing the ages at death of several family members, including Julius Eutyches, Valeria Iulia, Valeria Matrona, and Valerius Bonus.

EDCS-11300746.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Quadratus decurio municipii \n
EDCS-11300746.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ulpius Quadratus, decurion of the municipality.
EDCS-11300746.summary: A funerary inscription for Ulpius Quadratus, a decurion in a municipality.

EDCS-11300748.text_edition: \n PI GI SI curaverunt POLOCO NET Iustinus CI Valentinus V TICCVIT IIELIS VISCVS ATV Secundini SCVS \n
EDCS-11300748.translation: POLOCO NET, Iustinus, VALENTINUS, and the others cared for [the monument]. VISCVS ATV Secundini SCVS.
EDCS-11300748.summary: A fragmented inscription indicating individuals who cared for a monument, possibly a tomb or public structure.

EDCS-11300749.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salute Imperatoris Augusti civitas et regio Histriae curam agentibus Claudio Amando et Ulpio Hectore et Aelio ge archontibus regionis\n
EDCS-11300749.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the health of the Emperor Augustus. The province and region of Histria, with Claudius Amandus, Ulpius Hector, and Aelius [names] serving as archons of the region.
EDCS-11300749.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the emperor's health, made by the province of Histria under the regional archons Claudius Amandus, Ulpius Hector, and Aelius.

EDCS-11300750.text_edition: \n I(ovi)? O(ptimo)? M(aximo) pro salute Impperatorum ddominorum nnostrorum Marci Iuli Philippi et Marco sic Iulio sic Philippiano sic nobilissimo! Caesari! et Otaciliae Severae Augustae archontens sic regionis Histriae sub archontatu Aureli Euxini Leontisci COCIEI Aurelius Victor Ulpius Martinus Aelius Iulius Aurelii Castus Mucatralis Tiosisis Messor Frontinus nisal Ingenuus Herculanus MarcianusCI F\n
EDCS-11300750.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the health of our Lords the Emperors Marcus Julius Philippus and Marcus Julius Philippianus, the most noble Caesar, and Augusta Otacilia Severa. The archons of the region of Histria, under the archonship of Aurelius Euxinus Leontiscus, [list of names] Aurelius Victor, Ulpius Martinus, Aelius Iulius, Aurelii Castus, Mucatralis, Tiosisis, Messor Frontinus, Nisal, Ingenuus, Herculanus, Marcianus, [son] of F.
EDCS-11300750.summary: A dedication to Jupiter for the health of Emperors Philippus I and II, and Empress Otacilia Severa, made by the archons of Histria during the archonship of Aurelius Euxinus Leontiscus.

EDCS-11300752.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus have viator hic siti sunt Victor v<e=I>teranus alae Dardanorum? Sio Valerius VMIV \n
EDCS-11300752.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Here lie Victor, a veteran of the Dardanian cavalry ala, and Valerius. VMIV.
EDCS-11300752.summary: A funerary inscription for Victor, a veteran of the Dardanian cavalry ala, and Valerius.

EDCS-11300756.text_edition: \n Imperatori Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae tribunicia potestate consuli III et Vero Caesari cives Romani consistentes canabis legionis V Macedonicae sub Minicio Natale legato Augusti pro praetore dedicante Cominio Secundo legato Augusti\n
EDCS-11300756.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, in his tribunician power, consul for the third time, and to Caesar Verus, the Roman citizens residing at the camp of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, under Minicius Natalis, legate of the emperor, governor, with Cominius Secundus, legate of the emperor, dedicated [this].
EDCS-11300756.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the citizens of the camp of the Fifth Legion Macedonica to Emperors Antoninus Pius and Verus, likely dating to the joint reign of these emperors.

EDCS-11300757.text_edition: \n sub Fuficio Cornuto legato Augusti pro praetore dedicante Quinto Caecilio Reddito legato Augusti Titus Flavius Alexander veteranus legionis V Macedonicae domu Fabia Ancyra quinquennalis canabensium cum Iulia Florentina uxore et Flaviis Alexandro Valente Pisone Maximilla Respecta filiis de sua posuit item |(denarios) CCL ob honorem quinquennalitatis curiae donavit ex quo \n
EDCS-11300757.translation: Under Fuficius Cornutus, legate of the emperor, governor, with Quintus Caecilius Reddiditus, legate of the emperor, Titus Flavius Alexander, a veteran of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, from Fabia, Ancyra, quinquennial magistrate of the camp residents, with his wife Julia Florentina and his children Flavius Alexander Valens, Pison, Maxilla, and Respecta, placed this from his own property. He also donated 250 denarii for the honor of the quinquennial magistracy of the curia, from the income of which...
EDCS-11300757.summary: This inscription details a donation by Titus Flavius Alexander, a veteran and quinquennial magistrate of the camp of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, for public works and honors, dating to the period of imperial legates.

EDCS-11300758.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii et Aureli Veri Caesaris sub Iulio Severo legato Augusti pro praetore dedicante Aellio Optato legato Augusti Lucius Licinius domo Nicopoli Clemens veteranus legionis V Macedonicae quinquennalis canabensium et decurio Troesmensium cum Licinia Veneria coniuge Lucia Licinia filia et Iulio Clemente et Octavio Clementiano et Licinia Clementiana et Octavio Clemente et Licinio Clemente et Octavio Licinio nepotibus de suo posuit et dedit curiae CCL ob honorem quinquennalitatis ex quorum incrementis omnibus decurionibus sportulae dividerentur\n
EDCS-11300758.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. For the health of the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and Caesar Aurelius Verus, under Julius Severus, legate of the emperor, governor, with Aelius Optatus, legate of the emperor, Lucius Licinius Clemens, a veteran of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, from Nicopolis, quinquennial magistrate of the camp residents, and decurion of the Troesmensians, with his wife Licinia Veneria, his daughter Lucia Licinia, and his grandchildren Iulius Clemens, Octavius Clementianus, Licinia Clementiana, Octavius Clemens, Licinius Clemens, and Octavius Licinius, placed and dedicated this from his own property. He also gave 250 denarii for the honor of the quinquennial magistracy, from whose income all the decurions were to receive a gift.
EDCS-11300758.summary: This dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus was made by Lucius Licinius Clemens, a veteran and official of the Fifth Legion Macedonica and the Troesmensian community, for the health of the emperors, likely during the reign of Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-11300759.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelio Aeliano quaestori aedili IIviro municipii Troesmensium Aurelius ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11300759.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Aurelius placed this according to his vow, to Aelius Aelianus, quaestor, aedile, and duumvir of the municipality of Troesmensium.
EDCS-11300759.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Aurelius to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, in honor of Aelius Aelianus, an official of the municipality of Troesmensium, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300761.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Firmo veterano legionis V Macedonicae ex c(ustode) armorum domo Nicia sic vixit nnos LX Decuria Procula coniux et Valerii Firminus et Firmianus filii posuerunt\n
EDCS-11300761.translation: To the departed spirits. Valerius Firmus, a veteran of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, former keeper of the arms, from Nicia. He lived 60 years. His wife Decuria Procula and his sons Valerius Firminus and Firmianus placed this.
EDCS-11300761.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Valerius Firmus, a veteran of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, set up by his wife and sons, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300762.translation: Amandus, discharged with an honorable release.
EDCS-11300762.summary: This is a very brief inscription recording the honorable discharge of a soldier named Amandus, likely from the Roman army.

EDCS-11300765.text_edition: \n Numini Liberis Patris Marcus Antistius Caecina eques alae I Dardanorum Antoninianae ex viso posuit\n
EDCS-11300765.translation: To the divine Father Liber. Marcus Antistius Caecina, an equestrian of the First Auxiliary Wing of Dardanorum Antoniniana, placed this according to his vision.
EDCS-11300765.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Father Liber by Marcus Antistius Caecina, an equestrian officer serving in the auxiliary cavalry, during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300768.text_edition: \n Invicto Mithrae sacrum Lucius Valerius Fuscus |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae libens votum solvit\n
EDCS-11300768.translation: To the unconquered Mithras. Lucius Valerius Fuscus, centurion of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300768.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Lucius Valerius Fuscus, a centurion of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, to the god Mithras, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300769.text_edition: \n Quod bonum faustum felix vicani Petrenses qui contulerunt causa salutis corporis sui balineum faciundum quod opus effectum magisterio anni Nymphidi Maximi et Aeli Gemini quaestoribus vici Ulpio Romano et Cassio Primitivo curantibus operi Nymphidio Maximo supra scripto et Aelio Iulio\n
EDCS-11300769.translation: That it may be good, fortunate, and happy. The villagers of Petrenses, who contributed for the health of their bodies, to have a bathhouse built, which work was completed under the magistracy of Nymphidius Maximus and Aelius Geminius, quaestors, with Ulpius Romanus and Cassius Primitivus, village supervisors, for the work of Nymphidius Maximus, mentioned above, and Aelius Iulius.
EDCS-11300769.summary: This inscription details the construction of a bathhouse by the villagers of Petrenses for their health, under the supervision of various officials, likely during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300770.text_edition: \n R se vivi A Valerius Valens miles legionis XI Claudiae militavit annos III vixit nnos XXII filio bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11300770.translation: While alive. Valerius Valens, a soldier of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, served for 3 years, lived for 22 years. His son placed this for him, a well-deserving soldier.
EDCS-11300770.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Valerius Valens, a soldier of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, set up by his son, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300771.text_edition: \n Quintus Marcius Quadratus natione Libycus vixit annos LXXXV Quintus Marcius Provincialis filius faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11300771.translation: Quintus Marcius Quadratus, a Libyan by birth, lived 85 years. Quintus Marcius Provincialis, his son, had this made.
EDCS-11300771.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Quintus Marcius Quadratus and erected by his son, Quintus Marcius Provincialis.

EDCS-11300772.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Othi Seutis vivo se posuit sibi et Bithidiae Biti coniugi et Lupussi PEVER MCAAT \n
EDCS-11300772.translation: To the departed spirits. Othis Seutis set this up for himself while alive, and for his wife Bithidiae Biti, and for Lupussus.
EDCS-11300772.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Othis Seutis for himself and his family.

EDCS-11300773.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Durisses Biti vixit a[nnos ------\n
EDCS-11300773.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Durisses Biti lived [...] years.
EDCS-11300773.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span of Durisses Biti, with the exact duration lost.

EDCS-11300776.text_edition: \n fidius Helius domo Amurio vivo sibi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11300776.translation: Fidius Helius, from Amurium by origin, had this made for himself while alive.
EDCS-11300776.summary: A funerary epitaph commissioned by Fidius Helius for himself.

EDCS-11300779.text_edition: \n Asclepio et Hygiae Annaeus Pulcher |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae frumentarius ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11300779.translation: To Asclepius and Hygieia. Annaeus Pulcher, a supply officer ('frumentarius') of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11300779.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Asclepius and Hygieia by Annaeus Pulcher, a supply officer of the Fifth Macedonian Legion.

EDCS-11300783.text_edition: \n Invicto sacrum Nicephorus pro Gemino et se aram posuit votum libens solvit merito\n
EDCS-11300783.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered One. Nicephorus set up this altar for Geminus and for himself, and gladly fulfilled his vow deservedly.
EDCS-11300783.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus ('the Unconquered One') by Nicephorus for himself and Geminus.

EDCS-11300790.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVI consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus tribunicia potestate II consul II divi Pii filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepo[tes ------\n
EDCS-11300790.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Pius, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva [...]
EDCS-11300790.summary: The beginning of a dedication or official inscription by Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, listing their titles and divine ancestry, likely dating to around 175 AD.

EDCS-11300802.text_edition: \n D curam agentibus Tiberio Claudio Valente quinquennale et Celsio Celeriano et Claudio M magistris\n
EDCS-11300802.translation: under the care of Tiberius Claudius Valens, quinquennalis, and Celsius Celerianus and Claudius M, as magistrates.
EDCS-11300802.summary: A record of civic officials responsible for a particular task or area, listing Tiberius Claudius Valens and others.

EDCS-11300804.text_edition: \n ecveteranus oMarcianus Sextus Cassius Numidicus M(arcus) Ulpius Maximinus Caius Atulenus Narcissus Caius Iulius Epigonus Marcus Aemilius Optatus Caius Iulius Herma\n
EDCS-11300804.translation: Ecveteranus O, Marcianus, Sextus Cassius Numidicus, Marcus Ulpius Maximinus, Caius Atulenus Narcissus, Caius Iulius Epigonus, Marcus Aemilius Optatus, Caius Iulius Herma.
EDCS-11300804.summary: A list of names, possibly of veterans or officials, without further context.

EDCS-11300805.text_edition: \n Rom miles legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300805.translation: Rom, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, gladly fulfilled his vow deservedly.
EDCS-11300805.summary: A votive inscription by a soldier named Rom from the First Italian Legion.

EDCS-11300806.text_edition: \n Domino et Dominae libens animo posuit liburna Armata |(centuriae) Quinti Iuli Heliodori\n
EDCS-11300806.translation: Liburna Armata of the century of Quintus Iulius Heliodorus gladly set this up.
EDCS-11300806.summary: This is a dedication from Liburna Armata, belonging to the century of Quintus Iulius Heliodorus.

EDCS-11300807.text_edition: \n vixit annos VII Acilius Valens pater filiis karissimis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11300807.translation: He lived 7 years. Acilius Valens, his father, had this made for his dearest children.
EDCS-11300807.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a boy named Acilius Valens, who died at age seven, set up by his father.

EDCS-11300808.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Celso veterano et Flaviae Alexandrae coniugi et Publio Alexandro et Iuliae Ianuariae coniugi eius Iulius Valens ex signifero legionis XI Claudiae parentibus bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11300808.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Iulius Valens, a standard-bearer from the 11th Claudian Legion, set this up for his deserving parents, Publius Aelius Celsus, a veteran, and his wife Flavia Alexandra, and for Publius Alexander and his wife Iulia Ianueria.
EDCS-11300808.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a standard-bearer of the 11th Legion for his parents and their spouses.

EDCS-11300809.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Respecto vixit annos EA \n
EDCS-11300809.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Valerius Respectus lived X years.
EDCS-11300809.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the life span of Gaius Valerius Respectus.

EDCS-11300810.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Maria Ingenua vixit annos L et Aufidius Aq[uila ------\n
EDCS-11300810.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Maria Ingenua lived 50 years and Aufidius Aquila [---]
EDCS-11300810.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Maria Ingenua and Aufidius Aquila, recording Maria's age at death.

EDCS-11300811.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Flaviae coniugi et Iuliae Crispinae filiae et NO \n
EDCS-11300811.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Valeria Flavia, my wife, and Iulia Crispinna, my daughter, and NO [---]
EDCS-11300811.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph dedicated to a wife and daughter, with further incomplete dedications.

EDCS-11300812.text_edition: \n Krystallus alumnus Postumi praefecti classis apud fluentum Ibericum Romanus infans editus alumnus castris Martiis Hibera postquam viderit et Maura longe moenia nacta quiescit Moesica primaeve pubis indigus ut verna florum germina vento feruntur Thracico Achelous alumnus Postumi praefecti classis extima Cappadocum Ponti quae iungitur orae me genuit tellus moenia sunt Tyanae Hermogenes genitor nomen mihi erat Acheloo artibus excellens praeditus ingenio insignis mea forma homines mea funera acerba iam gemitu plangunt iam lacrimis opus est\n
EDCS-11300812.translation: Krystallus, an adopted son of Postumus, prefect of the fleet on the Iberian river, Roman, an infant born in the camp of Mars in Iberia, now rests having seen the distant walls of Maura; he died young, before experiencing the first bloom of youth, like flower buds carried off by the Thracian wind. Achelous, adopted son of Postumus, prefect of the fleet on the outermost coast of Cappadocia adjoining Pontus, the land of Tyana brought me forth; my father was Hermogenes. My name was Achelous, excelling in arts and gifted with outstanding intelligence; my beauty caused men to lament my bitter death with groans and tears.
EDCS-11300812.summary: This is a poetic funerary epitaph for a young boy named Krystallus who died in infancy, possibly linked to a military unit on the Iberian frontier.

EDCS-11300813.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Cocceius Sabinus [---]
EDCS-11300813.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Marcus Cocceius Sabinus.

EDCS-11300814.summary: This inscription records the name of a Roman naval fleet, the Classis Flavia Moesica.

EDCS-11300815.summary: This inscription records the name of a Roman naval fleet, the Classis Flavia Moesica.

EDCS-11300821.text_edition: \n Cornelius Nicander pater et Valeria Casta mater bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11300821.translation: Cornelius Nicander, father, and Valeria Casta, mother, placed this for one who deserved well.
EDCS-11300821.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their deceased child.

EDCS-11300823.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Faustino vixit annos LXX Valerio Victori vixit annos LXXV Flavius Festus |(centurio) legionis I It(alicae) patri suo posuit Dei Sancti Solis simulacrum consecratum die XIV Kalendis Decembribus debet singulis annis iusso sacro dominorum nostrorum Licini Augusti et Licini Caesaris ture cereis et profusionibus eodem die a praepositis et vexillationibus in castris Salsoviensibus agentibus exorari Valerius Romulus vir perfectissimus dux secutus iussionem describsit sic\n
EDCS-11300823.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Titus Flavius Faustinus, who lived 70 years, and Valerius Victor, who lived 75 years. Flavius Festus, a centurion of the I Italica legion, set this up for his father. A statue consecrated to the Divine Sun God, to be appeased annually on the 18th of November by the sacred command of our lords, the Augusti Licinius and Licinius Caesar, with incense and offerings, by those acting on that day in the Salsovienses camp, by the prefects and detachments. Valerius Romulus, a most distinguished man, governor, having followed the command, inscribed it thus.
EDCS-11300823.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring two individuals, likely father and son, and a subsequent dedication of a statue to the Sun God, referencing imperial decrees from the time of Licinius, likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-11300826.text_edition: \n Herculi Victori Lucius Iulius Iulianus qui et Rundacio quinquennalis ex voto posuit locus datus ex decreto ordinis\n
EDCS-11300826.translation: For Hercules Victor. Lucius Iulius Iulianus, also called Rundacio, a quinquennial official, set this up in fulfillment of a vow. The site was granted by decree of the council.
EDCS-11300826.summary: This is a votive dedication to Hercules Victor, set up by an local official in the early Imperial period.

EDCS-11301224.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano pontifici [maximo ------\n
EDCS-11301224.translation: To Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontiff [maximus ...
EDCS-11301224.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Emperor Hadrian, likely from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-11301226.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Aurelio Valerio Diocletiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo Germanico maximo tribuniciae potestatis patri patriae proconsuli Afranius Hannibalianus Iulius Asclepiodotus vviri eemminentissimi praeffecti praetorio devoti numini maiestatique eius Libero Patri Conservatori ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Flavius Zosimus primus pilus ex provincia Asia civitate Efisianorum sic votum posuit post pastum militum consulibus\n
EDCS-11301226.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, chief priest, conqueror of the Germans, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul. To Liber Pater the Preserver of our lords the Augusti. Afranius Hannibalianus, Julius Asclepiodotus, both most eminent men, prefects of the Praetorian Guard, devoted to his divine majesty and power. Flavius Zosimus, primus pilus, from the province of Asia, of the city of Ephesus, set up this vow thus, after the soldiers' feast, during the consulship of [names not specified].
EDCS-11301226.summary: This is a complex votive inscription dedicated to Emperor Diocletian and Liber Pater, set up by high-ranking officials from Ephesus, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-11301228.text_edition: \n ------ ddominorum nnostrorum] Auggustorum Flavius Tatianus primipilarius legionis V Macedonicae ex provincia Suria sic Palestina post pastum militum votum posuit \xe1\xbc\x88\xce\xbdt\xcf\x89\xce\xbd\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xbd\xce\xbf\xcf\x85\n
EDCS-11301228.translation: ... of our lords the Augusti. Flavius Tatianus, a former primus pilus of the V Macedonica legion, from the province of Syria, thus Palestine, set up a vow after the soldiers' feast. [Greek text follows: 'for health and well-being'].
EDCS-11301228.summary: This is a votive inscription for the well-being of the emperors, set up by a former primus pilus of the V Macedonica legion from Syria, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-11301229.text_edition: \n Pro salute adque sic incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Flavius Euforbius sic primipilarius legionis V Macedonicae ex provincia Asia civitate Focia post pastum militum statuam posuit\n
EDCS-11301229.translation: For the health and well-being of our lords the Augusti. Flavius Euforbius, a former primus pilus of the V Macedonica legion, from the province of Asia, of the city of Phocaea, set up a statue after the soldiers' feast.
EDCS-11301229.summary: This is a dedication of a statue for the emperors' health, set up by a former primus pilus of the V Macedonica legion from Phocaea, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-11301230.text_edition: \n Pomponius Maximianus CE secundum Icacidunum terminos posuit\n
EDCS-11301230.translation: Pomponius Maximianus set up boundary markers for Icacidunum.
EDCS-11301230.summary: This inscription records the placement of boundary markers by Pomponius Maximianus, likely for administrative or legal purposes.

EDCS-11301235.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Cai filio Papiria Valentiano primo pilo legionis I Italicae Severianae praefecto legionis I adiutricis Severianae Caius Valerius Plautianus eques Romanus pontifex et IIviralis coloniae patri locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11301235.translation: For Gaius Valerius Valentianus, son of Gaius Valerius, of the Papiria tribe, a former primus pilus of the I Italica Severiana legion, prefect of the I Adiutrix Severiana legion. Gaius Valerius Plautianus, a Roman knight, pontiff and IIviral of the colony, set up this site for his father, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11301235.summary: This is a funerary monument for a military officer, likely set up by his son, a local magistrate, in a Roman colony, possibly from the Severan period (early 3rd century AD).

EDCS-11301238.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Bassus Aneirinni\n
EDCS-11301238.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Bassus, son of Aneirinnus.
EDCS-11301238.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely set up by an individual named Bassus.

EDCS-11301239.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Corinthus Iovi Optimo Maximo votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301239.translation: Gaius Valerius Corinthus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-11301239.summary: This inscription records a soldier, Gaius Valerius Corinthus, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-11301240.text_edition: \n IoviIovi Maximo Dolicheno Sextus votum posuit dedit dedicavit laetus libens merito\n
EDCS-11301240.translation: Sextus gladly and deservedly placed and dedicated his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.
EDCS-11301240.summary: This inscription records Sextus placing and dedicating a vow to the syncretic deity Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.

EDCS-11301241.text_edition: \n MCMtri et sua Acastus Dianae et Apollini votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301241.translation: Acastus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Diana and Apollo for himself and his family.
EDCS-11301241.summary: This inscription records Acastus fulfilling a vow to Diana and Apollo for himself and his family, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301242.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno Apollonianus ducenarius pro se et suos sic votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-11301242.translation: Apollonianus, a centurion's aide, gladly and deservedly placed his vow for himself and his dependents to the Eternal God.
EDCS-11301242.summary: This inscription records Apollonianus, a ducenarius, placing a vow for himself and his family to an 'Eternal God', likely a deity worshipped in the region.

EDCS-11301244.text_edition: \n Deo invicto sacrum Viator Luci Gavi Maximi servus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301244.translation: Viator, a slave of Lucius Gavi Maximius, gladly and deservedly dedicated this sacred offering to the Invincible God.
EDCS-11301244.summary: This inscription records Viator, a slave, dedicating a sacred offering to an 'Invincible God'.

EDCS-11301245.text_edition: \n Genio loci et Tutellae Aurelius (A)elianus optio agens sacru sic comitatu votum solvit\n
EDCS-11301245.translation: Aurelius Aelianus, a optio (officer), gladly fulfilled his vow concerning the spirit of the place and its protection, along with his retinue.
EDCS-11301245.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Aelianus, an optio, fulfilling a vow to the Genius Loci and Tutela, likely in a military context.

EDCS-11301248.text_edition: \n Gaius Tironius Alexander miles legionis V Macedonicae votum solvit Dia sic Suriae sic\n
EDCS-11301248.translation: Gaius Tironius Alexander, a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica, gladly fulfilled his vow to Dea Syria.
EDCS-11301248.summary: This inscription records Gaius Tironius Alexander, a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica, fulfilling a vow to Dea Syria.

EDCS-11301249.summary: This short inscription records Valerius Saturninus making a dedication, likely a votive offering, to an unspecified deity.

EDCS-11301250.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Eutyches Augustalis coloniae votum posuit\n
EDCS-11301250.translation: Lucius Valerius Eutyches, an Augustalis of the colony, placed his vow.
EDCS-11301250.summary: This inscription records Lucius Valerius Eutyches, holding an Augustalis position in a colony, placing a votive offering.

EDCS-11301251.text_edition: \n Marcus Disius Iulianus duumviralis coloniae posuit\n
EDCS-11301251.translation: Marcus Disius Iulianus, a duumvir of the colony, placed [this].
EDCS-11301251.summary: This inscription records Marcus Disius Iulianus, a high-ranking magistrate (duumvir) of a colony, making a dedication.

EDCS-11301252.text_edition: \n Aeranus ex voto posuit civis Romanus\n
EDCS-11301252.translation: Aeranus, a Roman citizen, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301252.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Aeranus, a Roman citizen, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-11301253.text_edition: \n Resius Chronius Resi Albani |(centurionis) legionis V Macedonicae libertus vixit nnos XLV Titovius Faustus heres fecit\n
EDCS-11301253.translation: Resius Chronius, freedman of Resius Albani, a centurion of the Legio V Macedonica, lived 45 years. Titovius Faustus, his heir, erected this.
EDCS-11301253.summary: This tombstone records the life and death of Resius Chronius, a freedman of a centurion, with his heir Titovius Faustus commissioning the monument, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11301254.text_edition: \n Lucius Septimius Cai filius miles legionis V Macedonicae vixit annos XL militavit annos XXI hic situs est aculia liberta et coniux faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301254.translation: Lucius Septimius Caius, son of Caius, a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica, lived 40 years and served 21 years. Aculia, his freedwoman and wife, saw to its construction.
EDCS-11301254.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Lucius Septimius Caius, a soldier of Legio V Macedonica, with his freedwoman and wife Aculia commissioning the monument, probably from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301255.text_edition: \n Tiberius Iulius Icci filius Acutus dublicarius! alae Pansianae domo Treverorum vixit annos LX meruit nnos XXXVI hic situs est heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301255.translation: Tiberius Iulius Acutus, son of Icchus, a duplicarius of the ala Pansiana, from Treverorum, lived 60 years and served 36 years. His heir saw to its construction. He is buried here.
EDCS-11301255.summary: This tombstone records the life and service of Tiberius Iulius Acutus, a cavalryman of the ala Pansiana from Gallia Belgica, with his heir commissioning the monument, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301256.text_edition: \n Caius Roscius Cai <f=E>(ilius) Aniensis Capito Troada veteranus legionis V Macedonicae militavit nnos XXIV vixit nnos LV hic situs est Caius Valerius Pudens miles legionis V Macedonicae centuria Atili Veri heres fiduciarius faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-11301256.translation: Caius Roscius Capito Aniensis, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, served 24 years and lived 55 years. He is buried here. Caius Valerius Pudens, a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica in the century of Atilius Verus, his fiduciary heir, saw to its construction.
EDCS-11301256.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Caius Roscius Capito Aniensis, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica, with a fellow soldier from the same legion serving as his fiduciary heir, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301257.text_edition: \n veteranus et Caius Braetius Braeti et fratri \n
EDCS-11301257.translation: [Set up by] a veteran and Caius Braetius [in honor of his] brother.
EDCS-11301257.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a monument dedicated to a brother by a veteran and Caius Braetius, its date is uncertain but likely Imperial.

EDCS-11301258.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Gai filius Menenia Longinus Heraclea veteranus legionis V Macedonicae vixit annis L hic situs est Gaius Iulius Hermes libertus idemque heres optimo patrono suo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301258.translation: Caius Iulius Longinus, son of Gaius, of the Menenia tribe, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica in Heraclea, lived 50 years. He is buried here. Gaius Iulius Hermes, his freedman and heir, saw to its construction for his most excellent patron.
EDCS-11301258.summary: This tombstone commemorates Caius Iulius Longinus, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica, with his freedman and heir Gaius Iulius Hermes commissioning the monument, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301259.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Celerinae et Celerino filiis Celerina annorum V filia filius annorum III Magio VEr eques alae Getulorum filiis piissimis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301259.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Celerina [set up this monument] for her most devoted children, Celerina, aged 5, and Celerinus, aged 3. Magius Verus, an *eques* of the ala Getulorum, saw to its construction.
EDCS-11301259.summary: This funerary inscription records the premature deaths of two children, Celerina and Celerinus, with the monument commissioned by their mother Celerina and stepfather Magius Verus, a cavalryman, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301260.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Scribonius Publi filius Collina Epheso Varus veteranus legionis V Macedonicae militavit annis XXVI vixit nnis LV hic situs est Arellia sic Celerina et Scribonius Celer miles legionis eiusdem faciendum curaverunt coniunx et filius\n
EDCS-11301260.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Scribonius Varus, son of Publius, of the Collina tribe, an Ephesian, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, served 26 years and lived 55 years. He is buried here. Arellia Celerina, his wife, and Scribonius Celer, a soldier of the same legion, saw to its construction.
EDCS-11301260.summary: This tombstone commemorates Publius Scribonius Varus, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica, with his wife and son, a soldier of the same legion, commissioning the monument, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301261.text_edition: \n Caius Cornelius Iustus miles legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis Moecia Pelagonia militavit annos XVI vixit annos XXXV hic situs est Caius Cornelius Iustus filius faciendum curavit patri\n
EDCS-11301261.translation: Caius Cornelius Iustus, a soldier of the Legio XI Claudia pia fidelis, served 16 years and lived 35 years. He is buried here. Caius Cornelius Iustus, his son, saw to its construction for his father.
EDCS-11301261.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Caius Cornelius Iustus, a soldier of Legio XI Claudia, with his son commissioning the monument, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301262.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mucatralis Sitae filius natione Bessus miles cohortis II Flaviae Bessorum \n
EDCS-11301262.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Mucatralis, son of Sita, of Thracian origin (Bessus), a soldier of the II Flavia Bessorum cohort.
EDCS-11301262.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the deceased (Dis Manibus) for Mucatralis, a soldier in the II Flavia Bessorum cohort, likely a tombstone inscription.

EDCS-11301263.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Iunius Marci f(ilius) Claud(ia) Montanus miles cohortis XXII cohortis II praetoriae |(centurio) legionis I I Minerviae VIII Augustae |(centurio) legionis hic situs est vixit nnos LXII Antonia coniunx et heres piissimo ex testamento faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301263.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Junius Montanus, son of Marcus, of the Claudia tribe, a soldier of the XXII cohort, and centurion of the Praetorian cohort, and centurion of the I Minerva Legion and the VIII Augusta Legion, is buried here; he lived 62 years. Antonia, his wife and heir, had this made by his will.
EDCS-11301263.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Junius Montanus, a distinguished soldier and centurion, detailing his military career and age, erected by his wife and heir.

EDCS-11301265.text_edition: \n Ioses arcisinagogus et principales filius Maximini Pannoni sibi et Qyriae sic coniugi sui sic vivo suo sic memoriae dedicavit\n
EDCS-11301265.translation: Ioses, head of the synagogue and its leaders, son of Maximinius of Pannonia, dedicated this to himself and to his wife Qyria, alive and for memory.
EDCS-11301265.summary: This is a dedication to themselves and a wife by Ioses, a synagogue leader from Pannonia, likely on a building or monument.

EDCS-11301266.text_edition: \n Iordina non libens hac ocie sic eytiche sic vixit bene annis LXIV cum essem in provincia cum filio meo Tito Iulio Lapiissimo et sissae mi sic filio militantes annis \n
EDCS-11301266.translation: Iordina, not willingly in this rest, lived well for 64 years while I was in the province with my son Titus Julius Lapiissimus and Sissa, my son, serving in the military.
EDCS-11301266.summary: This inscription seems to be a personal testament or epitaph for Iordina, who lived 64 years and mentions her son and military service.

EDCS-11301267.text_edition: \n Marco Cocceio Marci filio Papiria Victori qui et Quiritino decurioni Oescensium ex testamento \n
EDCS-11301267.translation: To Marcus Cocceius Victor, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, also called Quiritinus, decurion of the people of Oescus, by his will.
EDCS-11301267.summary: This is an inscription recording a bequest by will to Marcus Cocceius Victor, a decurion of Oescus.

EDCS-11301268.text_edition: \n us Quinti filius tiano E decurio- aedilici- \n
EDCS-11301268.translation: [He was] son of Quintus, decurion, aedilician...
EDCS-11301268.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording the name and some official positions (decurion, aedilician) of an individual, likely a public monument.

EDCS-11301270.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tertullus Titi Iuli Saturnini li[bertus ------\n
EDCS-11301270.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tertullus, freedman of Titus Julius Saturninus...
EDCS-11301270.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription for Tertullus, a freedman of Titus Julius Saturninus.

EDCS-11301271.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription for Terentia Tertulla, a freedwoman.

EDCS-11301272.text_edition: \n Gaiae Valeriae Gai filiae Valentinae uxori Gaius Scopius Marcianus flamen Titi Vespasiani Claudius Aelius Optimus pontifex et har(uspex) coloniae sacerdos deae Romae ex testamento et praeceptis fili et per tutorem ex testamento eorum Claudius A[elius ------\n
EDCS-11301272.translation: To Gaia Valeria Valentina, daughter of Gaius, wife of Gaius Scopius Marcianus, flamen of Titi Vespasiani, Claudius Aelius Optimus, pontifex and haruspex of the colony, priest of the goddess Roma, by will and instructions of her son and through their guardian, Claudius Aelius...
EDCS-11301272.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument or tomb by Claudius Aelius Optimus, a priest and haruspex, for Gaia Valeria Valentina and others, fulfilling their wills.

EDCS-11301273.text_edition: \n Aelius Faventinus vir egregius procurator CC Illyrici vixit nnos LX Aelii Florentinus Villanus Peregrinus deccuriones coloniae Aureliae Dacicae Napocensium et Mammosa et Aurelius Gaurus VEI heredes secundum voluntatem testamenti ipsius per Aelium Philocalum libertum fecit\n
EDCS-11301273.translation: Aelius Faventinus, an outstanding man, procurator of CC Illyricum, lived 60 years. Aelii Florentinus Villanus Peregrinus, decurions of the colony Aurelia Dacica Napocensium, and Mammosa and Aurelius Gaurus VEI, heirs, made this according to his will through Aelius Philocalus, freedman.
EDCS-11301273.summary: This inscription records the funerary monument for Aelius Faventinus, a procurator, erected by his heirs, the decurions of the colony Aurelia Dacica Napocensium.

EDCS-11301275.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Aspronio CollinaIAIR vixit annos XXVIII Velli Niger et Blastus faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11301275.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Aspronius Collina lived 28 years. Velli Niger and Blastus saw to its construction.
EDCS-11301275.summary: A funerary monument dedicated to Caius Aspronius Collina by Velli Niger and Blastus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301276.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcia Valeria vixit annosII et \n
EDCS-11301276.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcia Valeria lived 2 years and...
EDCS-11301276.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcia Valeria, likely a child, with an incomplete record of her age, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301277.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Genucio Asellino et Caio Genucio Marci filio parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301277.translation: To the departed spirits. Parents placed this for Genucius Asellinus and Caius Genucius, son of Marcus.
EDCS-11301277.summary: A funerary monument set up by parents for Genucius Asellinus and Caius Genucius, son of Marcus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301278.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextus Vibius Valens immerens hic occisus sum una cum Valerio Ingenuo adfine meo sine ullo praemio et sine ulla ira vixi annos XXVIII gratulor parenti nostro qui bono praeside sanguen nostrum vindikavit Sextus Vibius Secundinus fratri piissimo posuit titul(um)\n
EDCS-11301278.translation: To the departed spirits. I, Sextus Vibius Valens, though undeserving, was killed here along with my relative Valerius Ingenuus, without reward and without anger. I lived 28 years. I congratulate our father who, with a good protector, avenged our blood. Sextus Vibius Secundinus placed this tombstone for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-11301278.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sextus Vibius Valens, who claims he was unjustly killed, set up by his brother Sextus Vibius Secundinus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301280.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attiae Romanae vixit annis LV Lucius Fabricius Pacatus pientissimae coniugi posuit\n
EDCS-11301280.translation: To the departed spirits. Attia Romana lived 55 years. Lucius Fabricius Pacatus placed this for his most dutiful wife.
EDCS-11301280.summary: A funerary monument erected by Lucius Fabricius Pacatus for his wife Attia Romana, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301281.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vettia Paulina vixit annos XXI hic sita est Valerius Mercurius coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-11301281.translation: To the departed spirits. Vettia Paulina lived 21 years. She lies here. Valerius Mercurius made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11301281.summary: A funerary monument erected by Valerius Mercurius for his wife Vettia Paulina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301284.text_edition: \n a nati matri pientissimae bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11301284.translation: ...for mother, most dutiful, well-deserving, they saw to its construction.
EDCS-11301284.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by children for their deserving mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301285.translation: ...the heir saw to its construction for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11301285.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by an heir for a well-deserving individual, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301286.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription recording the name and lifespan of an individual, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301292.text_edition: \n Valeriae Matronae coniugi obsequentissimae adque inconparabilissimae sic quae vixit annos Respectus \n
EDCS-11301292.translation: For Valeria Matrona, his most obedient and incomparable wife, who lived [...] years. Respectus...
EDCS-11301292.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Matrona, dedicated by her husband Respectus, recording her virtues and an incomplete lifespan, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301293.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ci Ce vixit annos annos XII e vi XXIIII filiis piissimis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11301293.translation: To the Divine Spirits. She lived 12 years and 24 days. Placed by her most dutiful sons for their well-deserving mother.
EDCS-11301293.summary: A funerary epitaph for a woman, likely erected by her sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301295.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus A H hic situs est sacerdos Lucius Umbricius Ingenuus \n
EDCS-11301295.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Here lies Lucius Umbricius Ingenuus, priest.
EDCS-11301295.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Umbricius Ingenuus, a priest, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301296.text_edition: \n CLA annos aetatis LXXX marito suo viva posuit\n
EDCS-11301296.translation: CLA, aged 80 years, placed this, alive, for her husband.
EDCS-11301296.summary: An epitaph set up by a woman named CLA for her husband, indicating she was alive when she commissioned it, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301302.summary: A dedication or funerary marker for an individual and their relatives, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301308.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Crescens vixit nnos Aelii nius et Iulianus et Publius fratres pientes et Publius Aelius Crescentinus filius una cum avia et fratribus bene merenti posuerunt opto sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-11301308.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Aelius Crescens lived [X] years. Aelius Celsus and Julianus and Publius, his brothers, and Publius Aelius Crescentinus, his son, together with their grandmother and brothers, placed this for the well-deserving man. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-11301308.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Crescens, erected by his brothers, son, and grandmother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301313.text_edition: \n Legionis V Macedonicae vexillarii sub Iulium Vibium votum\n
EDCS-11301313.translation: Of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, standard-bearers under Julius Vibius, a vow.
EDCS-11301313.summary: A dedication recording a vow made by standard-bearers of the Fifth Macedonian Legion under Julius Vibius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301314.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Eustomus vixit annos XXX cuius pater Marcus Ulpius Fructus pater infelix filio et sibi se vivo posuit\n
EDCS-11301314.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Ulpius Eustomus lived 30 years. His father, Marcus Ulpius Fructus, an unhappy father, placed this for his son and for himself while alive.
EDCS-11301314.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Eustomus, erected by his father Marcus Ulpius Fructus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301315.text_edition: \n Blandus Singiberti filius eques alae Bosporanorum natione U<b=F>ius hic situs est fratri LI faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-11301315.translation: Blandus, son of Singibertus, an equestrian of the ala Bosporanorum, of the Uby tribe, lies here. His brother LI had this made.
EDCS-11301315.summary: An epitaph for Blandus, an Uby tribesman serving as an equestrian in the ala Bosporanorum, whose brother commissioned the monument, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301316.text_edition: \n Avillia Lynche sic vixit annos triginta tres hic sita est Caius Fullonius decurio alae Scubulorum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301316.translation: Avillia Lynche lived thirty-three years. She lies here. Caius Fullonius, a decurion of the ala Scubulorum, had this made.
EDCS-11301316.summary: A funerary inscription for Avillia Lynche, commissioned by Caius Fullonius, a decurion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301317.text_edition: \n Caius Furius Valens Synao militavit nnos XIX vixit annos LXIV \n
EDCS-11301317.translation: Caius Furius Valens served in the Synao [cohort?] for 19 years, lived 64 years.
EDCS-11301317.summary: An epitaph commemorating Caius Furius Valens, detailing his military service and lifespan, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301318.text_edition: \n Plutoni et Proserpinae sacrum Iulia Prudentilla votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301318.translation: Sacred to Pluto and Proserpina, Iulia Prudentilla willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-11301318.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Pluto and Proserpina by Iulia Prudentilla.

EDCS-11301320.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae pro salute Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Augusti Arius Goryphus sic primus pilus legionis I Italicae Antoninianae fonte refecto arboribus institutis deae donum dedit curante Ario Diogeniano\n
EDCS-11301320.translation: For the health of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, Arius Goryphus, first spear-officer of the I Italica Antoniniana legion, having restored a fountain and planted trees, gave this gift to the August Diana, with Arius Diogenianus overseeing.
EDCS-11301320.summary: This is a dedication to Diana Augusta by Arius Goryphus, a centurion, for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording the restoration of a fountain and the planting of trees.

EDCS-11301325.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus tu viator qui transis resta perlege me vivo posui ego Maximus Marci patri meo Marco Crispini qui vixit annos LXXX et Valeriae Zileni matri meae qui sic vixit annos LX et Senpromiae sic Antoniae coniugi meae pientissimae qui sic vixit nnos XXXVII bene merentibus posui\n
EDCS-11301325.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. You who pass by, rest and read carefully: I, Maximus, placed this for my father Marcus, son of Marcius, who lived 80 years, and for my mother Valeria Zila, who lived 60 years, and for my most devoted wife Senpromia Antonia, who lived 37 years, having deserved well of them.
EDCS-11301325.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Maximus for his father Marcus, mother Valeria Zila, and wife Senpromia Antonia.

EDCS-11301326.translation: He placed this for Jupiter the Unconquered.
EDCS-11301326.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Invictus, with the dedicator unspecified.

EDCS-11301328.text_edition: \n Ielas Biti qui sacerdos vixit annis LX Mucapuius Dizae marito optimo bene merenti p(osuit) libens merito --\n
EDCS-11301328.translation: Mucapuius Dizae placed this for her husband Ielas Bitius, who lived 60 years as a priest, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11301328.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Mucapuius Dizae for her husband Ielas Bitius, a priest.

EDCS-11301330.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus have viator si studiosus es siste et perlege Aurelius Longinus miles praetorianus Scarini Busilae patri pientissimo vixit nnos LXX et Surae Pyrulae matri pientissimae superstanti titulum bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11301330.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Greetings, traveler; if you are studious, stop and read: Aurelius Longinus, a praetorian guardsman, set up this monument for his most devoted father Pientissimus, who lived 70 years, and for his most devoted mother Sura Pyrula, who is still alive, having deserved well of them.
EDCS-11301330.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelius Longinus, a praetorian guardsman, for his father and mother.

EDCS-11301334.text_edition: \n Cai filium Actum et Plotina--- Luciae filiae vivunt ius \n
EDCS-11301334.translation: Caius, son of Actus, and Plotina---, daughter of Lucius, live with rights.
EDCS-11301334.summary: This inscription seems to record the legal status or rights of Caius, son of Actus, and Plotina, daughter of Lucius.

EDCS-11301335.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus viator et titulum? lege si lex est legere tibi Publius Aelius Firmus veteranus legionis XI Claudiae se vivo et tani coniugi suae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11301335.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Traveler, read the inscription if it is your right to read: Publius Aelius Firmus, a veteran of the XI Claudia legion, set this up for himself while alive and for his deserving wife Tani.
EDCS-11301335.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by veteran Publius Aelius Firmus for himself and his wife.

EDCS-11301336.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris divi Traiani Parthici f<i=E>li divi Nervae nepotis Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate XX consulis III Marcus Antius Rufinus inter Thracas sic et Moesos finess posuit \n
EDCS-11301336.translation: By the authority of Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time, Marcus Antius Rufinus set this up among the Thracians and Moesians.
EDCS-11301336.summary: This inscription, issued by Emperor Hadrian, records the establishment of boundaries by Marcus Antius Rufinus in the Thracian and Moesian regions.

EDCS-11301341.text_edition: \n Si quis adoraverit deum habebit Heronem propitium adiuvante deo Herone erat adiutoroptimo patrono \n
EDCS-11301341.translation: If anyone worships the god, they will have Heron favorable; with the god's help, Heron was an assistant to the best patron.
EDCS-11301341.summary: This inscription refers to the worship of a god named Heron and his role as a helper to a patron.

EDCS-11301342.summary: A dedication to the goddess Diana by Aurelius Maximus, likely an offering.

EDCS-11301343.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by Valerius Proculus, likely an offering.

EDCS-11301344.translation: Taesi (made) the vow to Silvanus, the god.
EDCS-11301344.summary: This inscription records Taesi fulfilling a vow made to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-11301345.summary: A partial inscription dedicating something to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-11301346.translation: To the Holy God Silvanus and Diana.
EDCS-11301346.summary: A dedication to both the god Silvanus and the goddess Diana.

EDCS-11301348.text_edition: \n Aurelius Marceale miles sancto Silbano sic ex vota sic posuit\n
EDCS-11301348.translation: Aurelius Marceale, soldier, placed this as vowed to the Holy Silvanus, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-11301348.summary: A soldier named Aurelius Marceale dedicates this to Silvanus in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11301349.text_edition: \n Aurelius Victor eques cohortis primae civium Romanorum \n
EDCS-11301349.translation: Aurelius Victor, an equestrian of the first cohort of Roman citizens.
EDCS-11301349.summary: This inscription identifies Aurelius Victor as an equestrian officer in a specific Roman cohort.

EDCS-11301351.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that likely mentions the name Valerianus, possibly in relation to an imperial figure.

EDCS-11301352.text_edition: \n Marcianus veteranus legionis I Italicae votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301352.translation: Marcianus, a veteran of the I Italica legion, gladly and deservedly set up this vow.
EDCS-11301352.summary: A veteran named Marcianus of the I Italica legion fulfilled a vow by setting up this inscription.

EDCS-11301353.summary: This inscription records Iulius Valerius, a decurion (a cavalry officer in the Roman army or a local official), setting up a monument.

EDCS-11301355.text_edition: \n Silvanu sic Saltecaputeno Flavius Montanus miles ex voto sanctu incolumis propusivi sic pro salute\n
EDCS-11301355.translation: To Silvanus of Saltecaputas, Flavius Montanus, a soldier, in fulfillment of a vow, in thanks for his health and well-being, erected this.
EDCS-11301355.summary: A soldier named Flavius Montanus dedicates this monument to Silvanus of Saltecaputas for his health.

EDCS-11301356.text_edition: \n Saldaecaputenis Lucius Naevius Probus veteranus posuit Silvano et Dianae votum solvit libens numinibus posuit\n
EDCS-11301356.translation: Lucius Naevius Probus, a veteran from Saldaecaputae, set this up for Silvanus and Diana, and willingly paid his vow to their divine powers.
EDCS-11301356.summary: Lucius Naevius Probus, a veteran, dedicates a monument to Silvanus and Diana, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-11301357.text_edition: \n Sancto Silvano et Dianae Cocceius Priscus tessararius sic ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11301357.translation: Cocceius Priscus, a tessararius (paymaster), erected this to the holy Silvanus and Diana, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301357.summary: Cocceius Priscus, a tessararius, dedicates this to Silvanus and Diana, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-11301358.text_edition: \n Silvano sancto ex vico Longinopara heredes Eftecenthi Coci votum posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301358.translation: The heirs of Eftecenthus Coci from the village of Longinopara erected this monument to the holy Silvanus, fulfilling a vow.
EDCS-11301358.summary: Heirs of Eftecenthus Coci from the village of Longinopara erect a monument to Silvanus in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11301359.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pater patriae tribunicia potestate XV consul IIII praesidia et burgos ob tutelam provinciae Thraciae fecit curante Caio Gallonio Frontone Quinto Marcio Turbone legato Augusti pro praetore per fines civitatis Serdicensium regione Dyptensium praesidia numero IIII burgi sic numero XII phruri numero CIX\n
EDCS-11301359.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, in his fifteenth year of tribunician power and fourth consulship, built fortifications and towers for the protection of the province of Thrace, under the supervision of Gaius Gallonius Fronto, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, through the territory of the Serdicensian civitas in the Dyptensian region, with four fortifications, twelve towers, and 109 small forts.
EDCS-11301359.summary: Emperor Antoninus Pius records the construction of military defenses in Thrace, completed under the supervision of Legate Gaius Gallonius Fronto.

EDCS-11301360.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Tamiteno Marcus Iulius Frontinianus veteranus Auggustorum nnostrorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-11301360.translation: Marcus Iulius Frontinianus, a veteran of our emperors, willingly paid his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Tamitenus.
EDCS-11301360.summary: Marcus Iulius Frontinianus, a veteran, pays a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Tamitenus.

EDCS-11301361.text_edition: \n Lucius Varronius Felix veteranus legionis V Macedonicae Valeria Varronia Maxima filia et Caius Iulius Rusticus gener pientissimo posserunt sic\n
EDCS-11301361.translation: Lucius Varronius Felix, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, his daughter Valeria Varronia Maxima, and his son-in-law Caius Iulius Rusticus, erected this for their most dutiful [relative].
EDCS-11301361.summary: Lucius Varronius Felix, a veteran, and his family members erect a monument to a relative.

EDCS-11301362.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Silvanus veteranus legionis XI Claudiae vivo suo sibi et Valeriae Marciae carissimae coniugi sui sic Aurelio Sabino previgno sic vixit annos XXX et Aureliae filiae vixit annos XV Silvano nepoti vixit annos III\n
EDCS-11301362.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Silvanus, a veteran of the Legio XI Claudia, dedicated this for himself and his dearest wife Valeria Marcia while alive, and for his stepson Aurelius Sabinus, who lived 30 years, and for his daughter Aurelia, who lived 15 years, and for his grandson Silvanus, who lived 3 years.
EDCS-11301362.summary: Aurelius Silvanus, a veteran, sets up a family tombstone for himself, his wife, stepson, daughter, and grandson.

EDCS-11301363.text_edition: \n Herculi sancto Iulius Valens ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11301363.translation: Iulius Valens erected this to the holy Hercules, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301363.summary: Iulius Valens dedicates this monument to Hercules in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11301364.text_edition: \n Herculanus Chresti archibucolus vernaculorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301364.translation: Herculanus Chresti, an archibucolus (chief swineherd) of the vernaculi (slaves born in the house), willingly paid his vow, deservedly.
EDCS-11301364.summary: Herculanus Chresti, a chief swineherd, pays a vow.

EDCS-11301365.text_edition: \n Tiberius Plautius Crispus veteranus legionis quintae Macedonicae Tiberius Plautius Longinus miles patri filius fecit\n
EDCS-11301365.translation: Tiberius Plautius Longinus, son of the veteran Tiberius Plautius Crispus of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, erected this.
EDCS-11301365.summary: A son erected this monument for his father, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, likely in the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11301366.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Diernius pater filiae Pitanae vixit annos XXV\n
EDCS-11301366.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Diernius, father, for his daughter Pitana, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-11301366.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of a young woman named Pitana, erected by her father Diernius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301367.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband for his deceased wife, Prima, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301368.text_edition: \n ------] propro dominorum? nn(ostrorum) Severi et Antonini et Getae Caesaris et Iuliae Augustae territorio Dianensium Publius Aelius Victorinus ex magistratu et ex decurionatu suo posuit\n
