EDCS-11301368.translation: For the territory of the Dianenses, in honor of our lords Severus, Antoninus Caesar, Geta Caesar, and Julia Augusta, Publius Aelius Victorinus, from his magistracy and council, erected this.
EDCS-11301368.summary: An official dedication to emperors Severus, Caracalla, Geta, and Julia Domna by a local magistrate and council member, Publius Aelius Victorinus, dating to the Severan period (early 3rd century AD).

EDCS-11301369.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum C Severus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11301369.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. C. Severus erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301369.summary: A votive offering dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by C. Severus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301370.text_edition: \n Deanae Germetithae sacrum Marcus Iulius Niger voto posuit\n
EDCS-11301370.translation: Sacred to Deana Germetithae. Marcus Iulius Niger erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301370.summary: A votive offering to the local deity Deana Germetithae by Marcus Iulius Niger, likely from Roman provincial territory.

EDCS-11301371.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Valerius Paetus miles legionis I Italicae et Valerius Charito fratres pro salute sua et suorum votum posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301371.translation: For the well-being of themselves and their families, the brothers Valerius Paetus, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, and Valerius Charito fulfilled their vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-11301371.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by two brothers, one a soldier, for their health and that of their families, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301372.translation: To the Genius of the place, Publius Livius...
EDCS-11301372.summary: A dedication to the Genius Loci by Publius Livius, likely a fragment of a larger inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301375.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaia Aurelia vixit nnos XXIX Marcus Aurelius Dernaius bene merenti osori sic bene victae quindices sic annos nnr Claudius Atius Montanus frater con sic patre posuit\n
EDCS-11301375.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaia Aurelia lived 29 years. Marcus Aurelius Dernaius, her lover (?), and Claudius Atius Montanus, her brother, erected this for the well-deserving, this one who was well-lived for 15 years (?).
EDCS-11301375.summary: A complex funerary inscription for Gaia Aurelia, erected by her lover or partner and her brother, with potentially confusing dates and relationships, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301376.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Luci libertus Antigonus vivus sibi posuit hic situs est\n
EDCS-11301376.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Valerius Antigonus, freedman of Lucius Valerius, erected this for himself while alive; he is buried here.
EDCS-11301376.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Antigonus, who set it up for himself during his lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301377.text_edition: \n Apolloni et Dianae Aurelia Victorina Primi vilici uxor\n
EDCS-11301377.translation: To Apollo and Diana, Aurelia Victorina, wife of Primus the steward.
EDCS-11301377.summary: This is a dedication to the gods Apollo and Diana by Aurelia Victorina, the wife of a steward named Primus.

EDCS-11301379.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Valens veteranus ex legione I Italica vixit annis L coniux pientissimo coniugi suo Dis Manibus Valerius Valentinus filius Valerii Valentis vixit annis XXXV militavit legione prima beneficiarius legati mater pientissima posui cum coniugi sic SMPDX\n
EDCS-11301379.translation: To the Divine Shades. Valerius Valens, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, lived for 50 years. His most devoted wife placed this for her most devoted husband. Valerius Valentinus, son of Valerius Valens, lived for 35 years, served in the First Legion as a beneficiarius of the legate. His most devoted mother placed this with his wife.
EDCS-11301379.summary: This is a tombstone for Valerius Valens, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, erected by his wife and son, likely from the 2nd century CE given the legion's history.

EDCS-11301381.text_edition: \n et Caessarum Auggustorum Iovi aeterno sancto Conservatori Marcus Valerius Vitalis |(centurio) legionis I Italicae ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11301381.translation: To Jupiter Eternal, Holy Preserver, and to the Emperors. Marcus Valerius Vitalis, a centurion of the First Italian Legion, placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301381.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter and the Emperors by Marcus Valerius Vitalis, a centurion in the First Italian Legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301383.text_edition: \n Aurelius Victor miles legionis I Italica sic votum posuit\n
EDCS-11301383.translation: Aurelius Victor, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301383.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Victor, a soldier of the First Italian Legion.

EDCS-11301389.text_edition: \n Iulia Valentina filia vixit annos s Paulinus et filii Iulius Paulinus et Maxima parentes bene merentibus faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11301389.translation: Iulia Valentina lived for X years. Paulinus and his sons, Iulius Paulinus and Maxima, arranged for this to be made for the well-deserving parents.
EDCS-11301389.summary: This epitaph records the death of Iulia Valentina and was erected by her parents and siblings.

EDCS-11301390.translation: Ulpius Valens and Viator placed this.
EDCS-11301390.summary: This is a dedication or memorial placed by Ulpius Valens and Viator.

EDCS-11301391.text_edition: \n Luci filius Agathupus sic et Licinia Zue sic possuerunt bene merenti\n
EDCS-11301391.translation: Agathupus, son of Lucius, and Licinia Zue placed this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11301391.summary: This inscription records a dedication or memorial placed by Agathupus and Licinia Zue.

EDCS-11301393.text_edition: \n Libero Patri sacrum Marcus Rubrius Ius posuit\n
EDCS-11301393.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater. Marcus Rubrius Ius placed this.
EDCS-11301393.summary: This is a dedication to the god Liber Pater by Marcus Rubrius Ius.

EDCS-11301394.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Veneta vixit annis XXX Valerius Ianuarius coniugi carissimae bene merenti posuit memoriae causa\n
EDCS-11301394.translation: To the Divine Shades. Flavia Veneta lived for 30 years. Valerius Ianuarius placed this for his dearest wife, well-deserving, as a memorial.
EDCS-11301394.summary: This tombstone records the death of Flavia Veneta at age 30 and was erected by her husband, Valerius Ianuarius.

EDCS-11301396.text_edition: \n Sancto Pirmerulae Aurelius Pirurus miles alarix sic\n
EDCS-11301396.translation: To the Holy Pirmerula. Aurelius Pirurus, a soldier of the ala [---], [placed this].
EDCS-11301396.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Pirmerula by Aurelius Pirurus, a soldier of an auxiliary cavalry unit (ala).

EDCS-11301398.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Barbarianus |(centurio) legionis IIII Flaviae vixit nnos LI Aurelia Theonis uxor bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11301398.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Gaius Julius Barbarianus, a centurion of the IIII Flavia legion, lived 51 years. Aurelia Theonis, his wife, erected this for him, his deserving husband.
EDCS-11301398.summary: A funerary epitaph for a centurion named Gaius Julius Barbarianus, erected by his wife, dating likely to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301410.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Numisius Rufinus optio legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301410.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Dolichene. Numisius Rufinus, an optio of the I Italica legion, willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-11301410.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Numisius Rufinus, an optio in the I Italica legion, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301426.text_edition: \n Lucius Oppius Maximus sacerdos Matris Deum dendroforis sic et dumopiretis dono dedit\n
EDCS-11301426.translation: Lucius Oppius Maximus, priest of the Mother of the Gods, dedicated this through the dendrophori and dumopirietes.
EDCS-11301426.summary: A dedication by Lucius Oppius Maximus, a priest of the Mother of the Gods, to his collegia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301434.text_edition: \n Caius Popillius Onesiphorus vivos sic sibi posuit et Dis Manibus Pacciae Euphrosynes\n
EDCS-11301434.translation: Gaius Popillius Onesiphorus erected this for himself while alive and for the Divine Spirits of Paccia Euphrosyne.
EDCS-11301434.summary: A tomb inscription set up by Gaius Popillius Onesiphorus for himself and his deceased wife, Paccia Euphrosyne, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301436.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Basso decurioni veterano alaes sic primae Asturum militavit annos XXV vixit annos LX Flavia Longina patri bene merenti hic situs est\n
EDCS-11301436.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Tiberius Bassus, a veteran decurion of the ala Prima Asturum, served for 25 years and lived for 60 years. Flavia Longina, his deserving father, erected this here.
EDCS-11301436.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tiberius Bassus, a veteran decurion, erected by his daughter Flavia Longina, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301442.text_edition: \n Cassio Quinti liberto Chresimo patronus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301442.translation: The patron arranged for this to be made for Cassius Chresimus, his freedman.
EDCS-11301442.summary: An inscription recording that a patron commissioned a monument for his freedman, Cassius Chresimus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301446.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus memoriae Iuliae Cai libertae \n
EDCS-11301446.translation: To the Divine Spirits, in memory of Iulia, freedwoman of Caius.
EDCS-11301446.summary: A funerary dedication to Iulia, a freedwoman, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301449.text_edition: \n Paae Marci libertae Severae patronus bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301449.translation: The patron arranged for this to be made for Paea Severa, his freedwoman, his deserving former slave.
EDCS-11301449.summary: An inscription stating that a patron commissioned a monument for his freedwoman, Paea Severa, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301450.text_edition: \n Vivos sic sibi Sextus Caesernius Epitynchanus et Caeserniae Victoriae obitae coniugi carissimae bene meritae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301450.translation: Sextus Caesernius Epitynchanus arranged for this to be made while alive for himself and for Caesernia Victoria, his dearest and deserving departed wife.
EDCS-11301450.summary: A tomb inscription set up by Sextus Caesernius Epitynchanus for himself and his deceased wife, Caesernia Victoria, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301453.text_edition: \n Valerius Luci fiius Claudia Birbilo Celeia miles legionis I Italicae centuria Cassi Bassi vixit annos XXXVI militavit annos XVI frater impensa sua posuit\n
EDCS-11301453.translation: Valerius Claudius Birbilus Celeias, son of Lucius, a soldier of the I Italica legion, in the century of Cassius Bassus, lived for 36 years and served for 16 years. His brother erected this at his own expense.
EDCS-11301453.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Claudius Birbilus Celeias, a soldier in the I Italica legion, erected by his brother, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301465.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Muciano mi<l=I>(iti) legionis I Italicae s<t=I>ipendiorum XXIIII vixit hic situs est Iulius Castus <h=I>eres ex testamento Senties sic Matrones sic fecit \n
EDCS-11301465.translation: To the divine spirits. Aurelius Mucianus, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, with 24 years of service, lived here and is buried here. Julius Castus, his heir, made this in accordance with the will. Sentius Matrones made it thus.
EDCS-11301465.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Mucianus, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, erected by his heir Julius Castus and possibly Sentius Matrones.

EDCS-11301466.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Privatae matri Iulius Bassus Iulius Rufus Iulius Gemellus filii bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-11301466.translation: To the divine spirits. For Julia Privata, mother. Julius Bassus, Julius Rufus, and Julius Gemellus, her sons, made this for their deserving mother.
EDCS-11301466.summary: A funerary monument erected by three sons, Julius Bassus, Julius Rufus, and Julius Gemellus, for their mother Julia Privata.

EDCS-11301469.text_edition: \n Invicto Marcus Ulpius Modianus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301469.translation: To the Unconquered God. Marcus Ulpius Modianus, a centurion of the First Italian Legion, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11301469.summary: A dedication to the Unconquered God by Marcus Ulpius Modianus, a centurion of the First Italian Legion, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-11301474.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Attius annorum LX se vivo posuit et coniugi Dutuborini Porinis filiae annorum XXX Ziae Carceni Cardentis filiae annorum XXXX\n
EDCS-11301474.translation: To the divine spirits. Caius Julius Attius, aged 60, set this up during his lifetime for himself and for his wife Dutuborina Porina, daughter of Zia Carcenis, aged 40.
EDCS-11301474.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Caius Julius Attius for himself and his wife Dutuborina Porina, indicating he was alive at the time of erection.

EDCS-11301475.text_edition: \n Heroni Ithiostlae Lucius Tanicius Zosimus cum suis de suo posuit\n
EDCS-11301475.translation: For Heron, son of Ithios. Lucius Tanicius Zosimus, with his family, set this up at his own expense.
EDCS-11301475.summary: A funerary inscription for Heron, son of Ithios, erected by Lucius Tanicius Zosimus and his household.

EDCS-11301476.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Gerrius ex duplicario veteranus vixit annos L \n
EDCS-11301476.translation: To the divine spirits. Publius Aelius Gerrius, a veteran duplicarius, lived 50 years.
EDCS-11301476.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Gerrius, a veteran soldier (duplicarius), who lived for 50 years.

EDCS-11301477.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Iulia Flavi Aponi ttid \n
EDCS-11301477.translation: To the divine spirits. Flavia Iulia, daughter of Flavius Aponius.
EDCS-11301477.summary: A brief funerary inscription for Flavia Iulia, daughter of Flavius Aponius.

EDCS-11301478.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Iulia viva sibi et Claudiae Se[verae ------ suae posuit\n
EDCS-11301478.translation: To the divine spirits. Claudia Iulia, alive, set this up for herself and for Claudia Severa, her [relation].
EDCS-11301478.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Claudia Iulia for herself and Claudia Severa, likely her relative, during her lifetime.

EDCS-11301479.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelius Magnus librarius legionis I Italicae Severianae stipendiorum XIII vixit nnis XXX Cornelius Iustianus vixit annis XVIII Ovinnia sic Pretiosa \n
EDCS-11301479.translation: To the divine spirits. Cornelius Magnus, a scribe of the Severan First Italian Legion, with 13 years of service, lived 30 years. Cornelius Iustianus lived 18 years. Ovinnia Pretiosa, thus.
EDCS-11301479.summary: A funerary inscription recording the deaths of Cornelius Magnus, a legionary scribe, Cornelius Iustianus, and Ovinnia Pretiosa.

EDCS-11301480.text_edition: \n Deanae sacrum Titus Pacuvius Sabinus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301480.translation: Sacred to Deana. Titus Pacuvius Sabinus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-11301480.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to the goddess Deana by Titus Pacuvius Sabinus for himself and his household.

EDCS-11301482.text_edition: \n Domino et dominae Caeth Quinti puer saeptorum custos donum dedit\n
EDCS-11301482.translation: To the Lord and Lady, Caeth, keeper of the sheep pens, the boy, dedicated this gift.
EDCS-11301482.summary: This is a votive dedication by a boy named Caeth to the domestic deities (Domino et dominae).

EDCS-11301485.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Maximus miles legionis I Italicae in memoriam bruti Amicae posuit et T rogat et petet pro salute\n
EDCS-11301485.translation: Marcus Aurelius Maximus, a soldier of the I Italica legion, set this up in memory of his dear friend, and he asks and begs for (your) health.
EDCS-11301485.summary: This is a memorial erected by a soldier of the I Italica legion for a deceased friend.

EDCS-11301487.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tibalus Tibali vixit annis V\n
EDCS-11301487.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Tibalus lived for 5 years.
EDCS-11301487.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of a child named Tibalus.

EDCS-11301488.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Caius S S aram posuit voto\n
EDCS-11301488.translation: To the Unconquered God, Caius S erected this altar in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301488.summary: This is a votive altar dedicated to the Unconquered God by Caius S.

EDCS-11301489.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Florus Gerulonis buleuta vixit annis XXXX Cassia Nice coniux et Festivos filius bene merenti fecerunt Claudius Titi libertus scribendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301489.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Florus, a council member (buleuta) from Gerula, lived for 40 years. Cassia Nice, his wife, and Festivos, his son, made this for him well-deserving. Claudius, freedman of Titus, saw to its inscription.
EDCS-11301489.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Florus, a buleuta, set up by his wife and son, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11301490.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mani Fabrici Sabini vixit nnos LV hic situs est Iulia Secunda marito bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11301490.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Mani Fabricius Sabinus lived 55 years, he lies here. Iulia Secunda had this made for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11301490.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Mani Fabricius Sabinus, set up by his wife Iulia Secunda.

EDCS-11301493.translation: Valerius Dot dedicated this to Diana Totobisia.
EDCS-11301493.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Diana Totobisia by Valerius Dot.

EDCS-11301496.translation: Spurius Cleopater set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301496.summary: This is a votive dedication by Spurius Cleopater.

EDCS-11301497.text_edition: \n Iovi Sabadio sic et Mercurio Marcus E Celerinus pro se et suis votum solvit\n
EDCS-11301497.translation: To Jupiter Sabadius, and likewise to Mercury, Marcus E. Celerinus paid a vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-11301497.summary: This is a bilingual votive inscription to Jupiter Sabadius and Mercury by Marcus E. Celerinus.

EDCS-11301498.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Cai filius Gaeianus sic vixit annis XX sit tibi terra levis Caius Iulius Valens pater et Pompeia Ingenua mater filio pientissimo bene merenti posuerunt et sibi vivis\n
EDCS-11301498.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Caius Iulius Gaeianus, son of Caius Iulius, lived 20 years. May the earth be light upon you. Caius Iulius Valens, his father, and Pompeia Ingenua, his mother, set this up for their most devoted son, well-deserving, and also for themselves while alive.
EDCS-11301498.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Caius Iulius Gaeianus, set up by his parents who also dedicated it for themselves.

EDCS-11301499.text_edition: \n Valerius Maximus buleuta votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11301499.translation: Valerius Maximus, a council member, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11301499.summary: This is a votive inscription by Valerius Maximus, a council member, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11301500.translation: To the holy Heron [three illegible letters] from the city's council [three illegible letters] descendants [three illegible letters].
EDCS-11301500.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicating something to the holy Heron, possibly by a city council and mentioning descendants, date uncertain.

EDCS-11301502.text_edition: \n estus strategus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-11301502.translation: Estus, the strategos, willingly, happily, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11301502.summary: This is a votive inscription by Estus, a strategos (a Greek administrative official, often found in Roman provinces), likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11301503.translation: Severus erected a vow at Pistinis.
EDCS-11301503.summary: This is a votive inscription by Severus at a place named Pistinis, date uncertain.

EDCS-11301504.text_edition: \n Sancto Apollini Petronius Secundus ex votum sic posuit <q=O>ui et Burtilinus et Eilius libertus \n
EDCS-11301504.translation: Petronius Secundus erected this vow to the Holy Apollo as promised; also Burtilinus and Eilius, the freedman, [did likewise].
EDCS-11301504.summary: This is a votive inscription to Apollo by Petronius Secundus, with Burtilinus and his freedman Eilius also participating, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11301506.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto Maximus Baradi votum solvit\n
EDCS-11301506.translation: Maximus Baradi fulfilled his vow to Hercules Augustus.
EDCS-11301506.summary: This is a votive inscription by Maximus Baradi to Hercules Augustus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11301507.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Domitius Saturninus votum solvit\n
EDCS-11301507.translation: Domitius Saturninus fulfilled his vow to Liber Pater.
EDCS-11301507.summary: This is a votive inscription by Domitius Saturninus to Liber Pater (Bacchus), likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11301508.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Aurelius Aeternalis beneficiarius consularis ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11301508.translation: Aurelius Aeternalis, an aide to the consul, erected this in fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina.
EDCS-11301508.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Aeternalis, a consular aide, dedicating something to Jupiter and Juno, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11301509.text_edition: \n Aemilia Bettia pro nepotes sic suos sic posuit\n
EDCS-11301509.translation: Aemilia Bettia erected this for her descendants.
EDCS-11301509.summary: This is an inscription by Aemilia Bettia dedicating something for her descendants, date uncertain.

EDCS-11301511.text_edition: \n Agathodorus Diophanis vivos sic sibi faciendum curavit \xe1\xbc\x88\xce\xb3\xce\xb1\xce\xb8\xe1\xbd\xb9\xce\xb4\xcf\x89\xcf\x81\xce\xbf\xcf\x82 \xce\x94\xce\xb9\xce\xbf\xcf\x86\xe1\xbd\xb1\xce\xbd\xce\xbf\xcf\x85 \xce\x9d\xce\xb5\xce\xb9\xce\xba\xce\xb5\xe1\xbd\xba\xcf\x82 \xce\xb6\xe1\xbf\xb6\xce\xbd \xce\xba\xce\xb1\xe1\xbd\xb6 \xcf\x86\xcf\x81\xce\xbf\xce\xbd\xe1\xbf\xb6\xce\xbd \xe1\xbc\x91\xce\xb1\xcf\x85\xcf\x84\xe1\xbf\xb7 \xe1\xbc\x90\xcf\x80\xce\xbf\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xb7\xcf\x83\xce\xb5\xce\xbd \xe1\xbc\x80\xce\xbd\xce\xb5\xce\xbe\xce\xbf\xce\xb4\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xb1\xcf\x83\xcf\x84\xce\xbf\xce\xbd\n
EDCS-11301511.translation: Agathodorus, son of Diophanes, while alive, arranged for this to be made for himself, Agathodorus, son of Diophanes, the Greek, and his wife.
EDCS-11301511.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Agathodorus, son of Diophanes, for himself and his wife, likely from the Roman imperial period, and includes Greek text.

EDCS-11301512.text_edition: \n Iulianus Tauri sacerdos Isidis votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-11301512.translation: Iulianus, priest of Isis, willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-11301512.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Isis by a priest named Iulianus, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-11301513.text_edition: \n dinus sacerdos Empori Piretensium ex voto posuit pro Aeternale filio suo\n
EDCS-11301513.translation: Dinus, priest of Emporiae and the Piretenses, set this up according to his vow for his son Aeternale.
EDCS-11301513.summary: A priest named Dinus made a votive offering for his son Aeternale in the city of Emporiae.

EDCS-11600214.text_edition: \n Imperatori Nervae Traiano Caesari Augusto Germanico Dacico Titus Turpilius Hermes dedit dedicavit civibus Romanis consistentibus Callatis circa Caium Iulium Proculum nnem perpetuom sic\n
EDCS-11600214.translation: Titus Turpilius Hermes gave and dedicated this to Emperor Nerva Trajanus Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, for the Roman citizens living in Callatis, concerning Gaius Iulius Proculus, in perpetuity, as follows.
EDCS-11600214.summary: This is a dedication by Titus Turpilius Hermes to Emperor Trajan, likely commemorating a civic improvement or benefaction for Roman citizens in Callatis.

EDCS-11600221.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Sabina vixit annis XXV \n
EDCS-11600221.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavia Sabina lived 25 years.
EDCS-11600221.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Sabina, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-11700819.text_edition: \n o Honoratus vir clarissimus vicarius Thr(aciae) dedit pondo L\n
EDCS-11700819.translation: Honoratus, a most distinguished man, vicar of Thrace, gave 50 pounds.
EDCS-11700819.summary: This record details a gift of 50 pounds made by Honoratus, a high-ranking official (vicar) in the province of Thrace.

EDCS-11700822.text_edition: \n Aia Nainitia Valerius Valens votum ex vico Siamao\n
EDCS-11700822.translation: Valerius Valens, from the village of Siamao, [fulfilled] his vow.
EDCS-11700822.summary: Valerius Valens, from the village of Siamao, fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-11700832.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Pac, who died at the age of 50.

EDCS-11700850.summary: This is a fragment likely referring to Caius Antonius, possibly a dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-11800252.translation: [...] centurion of the [...] legion, [...] centurion of the incomparable legion.
EDCS-11800252.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning centurions from an unspecified legion, possibly honoring an individual or unit.

EDCS-11800261.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Augusti Antonino sic Pii voveit sic vico Narcissiano a magistro Pontiano Valente aram posuit de suo et dedit in vico Narcissiano MC\n
EDCS-11800261.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. For the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius, [he] vowed, as follows. In the Narcissian district, from the master Pontianus Valens, he set up an altar at his own expense and gave it in the Narcissian district, 100 [units].
EDCS-11800261.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, made by Pontianus Valens for the emperor's safety, including the erection of an altar and a donation, dating to the reign of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-11800264.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcinus pro se possuit sic columneam sic votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-11800264.translation: Marcinus erected this column for himself and willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-11800264.summary: This inscription records Marcinus's dedication of a column and payment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely a private votive offering.

EDCS-11800270.text_edition: \n ETVTICF [[]] Augusti Aurelius Valerianus pater dumi et Flavia Nona mater dumi sacratis dum<i=T> curante Dionusio sic vixillario sic dederunt dedicaverunt \n
EDCS-11800270.translation: Aurelius Valerianus, father of Dumius, and Flavia Nona, mother of Dumius, gave and dedicated this shrine to Augustus, with Dionysius acting as administrator.
EDCS-11800270.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a shrine to the deified Augustus by the parents of Dumius, under the supervision of Dionysius, likely dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-11800271.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Arrenti filius Quirina Saturninus duplicarius veteranus alae Asturum vixit annos LXIIII militavit annos XXXII uxor et liberi faciundum curaverunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-11800271.translation: Tiberius Claudius Saturninus, son of Arrentus, of the Quirina tribe, a duplicarius veteran of the ala Asturum, lived 64 years and served 32 years; his wife and children arranged for this to be made; he is buried here.
EDCS-11800271.summary: This tombstone records the life and military service of Tiberius Claudius Saturninus, a veteran of the ala Asturum, erected by his family.

EDCS-11800272.text_edition: \n Marco Iulio Tertullo veterano cohortis I Commagenorum Mithridates miles cohortis eiusdem et Barales bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11800272.translation: Mithridates, a soldier of the 1st Cohort of Commageni, and Barales arranged for this monument to be made for Marcus Julius Tertullus, a deserving veteran of the same cohort.
EDCS-11800272.summary: This tombstone records the erection of a monument for Marcus Julius Tertullus, a veteran of the 1st Cohort of Commageni, by fellow soldiers.

EDCS-11800273.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cocceio Ianuario et Marco filiis qui vixit annis XV Marcus annis I Cocceius Marcinus pater posuit\n
EDCS-11800273.translation: For the Spirits of the Underworld: Marcus Cocceius Marcinus, the father, set this up for his sons Cocceius Ianuarius and Marcus, who lived 15 years and 1 year respectively.
EDCS-11800273.summary: This tombstone records the death of two young sons, Cocceius Ianuarius and Marcus, and was set up by their father, Marcus Cocceius Marcinus.

EDCS-11800274.text_edition: \n Marco Sufenati Marci filio Palatina Titiano militi legionis V Macedonicae beneficiario consularis vixit annos XXV militavit annos VII parentes filio piissimo fecerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-11800274.translation: For Marcus Sufenatius Titianus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, a soldier of the V Macedonica legion, a beneficiarius consularis, who lived 25 years and served 7 years; his parents made this for their most devoted son; he is buried here.
EDCS-11800274.summary: This tombstone records the life and military career of Marcus Sufenatius Titianus, a soldier in the V Macedonica legion, erected by his parents.

EDCS-11800275.text_edition: \n Quintus Allidius Celer veteranus cohortis I Lusitanorum militavit annis XXVI vixit nnis L Quintus Allidius Potitus patrono bene merito posuit\n
EDCS-11800275.translation: Quintus Allidius Potitus set this up for his well-deserving patron, Quintus Allidius Celer, a veteran of the 1st Cohort of Lusitanorum, who served 26 years and lived 50 years.
EDCS-11800275.summary: This tombstone records the death of Quintus Allidius Celer, a veteran of the 1st Cohort of Lusitanorum, erected by his patron.

EDCS-11800277.translation: Marcianus set this up for his father.
EDCS-11800277.summary: This inscription records a son, Marcianus, setting up a monument for his father.

EDCS-11800278.text_edition: \n liuutio centurio semper gemens sepulcro honoravit deliniens suos maerores vixit annis V diebus XII tu qui \n
EDCS-11800278.translation: The centurion Iulius, always groaning, honored with a tomb, expressing his sorrows, lived 5 years and 12 days. You who...
EDCS-11800278.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the life and premature death of a centurion named Iulius, possibly a tombstone.

EDCS-11800279.text_edition: \n I Sixta sic Victorii filiae stena Ovi filia vixit annorum VIIII a Victoriana uxor supra scriptis <b=D>(ene) <m=N>(erentibus) <f=E>(ecit) \n
EDCS-11800279.translation: Sixta, daughter of Victor, and Ste[n]a, daughter of Ovius, lived 9 years. Victoriana, wife of the above-mentioned, made this for them in grief.
EDCS-11800279.summary: This tombstone records the deaths of two young girls, Sixta and Stena, and was erected by Victoriana, their mother.

EDCS-11800281.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus hic ego qui i[aceo ------\n
EDCS-11800281.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, here I who lie [in this tomb]...
EDCS-11800281.summary: A funerary inscription for an unnamed individual, recording the beginning of a dedication to the underworld deities, with a substantial lacuna indicating missing information.

EDCS-11800282.text_edition: \n Ulpius Hortensius vixit annos XX et Ulpius Balimber vixit annos XVIII Cocceia Hortensia Coccei Hortensi |(centurionis) legionis XI Claudiae filia Ulpi Balimberis principis legionis I Italicae uxor filiis piissimis fecit\n
EDCS-11800282.translation: Ulpius Hortensius lived for 20 years and Ulpius Balimber lived for 18 years. Cocceia Hortensia, daughter of Cocceius Hortensius, a centurion of the 11th Claudian Legion, and wife of Ulpius Balimber, a primus pilus of the 1st Italica Legion, made this for her most devoted sons.
EDCS-11800282.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating two individuals, Ulpius Hortensius and Ulpius Balimber, and dedicated by Cocceia Hortensia, likely their mother or wife, possibly dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE based on legion affiliations.

EDCS-11800283.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Numerio Valenti militi legionis XI Claudiae et Caio Domitio Lucius Sextilius Fuscus |(centurio) cohortis I Thracum heres bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11800283.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Numerius Valens, a soldier of the 11th Claudian Legion, and for Gaius Domitius. Lucius Sextilius Fuscus, a centurion of the 1st Cohort of Thracians, his heir, placed this for those who deserved well.
EDCS-11800283.summary: A funerary dedication for two soldiers, Gaius Numerius Valens and Gaius Domitius, with the monument erected by Lucius Sextilius Fuscus, a centurion and their heir, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11800284.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saturninus Biti vixit annis XXXX et Curitthie filius et Valeria et Sabina filias sic eius Ziles marita suo marito bene merenti titulum posuit\n
EDCS-11800284.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Saturninus Biti lived for 40 years, and his son Curitthie, and his daughters Valeria and Sabina. His wife Ziles placed this monument for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11800284.summary: A funerary epitaph for Saturninus Biti, who lived 40 years, set up by his wife Ziles and listing his children, indicating a family tomb or commemoration.

EDCS-11800285.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Egnatius...
EDCS-11800285.summary: The beginning of a funerary inscription for Gaius Egnatius, with a lacuna indicating missing details about his life or the monument's purpose.

EDCS-11800286.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Matronae vixit annis XXXV Valens eques vexillarius cohortis I Cilicum et Val\n
EDCS-11800286.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Claudia Matrona, who lived for 35 years. Valens, an equestrian officer and standard-bearer of the 1st Cohort of Cilicians, and Val[...]
EDCS-11800286.summary: A funerary inscription for Claudia Matrona, who lived 35 years, with a partial dedication by Valens, an equestrian officer, suggesting a monument erected by her husband or close relative.

EDCS-11800287.text_edition: \n qui vixit nnos XLVI menses VII dies XVI Antonia Severa coniux cum filiis communibus Titio Marciano et Titia Marciola t(itulum) carissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-11800287.translation: Who lived for 46 years, 7 months, and 16 days. Antonia Severa, his wife, with their common children Titius Marcianus and Titia Marciola, placed this monument for their dearest.
EDCS-11800287.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed man who lived 46 years, 7 months, and 16 days, erected by his wife Antonia Severa and their children.

EDCS-11800288.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Valeriano innocentiae pleno qui vixit nnos XIIII menses XI dies XV filius Valeriani ducenarii Valerius Florentinus parenti posuit\n
EDCS-11800288.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aurelius Valerianus, full of innocence, who lived for 14 years, 11 months, and 15 days. His son Valerius Florentinus, son of Valerianus the ducenarius, placed this for his parent.
EDCS-11800288.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Valerianus, a boy who died at 14 years old, erected by his father Valerius Florentinus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11800290.translation: Aurelius Putinga, having the right of children...
EDCS-11800290.summary: An inscription fragment mentioning Aurelius Putinga and the legal status of having children ('ius liberorum'), possibly related to a legal document or a funerary dedication.

EDCS-11800291.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et perpetuae securitatis sic hic vis LNIer est semper quem dequerentem questus adque sic quae brevi lectu Claudius nomine quem inflorentem posuerunt parentes praenomen Aurelius nomenque Claudius habebat veteranus quidem legionis XI Claudiae Certus octiens sic decies subito fata rapuerunt tenebris et licet sine liberos sic alumnos sic heredes remisi et modo post obitum reddo meae vitis sic rationem nunc opto ut hic lapes sic aeternam sedem quiescat et me salvum pius et heres Hermes patrono titulum posuit honorandum sepulcro\n
EDCS-11800291.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed and for eternal security. Thus he lies here always lamenting, having complained and having suffered. Claudius, by name, whom his parents placed here while flourishing. He bore the praenomen Aurelius and the nomen Claudius, indeed he was a veteran of the 11th Claudian Legion. Certus, eight times, ten times, suddenly fate snatched him away. Although without children, like adopted sons, like heirs, he sent them away. And now after death, I return the account of my life. Now I wish that this stone may be an eternal seat for his rest, and that my patron, pious and heir Hermes, placed this monument to honor the tomb.
EDCS-11800291.summary: A complex funerary inscription for Claudius (also Aurelius), a veteran of the 11th Claudian Legion, who died suddenly; it records his lamentations, his relationships as a patron and perhaps father figure, and is dedicated by his heir Hermes.

EDCS-11800294.text_edition: \n Cornelius Valentinus miles cohortis I Cilicum Philippianae filius Corneli Valentini ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11800294.translation: Cornelius Valentinus, a soldier of the First Cohort of Cilicians, son of Cornelius Valentinus, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11800294.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by Cornelius Valentinus, a soldier, for himself.

EDCS-11800295.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Marcus Sattenius Fe.
EDCS-11800295.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Sattenius Fe.

EDCS-11800297.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextus Catonius Terminalis legionis V Macedonicae \n
EDCS-11800297.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Sextus Catonius Terminalis of the Fifth Macedonian Legion.
EDCS-11800297.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Catonius Terminalis, a soldier of the Fifth Macedonian Legion.

EDCS-11800301.text_edition: \n Heroni Invicto Sicinius Porphyrio pro salutem sic suam sic et suorum ex imperio Heronis libens animo feci Glabrione et Homullo consulibus\n
EDCS-11800301.translation: To Heron the Unconquered, I, Sicinius Porphyrio, willingly made this for the safety of myself and my family, by Heron's command. Under the consulship of Glabrio and Homullus.
EDCS-11800301.summary: This is a dedication to the god Heron the Unconquered by Sicinius Porphyrio, possibly during the consulship of Glabrio and Homullus (late 1st or early 2nd century CE).

EDCS-11800319.text_edition: \n Dii(s) Inferis Manibus Campanus ex decurione vixit annis Asholes coniugi bene merenti posuit avetes sic superi\n
EDCS-11800319.translation: To the gods of the underworld and the departed. Campanus, formerly a decurion, lived for [number] years. He set this up for his deserving wife, Asholes. Greetings from above.
EDCS-11800319.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Campanus, a former decurion, for his deceased wife Asholes.

EDCS-11800320.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Valerius Iustinus, son of Iustus.
EDCS-11800320.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valerius Iustinus.

EDCS-11800321.text_edition: \n Dis Inferis Manibus Victor filius CorneliIA vixit annis XVI cui sapulchrum sic fecit et titulum posuit Cornelius pater filio pientissimo Have\n
EDCS-11800321.translation: To the gods of the underworld and the departed. Victor, son of Cornelius IA, lived for 16 years. Cornelius, his most devoted father, made his tomb and set up this inscription. Farewell.
EDCS-11800321.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Cornelius for his son Victor, who died at age 16.

EDCS-11800324.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Ulpius F IAS \n
EDCS-11800324.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Caius Ulpius F IAS.
EDCS-11800324.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Ulpius F IAS.

EDCS-11800327.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Firmus veteranus ex decurione alae II Arabacorum sic vixit annis L Aurelia Vettia coniux et Aurelia Firmina filia bene merenti marito et patri posuerunt ave viator et vale\n
EDCS-11800327.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Firmus, a veteran and former decurion of the Second Ala of Arabs, lived for 50 years. Aurelia Vettia, his wife, and Aurelia Firmina, his daughter, set this up for their deserving husband and father. Greetings traveler, and farewell.
EDCS-11800327.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by the wife and daughter for Aurelius Firmus, a veteran decurion.

EDCS-11800328.text_edition: \n Maioribus sanctis Sulpicianus Timocrati ex voto posuit pro se et suos sic\n
EDCS-11800328.translation: To the holy ancestors. Sulpicianus set this up for Timocrates in fulfillment of a vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-11800328.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by Sulpicianus for Timocrates.

EDCS-11800333.text_edition: \n Mama Dadae liberta patrono bene merenti titulum posuit de suo segundum sic voluctatem sic testamenti ave viator\n
EDCS-11800333.translation: Mama Dadae, freedwoman, erected this monument at her own expense for her well-deserving patron, according to his wishes and his will. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-11800333.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by a freedwoman for her patron, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800334.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mucatrio Seutonis filius vixit nnos XXXV Seuto SOP \n
EDCS-11800334.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mucatrio, son of Seuto, lived 35 years.
EDCS-11800334.summary: A tombstone recording the name, parentage, and age at death of Mucatrio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800335.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus te in domo alta sapientia? --- evictus E \n
EDCS-11800335.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [You who are] in a high house with wisdom? ... defeated...
EDCS-11800335.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the 'dis manibus' (spirits of the departed), with partially preserved text that suggests a narrative or eulogy, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800338.summary: A fragment of an inscription mentioning Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus as pontifex maximus, likely a public monument from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800340.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-11800340.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Antonini Magni Pii filius divi Severi Pii nepos Marcus Aurellius sic Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XII consul III pater patriae proconsul nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohortibus praetoriis Alexandrianis decem I II III IIII V VI VII VIII VIIII X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribui conubii dumtaxat cum singulis et primis uxoribus ut aetiam sic si peregrini iuris feminas in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias Lucio Valerio Maximo et Cnaeo Cornelio Paterno consulibus cohortis V praetoriae Alexandrianae piae vindicis Aelio Aurelio Aeli filio Attico Acamantia Doryleo descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Antonini Magni Pii filius divi Severi Pii nepos Marcus Aurellius sic Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XII consul III pater patriae proconsul nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohortibus praetoriis Alexandrianis decem I II III IIII V VI VII VIII VIIII X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribui conubii dumtaxat cum singulis et primis uxoribus ut aetiam sic si peregrini iuris feminas in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos <tollant> ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos Ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias Maximo et Paterno consulibus cohortis V praetoriae Alexandrianae piae vindicis Aelio Aurelio Aeli filio Attico Acamantia Doryleo descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Marci Aureli Sabiniani Marci Aureli Dionysi Ulpi Pontentis sic Valeri Vitaliani Marci Aureli Amandi Cai Ulpi Valentis Titi Claudi Muciani\n
EDCS-11800340.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The names of the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Alexandrian cohorts, the pious vindicators, who faithfully and bravely performed their military service, to be granted the right of marriage, but only with single and first wives, so that even if they have joined women of peregrine status in marriage, they may have children as if born from two Roman citizens. On the day before the 7th Ides of January, in the consulship of Lucius Valerius Maximus and Cnaeus Cornelius Paternus, for Aelius Aurelius Atticus, son of Aelius Doryleus, of the 5th Praetorian Alexandrian cohort, the pious vindicator. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near Minerva. Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The names of the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Alexandrian cohorts, the pious vindicators, who faithfully and bravely performed their military service, to be granted the right of marriage, but only with single and first wives, so that even if they have joined women of peregrine status in marriage, they may have children as if born from two Roman citizens. On the day before the 7th Ides of January, in the consulship of Maximus and Paternus, for Aelius Aurelius Atticus, son of Aelius Doryleus, of the 5th Praetorian Alexandrian cohort, the pious vindicator. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near Minerva. Of Marcus Aurelius Sabinianus, Marcus Aurelius Dionysus, Ulpius Potens, Valerius Vitalianus, Marcus Aurelius Amandus, Caius Ulpius Valens, Titus Claudius Mucianus.
EDCS-11800340.summary: An imperial rescript issued by Emperor Severus Alexander granting the right of Roman citizenship to auxiliary soldiers and their offspring upon marriage to Roman citizens or peregrine women, inscribed on a bronze tablet in Rome, dating to 222-235 AD.

EDCS-11800341.text_edition: \n veterani et cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Quintionis curam agentibus magistris Aelio Bellico et Mucaporo Ditugenti et quaestore Claudio Ianuario Idibus Iuniis Imperatore Vero III et Quadrato consulibus\n
EDCS-11800341.translation: The veterans and Roman citizens and Bessi living in the district of Quintio, with Aelius Bellicus and Mucapor Ditugentus acting as magistrates and Claudius Ianuarius as quaestor, on the Ides of June, in the consulship of the Emperor Verus for the 3rd time and Quadratus.
EDCS-11800341.summary: A record of local administration and a specific date (June 13th, 142 AD), mentioning veterans, Roman citizens, Bessi people, magistrates, and a quaestor in the vicus Quintionis.

EDCS-11800342.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salute Imperatoris Augusti civitas et regio Histriae curam agentibus Claudio Ianuario et Lupo T\n
EDCS-11800342.translation: For Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the safety of the Emperor Augustus, the citizenry and region of Histria, with Claudius Ianuarius and Lupus acting as curators.
EDCS-11800342.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the safety of Emperor Augustus, made by the citizens and region of Histria, with named officials, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-11800343.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonius Dolentis vixit nnos XVIII Salvia Anicii filio bene merenti posuit et Valerius Crescens\n
EDCS-11800343.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonius Dolentis lived 18 years. Salvia, daughter of Anicius, erected [this] for her well-deserving son, and Valerius Crescens.
EDCS-11800343.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonius Dolentis, recording his age at death and erected by his mother Salvia and Valerius Crescens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800344.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Herculano Seuti Aurelia Dusia Seuti coniux et Aurelius Cocceius heres et Aurelius Genialis heres et Aurelius Claudius heres et Aurelius Vindix sic heres fili et heredes bene merenti patri memoriae causae titulum posuerunt ave viator et vale haec sunt\n
EDCS-11800344.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Herculanus. Seutia Aurelia, wife of Seutus, and Aurelius Cocceius, heir, and Aurelius Genialis, heir, and Aurelius Claudius, heir, and Aurelius Vindix, heir, and the heirs, erected this monument for their deserving father for remembrance. Farewell, traveler, and be well. These are [the facts].
EDCS-11800344.summary: A funerary monument erected by the wife and heirs for Aurelius Herculanus, including a common closing exhortation to the passerby, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800353.text_edition: \n sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Parthici Medici vicus Arcidava per \n
EDCS-11800353.translation: Sacred for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus Medicus. The district of Arcidava, through...
EDCS-11800353.summary: A dedication for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, mentioning his titles, and attributed to the district of Arcidava, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800354.text_edition: \n Iussu et ex decreto viri clarissimi Ovini Tertulli consularis termini positi inter Messiam Pudentillam et vicanos Buteridavenses per Vindium Verianum praefectum classis\n
EDCS-11800354.translation: By order and decree of the most distinguished man Ovinius Tertullus, consular, boundary stones were placed between Messia Pudentilla and the villagers of Buterida, through Vindius Verianus, prefect of the fleet.
EDCS-11800354.summary: This inscription records the placement of boundary stones by a fleet prefect, on the order of a consular official, between a private individual and villagers, likely in the late Roman period.

EDCS-11800356.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Marci Aureli Veri Caesaris \n
EDCS-11800356.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest for the safety of Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar.
EDCS-11800356.summary: This is a religious dedication to Jupiter, made for the well-being of the emperor Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar, likely dating to his reign (161-180 AD).

EDCS-12100792.text_edition: \n ------ D]acicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII imperator VII consul VI pater patriae peditibus et equitibus qui militaverunt in cohortibus duabus quae appellantur II Bracaraugustanorum et IIII Gallorum et sunt in Thracia sub Statilio Maximo quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione per Iuventium Celsum quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XIIII Kalendas Augustas Caio Clodio Nummo Lucio Caesennio Sospite consulibus cohortis IIII Gallorum cui praeest Caius Vindilicius Cai filius Publilia Fontanus ex equite Caio Iulio Cai filio Valente Tralli et Iulio Cai filio eius et Valentinae filiae eius et Gaiae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVII imperator VI consul VI pater patriae peditibus et equitibus qui militaverunt in cohortibus duabus quae appellantur II Bracaraugustanorum et IIII Gallorum et sunt in Thracia sub Statilio Maximo quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione per Iuventium Celsum quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes Essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XIIII Kalendas Augustas Caio Clodio Nummo Lucio Caesennio Sospite consulibus cohortis IIII Gallorum cui praeest Caius Vindilius sic Cai filius Publilia Fontanus ex equite Caio Iulio Cai filio Valenti Tralli et Iulio filio eius et Valentinae filiae eius et Gaiae filiae eius Tiberi Iuli Urbani Publi Cauli Vitalis Luci Pulli Verecundi Cai Vettieni Hermetis Publi Atini Trophimi Auli Cascelli Proculi Luci Pulli Anthi\n
EDCS-12100792.translation: For the foot soldiers and cavalry who served in the two cohorts called II Bracaraugustanorum and IIII Gallorum and are in Thrace, having completed twenty-five or more years of service, and were discharged with an honorable dismissal under Statilius Maximus, and for their children and descendants, [the Emperor] gave citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would marry later, but only one wife each, on the 19th day before the Kalends of August, when Gaius Clodius Nummus and Lucius Caesennius Sospes were consuls. [This record concerns] the cohort IIII Gallorum, commanded by Gaius Vindilius, son of Caius, from Publius Fontanus of the equestrian order, Gaius Julius Valens, son of Caius, of Tralles, and his son Julius, and his daughter Valentina, and his daughter Gaia. This has been transcribed and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of Divus Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. [This grant of citizenship was made by] Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Trajan Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 17th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the fatherland, to the foot soldiers and cavalry who served in the two cohorts called II Bracaraugustanorum and IIII Gallorum and are in Thrace, having completed twenty-five or more years of service, and were discharged with an honorable dismissal under Statilius Maximus, and for their children and descendants, [the Emperor] gave citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would marry later, but only one wife each, on the 19th day before the Kalends of August, when Gaius Clodius Nummus and Lucius Caesennius Sospes were consuls. [This record concerns] the cohort IIII Gallorum, commanded by Gaius Vindilius, son of Caius, from Publius Fontanus of the equestrian order, Gaius Julius Valens, son of Caius, of Tralles, and his son Julius, and his daughter Valentina, and his daughter Gaia, Tiberius Julius Urbanus, Publius Caelius Vitalis, Lucius Pullus Verecundus, Gaius Vettienus Hermetis, Publius Atinius Trophimus, Aulus Cascellius Proculus, Lucius Pullus Anthus.
EDCS-12100792.summary: This is a military diploma issued under Emperor Trajan (dated to 103 AD) granting Roman citizenship and connubium to auxiliary soldiers of Cohorts II Bracaraugustanorum and IIII Gallorum serving in Thrace upon honorable discharge after 25 years, along with their descendants.

EDCS-12100817.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100817.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul III pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis III et numero equitum Illyricorum et cohortibus VIIII quae appellantur I Asturum et Hispanorum et I Claudia Gallorum Capitoniana et I Flavia Commagenorum et I Bracaraugustanorum et I Tyriorum sagittaria III Gallorum et I Augusta Pacensis Brittonum |(miliaria) I Hispanorum veterana II Flavia Numidarum II Flavia Bessorum II Gallorum et sunt in Dacia inferiore sub Aquila Fido quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ex numero equitum Illyricorum ex gregale Bitho Solae filio Besso Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul III pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis III et numero equitum Illyricorum et cohortibus VIIII quae appellantur I Asturum et Hispanorum et I Claudia Gallorum Capitoniana et I Flavia Commagenorum et I Bracaraugustanorum et I Tyriorum sagittaria et III Gallorum et I Augusta Nervia Pacensis Brittonum |(miliaria) et I Hispanorum veterana et II Flavia Numidarum II Flavia Bessorum et II Gallorum et sunt in Dacia inferiore sub Aquila Fido quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis Idibus Decembribus Marco Barbio Aemiliano Tito Flavio Iuliano consulibus ex numero equitum Illyricorum ex gregale Bitho Solae filio Besso descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Tiberi Claudi Menandri Publi Atti Severi Luci Pulli Daphni Publi Atti Festi Titi Flavi Lauri Marci Sentili Iasi Cai Iuli Silvani\n
EDCS-12100817.translation: For the cavalry and foot soldiers who served in the three alae and the troop of Illyrian cavalry and the nine cohorts named I Asturum et Hispanorum, I Claudia Gallorum Capitoniana, I Flavia Commagenorum, I Bracaraugustanorum, I Tyriorum sagittaria, III Gallorum, I Augusta Pacensis Brittonum, I Hispanorum veterana, II Flavia Numidarum, II Flavia Bessorum, and II Gallorum, and are in Lower Dacia under Aquila Fidus, having completed twenty-five years of service, and were discharged with an honorable dismissal, and for their children and descendants, [the Emperor] granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not possess it, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would marry later, but only one wife each, chosen from the troop of Illyrian cavalry, from the trooper Bithus, son of Sola, a Besso. [This grant was made by] Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, to the cavalry and foot soldiers who served in the three alae and the troop of Illyrian cavalry and the nine cohorts named I Asturum et Hispanorum, I Claudia Gallorum Capitoniana, I Flavia Commagenorum, I Bracaraugustanorum, I Tyriorum sagittaria, III Gallorum, I Augusta Nervia Pacensis Brittonum, I Hispanorum veterana, II Flavia Numidarum, II Flavia Bessorum, and II Gallorum, and are in Lower Dacia under Aquila Fidus, having completed twenty-five or more years of service, and were discharged with an honorable dismissal, and for their children and descendants, [the Emperor] granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not possess it, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would marry later, but only one wife each. This was granted on the Ides of December, when Marcus Barbilius Appius and Titus Flavius Iulianus were consuls. [This refers to] from the troop of Illyrian cavalry, from the trooper Bithus, son of Sola, a Besso. This has been transcribed and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of Divus Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. [The scribes were] Tiberius Claudius Menander, Publius Attius Severus, Lucius Pullus Daphnis, Publius Attius Festus, Titus Flavius Laurus, Marcus Sentilius Ias, Gaius Julius Silvanus.
EDCS-12100817.summary: This is a military diploma issued under Emperor Antoninus Pius (dated to 138 AD) granting Roman citizenship and connubium to auxiliary soldiers serving in Lower Dacia upon honorable discharge after 25 years, along with their descendants.

EDCS-12100850.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100850.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Antonini Magni Pii filius divi Severi Pii nepos Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul pater patriae nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohhortibus praetoriis Severianis X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribui conubii dumtaxat cum singulis et primis uxoribus ut etiamsi perigrini sic iuris feminas in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos Imperator Caesar divi Antonini Magni Pii filius divi Severi Pii nepos Marcus Aurellius sic Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul pater patriae nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohortibus praetoris Severianis decem I II III IIII V VI VII VIII VIIII X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribui conubii dumtaxat cum singulis et primis uxoribus ut etiamsi peregrini iuris feminas in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias Appio Claudio Iuliano II Caio Bruttio Crispino consulibus cohors X praetoria Severiana pia vindex Marco Aurelio Marci filio Ulpia Muciano Pautalia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam\n
EDCS-12100850.translation: For the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Severian Cohorts, the pious and victorious ones, who performed their military service piously and bravely, the right of marriage is granted, but only with one and their first wife; so that even if they marry women of peregrine status, they may nonetheless have children as if born of two Roman citizens. [This grant was made by] Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul, father of the fatherland. [This concerns] the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Severian Cohorts, the pious and victorious ones, who performed their military service piously and bravely, the right of marriage is granted, but only with one and their first wife; so that even if they marry women of peregrine status, they may nonetheless have children as if born of two Roman citizens. This was granted on the 6th day before the Ides of January, when Appius Claudius Iulianus (for the second time) and Gaius Bruttius Crispinus were consuls. [This refers to] Cohort X Praetoria Severiana Pia Vindex, for Marcus Aurelius, son of Marcus, Ulpius Mucianus, from Pautalia. This has been transcribed and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of Divus Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva.
EDCS-12100850.summary: This is a military diploma issued under Emperor Severus Alexander (dated to 224 AD) granting the right of legal marriage to soldiers of the Praetorian Severian Cohorts, ensuring their children born of peregrine wives would have Roman citizenship.

EDCS-12100851.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100851.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul pater patriae proconsul nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohortibus praetoriis Maximinianis decem I II III IIII V VI VII VIII VIIII X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribui conubii dumtaxat cum singulis et primis uxoribus ut aetiam sic si peregrini iuris feminae in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias Imperatore Caesare Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felice Augusto Marco Pupieno Africano consulibus cohortis V praetoriae pr Maximinianae piae vindicis Marco Aurelio Marci filio Ulpia Vellico Serdica descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul pater patriae proconsul nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohortibus praetoriis Maximinianis decem I II III IIII V VI VII VIII VIIII X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribui conubii dumtaxat cum singulis et primis uxoribus ut aetiam sic si peregrini iuris feminae in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias Imperatore Caesare Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felice Augusto Marco Pupieno Africano consulibus cohortis V praetoriae pr Maximinianae piae vindicis Marco Aurelio Marci filio Ulpia Vellico Serdica descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Cai Popili Fortunati Marci Molli Agathopi Publi Septimi Bassi Cai Massilli Ingenui Marci Aureli Dizes Marci Aureli Diogenetis Titi Claudi Bassi\n
EDCS-12100851.translation: For the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Maximinian Cohorts, the pious and victorious ones, who performed their military service piously and bravely, the right of marriage is granted, but only with one and their first wife; so that even if they marry women of peregrine status, they may nonetheless have children as if born of two Roman citizens. [This grant was made by] Emperor Caesar, Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul. [This concerns] the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Maximinian Cohorts, the pious and victorious ones, who performed their military service piously and bravely, the right of marriage is granted, but only with one and their first wife; so that even if they marry women of peregrine status, they may nonetheless have children as if born of two Roman citizens. This was granted on the 6th day before the Ides of January, when Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus and Marcus Pupienus Africanus were consuls. [This refers to] Cohort V Praetoria Maximinian Pia Vindex, for Marcus Aurelius, son of Marcus, Ulpius Vellicus, from Serdica. This has been transcribed and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of Divus Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. [The scribes were] Gaius Popilius Fortunatus, Marcus Mollius Agathopus, Publius Septimius Bassus, Gaius Massilius Ingenuus, Marcus Aurelius Dizes, Marcus Aurelius Diogenetes, Titus Claudius Bassus.
EDCS-12100851.summary: This is a military diploma issued under Emperor Maximinus Thrax (dated to 236 AD) granting the right of legal marriage to soldiers of the Praetorian Maximinian Cohorts, ensuring their children born of peregrine wives would have Roman citizenship.

EDCS-12100905.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100905.translation: For the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Severian Cohorts, the pious and victorious ones, who performed their military service piously and bravely, the right of marriage is granted, but only with one and their first wife; so that even if they marry women of peregrine status, they may nonetheless have children as if born of two Roman citizens. [This grant was made by] Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland. [This concerns] the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Severian Cohorts, the pious and victorious ones, who performed their military service piously and bravely, the right of marriage is granted, but only with one and their first wife; so that even if they marry women of peregrine status, they may nonetheless have children as if born of two Roman citizens. This was granted on the 6th day before the Ides of January, when Quintus Aiacius Modestus (for the second time) and Marcus Maecius Probus were consuls. [This refers to] Cohort I Praetoria Severiana Pia Vindex, for Marcus Aurelius, son of Marcus, Ulpius Secundus, from Nicopolis. This has been transcribed and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of Divus Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva.
EDCS-12100905.summary: This is a military diploma issued under Emperor Severus Alexander (dated to 230 AD) granting the right of legal marriage to soldiers of the Praetorian Severian Cohorts, ensuring their children born of peregrine wives would have Roman citizenship.

EDCS-12200271.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Caius Iulius Domitianus immunis librarius ex voto\n
EDCS-12200271.translation: To Sol Invictus, Gaius Julius Domitianus, immunis librarius, from a vow.
EDCS-12200271.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Sol Invictus made by Gaius Julius Domitianus, an immunis librarius (a clerk exempt from certain duties), likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12200273.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Sammius Terentianus praefectus kastrorum\n
EDCS-12200273.translation: Titus Flavius Sammius Terentianus, prefect of the camp.
EDCS-12200273.summary: This is a simple designation of an individual, Titus Flavius Sammius Terentianus, who held the position of prefect of the camp (praefectus castrorum), likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12200275.text_edition: \n Aesculapium ex donis argenteum pondo V unciis V Caius Mansuanius Severus legatus Augusti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-12200275.translation: Caius Mansuanius Severus, legate of the emperor, saw to the making of Aesculapius from silver gifts weighing 5 pounds and 5 ounces.
EDCS-12200275.summary: This inscription records that Gaius Mansuanius Severus, a legate of the emperor, commissioned a statue of Aesculapius made from silver donations, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12200276.text_edition: \n Quintus Apidius Sabinus hastatus legionis I Italicae ex trecenario donum dedit\n
EDCS-12200276.translation: Quintus Apidius Sabinus, a hastatus of the I Italica legion, gave this gift as a trecenarius.
EDCS-12200276.summary: This inscription records a military dedication made by Quintus Apidius Sabinus, a soldier of the I Italica legion.

EDCS-12200277.text_edition: \n Asclepio Saoro Lucius Appius ani tesserarius legionis I Italicae donum dedit\n
EDCS-12200277.translation: Lucius Appius, a tesserarius of the I Italica legion, gave this gift to Asclepius Saorus.
EDCS-12200277.summary: This inscription records a military dedication made by Lucius Appius to the god Asclepius Saorus.

EDCS-12200278.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hy<g=C>iae Ae<l=I>(ius) Macedo medicus pro salute sua posuit \n
EDCS-12200278.translation: Ae(l)ius Macedo, a physician, erected this for his own well-being to Aesculapius and Hygieia.
EDCS-12200278.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by the physician Ae(l)ius Macedo for his health, dedicated to Aesculapius and Hygieia.

EDCS-12200279.text_edition: \n ati librarii Septimius Ius T C M \n
EDCS-12200279.translation: Septimius Ius, a scribe, [---] for the statist.
EDCS-12200279.summary: This very short inscription appears to record the name of a scribe, Septimius Ius, possibly related to administrative duties.

EDCS-12200280.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Caius Caesellius Vitalis veteranus legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12200280.translation: Caius Caesellius Vitalis, a veteran of the I Italica legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina.
EDCS-12200280.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Caesellius Vitalis, a veteran of the I Italica legion, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter and Juno.

EDCS-12200281.text_edition: \n Sabadio sic sacrum Iulius Dolens cum suis posuit\n
EDCS-12200281.translation: This sacred precinct to Sabadius was erected by Iulius Dolens with his family.
EDCS-12200281.summary: This inscription records the erection of a sacred space to Sabadius by Iulius Dolens and his household.

EDCS-12200282.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Saturninus veteranus legionis XI Cl[audiae ------\n
EDCS-12200282.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Marcus Aurelius Saturninus, a veteran of the XI Claudia legion, [died].
EDCS-12200282.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Marcus Aurelius Saturninus, a veteran of the XI Claudia legion.

EDCS-12200283.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Ditusanus strator tribuni vixit annos XLVIII et Claudia Cocceia coniux memoriam posuit vivo suo qui dimissus est in barbarico et Aurelia Aelia filia eius et heres\n
EDCS-12200283.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aurelius Ditusanus, aide to a tribune, lived 48 years, and his wife Claudia Cocceia erected this memorial while he was alive, who was discharged in the provinces, and their daughter and heir Aurelia Aelia.
EDCS-12200283.summary: This is a memorial tomb inscription for Aurelius Ditusanus, an aide to a tribune, erected by his wife and daughter.

EDCS-12200284.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Marco veterano ex praetoriano vixit annos XLIII militavit annos XVIII Aurelia Sispiris coniux et Aurelia Marcia filia et heredes posuerunt\n
EDCS-12200284.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aurelius Marcus, a veteran of the Praetorian Guard, lived 43 years and served 18 years. His wife Aurelia Sispiris and daughter Aurelia Marcia and heirs erected this.
EDCS-12200284.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Aurelius Marcus, a veteran of the Praetorian Guard, erected by his wife and daughter.

EDCS-12200285.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Diurdano Decibali sic veterano Quintus Priscus filius et Felix libertus bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-12200285.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Priscus, son, and Felix, freedman, erected this for Diurdianus Decibali, a deserving veteran.
EDCS-12200285.summary: This is a tomb inscription honoring Diurdanus Decibali, a veteran, erected by his son and freedman.

EDCS-12200286.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Castus cornicularius vixit annos XLVIII Aelia Sabina coniux et Valerius Aeternalis filius et heredes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-12200286.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Castus, a cornicularius, lived 48 years. Aelia Sabina, his wife, and Valerius Aeternalis, his son and heirs, set this up for him who deserved well.
EDCS-12200286.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Castus, set up by his wife and son, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12200287.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti Flavius Germanus IIver sic designatus et Ailii sic Flavius et Marinus sacerdotes posuerunt\n
EDCS-12200287.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus. For the safety of our lord Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Flavius Germanus, as a junior official, and Ailius Flavius and Marinus, priests, set this up.
EDCS-12200287.summary: An altar dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus for the emperor's health, by officials and priests, likely during the reign of Severus Alexander (222-235 AD).

EDCS-12200288.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolocheno sic pro salute Imperatoris Iulius Alexander et Germanuus Baronas sacerdotes posuerunt\n
EDCS-12200288.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus. For the safety of the Emperor, Julius Alexander and Germanus Baronas, priests, set this up.
EDCS-12200288.summary: An altar dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus for the emperor's health, by priests, likely dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-12200292.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Antonini Pii et Aureli Veri Caesaris posuerunt Titus Aelius Mucatralis veteranus alae I Flaviae Gaetulorum et Seutes Mucatralis magister de suis Glabrione et Homullo consulibus\n
EDCS-12200292.translation: For the safety of Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Pius and Caesar Aurelius Verus, Titus Aelius Mucatralis, a veteran of the I Flavia Gaetulorum cavalry wing, and Seutes Mucatralis, a magistrate, set this up from their own funds, under the consulship of Glabrio and Homullus.
EDCS-12200292.summary: A dedication for the safety of emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius, by a veteran and a magistrate, likely from the Antonine period (mid-2nd century AD).

EDCS-12200293.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Exuperatus decurio civitatis Histriae vixit nnos L relictis filiis Aurelia Iovina coniux bene merenti memoriam posuit vale viator\n
EDCS-12200293.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Exuperatus, a decurion of the civitas Histriae, lived 50 years. Leaving behind his sons, Aurelia Iovina, his wife, set up this memorial for him who deserved well. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-12200293.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Exuperatus, a decurion from Histria, set up by his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12300208.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
