EDCS-12300208.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIIII imperator XXVII pater patriae censor consul V equitibus qui militant in alis quinque quae appellantur veterana Gallorum et Thraecum sic et Gallorum et Thraecum sic Antiana et Gallorum et Thraecum sic cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XIIII Kalendas Iulias Marco Asinio Marcello Manio Acilio Aviola consulibus alae Gallorum et Thraecum sic Antianae cui praeest Marcus Milonius Verus Iunianus equiti Romaestae Rescenti filio Spiuro descriptum et recognitum ex tabula ahenea sic quae fixa est in capitolio in aedem opis in pronaevo sic latere dexteriore Sexti Magi Bessi Rufi nauarchi Cai Cassi Longini tribuni Bessi Marci Valeri Firmi tribuni Luci Numeri Lupi tribuni Bessi Marci Titini Macri centurionis Sexti Apulei Macri centurionis Luci Valeri Volseni missic<i=L> Bessi \n
EDCS-12300208.translation: This inscription, copied and verified from a bronze tablet affixed in Rome on the Capitol in the temple of Ops in the pronaos on the right side, concerning the equites who serve in five cavalry wings named Gallorum et Thracum, Gallorum et Thracum Antiana, and Gallorum et Thracum, along with their wives whom they had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, at most one each, before the 14th day before the Kalends of July, under the consulship of Marcus Asinius Marcellus and Manius Acilius Aviola, for the cavalryman Romaestes, son of Resentius, son of Spiurus, of the Gallorum et Thracum Antiana cavalry wing, commanded by Marcus Milonius Verus Iunianus, and recorded by Sextus Magius Bessi Rufus, naval captain, and Titus Cassius Longinus, Bessi, and Marcus Valerius Firmus, Bessi, and Lucius Numerius Lupus, Bessi, and Marcus Titinius Macrus, centurion, and Sextus Apuleius Macrus, centurion, and Lucius Valerius Volsenus, Bessi.
EDCS-12300208.summary: A decree granting citizenship and the right of marriage to veterans and their families, inscribed on bronze and publicly displayed, dating to the consulship of Asinius Marcellus and Acilius Aviola (138 AD).

EDCS-12300217.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300217.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator VI pater patriae consul III veteranis qui militaverunt in in classe Misenensi sub Sexto Lucilio Basso qui sena et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerunt et sunt deducti Paestum quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est iis civitas data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem V Idus Februarias Imperatore Caesare Vespasiano Augusto III Marco Cocceio Nerva consulibus Tutio Buti filius Dacus tabula I pagina V loco XI descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio in podio parte exteriore arae gentis Iuliae contra signum Liberi Patris Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator VI pater patriae consul III veteranis qui militaverunt in classe Misenensi sub Sexto Lucilio Basso qui sena et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerunt et sunt deducti Paestum quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus\n
EDCS-12300217.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, Imperator for the 6th time, father of the country, consul for the 3rd time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had (or those they would later marry, one each) to the veterans who served in the Misenum fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who had completed twenty years or more of service and were settled at Puteoli, whose names are listed below, to them and their children and descendants, before the 5th day before the Ides of February, under the consulship of Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus for the 3rd time and Marcus Cocceius Nerva. Copied and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, in the outer base of the temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, in front of the statue of Liber Pater.
EDCS-12300217.summary: An imperial decree granting citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of the Misenum fleet, issued during Vespasian's reign (71 AD).

EDCS-12300225.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300225.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 18th time, father of the country, censor, consul for the 7th time, designated consul for the 8th time, grants the right of marriage to the speculatores (bodyguards) who served in my praetorian guard, and also to the soldiers who served in the nine praetorian cohorts and the four urban cohorts, who have performed their military service bravely and piously. They may marry, however, only one wife each, and even if they marry women of peregrine status, their children will be considered legitimate, as if born of two Roman citizens. This was decreed before the 4th day before the Nones of December, under the consulship of Galeo Tettienus Petronianus and Marcus Fulvius Gillo. This record, for Lucius Ennius Ferox, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, from Aquis Statellis, a soldier in the VI Praetorian Cohort, was copied and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the base of Jupiter Africus.
EDCS-12300225.summary: An imperial rescript issued by Vespasian during his consulship with Gillo (75 AD), granting Roman soldiers the right to marry peregrine women and legitimize their children.

EDCS-12300226.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XVIIII pater patriae censor consul VIII peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohortibus octo I Cantabrorum I Thracum Syriaca I Sugambrorum tironum II Lucensium III et VIII Gallorum Cilicum Mattiacorum quae sunt in Moesia sub Sexto Vettuleno Ceriale qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerunt quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem VII Idus Februarias Lucio Ceionio Commodo Decimo Novio Prisco consulibus cohorti Cilicum cui praeest Publius Seppienus Publi filius Pollia Aelianus pediti Perasi Publi filio Aegis descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio post piscinam in tribunalis deorum parte posteriore Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XVIIII pater patriae censor consul VIII peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohortibus octo I Cantabrorum I Thracum Syriaca I Sugambrorum tironum II Lucensium III et VIII Gallorum Cilicum Mattiacorum quae sunt in Moesia sub Sexto Vettuleno Ceriale qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerunt quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem VII Idus Februarias Lucio Ceionio Commodo Decimo Novio Prisco consulibus cohorti Cilicum cui praeest Publius Seppienus Publi filius Pollia Aelianus pediti Perasi Publi filio Aegis descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio Sexti Priverni Celeris Publi Atini Rufi Cnaei Pompei Maximii Marci Veturi Montani Marci Stlacci Iuvenalis Luci Naevi Vestalis Marci Lolli Rufi\n
EDCS-12300226.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 9th time, Imperator for the 19th time, father of the country, censor, consul for the 8th time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had (or those they would later marry, one each) to the infantry and cavalry soldiers who served in eight cohorts (I Cantabrorum, I Thracum Syriaca, I Sugambrorum tironum, II Lucensium, III and VIII Gallorum Cilicum, Mattiacorum) in Moesia under Sextus Vettulenus Cerialis, who had completed twenty years or more of service, and whose names are listed below, to them and their children and descendants, before the 7th day before the Ides of February, under the consulship of Lucius Ceionius Commodus and Decimus Novius Priscus. This record, for Perasus, son of Publius, of the Aegis tribe, an infantryman in the Cilicum Cohort commanded by Publius Seppienus Aelianus, was copied and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, behind the pool in the rear part of the tribunal of the gods.
EDCS-12300226.summary: An imperial decree granting citizenship and marriage rights to soldiers serving in various cohorts in Moesia, issued by Vespasian during his consulship with Novius Priscus (79 AD).

EDCS-12300248.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300248.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, grants citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had (or those they would later marry, one each) to the cavalry and infantry soldiers serving in three cavalry wings and six cohorts (Gallorum Flaviana, I Pannoniorum, II Hispanorum, Arava(co)rum, I Sugambrorum veterana, I Bracaraugustanorum, I Hispanorum veterana, II Mattiacorum, II Gallorum, and Ubiorum) in Lower Moesia under Quintus Pomponius Rufus, as well as those discharged honorably who completed twenty years or more of service, whose names are listed below, to them and their children and descendants. This was decreed before the 19th day before the Kalends of September, under the consulship of Quintus Fabio Barbaro and Aulus Caecilius Faustinus, for Marcus Antonius Rufus, son of Marcus, of the Abretten tribe, and his son Marcus, infantryman of the II Gallorum cohort commanded by Visulanius Crescens. Copied and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near the temple of Minerva.
EDCS-12300248.summary: An imperial decree by Trajan granting citizenship and marriage rights to auxiliary soldiers serving in Moesia, issued during his consulship with Caecilius Faustinus (98 AD).

EDCS-12300347.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300347.translation: For the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian cohorts, the Pious Defenders, the right of lawful marriage is granted, specifically with a first wife only, so that even if they have taken foreign women as wives, they may have children as if born from two Roman citizens. Dated the 7th day before the Ides of January, during the consulship of Emperor Severus Alexander (for the second time) and Marcellus (for the second time). Cohort X Praetoria Severiana Pia Vindex. Recorded and verified from the bronze tablet, which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the Divine Augustus at the Minerva.
EDCS-12300347.summary: This inscription is a rescript issued by Emperor Severus Alexander in 224 CE, granting the right of legal marriage to praetorian soldiers, recorded on a bronze tablet.

EDCS-12800385.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli et Genio municipii Traiani Tropaei per Quintio! Prisciano sic et Iulio! Marco sic IIviri sic quinquennales aediles Crescens et Sabinus Marcus et Severus qaestores scribae Valentinus et Postumus Commodo et <B>yrro sic consulibus\n
EDCS-12800385.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius and the Genius of the municipium of Trajanus Tropaeum, through Quintius Priscianus and Julius Marcus, the highest magistrates, and Crescens and Sabinus, quinquennial aediles, and Marcus and Severus, quaestors, with Valentinus and Postumus, scribes, during the consulship of Commodus and Byrrhus.
EDCS-12800385.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius and the Genius of the municipium of Trajanus Tropaeum, listing various local magistrates, likely from the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12800386.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Fufidius Lucianus decurio municipii deceptus a Castabocos sic IIviratu suo cui merito titulum posuit Ulpia Marcia coniuxs sic\n
EDCS-12800386.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Fufidius Lucianus, a decurion of the municipium, deceived by the Castaboci during his tenure as aedile, for whom his wife Ulpia Marcia rightly erected this monument.
EDCS-12800386.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Fufidius Lucianus, a decurion and former aedile of a municipium, whose wife Ulpia Marcia erected the monument after he was wronged by the Castaboci.

EDCS-13301353.text_edition: \n Apollini Cendrisseno nius K ex voto\n
EDCS-13301353.translation: To Apollo Cendrissenus, from a vow.
EDCS-13301353.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo Cendrissenus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301354.text_edition: \n Bono Eventui Augusto sacrum Maesius primus pilus\n
EDCS-13301354.translation: Sacred to Good Fortune Augustus, from Maesius, a primus pilus.
EDCS-13301354.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the divinity Good Fortune Augustus by Maesius, a retired centurion (primus pilus), probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301356.translation: Sacred to Good Fortune, from Aurelius Reg...
EDCS-13301356.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the divinity Good Fortune, offered by Aurelius Reg[...], likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301358.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno Sancto Aurelius Statianus actor periculo maris liberatus ex voto promisso restituit\n
EDCS-13301358.translation: To the Eternal Holy God. Aurelius Statianus, an agent, having been freed from danger at sea, restored this in fulfillment of a promised vow.
EDCS-13301358.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Statianus, an agent, to the 'Eternal Holy God', after surviving a peril at sea, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301360.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae sacrum Marcus Ulpius Napucae sic Peregrinus tribunus legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13301360.translation: Sacred to Diana Augusta. Marcus Ulpius Peregrinus, tribune of the First Italian Legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13301360.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Diana Augusta by Marcus Ulpius Peregrinus, a military tribune serving in the Legio I Italica, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301363.text_edition: \n Hygiam ex donis argenteam pondo IIII unciis VII Marcus Clodius Laetus legatus Augusti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-13301363.translation: Marcus Clodius Laetus, legate of the emperor, saw to the making of a silver statue of Hygeia, weighing 4 pounds and 7 ounces.
EDCS-13301363.summary: This inscription records that Marcus Clodius Laetus, an imperial legate, commissioned the creation of a silver statue of Hygeia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301364.text_edition: \n Hygiae sacrum Flavius Honoratus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae donum dedit\n
EDCS-13301364.translation: Sacred to Hygeia. Flavius Honoratus, a centurion of the First Italian Legion, gave this gift.
EDCS-13301364.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hygeia by Flavius Honoratus, a centurion in the Legio I Italica, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301366.text_edition: \n Iovi votum merito Lucius Cultius signifer\n
EDCS-13301366.translation: To Jupiter, Lucius Cultius, standard-bearer, a vow in return for a deserved favor.
EDCS-13301366.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter by a standard-bearer, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-13301367.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Antonius Super votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13301367.translation: Marcus Antonius Super willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-13301367.summary: A soldier, Marcus Antonius Super, records the fulfillment of his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301369.text_edition: \n VSLL Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius C Exa votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13301369.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Gaius C Exa willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13301369.summary: This inscription records Gaius C Exa fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely a military dedication from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301370.text_edition: \n Concordia consacranis Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae et Minervae Caius Staboratius veteranus ex benef(iciario) consularis consacranis Iovianorum ob ohnorem sic inmunitatis eius donum dedit\n
EDCS-13301370.translation: To Concordia, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, and Minerva, Gaius Staboratius, a veteran and formerly beneficiary of the consular legate, gave this gift to the 'consecranii' of the Jovian association in honor of his immunity.
EDCS-13301370.summary: A veteran and former consular beneficiary dedicates a gift to Concordia, Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva for an association of 'Joviani', likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301371.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Depulsori pro salute domini nostri Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti Gaius Baienius Gai filius Claudia Ianuarius Celeia primus pilus legionis I Italicae Severianae ex voto posuit dedicatum III Nonas Octobres Albino et Maximo consulibus per Lucium Mantennium Sabinum legatum Augusti pro praetore et Servaeum Cornelianum legatum legionis adiutrix legio prima tirone probato caliga prima cum compare centurione cto Baienium ] ACVMA felice potM DOMRomam misisti SA Iovi primus pilus tibi sancto ex voto posuit POIanuarius aram\n
EDCS-13301371.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Depulsor, for the health of our lord Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, Gaius Baienius, son of Gaius, Claudia Ianuarius, Celeia, a senior centurion of the I Italica Severiana legion, set up this altar from a vow, dedicated on the third day before the Nones of October, when Albinus and Maximus were consuls, through Lucius Mantennius Sabinus, imperial legate with proconsular authority, and Servaeus Cornelius, legate of the legion Adjutrix, the first cohort, a qualified recruit with his comrade, the centurion, Baienius... to Jupiter, the senior centurion, set up this altar for you, holy one, from a vow.
EDCS-13301371.summary: This lengthy inscription records a senior centurion, Gaius Baienius, dedicating an altar to Jupiter for the health of Emperor Severus Alexander, dated to 224 CE, and also mentions other military officials and units.

EDCS-13301376.text_edition: \n Lunae sacrum Caius Tullius Cai filius Collina Apollinaris Scythopoli primus pilus Aquilae legionis I Italicae donum dedit\n
EDCS-13301376.translation: Sacred to Luna, Gaius Tullius Apollinaris, son of Gaius, of the Collina tribe, senior centurion of the I Italica legion's Eagle standard, from Scythopolis, gave this gift.
EDCS-13301376.summary: A senior centurion of the I Italica legion, Gaius Tullius Apollinaris, dedicates a gift to the goddess Luna, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301377.text_edition: \n Marti et Genio armamentarii Valerius Crescens custos armorum\n
EDCS-13301377.translation: To Mars and the Genius of the armory, Valerius Crescens, keeper of the arms.
EDCS-13301377.summary: Valerius Crescens, keeper of arms, makes a dedication to Mars and the Genius of the armory, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301378.text_edition: \n Marti Victori legionis I Italicae Antoninianae pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti divi Antonini fili Marcus Valerius Marci Valeri Mucacenti filius Quirina Flavianus domo Cirta primus pilus ex equite Romano Aquilae dono dedit\n
EDCS-13301378.translation: To Mars Victor, for the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, Marcus Valerius Flavianus, son of Marcus Valerius Mucacanthus, of the Quirina tribe, from Cirta, senior centurion, formerly a Roman knight, gave this gift to the Eagle standard of the legion I Italica Antoniniana.
EDCS-13301378.summary: A senior centurion of the I Italica Antoniniana legion, Marcus Valerius Flavianus, dedicates a gift to Mars Victor for the health of Emperor Caracalla, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301381.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Caius Iulius Maximus praefectus castrorum legionis I Italicae\n
EDCS-13301381.translation: To the Unconquered God, Gaius Julius Maximus, prefect of the camp of the I Italica legion.
EDCS-13301381.summary: Gaius Julius Maximus, prefect of the camp of the I Italica legion, makes a dedication to the Unconquered God, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301384.text_edition: \n Deae sanctae Placidae Iulius Statilis Augustalis municipii Novensium ex voto posuit numinis monitu\n
EDCS-13301384.translation: To the holy goddess Placida, Iulius Statilis Augustalis of the municipality of Novensium set up this monument from a vow, at the god's prompting.
EDCS-13301384.summary: Iulius Statilis Augustalis of Novensium sets up a monument to the goddess Placida, motivated by divine inspiration, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13301386.translation: Fortunatius Lucilius placed this at the Augustan crossroads.
EDCS-13301386.summary: This is a dedication placed at a crossroads by Fortunatius Lucilius, likely in the imperial period.

EDCS-13301387.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Iucundus Quadriviis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13301387.translation: Lucius Valerius Iucundus gladly fulfilled his vow at the crossroads as was deserved.
EDCS-13301387.summary: This records Lucius Valerius Iucundus fulfilling a vow at a crossroads, likely in the imperial period.

EDCS-13301388.translation: Valerius Eutyches to the holy Sarapis.
EDCS-13301388.summary: This is a dedication to the god Sarapis by Valerius Eutyches, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-13301391.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Victoriae Augustae Pantheae sanctissimae Lucius Maximius Luci filius Voltiniae Gaetulicus Viennae quod tiro aput sic legionem XX Valeriam victricem voveram nunc primus pilus legionis I Italicae stipendiorum LVII votum solveram Marullo et Aeliano consulibus\n
EDCS-13301391.translation: For the health of the Emperor, for the most sacred Augusta Victoria, Lucius Maximius Gaetulicus, son of Lucius, of the Voltinian tribe from Vienna, who as a recruit with the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix had made a vow, now as primus pilus of the 1st Italian Legion, after 57 years of service, fulfilled his vow, in the consulship of Marullus and Aelianus.
EDCS-13301391.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Maximius Gaetulicus, a primus pilus of the 1st Italian Legion, detailing his vow for the Emperor's health and mentioning his service and consuls, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13301393.text_edition: \n O et nova ordinatione promotus ex optione EQ inpositus in ordinem legionis I Italicae factus primus pilus legionis supra scriptae ex voto posuit felix legio I Italica victrix pia semper ubique\n
EDCS-13301393.translation: Promoted by a new arrangement and appointed from the choice of the prefect, placed in the order of the 1st Italian Legion, having become primus pilus of the aforementioned legion, he placed this according to his vow; may the 1st Italian Legion be victorious, pious, always and everywhere.
EDCS-13301393.summary: This inscription celebrates a promotion to primus pilus in the 1st Italian Legion and expresses a vow and a wish for the legion's success, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13301394.text_edition: \n ------] NO Nestor Demetri et Hedone Nymphi ex voto\n
EDCS-13301394.translation: [...] Nestor to Demetrius and Hedone Nymphe, according to the vow.
EDCS-13301394.summary: This is a dedication by Nestor to Demetrius and Hedone Nymphe, likely a funerary or votive marker, with an incomplete date.

EDCS-13301395.summary: This inscription records the name and title of Titus Aurelius Bithus, a camp prefect, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13301396.text_edition: \n ------ pri]nceps legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13301396.translation: [...] princeps of the 1st Italian Legion gladly fulfilled his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-13301396.summary: This incomplete inscription records a princeps of the 1st Italian Legion fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13301397.text_edition: \n nus miles legionis I Ital(icae) sacerdos\n
EDCS-13301397.translation: a soldier of the 1st Italian Legion, a priest.
EDCS-13301397.summary: This fragmented inscription identifies a soldier of the 1st Italian Legion who also served as a priest, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13301401.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription containing only a possible personal name, Antoninus, from the Roman period.

EDCS-13301404.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico patri patriae pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IIII imperatori VIII consuli II primi ordines et centuriones legionis I Italicae Aurelius Concessus Flavius Constans Sabinius Pudens Fannius Semninius Baebius Urvinianus nnius Deccius cohors II Numisius Florus Quietus (A)elius Eufeminus Dovius Fortunatus Aelius Cydias cohors III Claudius Dexter Granius Iealida Iulius Co Iulius Ni cohors IIII Mufeius Ter cohors V Num F Petronius Florus Humidius cohors VI \n
EDCS-13301404.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, acclaimed imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time; by the primi ordines and centurions of the First Italian Legion, the second cohort, the third cohort, the fourth cohort, the fifth cohort, and the sixth cohort, [names follow].
EDCS-13301404.summary: This inscription records a dedication by officers and soldiers of the First Italian Legion to Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, likely dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-13301410.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning a pontiff, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-13301414.translation: to the most distinguished man, a fetial [restored]
EDCS-13301414.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription honoring a distinguished man who held the priestly office of fetial.

EDCS-13301416.text_edition: \n Dedicata Kalendis Mais Maximo et Paterno consulibus per Fonteium Maximum virum clarissimum legatum legionis\n
EDCS-13301416.translation: Dedicated on the Kalends of May, in the consulship of Maximo and Paterno, by Fonteius Maximus, a most distinguished man, legate of the legion.
EDCS-13301416.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument on May 1st, during the consulship of Maximo and Paterno, and was made by Fonteius Maximus, a legionary legate.

EDCS-13301418.summary: This very short inscription is a dedication to an 'eternal lord', possibly a deity or a powerful emperor.

EDCS-13301419.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Macenio veteranus legionis I Minerviae domo Cappadocia vixit annos LX Marcus Valerius Macenio filius et heres patri karissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-13301419.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Macenio, veteran of the First Minervian Legion, from Cappadocia, lived for 60 years. Marcus Valerius Macenio, son and heir, had this made for his dearest father.
EDCS-13301419.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Marcus Aurelius Macenio, a veteran of the First Minervian Legion, funded by his son and heir.

EDCS-13301420.translation: Marcus Aurelius, scribe, from the place of military service.
EDCS-13301420.summary: This inscription identifies Marcus Aurelius as a scribe who served in the military.

EDCS-13301422.text_edition: \n ------] Caio Bruttio Cai filio Claudia Gouto Viruno veterano legionis I Italicae vixit nnos LXXX hic situs est Caius Vibius Therapo amico bene merenti secundum formulam testamenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-13301422.translation: [... to] Gaius Bruttius, son of Gaius, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, lived for 80 years. Here he lies. Gaius Vibius Therapo had this made for his well-deserving friend, according to the terms of his will.
EDCS-13301422.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Gaius Bruttius, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, commissioned by his friend Gaius Vibius Therapo.

EDCS-13301423.text_edition: \n Publius Farfinias Publi filius Pollia Severus Fano Fortunae cornicen legionis VIII Augustae vixit annos L militavit annos XXX hic situs est Vitalis et Ferox fili faciundum curaverunt\n
EDCS-13301423.translation: Publius Farfinias Severus, son of Publius, of the Pollian tribe, from Fano, horn-player of the Eighth Augustan Legion, lived for 50 years, served for 30 years. Here he lies. His sons Vitalis and Ferox had this made.
EDCS-13301423.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Publius Farfinias Severus, a veteran and horn-player of the Eighth Augustan Legion, commissioned by his sons.

EDCS-13301424.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Quietae et Flavio Decebalo veterano legionis I Italicae Severianae marito eius Sammonia Antonia et Aurelius Ianuarius veteranus et Aurelius Elpidephorus heredes ex testamento faciendum curaverunt optamus sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-13301424.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelia Quieta and her husband Flavius Decebalus, veteran of the First Italian Severian Legion. Sammonia Antonia, Aurelius Ianuarius, veteran, and Aurelius Elpidephorus, heirs, had this made according to the will. We wish the earth may be light upon you.
EDCS-13301424.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Aurelia Quieta and her husband Flavius Decebalus, a veteran of the First Italian Severian Legion, commissioned by their heirs.

EDCS-13301425.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Fabia Ancyra Magno veterano legionis I Italicae vixit annos C\n
EDCS-13301425.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Julius Ancyra Magnus, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, lived for 100 years.
EDCS-13301425.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Ancyra Magnus, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, who lived to be 100 years old.

EDCS-13301428.text_edition: \n Cai filius Papiria Severus Oesci veteranus legionis I Italicae et Marciae Marcellae coniugi fecit hoc monumentum positum in fronte pedes LXXX in agro pedes LXXX heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-13301428.translation: Severus Oesci, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, and Marcia Marcella, his wife, made this monument for himself; it is set up 80 feet in front and 80 feet in depth in the land, and the heir will not inherit it.
EDCS-13301428.summary: A funerary monument set up by Severus Oesci, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, for himself and his wife Marcia Marcella, with specific dimensions and inheritance clauses.

EDCS-13301430.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Publiae qui vixit nnos XIIII Aurelius Victor Perburidavensis qui et Buricodavensis et Aelia Dionysia parentes filiae carissimae\n
EDCS-13301430.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aelia Publia, who lived 14 years. Aurelius Victor of Perburida, also known as Buricodavensis, and Aelia Dionysia, her parents, for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-13301430.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia Publia, a 14-year-old girl, dedicated by her parents, Aurelius Victor and Aelia Dionysia.

EDCS-13301431.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoniae Optatae quae Maura vixit nnos XXX Titus Aelius Glaphyrus coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-13301431.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Antonia Optata, who was called Maura, who lived 30 years. Titus Aelius Glaphyrus placed this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-13301431.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonia Optata (Maura), who lived 30 years, dedicated by her husband Titus Aelius Glaphyrus.

EDCS-13301432.text_edition: \n Vivi sibi Marcus Antonius Marci filius domo Crustumiae Arniae Aprio ex Thuscia et Tannonia Valentina parentes item Marco Antonio Valentino vixit annos X menses III et Antoniae Aprullae vixit annos VIIII menses VI filis piissimis obitis fecerunt\n
EDCS-13301432.translation: For themselves, Marcus Antonius, son of Marcus, from Crustumia, Arnia Aprio from Etruria, and Tannonia Valentina, parents; likewise for their most dutiful sons Marcus Antonius Valentinus, who lived 10 years and 3 months, and Antonia Aprulla, who lived 9 years and 6 months, having passed away, they made this.
EDCS-13301432.summary: A funerary monument made by parents Marcus Antonius and Arnia Aprio, and Tannonia Valentina for themselves and their deceased sons Marcus Antonius Valentinus and Antonia Aprulla.

EDCS-13301433.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Atronio Hermeti et Atroniae Tycheni coniugi eius Marcus Atronius Valens filius et heres parentibus bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-13301433.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Atroniu Hermetus and his wife Atronia Tyche. Marcus Atroniu Valens, son and heir, placed this for his deserving parents.
EDCS-13301433.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Marcus Atroniu Valens to his parents, Marcus Atroniu Hermetus and Atronia Tyche.

EDCS-13301435.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Fuscino Valeria Iulia coniux virginio pientissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-13301435.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Flavius Fuscinius. Valeria Iulia, his wife, placed this for her most dutiful and deserving husband.
EDCS-13301435.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Valeria Iulia to her husband Flavius Fuscinius.

EDCS-13301437.text_edition: \n Grania Marci liberta Fusca vixit nnos XXXV hic sita est Marcus Granius Marci libertus Abascantus coniugi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-13301437.translation: Grania Fusca, freedwoman of Marcus, lived 35 years. She is buried here. Marcus Granius Abascantus, freedman of Marcus, took care to have this made for his wife.
EDCS-13301437.summary: A funerary epitaph for Grania Fusca, a freedwoman, who lived 35 years, commissioned by her husband Marcus Granius Abascantus.

EDCS-13301441.text_edition: \n Memoriae Quinti Pulli Agathiae Pullia Agathe marito pientissimo cum Pullio Agathia et Pullio Agathodoro filiis et sibi vivis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-13301441.translation: For the memory of Quintus Pullius Agathia. Pullia Agathe, with her sons Pullius Agathodorus and Pullius Agathia, and for herself, took care to have this made while alive for her most dutiful husband.
EDCS-13301441.summary: A funerary monument prepared by Pullia Agathe for her husband Quintus Pullius Agathia and their sons, and for herself while alive.

EDCS-13301442.text_edition: \n ino veterano legionis I Italicae IMIACAM annorum LXVII Titii r et Heraclius erini et Claudius Surianus Surianae heredes IDE\n
EDCS-13301442.translation: [...]ino, a veteran of the First Italian Legion, 67 years old. Titii [son] and Heraclius, heirs of Erinius and Claudius Surianus, [for] Suriana. IDE [...]
EDCS-13301442.summary: A funerary epitaph for a veteran of the First Italian Legion, aged 67, with a partial record of his heirs.

EDCS-13301446.text_edition: \n us D veteranus legionis I Italicae heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-13301446.translation: He who was a veteran of the I Italica legion made it to be done.
EDCS-13301446.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument erected by a veteran of the I Italica legion.

EDCS-13301452.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aulus Iunius Tiberius Iulius Artemo Vinulus pecunia sua\n
EDCS-13301452.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aulus Junius Tiberius Julius Artemo Vinulus at his own expense.
EDCS-13301452.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, funded by Aulus Junius Tiberius Julius Artemo Vinulus.

EDCS-13301453.summary: This inscription records a monument made by Vitalis.

EDCS-13301476.summary: This inscription records the name and status of Aemilius Ianuarius, a soldier.

EDCS-13301482.summary: This inscription likely records a military unit or a grave marker for a soldier from the 70th century of Baebius.

EDCS-13301495.summary: This inscription records a soldier of the I legion, with a large portion missing.

EDCS-13301496.text_edition: \n ------ m]iles leg(ionis) I Italicae fil A\n
EDCS-13301496.translation: Soldier of the I Italica legion, son of A[...].
EDCS-13301496.summary: This inscription records a soldier of the I Italica legion, son of A[...], with much of the text missing.

EDCS-13301507.translation: Viebaia of the I Italica legion.
EDCS-13301507.summary: This inscription appears to record the name and legion of a woman named Viebaia, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-13301510.text_edition: \n mae et Aurelius us tribunus legionis supra scriptae matri ssimae\n
EDCS-13301510.translation: To his most excellent mother Aurelius [son] of [...], tribune of the aforementioned legion.
EDCS-13301510.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aurelius, a tribune of a legion, to his most excellent mother.

EDCS-13302617.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno Castus duumvirales sic pro se et suos sic inio consule votum posuit\n
EDCS-13302617.translation: To the Eternal God, Castus, who held duumviral office, made a vow for himself and his family in the consulship of [...].
EDCS-13302617.summary: This inscription records a vow made to the Eternal God by Castus, a former duumvir, for himself and his family.

EDCS-13302619.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Publius Valerius Macrinus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-13302619.translation: Publius Valerius Macrinus dedicated this to Liber Pater in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-13302619.summary: A private dedication to the god Liber Pater by Publius Valerius Macrinus.

EDCS-13302622.text_edition: \n LVIV ERAS Proculino exxarcho qui militavit Sacidavae vixit annos L menses III dies V Nene coniugi pientissimo posuit titulum p O\n
EDCS-13302622.translation: Nene placed this monument for her most devoted husband, Proculinus, exarch who served at Sacidava, who lived 50 years, 3 months, and 5 days.
EDCS-13302622.summary: A tombstone erected by Nene for her husband Proculinus, a former exarch, who died at Sacidava.

EDCS-13302643.translation: Dizzobeteles willingly, in fulfillment of a vow, [dedicated this] for his deserts.
EDCS-13302643.summary: A private dedication of uncertain type, likely to a deity, by Dizzobeteles.

EDCS-13302722.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-13302722.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XVII consul XIIII censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in alis tribus et cohortibus decem et septem quae appellantur II Pannoniorum III Augusta Thracum veterana Gallica I Flavia civium Romanorum I milliaria I Lucensium I Ascalonitanorum I Sebastena I Ituraeorum I Numidarum II Italica civium Romanorum II Thracum civium Romanorum II classica III Augusta Thracum III Thracum Syriaca IIII Bracaraugustanorum IIII Syriaca IIII Callaecorum Lucensium Augusta Pannoniorum Musulamiorum et sunt in Syria sub Publio Valerio Patruino qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant sic quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem VI Idus Novembres Marco Otacilio Catulo Sexto Iulio Sparso consulibus cohortis Musulamiorum cui praeest Marcus Caecilius September pediti Gorio Stibi filio Dacus sic descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio in latere sinistro tabulari publici Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XVII consul XIIII censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in alis tribus et cohortibus decem et septem quae appellantur II Pannoniorum III Augusta Thracum veterana Gallica I Flavia civium Romanorum I milliaria I Lucensium I Ascalonitanorum I Sebastena I Ituraeorum I Numidarum II Italica civium Romanorum II Thracum civium Romanorum II classica III Augusta Thracum III Thracum Syriaca IIII Bracaraugustanorum IIII Syriaca IIII Callaecorum Lucensium Augusta Panno[niorum ------\n
EDCS-13302722.translation: Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, acclaimed imperator for the 17th time, consul for the 14th time, perpetual censor, father of the fatherland, granted citizenship and the right to marry to the cavalrymen and infantrymen serving in three alae and seventeen cohorts, namely the II Pannoniorum, III Augusta Thracum, veterana Gallica I, Flavia civium Romanorum I, milliaria I, Lucensium I, Ascalonitanorum I, Sebastena I, Ituraeorum I, Numidarum II, Italica civium Romanorum II, Thracum civium Romanorum II, classica III, Augusta Thracum III, Thracum Syriaca IIII, Bracaraugustanorum IIII, Syriaca IIII, Callaecorum Lucensium Augusta Pannoniorum Musulamiorum, who had completed twenty-five years of service or more, and to their children and descendants, and to those who were unmarried at the time citizenship was granted, but who subsequently married, provided that each man took only one wife. This was decreed on the 10th day before the Kalends of November, when Marcus Otacilius Crassus and Sextus Julius Sparsus were consuls. This decree concerning the infantryman Gorius, son of Stibus, a Dacian, of the Musulamiorum cohort, commanded by Marcus Caecilius September, was written down and confirmed from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the left side of the public records office. Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, acclaimed imperator for the 17th time, consul for the 14th time, perpetual censor, father of the fatherland, [granted citizenship...] to the cavalrymen and infantrymen serving in three alae and seventeen cohorts, namely the II Pannoniorum, III Augusta Thracum, veterana Gallica I, Flavia civium Romanorum I, milliaria I, Lucensium I, Ascalonitanorum I, Sebastena I, Ituraeorum I, Numidarum II, Italica civium Romanorum II, Thracum civium Romanorum II, classica III, Augusta Thracum III, Thracum Syriaca IIII, Bracaraugustanorum IIII, Syriaca IIII, Callaecorum Lucensium Augusta Pannoniorum [---
EDCS-13302722.summary: This is a rescript issued by Emperor Domitian in 88 CE, granting Roman citizenship and connubium to veteran soldiers serving in specific auxiliary units in Syria, with the original decree inscribed on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-13600178.text_edition: \n Herculi sacrum pro salute Antonini Augusti Pii et Veri Caesaris veterani et cives Romani et consistentes Abrito ad canabas posuerunt\n
EDCS-13600178.translation: Veterans and Roman citizens and residents at Abrito, near the canabae, dedicated this sacred precinct to Hercules for the safety of Antoninus Augustus Pius and Verus Caesar.
EDCS-13600178.summary: A dedication to Hercules by veterans and residents at Abrito for the well-being of Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus.

EDCS-13600179.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Aurelius Victorinus Victoris filius princeps vici Tautiomosis libens votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-13600179.translation: Aurelius Victorinus, son of Victor, leader of the vicus Tautiomosis, willingly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, rightly so.
EDCS-13600179.summary: A private dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Victorinus, a local official, in the vicus Tautiomosis.

EDCS-13600180.text_edition: \n Licinius Invictus Augustus ob diem X suorum Officina Flavi Nicani M B N\n
EDCS-13600180.translation: Licinius Invictus Augustus, for the 10th day of his reign, [dedicated this from] the workshop of Flavius Nicanus.
EDCS-13600180.summary: A dedication by Licinius Invictus Augustus, likely celebrating his tenth anniversary, possibly from a workshop.

EDCS-13600274.text_edition: \n Aurelius Aelianus custos armorum legionis XVI Flaviae firmae militavit nnos XI vixit nnos XXX Iulius Mar miles gener posuit bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-13600274.translation: Aurelius Aelianus, armourer of the Legio XVI Flavia Firma, served for 11 years and lived for 30 years. Julius Mar, his son-in-law and soldier, erected this for him as he deserved well.
EDCS-13600274.summary: A tombstone erected by Julius Mar for his father-in-law, Aurelius Aelianus, an armourer in Legio XVI Flavia Firma.

EDCS-13600275.text_edition: \n ------ memoriae aet]ernae Aurelio Saturnino ex primo pilo ornato ornamentis vixit nnis LI et Flavia coniux eius eius ei vixit annis XXX ERAX AGLAIS eorum posuit sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-13600275.translation: [... to eternal memory] Aurelius Saturninus, from the rank of primus pilus, honored with decorations, lived for 51 years, and Flavia his wife, who lived with him for 30 years. Their children Agra and Aglais placed this. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-13600275.summary: A tombstone for Aurelius Saturninus, a retired centurion, and his wife Flavia, erected by their children.

EDCS-13600278.text_edition: \n Porobono Martinus con sic coniuge ex voto aram posuit\n
EDCS-13600278.translation: Porobono and Martinus, husband and wife, placed this altar in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-13600278.summary: A joint votive offering of an altar by a married couple, Porobono and Martinus.

EDCS-13600279.text_edition: \n Heroni Surgethiae idemque praehibenti Aurelius Maturus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae Antoninianae cum convibis sic suis celebravit IET O M votum libens posuit eodem cons(ule)\n
EDCS-13600279.translation: Aurelius Maturus, centurion of the XI Claudia Antoniniana legion, celebrated with his soldiers and benefactors as well, dedicating a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus with pleasure, during the same consulship.
EDCS-13600279.summary: This inscription records a soldier's vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely made in gratitude or fulfillment of a promise.

EDCS-13600280.text_edition: \n Dianae sanctae venatrici Gaius Firminius Lucanus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae Antoninianae domo Dacia\n
EDCS-13600280.translation: To Diana the holy huntress, Gaius Firminius Lucanus, centurion of the I Italica Antoniniana legion, from Dacia.
EDCS-13600280.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Diana, made by a centurion of the I Italica legion, originating from Dacia.

EDCS-13600292.text_edition: \n Numini perpetuo domini nostri invictissimi Imperatoris Caesaris [Cai Iuli Veri] [Maximini] Pii Felicis Augusti pontificis maximo sic tribunicia potestate patris patriae proconsulis numerus civium Romanorum Maxim[inia]nus devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-13600292.translation: To the everlasting divine power of our most invincible lord Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, the numerus civium Romanorum Maximinianus, devoted to his divine power and majesty.
EDCS-13600292.summary: This inscription records the devotion of a Roman citizen unit, the Maximinianus, to the divine power and majesty of Emperor Maximinus Thrax.

EDCS-13600293.text_edition: \n Pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Apollini sancto Aurelius Bonitus optio spei legionis I Italicae \n
EDCS-13600293.translation: For the health of our lords Augusti, Aurelius Bonitus, optio spei of the I Italica legion, to Apollo the holy.
EDCS-13600293.summary: This inscription is a prayer for the health of the emperors, dedicated to Apollo by a soldier of the I Italica legion.

EDCS-13600294.text_edition: \n Feliciter Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Valeriano Pio Felici invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli II proconsuli cohors III collecta devota numini maiestatique eius dedicante Aelio Maximo tribuno\n
EDCS-13600294.translation: Hail to the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, consul for the second time, proconsul. The III Cohort, devoted to his divine power and majesty, dedicates this under the command of Aelius Maximus, tribune.
EDCS-13600294.summary: This inscription records the dedication of the III Cohort to Emperor Valerian I, under the command of Tribune Aelius Maximus.

EDCS-13600295.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae Aurelius Titus vexillarius equitum legionis I Italicae Gordianae Augustae in regione Montanensium cum suis ex voto posuit votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13600295.translation: To Diana Regina, Aurelius Titus, vexillarius (standard-bearer) of the cavalry of the I Italica Gordiana Augusta legion, in the region of the Montanenses, with his men, has erected this from a vow and willingly fulfilled his vow with merit.
EDCS-13600295.summary: This inscription records a vexillarius of the I Italica legion dedicating a monument to Diana Regina for his unit and himself, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-13700322.text_edition: \n Aurelius Iovinus miles legionis XI Claudiae voto posuit\n
EDCS-13700322.translation: Aurelius Iovinus, a soldier of the XI Claudia legion, erected this from a vow.
EDCS-13700322.summary: This inscription records a soldier of the XI Claudia legion dedicating a monument due to a vow.

EDCS-13700325.translation: Valens erected this from a vow.
EDCS-13700325.summary: This inscription records a vow made and fulfilled by an individual named Valens.

EDCS-13800067.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aurelius Sirus militavit in legione prima Italica annos VIIII INRELII Aurelia EFIEC ucxor sic de suo posuit\n
EDCS-13800067.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Aurelius Sirus served in the First Italica legion for nine years. Aurelia EFIEC, his wife, erected this from her own resources.
EDCS-13800067.summary: This tombstone records the service of Publius Aurelius Sirus in the I Italica legion and is dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-13800068.text_edition: \n Dianae Plestrensi Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto legatus Augusti pro praetore HS VII Prisco consulibus\n
EDCS-13800068.translation: To Diana Plestrensis, from Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus. The legate of Augustus pro praetore, with Priscus as consul, [spent] 7,000 sesterces.
EDCS-13800068.summary: This inscription records an expenditure of 7,000 sesterces by a legate, likely for a public work or religious purpose, honoring Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-13800069.text_edition: \n Herculi sacrum Apollonius PA IPSVM fecit Caius Rabirius -- Alexander -- votum solvit l(ibens)? m(erito)\n
EDCS-13800069.translation: Sacred to Hercules. Apollonius, son of Pa[--], made this. Caius Rabirius Alexander fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-13800069.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Hercules, set up by Apollonius and Caius Rabirius Alexander, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15000079.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cives Romani et Lai consistentes vico Secundini posuerunt pro salutem sic Imperatorum dominorum nnostrorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini curam agentibus magistris Artema Dioscoridentis sic et Iusstino sic Valeri Impperatoribus Severo III et Antonino consulibus\n
EDCS-15000079.translation: The Roman citizens and Latins living in the street of Secundinus have erected this for the safety of our Emperors, our lords Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, with Artema Dioscoridentis and Iustinus acting as magistrates, during the consulship of Emperors Severus III and Antoninus.
EDCS-15000079.summary: This is a public dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Roman and Latin citizens for the safety of Emperors Septimius Severus and Antoninus (likely Caracalla), dating to around 200 CE.

EDCS-15000080.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cives Romani et Lai consistentes vico Secundini posuerunt pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti curam agentibus maggistris Flavio Valente et Valerio Gogenis Imperatore domino nostro [Ant][onino] Augusto III et Valerio Comazonte consulibus\n
EDCS-15000080.translation: The Roman citizens and Latins living in the street of Secundinus have erected this for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, with Flavius Valens and Valerius Gogenis acting as magistrates, during the consulship of our lord Emperor Antoninus Augustus III and Valerius Comazon.
EDCS-15000080.summary: This is a public dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Roman and Latin citizens for the safety of Emperor Antoninus (likely Caracalla), dating to around 215 CE.

EDCS-15500105.summary: This very brief inscription, likely a funerary marker, simply repeats the name Varinia, possibly indicating a woman or a tomb belonging to her, and is undatable without further context.

EDCS-15600001.text_edition: \n Iulius Saturio Iuli libertus domo Haeduus missicius alae Capitoni vixit annos LXXX meruit annos XXXV hic situs est Agatho libertus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-15600001.translation: Iulius Saturio, freedman of Iulius, from the territory of the Haedui, a retired soldier of the ala Capitoni, lived 80 years, served 35 years, lies here. Agatho, his freedman, saw to the erection of this monument.
EDCS-15600001.summary: This tombstone records the life of Iulius Saturio, a retired cavalryman of the ala Capitoni, who died at 80 and served for 35 years, with his freedman Agatho commissioning the monument, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15700070.text_edition: \n Cum primum veni Montanis et numina vidi deabus votum vovi ut potui posui Mallia Aemiliana domo Roma frumento publico cum filio suo restituit\n
EDCS-15700070.translation: As soon as I came to the Montani and saw the divine powers of the goddesses, I made the vow that I could, I set up. Mallia Aemiliana, from Rome, with public grain, restored this with her son.
EDCS-15700070.summary: This inscription records Mallia Aemiliana making a vow and dedication to goddesses of the Montani region, possibly related to a public building restoration, and is undatable.

EDCS-15700156.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Coto veterano alae II Arabacorum sic vixit annis XXXXVII Aureli Helpideforus sic et Papias et Iulia Iuliana coniunx patro[no ------\n
EDCS-15700156.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelius Cotus, veteran of the II ala of Arabs, lived 47 years. Aurelius Helpideforus and Papias and his wife Iulia Iuliana, patron [---]
EDCS-15700156.summary: This funerary inscription is for Aurelius Cotus, a veteran of the II ala of Arabs, who lived to 47, and was set up by Aurelius Helpideforus, Papias, and his wife Iulia Iuliana, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15700195.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordiani Augusti Castor et Licinius sacerdotes bis sportulas dederunt\n
EDCS-15700195.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the safety of Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Augustus. Castor and Licinius, priests, gave donations twice.
EDCS-15700195.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus for the safety of Emperor Gordian III, made by the priests Castor and Licinius, likely dating to Gordian's reign (238-244 CE).

EDCS-15800373.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Germanus vixit annos LXXVIII Caius Valerius Valens beneficiarius legati legionis XI Claudiae filius patri ]\n
EDCS-15800373.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Valerius Germanus lived 78 years. Caius Valerius Valens, a beneficiarius of the legate of the XI Claudia legion, son, to his father]
EDCS-15800373.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Caius Valerius Germanus, who lived to 78, and was set up by his son Caius Valerius Valens, a staff officer (beneficiarius) of the XI Claudia legion, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-15800375.text_edition: \n legionis I Italicae sancto Heroni votum posuit\n
EDCS-15800375.translation: To the holy Heron, from the I Italica legion, [he] set up a vow.
EDCS-15800375.summary: This inscription records a vow dedicated to the deity Heron by a soldier from the I Italica legion, undatable without further context.

EDCS-15800376.text_edition: \n Deo Apollini Antonius Valentinus IIvir coloniae Napocae\n
EDCS-15800376.translation: To the god Apollo, Antonius Valentinus, a duovir of the colony of Napoca.
EDCS-15800376.summary: This is a dedication to the god Apollo by Antonius Valentinus, an official of the colony of Napoca, likely a votive inscription.

EDCS-15800377.text_edition: \n Heroni Aurelius Mucapaibes miles ex votum sic libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-15800377.translation: To Heron, Aurelius Mucapaibes, a soldier, gladly and deservedly set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15800377.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Mucapaibes, a soldier, dedicated to Heron.

EDCS-15800378.translation: [---] Montanus, a decurion of the Montanenses, set this up.
EDCS-15800378.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the erection of a monument by Montanus, a decurion of the Montanenses.

EDCS-15800379.text_edition: \n Sacrum cohors I Thracum Syriaca cui praeest Caius Navius Quadratus pra<e=S>fectus \n
EDCS-15800379.translation: Sacred to the 1st Thracian Syrian Cohort, commanded by Caius Navius Quadratus, prefect.
EDCS-15800379.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a military unit, the 1st Thracian Syrian Cohort, and names its commander, Caius Navius Quadratus.

EDCS-15800380.text_edition: \n Sacrum Aurelius Avidius Felix veteranus et Vibia Helpis Dianae votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-15800380.translation: Aurelius Avidius Felix, a veteran, and Vibia Helpis gladly and deservedly paid their vow to Diana.
EDCS-15800380.summary: This is a votive inscription to Diana by a veteran, Aurelius Avidius Felix, and Vibia Helpis.

EDCS-15800383.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salute Imperatoris et vici Celeris curam agente Ulpium sic Ulpianum sic magistro Imperatore Vero Caesare et Quintillo consulibus de suo posuit\n
EDCS-15800383.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the safety of the Emperor and the district of Celer, with Ulpius Ulpianus undertaking the care, under the magistracy of the master Emperor Verus Caesar and Quintillus as consuls, he set this up at his own expense.
EDCS-15800383.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the Emperor's safety, set up by an official during the consulship of Verus Caesar and Quintillus.

EDCS-15900078.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Octavius Firmi filius Palatina Aper Nicomedia medicus vixit annis plus minus XLIX et Lisame Polla vixit annis plus minus LXV quae Octavio vivo natis vivis legem naturae absolvit Marcus Octavius Palatina Domitius Nicomedia veteranus legionis V Macedonicae matri suae et fratri pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-15900078.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Octavius Aper, son of Firmius, of the Palatine tribe, a physician from Nicomedia, lived more or less 49 years, and Lisame Polla lived more or less 65 years, who completed the law of nature while Octavius was alive and their children were alive. Marcus Octavius Palatina Domitius, a veteran of the 5th Macedonian Legion, set this up for his most dutiful mother and brother.
EDCS-15900078.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a veteran for his mother and brother, recording their ages and professions, dating to the period of Emperor Verus.

EDCS-15900128.translation: A vow to the crossroads by Aurelius Decimus.
EDCS-15900128.summary: This is a brief votive inscription to the crossroads by Aurelius Decimus.

EDCS-16000031.text_edition: \n Invicto Mithrae Quintus Samicius Serenus architectus salariarius legionis XI Claudiae posuit\n
EDCS-16000031.translation: Quintus Samicius Serenus, an architect-salaried employee of the 11th Claudian Legion, set this up to the unconquered Mithras.
EDCS-16000031.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by Quintus Samicius Serenus, an architect serving in the 11th Claudian Legion.

EDCS-16000032.text_edition: \n ES tradidimus genitori Valentio |(centurioni) legionis XI Claudiae cum Alexandram sic coniugem sic suam sic sephulcrum sic titulo ornaverunt suovis sic T\n
EDCS-16000032.translation: We have handed over to our father Valentio, a centurion of the 11th Claudian Legion, with Alexandra his wife, and to ourselves they adorned the tomb with this inscription at their own expense.
EDCS-16000032.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the dedication of a tomb by a soldier of the 11th Claudian Legion for himself, his wife, and his father.

EDCS-16000035.summary: This inscription records the name of the First Italian Legion, likely a military standard or marker.

EDCS-16000323.summary: This is a dedication to Heron, the Holy, likely on a small altar or monument.

EDCS-16000324.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Heron, the Holy, with an incomplete recipient name.

EDCS-16000325.translation: Valerius placed this altar to Eron, the Holy, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-16000325.summary: This is a votive altar dedicated to Eron, the Holy, by Valerius.

EDCS-16000326.text_edition: \n Deo Sancto Herrone Aurelius Valens praefectus equitum scutariorum pro salute sua et vexillationis Capidabensium sic aram posuit\n
EDCS-16000326.translation: Aurelius Valens, prefect of the scutariorum cavalry, placed this altar for his own health and that of the detachment from Capidava.
EDCS-16000326.summary: This is a votive altar dedicated by Aurelius Valens, a cavalry prefect, for his own health and that of his detachment.

EDCS-16100196.text_edition: \n ienus colonia Agrippinensium VE miles legionis VIII Augustae centuria Quinti Vetti annorum XLIIX stipendiorum XXVI hic situs est heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-16100196.translation: Here lies Venus, soldier of the VIII Augusta legion, of Quintus Vettius's century, 48 years old with 26 years of service; his heir had this made.
EDCS-16100196.summary: This is a tombstone for Venus, a soldier of the VIII Augusta legion, who died at age 48 with 26 years of service.

EDCS-16100198.text_edition: \n Caius Aurelius filius Galeria Vegetus Clunia miles legionis I Italicae F R stipendiorum XXIII nnorum XL militavit in cohorte V Asturum inde translatus in legione I Italica hic situs est ex testamento\n
EDCS-16100198.translation: Gaius Aurelius Vegetus, son of Galeria and Aurelius, of the tribe Galeria, a soldier of the I Italica legion, F R, 23 years of service, 40 years old, served in the 5th cohort of the Astures, then was transferred to the I Italica legion; he lies here, as stipulated in his will.
EDCS-16100198.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Aurelius Vegetus, a soldier of the I Italica legion, who died at age 40 with 23 years of service.

EDCS-16100199.text_edition: \n Valeria Luci liberta Elphis sic vixit nnos XXX hic sita est Caius Annius Fuscus cornicen contubernali suae SV\n
EDCS-16100199.translation: Elphis, freedwoman of Lucia, lived 30 years and lies here. Gaius Annius Fuscus, a cornicen, had this made for his beloved tent-mate.
EDCS-16100199.summary: This is a tombstone for Elphis, a freedwoman, set up by her tent-mate Gaius Annius Fuscus.

EDCS-16100200.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Accepto equiti Romano Titus Flavius Carantinus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae pater et Catonia Ingenua mater ponendum curaverunt vixit annum I menses II dies XIII titulum merenti curaverunt\n
EDCS-16100200.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Flavius Carantinus, centurion of the I Italica legion, father, and Catonia Ingenua, mother, had this made for Titus Flavius Acceptus, a Roman cavalryman. He lived 1 year, 2 months, and 13 days. They had this monument made for him, who deserved it.
EDCS-16100200.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Flavius Acceptus, a Roman cavalryman who died in infancy, set up by his parents.

EDCS-16100201.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atiliae Cai filiae Chrysidi vixit nnos XXXX hic sita est Caius Valerius Cai filius Festus coniugi piissimae et \n
EDCS-16100201.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Atilia Chrysida, daughter of Caius. She lived 40 years and lies here. Gaius Valerius Festus, son of Caius, for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-16100201.summary: This is a tombstone for Atilia Chrysida, set up by her husband Gaius Valerius Festus.

EDCS-16100202.translation: To the father Titinius, Titinius [dedicated this].
EDCS-16100202.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Titinius to his father, also named Titinius, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-16100203.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus us Marci filius Marus Antoniusr patri FIORAE Antoniae sorori AFIRO Antonius \n
EDCS-16100203.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marus, son of Marcus, to his father Antonius; to his sister Aphira, Antonius.
EDCS-16100203.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Antonius for his father Marus and his sister Aphira, dedicated to the spirits of the departed.

EDCS-16100275.text_edition: \n Silvano et Silvestribus Ulpius Eptezenus princeps duplicarius legionis I Italicae voto posuit\n
EDCS-16100275.translation: To Silvanus and the Silvan deities, Ulpius Eptezenus, princeps duplicarius of the First Italian Legion, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-16100275.summary: This is a votive offering to Silvanus and woodland spirits by Ulpius Eptezenus, a centurion, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-16200796.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto Aurelius Disdosi miles votum posuit\n
EDCS-16200796.translation: Aurelius Disdosi, a soldier, set this up as a vow to Hercules Invictus.
EDCS-16200796.summary: This is a votive inscription to Hercules Invictus by a soldier named Aurelius Disdosi, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16200798.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Latinus ex signifero veteranus legionis V Macedonicae vixit annis LX Aufidia Avita coniux eius heres pientissima bene merenti posuit de suo\n
EDCS-16200798.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulpius Latinus, a veteran ensign of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, lived for 60 years. His most devoted wife Aufidia Avita, his heir, set this up for the well-deserving.
EDCS-16200798.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ulpius Latinus, a veteran soldier, set up by his wife and heir, Aufidia Avita.

EDCS-16200800.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cives Romani et Lai consistentes regione Sitri sic vico Secundini posuerunt pro salutem sic dominorum nostrorum Imperatorum Luci Septumi sic Severi Pertenacis sic et Imperatoris Marci Aureli [Antonini ------\n
EDCS-16200800.translation: The Roman citizens and Lai living in the region of Sitri, in the street of Secundinus, set this up for the health of our lords the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax and also Emperor Marcus Aurelius [Antoninus...].
EDCS-16200800.summary: This is a dedication by Roman citizens and locals for the health of Emperors Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16200801.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae cives Romani et Lai consistentes vico Secundini posuerunt pro salute Imperatoris Marci Antoni Gordianus sic cura agentibus maggistris Bonoso Bonunis et Iusto Iustini Pio et Proculo consulibus\n
EDCS-16200801.translation: The Roman citizens and Lai living in the street of Secundinus set this up for the health of Emperor Marcus Antoninus Gordianus, under the supervision of the magistrates Bonosus son of Bonunis and Justus son of Iustinus, and Pius and Proculus as consuls.
EDCS-16200801.summary: This is a dedication by Roman citizens and locals for the health of Emperor Gordian III, referencing specific magistrates and consuls, dated to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-16200803.text_edition: \n Nymphis et Silvano sacrum pro salutem sic Imperatoris Titi Aeli Antonini Hadr<i=E>ani Augusti et Aureli Veri Caesaris cura agentibus Saturnino et Bizienem sic magistris et Terentio Materno quaestorem sic Idibus Iuniis Quinto Statio Maximo et Avito consulibus \n
EDCS-16200803.translation: Sacred to the Nymphs and Silvanus for the health of Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Hadrianus Augustus and Aurelius Verus Caesar, under the supervision of magistrates Saturninus and Bizienus, and Terentius Maternus as quaestor, in the Ides of June, with Quintus Stasius Maximus and Avitus as consuls.
EDCS-16200803.summary: This is a dedication for the health of Emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius, mentioning magistrates and consuls, dated to 162 AD.

EDCS-16200894.text_edition: \n Deo Maguti pro salute fratrorum sic Iulius Herculanus aram ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-16200894.translation: To the god Magutus, for the health of his brothers, Iulius Herculanus set up this altar in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-16200894.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Magutus by Iulius Herculanus for the welfare of his brothers, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16200967.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Maximus eques cohortis II Lucensium singularis vixit a[nnos ------\n
EDCS-16200967.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Iulius Maximus, an equestrian soldier of the Cohors II Lucensium, a singularis, lived for [years...].
EDCS-16200967.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Maximus, a soldier in the 2nd Cohort of Lucensium, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16200968.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo veterani legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis missi IIII consulatuum qui militare coeperunt Commodo et Pompeiano et Lucio Aelio II consulibus et Nigro et Camarino Imperatore Antonino II missi ab Marco Aurelio Antonino et Lucio Aurelio Vero Augustis sub Servilio Fabiano legato Augustorum pro praetore et Cornelio Plotiano legato\n
EDCS-16200968.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the veterans of the legion Legio XI Claudia Pia Fidelis, sent from four consulships, who began military service under Commodus and Pompeianus and Lucius Aelius as consuls, and under Niger and Camarinus, with Antoninus II as emperor; sent by Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augusti, under Servilius Fabianus, imperial legate with praetorian power, and Cornelius Plotianus, legate.
EDCS-16200968.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by veterans of Legio XI Claudia, sent from four consulships, and likely dates to the reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus.

EDCS-16200969.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et municipii Aureli Durostori Caius Terentius Herodianus et Lucius Numerius Ponticus IIviri iure dicundo Titus Flavius Papirianus et Claudius Saturninus aediles et Quintus Visellius Lollianus Titus Lucretius Felix quaestores nostri\n
EDCS-16200969.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and Juno Regina, for the health of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and of the municipality of Aurelius Durostorum, Caius Terentius Herodianus and Lucius Numerius Ponticus, duovirs with judicial power, Titus Flavius Papirianus and Claudius Saturninus, aediles, and Quintus Visellius Lollianus and Titus Lucretius Felix, our quaestors.
EDCS-16200969.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the health of the emperor and the municipality of Aurelius Durostorum, made by the leading magistrates of the town, likely during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-16201045.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Antonini Hadriani Augusti Pii et Marci Aureli Veri Caesaris veterani et cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Quintionis curam agentibus magistris Claudio Gaius sic et Durisse Bithi Idibus Iunis Orfito et Prisco consulibus et quaestore Servilio Primigenio\n
EDCS-16201045.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Antoninus Hadrianus Augustus Pius and of Caesar Marcus Aurelius Verus, the veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi living in the Quintionis district, with Claudius Gaius and Durisse Bithi acting as magistrates and Servilius Primigenius as quaestor, on the Ides of June, during the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus.
EDCS-16201045.summary: This dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus is for the health of emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Verus, made by veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi in the Quintionis district, and is dated to the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus.

EDCS-16201046.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris veterani et cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Quintionis curam agentibus magistris Tiberio Firmo et Valerio Cutiunis et quaestore Flavio Secundo Idibus Iunis Apro II et Pollione II consulibus\n
EDCS-16201046.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperor, the veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi living in the Quintionis district, with Tiberius Firmus and Valerius Cutiunis acting as magistrates and Flavius Secundus as quaestor, on the Ides of June, during the consulship of Aper II and Pollio II.
EDCS-16201046.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the emperor's health, made by veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi in the Quintionis district, and is dated to the second consulship of Aper and Pollio.

EDCS-16201047.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris veterani et cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Quintionis curam agentibus magistris Aelio Bellico et Mucatralo Doli et quaestore Dotu Zinebti Idibus Iunis Pisone et Iuliano consulibus\n
EDCS-16201047.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperor, the veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi living in the Quintionis district, with Aelius Bellicus and Mucatralus Doli acting as magistrates and Dotus Zinebti as quaestor, on the Ides of June, during the consulship of Piso and Iulianus.
EDCS-16201047.summary: This dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the emperor's health was made by veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi in the Quintionis district, and is dated to the consulship of Piso and Iulianus.

EDCS-16201048.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Augusti veterani et cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Quintionis curam agentibus magistris Iulio Geminio et Genicio Brini et quaestore Cocceio Firmo Idibus Iunis Prisco et Apollonare sic consulibus\n
EDCS-16201048.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperor Augustus, the veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi living in the Quintionis district, with Iulius Geminus and Genicius Brini acting as magistrates and Cocceius Firmus as quaestor, on the Ides of June, during the consulship of Priscus and Apollonaris.
EDCS-16201048.summary: This inscription dedicates a sanctuary to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the emperor's health, made by veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi in the Quintionis district, and is dated to the consulship of Priscus and Apollonaris.

EDCS-16201636.text_edition: \n Marcus Vettius Felix ex decurione alae vixit nnos LX et Aureliae Faustinae coniugi eius Ulpius Felix strator consularis legionis XI Claudiae filius patri et matri bene merentibus post obitum eorum posuit sit tibi sic terra leves sic ave viator et vale\n
EDCS-16201636.translation: Marcus Vettius Felix, formerly a decurion of an ala, lived for 60 years. Ulpius Felix, strator of a consular legion (Legio XI Claudia), set this up for his father and mother, Aurelia Faustina his wife, who deserved well of them, after their death: may the earth lie lightly upon you, hail traveler, and farewell.
EDCS-16201636.summary: This tombstone records the death of Marcus Vettius Felix, a former decurion, and Aurelia Faustina, wife of Ulpius Felix, a strator of Legio XI Claudia, who erected the monument for his parents.

EDCS-16201637.text_edition: \n vixit annos XXX Titinius Severinus sescuplicarius sic ales sic II Arabacorum et Titinius Ia<n=M>uaris filii tituli sic memoriae causae posuerunt ave viator et vale \n
EDCS-16201637.translation: Titinus Severinus, a sescuplicarius of the ala II Arabacorum, lived for 30 years, and his son Titinius Ianuaris set this up for the sake of memory. Hail traveler, and farewell.
EDCS-16201637.summary: This tombstone records the death of Titinus Severinus, a sescuplicarius in the ala II Arabacorum, and was erected by his son Titinius Ianuaris.

EDCS-16201638.text_edition: \n C circitor de vixillatione sic XIII Catafractariorum quae est Trimammio vixit annis XXXIII militavit XIIV- \n
EDCS-16201638.translation: C., a circitor from the detachment of 13 Cataphractarii, which is at Trimammio, lived for 33 years and served for 14 years.
EDCS-16201638.summary: This inscription records the details of a soldier, C., a circitor from a detachment of cataphractarii, noting his age at death and length of service.

EDCS-16201640.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Antonini Hadriani Augusti Pii et Aureli Veri Caesaris veterani et cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Quintionis auditorio restituto curam agentibus Sulpicio Narcisso et Derzeno Aulupori magistratis sic et Cocceio Phoebo quaestore sigillum posuerunt\n
EDCS-16201640.translation: To Hercules the Unconquered, for the health of the Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Hadrianus Augustus Pius and of Caesar Aurelius Verus, the veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi living in the Quintionis district, have restored the auditorium, with Sulpicius Narcissus and Derzenus Auluporus acting as magistrates and Cocceius Phoebus as quaestor, they placed this dedication.
EDCS-16201640.summary: This inscription records the restoration of an auditorium to Hercules Invictus for the health of emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Verus, made by veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi in the Quintionis district.

EDCS-16201786.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepote divi Nervae pronepote Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patre patriae pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VII consule IIII imperatore II a LX Pristis per cohortem II Mattiacorum Tiberius Claudius Saturninus legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
