EDCS-16201786.translation: Under the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time, emperor for the 2nd time, sixty years old, from Pristis, Tiberius Claudius Saturninus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, through the 2nd Cohort of Mattiacans.
EDCS-16201786.summary: This inscription records a building or dedication made by Tiberius Claudius Saturninus, governor of a province, on behalf of the 2nd Cohort of Mattiacans, under Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-16300572.text_edition: \n Heroni Sancto Antonius Mercurius eques numeri C P ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-16300572.translation: Antonius Mercurius, an equestrian soldier of the auxiliary cohort CP, set this up in fulfillment of a vow to Heron the Holy.
EDCS-16300572.summary: This is a votive inscription by Antonius Mercurius, a cavalryman in an auxiliary unit, dedicating something to the deity Heron.

EDCS-16400539.text_edition: \n Aurelius Surius miles cohortis Pimasensium Aurelianae Heroni ex voto posit\n
EDCS-16400539.translation: Aurelius Surius, a soldier of the 1st Cohort of Pimasenoi of Aureliana, set this up for Heron in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-16400539.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Surius, a soldier in an auxiliary cohort, dedicated to Heron.

EDCS-16600446.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus P tribunicia potestate XII consul III proconsul pater patriae CIASMIAND Dacorum constituit VR \n
EDCS-16600446.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, father of the fatherland, established the CIASMIAND of the Dacians.
EDCS-16600446.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a Dacian administrative district or similar entity by Emperor Severus Alexander, dated to his 12th tribunician power and 3rd consulship (around 231-232 CE).

EDCS-16800337.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Herculi Invicto Cereri Libero Patri pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii et Aureli Caesaris liberorumque eorum Tito Vitrasio Pollioni legato Augusti pro praetore Marcus Stabius Marci filius Fabia Colonus domo Luca tribunus militum legionis XI Claudiae dono dedit\n
EDCS-16800337.translation: Marcus Stabius Marci filius Colonus, from Lucca, a military tribune of the 11th Claudian Legion, dedicated this gift to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Invincible Hercules, Ceres, and Liber Pater, for the safety of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, and Caesar Aurelius, and their children, through Titus Vitrasius Pollio, imperial legate with praetorian authority.
EDCS-16800337.summary: This is a dedication to several gods for the safety of the imperial family, made by Marcus Stabius Colonus, a military tribune, through the governor Titus Vitrasius Pollio, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-16800339.text_edition: \n Neptuno Augusto sacrum vexillationes legionis I Italicae Moesiacae et V Macedonicae Tropaeo Traiani sub curam sic Eptidi Modesti |(centurionis) legionis V Macedonicae et Vaaleri sic Clementis |(centurionis) legionis I Italicae votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-16800339.translation: Detachment units of the I Italica Moesiacae Legion and the V Macedonica Legion, under the command of Eptidius Modestus (centurion) of the V Macedonica Legion and Valerius Clemens (centurion) of the I Italica Legion, having completed twenty-five years of service and more, fulfilled their vow to Neptune Augustus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-16800339.summary: This inscription records detachments from two legions fulfilling a vow to Neptune Augustus after long service, under the supervision of two centurions, likely in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-24400394.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-24400394.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XI consul III equitibus et peditibus exercitus pii fidelis qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XV quae appellantur Afrorum veterana et I Thracum et Gallorum et I Thracum Classiana civium Romanorum torquata victrix et I Noricorum civium Romanorum et Sulpicia civium Romanorum et I Flavia Hispanorum et I Latobicorum et Varcianorum et I Pannoniorum et Dalmatarum et I Raetorum civium Romanorum et I Classica et I Lucensium et II Varcianorum et II civium Romanorum et II Hispanorum et II Asturum et III Breucorum et IIII Thracum et VI Brittonum et VI Breucorum et VI Raetorum et sunt in Germania inferiore sub Lucio Coelio Rufo quinis et vicenis item classicis senis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XIII Kalendas Septembres Quinto Tineio Rufo Marco Licinio Celere Nepote consulibus cohortis IIII Thracum piae fidelis cui praeest Lucius Porcius Crescens ex equite sae Natusis filio Daco descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XI consul III equitibus et peditibus exercitus pii fidelis qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XV quae appellantur Afrorum veterana et I Thracum et Gallorum et I Thracum Classiana civium Romanorum torquata victrix et I Noricorum civium Romanorum et Sulpicia civium Romanorum et I Flavia Hispanorum et I Latobicorum et Varcianorum et I nnoniorum et Dalmatarum et I Raetorum civium Romanorum et I Classica et I Lucensium et II Varcianorum et II civium Romanorum et II Hispanorum et II Asturum et III Breucorum et IIII Thracum et VI Brittonum et VI Breucorum et VI Raetorum et sunt in Germania inferiore sub Lucio Coelio Rufo et classicis senis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscri<p=B>(ta) sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut \n
EDCS-24400394.translation: Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, to the cavalry and infantry of the pious and faithful army serving in Lower Germany under Lucius Coelius Rufus, has granted citizenship and the right of marriage to those who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and similarly to career soldiers with twenty-six or more years of service, who have been honorably discharged, and whose names are listed below; and to their children and descendants; with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, provided each man took only one wife. This was done on the 13th day before the Kalends of September [August 20th], when Quintus Tineius Rufus and Marcus Licinius Celer Nepos were consuls. This grant of citizenship and marriage rights is confirmed and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed to the wall in Rome behind the Temple of the deified Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. This grant applies to the 4th Cohort of Thracians, pious and faithful, commanded by Lucius Porcius Crescens, son of Saenatus, a Dacian, [and to the] cavalry and infantry of the pious and faithful army serving in Lower Germany under Lucius Coelius Rufus, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and similarly career soldiers with twenty-six or more years of service, who have been honorably discharged, and whose names are listed below.
EDCS-24400394.summary: This is an imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Hadrian to auxiliary soldiers and career soldiers serving in Lower Germany, issued during his 11th tribunician power and 3rd consulship (around 128-129 CE), as recorded on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-24400846.text_edition: \n Numini Sarapi Quintus Cassicius lianus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-24400846.translation: Quintus Cassicius Ialianus set this up in fulfillment of a vow to the divine Sarapis.
EDCS-24400846.summary: This is a votive inscription by Quintus Cassicius Ialianus dedicated to the god Sarapis.

EDCS-24600753.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani nepoti divi Nervae pronepoti Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli IIII patri patriae cohors II Mattiacorum\n
EDCS-24600753.translation: The 2nd Cohort of Mattiacorum to Emperor Caesar Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-24600753.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the 2nd Cohort of Mattiacorum to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from the period of his 4th consulship (140-144 CE).

EDCS-26600736.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Disius G Augustalis coloniae Ulpiae Oi<s>ci sic botun sic posuit\n
EDCS-26600736.translation: Marcus Disius G. Augustalis of the colony of Colonia Ulpia Oescus set this up for Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Hercules.
EDCS-26600736.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Hercules made by Marcus Disius Augustalis from the colony of Oescus, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27100021.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris divi Traiani Parthici filio sic divi Nervae nepotis Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae pontificis maximo sic tribuniciae potestatis XX consulis III Antius Rufinus inter Moesos et Thraces fines posuit\n
EDCS-27100021.translation: By authority of the Emperor, Caesar, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, father of the fatherland, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time, Antius Rufinus established this boundary between the Moesians and the Thracians.
EDCS-27100021.summary: This is a boundary stone set up by Antius Rufinus, likely during the reign of Hadrian, to mark the border between Moesia and Thrace.

EDCS-27100022.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Caius Iulius Carianus primus pilus legionis I Italicae ex equite Romano\n
EDCS-27100022.translation: To Liber Pater, Gaius Julius Carianus, first centurion of the I Italica legion, formerly a Roman knight.
EDCS-27100022.summary: This dedication is made to the god Liber Pater by Gaius Julius Carianus, a former Roman knight and first centurion of the I Italica legion.

EDCS-27100023.text_edition: \n Numini Augustorum et Genio publici portorii Hermes Iuliorum Ianuari Capitonis Epaphroditi conductorum publici portorii Illyrici et ripae Thraciae servus vilicus posuit\n
EDCS-27100023.translation: To the divine powers of the Augusti and the public genius of the tax collectors, Hermes, slave and steward of the public tax collectors of Illyricum and the Thracian shore, belonging to the Iulii, erected this.
EDCS-27100023.summary: This inscription records the erection of something by Hermes, a slave and steward for tax collectors operating in Illyricum and Thrace, dedicated to the divine Augusti and the public genius.

EDCS-27100024.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et numini Augusti nostri et publici portorii procuratore Avianio Bellico Maceio Caesaris nostri servus vilicus vectigalis Illyrici Idibus Septembribus Mamertino et Rufo consulibus\n
EDCS-27100024.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, and to the divine spirit of our Emperor, and to the public genius of the tax collectors, by Avianius Bellicus Maceius, procurator of our Emperor, a slave and steward of the Illyrian revenue, erected this on the Ides of September during the consulship of Mamertinus and Rufus.
EDCS-27100024.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, the Emperor's divine spirit, and the public genius of the tax collectors, set up by Avianius Bellicus Maceius, a procurator's steward, during the consulship of Mamertinus and Rufus.

EDCS-27100025.text_edition: \n Tito Iulio Capitoni conductori publici portorii Illyrici et Ripae Thraciae omnibus honoribus ab ordine coloniae Flaviae Sirmiatium honorato et sententiae dicundae item sacerdotalibus ab ordine coloniae Ulpiae Oescensium et statuam aere collato decretis iam pridem ab eodem ordine ornamentis IIviralibus item decurionalibus ornamentis honorato ab ordinibus coloniarum Ulpiae Poetovionensis ex Pannonia superiore Ulpiae Ratiariae ex Moesia superiore Traianae Sarmizegethusensium ex Dacia superiore item IIviralibus ab ordine municipii Romulensium buleutae civitatis Ponticae Tomitanorum patrono Augustalium coloniae Ulpiae Oescensium ordo coloniae Ulpiae Oescensium statuam aere collato cum ornamentis sacerdotalibus ex decreto et ornamentis IIviralibus iam pridem honorato ob eius erga se merita honore contentus impendium remisit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27100025.translation: To Titus Julius Capitoni, contractor for the public taxes of Illyricum and the Thracian Shore, who has been honored with all distinctions by the order of the colony Flavia Sirmiatium and the right to speak in council, and priesthoods by the order of the colony Ulpia Oescensium, and a bronze statue with pre-existing honors including IIviral and decurional distinctions, and also honored by the orders of the colonies Ulpia Poetovionensis from Pannonia Superior, Ulpia Ratiaria from Moesia Superior, Traiana Sarmizegethusium from Dacia Superior, and with IIviral honors by the order of the municipium Romulensium, and a councilor of the Pontic city of Tomis, patron of the Augustales of the colony Ulpia Oescensium, the order of the colony Ulpia Oescensium, content with the honors already long granted for his merits, remitted the expenses for the bronze statue, which was decreed by the council of decurions, and the place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27100025.summary: This lengthy inscription details the numerous honors bestowed by various colonies and cities upon Titus Julius Capitoni, a tax contractor, and his subsequent remission of expenses for a statue erected in his honor.

EDCS-27100026.text_edition: \n Dis? Manibus et bonae memoriae Fronto Auggustorum nnostrorum dispensator Moesiae inferioris floribus ut saltem requiescant membra iucundis Aeliae carae mihi nunc hoc inclusae sepulcro regina Ditis magni regis precor hoc te nam meruit haec multa suis pro laudibus a me inmeritae propere solventem fila dearum quae globo Parcarum revolta cuncta gubernant qualis enim fuerit vita quam deinde pudica si possem effari cithara suaderem ego Manes haec primum casta quot te audire libenter et mundi spatia Ditis quoque regia noverunt hanc precor Elysiis iubeas consistere campis et myrta redimire comas et tempora flore Lar mihi haec quondam haec spes haec unica vita et vellet quod vellem nollet quoque ac si ego nollem intima nulla ei quae non mihi nota fuere nec labos sic huic defuit nec vellerem inscia fila parca manu set sic larga meo in amore mariti nec sine me cibus huic gratus nec munera Bacchi consilio mira cata mente nobili fama carmini possessor faveas precor ac precor ut tu hanc tituli sedem velles decorare quodannis et foveas aevi monumentum tempore grato roscida si rosula seu grato flore amaranthi et multisgenerum pomis variisque novisque ut possit toto refoveri temporis anno\n
EDCS-27100026.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead and to the good memory of Fronto, steward of our Augusti in Lower Moesia. May your limbs rest in flowers, beloved Aelia, now enclosed in this tomb, queen of the great king of the underworld. I beg you, for she deserved much praise from me, undeservedly hastening the threads of the Fates who govern all things spun by the fates. For if I could express what a chaste life she led, I would persuade with a lyre. Manes, I pray, may you hear this first, chaste one, and may Pluto's kingdom also know the spaces of the world. I beg you, command her to rest in the Elysian fields, and to crown her hair with myrtle and her temples with flowers. She was my household god, my hope, my only life. She wished what I wished, and would not wish what I did not wish. No inner thoughts of hers were unknown to me, nor did she lack toil, nor did she unknowingly spin the threads of the Fates with her hand, but she spun them generously with the love of her husband. Without me, no food was pleasing to her, nor the gifts of Bacchus. With admirable judgment and noble fame, may the owner favor my song. I beg and pray that you would wish to adorn this title-seat every year and foster this monument of time with a pleasant season, with dew-kissed roses, or with the pleasing flower of amaranth, and with many and various new fruits, so that she may be refreshed throughout the entire year.
EDCS-27100026.summary: This is an elaborate funerary epitaph for Aelia, wife of Fronto, the steward of Lower Moesia, expressing deep grief, praising her virtues, and invoking the underworld deities to ensure her peaceful afterlife.

EDCS-27100027.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae Aureliae Marcellinae Oescensis pientissimae feminae habens sic ius liberorum filiae quondam Marcellini ex praefecto legionis III Gallicae Danavae Damasco quae vixit nnos L Turranius Leontius praesbyter sic coniugi benae sic meritae memoriam et sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-27100027.translation: To the good memory of Aurelia Marcellina of Oescus, a most devoted woman, having the rights of children, formerly daughter of Marcellinus, former prefect of the III Gallica legion on the Danubius, who lived 50 years. Turranius Leontius, priest, made this memorial for his good and deserving wife and for himself during his lifetime.
EDCS-27100027.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelia Marcellina, set up by her husband Turranius Leontius, a priest, commemorating her life and virtues.

EDCS-27100028.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Cornelio Mampsalachano vixit annos VI Lucius Cornelius Fronto veteranus ex imaginifero legionis I Italicae pater et Vibia Marcia mater filio pientissimo honoris causa bene merenti posuerunt viatoris sic havetis\n
EDCS-27100028.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Cornelus Mampsalachanus lived for 6 years. Lucius Cornelius Fronto, a veteran and former standard-bearer of the I Italica legion, and Vibia Marcia, his parents, set this up for their most devoted son, who deserved well, as an honor. Travelers, greet him!
EDCS-27100028.summary: This is a tombstone set up by the parents for their young son, Lucius Cornelius Mampsalachanus, who died at the age of six.

EDCS-27100029.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Metellus Papiriae sic Iustus Ticino vixit annos LXV et Metella Felicia vixit annos <X=V>L<III=N> hic siti sunt filii parentibus bene merentibus faciendum curaverunt \n
EDCS-27100029.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Metellus Papirius Iustus lived 65 years and Metella Felicia lived 58 years. They are buried here. Their children, who deserved well of their parents, took care to have this made.
EDCS-27100029.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lives of Lucius Metellus Papirius Iustus and Metella Felicia, erected by their children.

EDCS-27100030.text_edition: \n ASSN Caius Cornelius Te Quintus Cassius V Caius Iemonius La Caius Vibius Eutyches Lucius Hebeius Rufus Quintus Cassius Modestus Quintus Cassius Severus Caius Vibius Natalis \n
EDCS-27100030.translation: ASSN Caius Cornelius Te Quintus Cassius V Caius Iemonius La Caius Vibius Eutyches Lucius Hebeius Rufus Quintus Cassius Modestus Quintus Cassius Severus Caius Vibius Natalis
EDCS-27100030.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly related to a collegium or association, with abbreviations preceding some of them.

EDCS-27100031.text_edition: \n Valerius Marci filius Fabia Mer Caius Attius Ius\n
EDCS-27100031.translation: Gaius Attius Mer, son of Valerius Marcius, of the Fabian tribe.
EDCS-27100031.summary: This inscription records the tribal affiliation and name of Gaius Attius Mer, likely a funerary marker or an identity tag from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-27100036.text_edition: \n Atti Caius Antonius Eutyches archidendrophorus pro salute sua posuit\n
EDCS-27100036.translation: Antonius Eutyches, a standard-bearer (archidendrophorus), erected this for his own health.
EDCS-27100036.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Antonius Eutyches, a standard-bearer, for his well-being, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100037.text_edition: \n Matri Deum Magnae pro salute adque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum et Caessarum Aurelius Firminianus vir perfectissimus dux limitis provinciae Scythiae bonis auspiciis consecravit\n
EDCS-27100037.translation: For the Great Mother Goddess, for the health and safety of our lords the Augusti and Caesars, Aurelius Firminianus, a most accomplished man and dux of the province of Scythia's frontier, consecrated this under good auspices.
EDCS-27100037.summary: This inscription records the consecration of a monument by Aurelius Firminianus, a high-ranking military official, to the Mother Goddess for the well-being of the emperors, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100038.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VIII consuli III patri patriae res publica Tomitanorum \n
EDCS-27100038.translation: The Republic of Tomis dedicated this to Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-27100038.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the city of Tomis to Emperor Hadrian, likely erected during his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-27100040.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Longino Quinto Marcio Turboni legato Augusti pro praetore Titius Crispus cornicularius eius\n
EDCS-27100040.translation: Titius Crispus, his adjutant (cornicularius), dedicated this to Titus Flavius Longinus Quintus Marcius Turbo, legate of the emperor with the powers of a proconsul.
EDCS-27100040.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Titius Crispus, an adjutant, to his superior officer Titus Flavius Longinus Quintus Marcius Turbo, a Roman governor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100043.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Longinus ex decurione veterano buleuta Tomitanorum se vivo sibi et Ulpiae Aquilinae coniugi suae memoriam fecit in praedio suo\n
EDCS-27100043.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Ulpius Longinus, a veteran decurion and council member of Tomis, made this memorial for himself and his wife Ulpia Aquilina while still alive on his own land.
EDCS-27100043.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument set up by Marcus Ulpius Longinus, a retired military officer and civic official, for himself and his wife in Tomis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100044.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Domitio Capetolino sic |(centurioni) legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis domo Capetoliade vixit annis XXXII factus |(centurio) vixit menses \n
EDCS-27100044.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Domitius Capetolinus, a centurion of the 11th Claudian legion, piously faithful, from Capetoliade, lived for 32 years; after being made a centurion, he lived for [blank] months.
EDCS-27100044.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Domitius Capetolinus, a centurion in the 11th Legion, detailing his origins and lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100045.text_edition: \n Marcus Attius Marci filius Firmus memoriam sibi et Cocceiae Iuliae coniugi suae se vivo fecit loci princeps \n
EDCS-27100045.translation: Marcus Attius Firmus, son of Marcus, the local leader, made this memorial for himself and his wife Cocceia Iulia while still alive.
EDCS-27100045.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument erected by Marcus Attius Firmus, a local official, for himself and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100046.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto divi Severi nepoti divi Antonini filio dedicante Tito Flavio Novio Rufo legato Augusti pro praetore Marcus Ulpius Antipater sacerdos provinciae et bis duumviralis ob honorem pontificatus\n
EDCS-27100046.translation: Marcus Ulpius Antipater, priest of the province and twice duumvir, dedicated this for the honor of the priesthood to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, grandson of the divine Severus, son of the divine Antoninus, with Titus Flavius Novius Rufus, legate of the emperor with the powers of a proconsul, presiding.
EDCS-27100046.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Ulpius Antipater, a provincial priest and former magistrate, to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, during the governorship of Titus Flavius Novius Rufus, likely from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-27100047.text_edition: \n Marco Pontio Laeliano clarissimo viro patri Ponti Laeliani legati Augusti pro praetore ordo Troesmensium\n
EDCS-27100047.translation: The council of the people of Troesmis honored Marcus Pontius Laelianus, a most distinguished man, father of Pontius Laelianus, legate of the emperor with the powers of a proconsul.
EDCS-27100047.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication by the city council of Troesmis to Marcus Pontius Laelianus, father of a Roman governor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100048.text_edition: \n Publio Vigellio Raio Plario Saturnino Atilio Braduano Caucidio Tertullo legato Augusti ordo Troesmensium ex decreto suo\n
EDCS-27100048.translation: To Publius Vigellius Raio Plarius Saturninus Atilius Braduanus Caucidius Tertullus, Imperial Legate, by decree of the council of the people of Troesmis.
EDCS-27100048.summary: This is a dedication by the council of Troesmis to their imperial legate, Publius Vigellius Raio Plarius Saturninus Atilius Braduanus Caucidius Tertullus.

EDCS-27100049.text_edition: \n Tiberio Veturio Tiberi filio Aemilia Mauretano Fundis praefecto castrorum legionis V Macedonicae ordo Troesmensium\n
EDCS-27100049.translation: To Tiberius Veturius Mauretus, son of Tiberius, of the Aemilia tribe, from Fundi, prefect of the camp of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, by the council of the people of Troesmis.
EDCS-27100049.summary: This is an honorary inscription by the council of Troesmis to Tiberius Veturius Mauretus, prefect of the camp of the Fifth Macedonian Legion.

EDCS-27100062.text_edition: \n X legionis primae Italicae Lucius Iulius Lucilianus legatus Augusti legionis I Italicae pondera examinata signavit\n
EDCS-27100062.translation: Lucius Iulius Lucilianus, Imperial Legate of the First Italian Legion, verified and sealed the weights for the First Italian Legion.
EDCS-27100062.summary: This inscription records the verification and sealing of weights by Lucius Iulius Lucilianus, legate of the First Italian Legion.

EDCS-27800422.text_edition: \n ------] - Braetius Favor veteranus legionis V Macedonicae militavit annos XXVI vixit annos LX Valerius Ingenuus filius et heres patri pientissimo et Flavia Firma coniugi bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800422.translation: Braetius Favor, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, served for 26 years and lived for 60 years. Valerius Ingenuus, his son and heir, and Flavia Firma, his wife, erected this to their most devoted father and well-deserving wife.
EDCS-27800422.summary: This tombstone records the life and service of Braetius Favor, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, and was erected by his son and wife.

EDCS-27800423.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Manius Bassianus vixit annis LXII buleuta Histriae Manius Vitales sic nepos eius vixit annis VIIII Vitalianus Bassianus filius eius et Scapullia Gemella coniunx eius sibi et et sibi parentibus bene meritis posuerunt have viator\n
EDCS-27800423.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Titus Manius Bassianus lived 62 years. Manius Vitales, his grandson, who lived 9 years. Vitalianus Bassianus, his son, and Scapullia Gemella, his wife, erected this for themselves and for their well-deserving parents. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-27800423.summary: This tombstone records the death of Titus Manius Bassianus, his son Vitalianus Bassianus, and grandson Manius Vitales, and was erected by the son and wife.

EDCS-27800424.text_edition: \n I nomini e qu<i=E> vixit annis X Eirene quae vixit annis XXXV \n
EDCS-27800424.translation: To I, who lived 10 years, and Eirene, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-27800424.summary: This is a tombstone for two individuals, I and Eirene, recording their ages at death.

EDCS-27800426.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Dionysiae vixit annos XXV Valerius Clementinus maritus eius bene merenti coniugi carissimae posuit\n
EDCS-27800426.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aelia Dionysia lived 25 years. Valerius Clementinus, her husband, erected this to his most beloved and deserving wife.
EDCS-27800426.summary: This tombstone records the death of Aelia Dionysia and was erected by her husband, Valerius Clementinus.

EDCS-27800427.text_edition: \n Heroi Invicto sacro sic pro salute Imperatoris Valeri Zosimus et Callinicus et Phoebio posuerunt de suo X Kalendas Iulias Cornelio et Catio consulibus\n
EDCS-27800427.translation: Sacred to the Invincible Hero. Thus for the safety of the Emperor, Valerius Zosimus, Callinicus, and Phoebius erected this from their own funds on the 10th day before the Kalends of July, when Cornelius and Catins were consuls.
EDCS-27800427.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Invincible Hero for the Emperor's safety by Valerius Zosimus, Callinicus, and Phoebius, dated to 198 CE.

EDCS-27800428.text_edition: \n Genio loci Caius Prastina Messallinus legatus Augusti pro praetore \n
EDCS-27800428.translation: To the Genius of the place. Caius Prastina Messallinus, Imperial Legate Pro Praetore.
EDCS-27800428.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius loci by Caius Prastina Messallinus, an imperial legate pro praetore.

EDCS-27800429.text_edition: \n Heroi et Domno matrem sic Romanorum subscriptorum Menia Iuliane Tiana Lucius Antonius Capito Nicomedia Gaius Licinius Clemens natus Abonutichu sic Gaius Aurelius Alexandrus natus Heraclia sic Cludius Secundus natus Abonutichus sic Gaius Gabinius Modestus natus Perintho Ailius sic Barbario natus Nicomedia Aurelius Vitus natus Caesaria sic Vettius Ponticus natus Tio Fabius Paulinus natus Mazaca Titus Ailius sic Pompeius natus Tio Papirius Celer natus CII \n
EDCS-27800429.translation: To the Hero and Lord. Thus, the following Romans subscribed: Menia Iuliane Tiana, Lucius Antonius Capito from Nicomedia, Gaius Licinius Clemens born in Abonutichus, Gaius Aurelius Alexandrus born in Heraclia, Claudius Secundus born in Abonutichus, Gaius Gabinius Modestus born in Perinthus, Aelius Barbario born in Nicomedia, Aurelius Vitus born in Caesarea, Vettius Ponticus born in Tio, Fabius Paulinus born in Mazaca, Titus Aelius Pompeius born in Tio, Papirius Celer born in CII (probably a place or tribe).
EDCS-27800429.summary: This inscription records a group of individuals from various cities who subscribed to honor the Hero and Lord.

EDCS-27800431.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Heroni Quintus Trebellius Quinti filius Fabia Maximus Roma ex trecenario |(centurio)? legionis V Macedonicae I hastatus posterior |(centurio)? cohortis IIII praetoriae votum solvit\n
EDCS-27800431.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Hero. Quintus Trebellius Maximus, son of Quintus, of the Fabian tribe, from Rome, formerly a centurion of the rank of trecenarius of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, then a hastatus posterior of the Fourth Praetorian Cohort, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27800431.summary: A military man, Quintus Trebellius Maximus, dedicates an offering to Jupiter, likely during the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-27800432.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cerdo pro se et filibus sic suis aram posuit et votum solvit votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-27800432.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Cerdo set up this altar for himself and his sons and his wives, and fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-27800432.summary: Cerdo dedicates an altar to Jupiter for his family, likely in the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-27800440.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudianus ex speculatoribus vixit annos XXXXIIII menses V dies X qui semperIIribus et atlectionibus sic versatus \n
EDCS-27800440.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudianus, formerly a *speculator*, lived 44 years, 5 months, and 10 days, and was always involved in military duties and detachments.
EDCS-27800440.summary: A tombstone records the life and service of Claudianus, a *speculator*, who died at age 44.

EDCS-27800441.text_edition: \n Marius Celsus miles cohor(tis) VII Gallorum vixit annos XXXX militavit annos XXIIII titulum possuerunt sic liberti vivo Marius Merqurius sic et Maria Cale et Marius Tara\n
EDCS-27800441.translation: Marius Celsus, a soldier of the Seventh Gallic Cohort, lived 40 years and served 24 years. His freedmen, Marius Mercurius, Maria Cale, and Marius Tara, set up this monument to him while he was alive.
EDCS-27800441.summary: Freedmen erect a tombstone for Marius Celsus, a soldier who served for 24 years and lived to 40.

EDCS-27800443.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Valerius Pacatus militavit in legione V Macedonica beneficiarius consularis duplicarius \n
EDCS-27800443.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Valerius Pacatus served in the Fifth Macedonian Legion as a *beneficiarius consularis* and a *duplicarius*.
EDCS-27800443.summary: A tombstone records the military service of Publius Valerius Pacatus in the Fifth Macedonian Legion.

EDCS-27800444.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Severus vaeteranus sic sibi et memoriae Cinenes maritae bene meritae de suo posuit\n
EDCS-27800444.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Severus, a veteran, set this up for himself and for the memory of his well-deserving wife Cinenes.
EDCS-27800444.summary: A veteran, Valerius Severus, sets up a memorial for himself and his wife Cinenes.

EDCS-27800445.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Valens veteranus classis Flaviae Moesie sic memoriam feci vivo meo mi et dulcissime sic coniugi meae \xcf\x84\xe1\xbd\xb4\xce\xbd \xce\xb3\xce\xbb\xcf\x85\xce\xba\xcf\x85\xcf\x84\xe1\xbd\xb1\xcf\x84\xce\xb7\xce\xbd \xcf\x83\xe1\xbd\xbb\xce\xbd\xce\xb2\xce\xb9\xce\xbf\xce\xbd\xce\x87 \xe1\xbd\x83\xcf\x82 \xe1\xbc\x82\xce\xbd \xe1\xbc\x84\xce\xbb\xce\xbb\xce\xbf\xce\xbd \xcf\x84\xce\xb9\xce\xbd\xe2\x80\x99 \xe1\xbc\x80\xce\xbd\xcf\x84\xe1\xbd\xb3\xce\xb8\xe1\xbf\x83 \xce\xbd\xce\xb5\xce\xba\xcf\x81\xe1\xbd\xb8\xce\xbd \xe1\xbc\xa2 \xcf\x80\xce\xbf\xce\xbb\xe1\xbd\xb5\xcf\x83\xce\xb5\xce\xb9 <\xcf\x84=\xce\x98>\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xbd<\xe1\xbf\x83=\xce\x95> \xcf\x84\xe1\xbf\xb7 \xcf\x86\xe1\xbd\xb7\xcf\x83\xce\xba\xe1\xbf\xb7 \xce\xb4(\xce\xb7\xce\xbd\xe1\xbd\xb1\xcf\x81\xce\xb9\xce\xb1) \xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x94 \n
EDCS-27800445.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Valens, a veteran of the Flavia Moesia fleet, made this memorial for myself and for my dearest wife, who was a good and supportive spouse, while I was still alive.
EDCS-27800445.summary: A naval veteran, Valerius Valens, erects a memorial for himself and his wife, likely during the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-27800446.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27800446.text_edition: \n mento annis VII fecit stipendia XXIIII et vixit annis XLII et spiritum naturae suae reddedit sic Aurelia Claudia coniux bene merente sic virginio suo et pare sic hunc titulo sic una cum filiis suis posuit valete viatores\n
EDCS-27800446.translation: He served for 7 years, completed 22 years of military service, and lived 42 years, then returned his spirit to nature. Aurelia Claudia, his wife, along with her sons, set up this monument for her deserving husband Virginus.
EDCS-27800446.summary: A tombstone erected by a wife and sons records the military service and life of Virginus.

EDCS-27800447.text_edition: \n VNE veteranus legiones sic XI Claudiae piae fidelis vixit annos XXXXVI Aurelius Sabinus heres Aurelio Sabino filio veterani \n
EDCS-27800447.translation: VNE, a veteran of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, lived 46 years. Aurelius Sabinus, heir of Aurelius Sabinus, a veteran, [erected this for his son].
EDCS-27800447.summary: A tombstone for a veteran named Aurelius Sabinus, son of a veteran of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, who lived to 46.

EDCS-27800448.text_edition: \n vixit annos XL militavit annos XIX Longina posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-27800448.translation: She lived 40 years and served 19 years. Longina set this up for her deserving [husband/relative].
EDCS-27800448.summary: A tombstone records the life of an individual who lived 40 years and served 19 years, erected by Longina.

EDCS-27800451.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Apollonius Dadae Mamae Dadae matri suae sacerdoti Tomitanorum bene merenti\n
EDCS-27800451.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Apollonius, son of Dada and Mama, erected this for his mother, Dada, priestess of the Tomitani, who deserved well.
EDCS-27800451.summary: This is a funerary dedication from the 2nd or 3rd century CE for a priestess named Dada, erected by her son Apollonius.

EDCS-27800452.text_edition: \n Caius Arrius Quintianus bis duumviralis et augur municipii Troesmensis veterani filius et Claudia Servata uxor se vivis memoriam sibi fecerunt curantibus Arriis Quintiano filio et Ianuario liberto\n
EDCS-27800452.translation: Gaius Arrius Quintianus, twice duumvir and augur of the municipium of Troesmis, son of a veteran, and his wife Claudia Servata, made this memorial for themselves while alive, under the care of their son Arrius Quintianus and their freedman Ianuarius.
EDCS-27800452.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument erected by Gaius Arrius Quintianus and Claudia Servata, important local officials in Troesmis, likely in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27800454.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Tertullo Claudi Tertulli et Laberiae Chrysaidis filio morte erepto annorum XVII et mense sic XII dulcissimi fili corpus in pyalidem parentes eius ponendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-27800454.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Tertullus, son of Claudius Tertullus and Laberia Chrysais, taken by death at the age of 17 years and 12 months, his dearest son, his parents arranged for his body to be placed in this tomb.
EDCS-27800454.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a young man, Tiberius Claudius Tertullus, at 17 years and 12 months, and was set up by his parents, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27800455.text_edition: \n Castus Muca<p=R>ori a vico C<l=I>ementian(o) vixit annis VI Sedida Tiretitis <uc=CV>ssor sic <una=VAL> cum <L=C>on<g=C>ino et Martia et Valerio \n
EDCS-27800455.translation: Castus, son of Mucapor, from the Clementine district, lived for 6 years. Sedida, daughter of Tiretis, a weaver, with her brother Ianuarius and Martia and Valerius.
EDCS-27800455.summary: This inscription records the short life of Castus, son of Mucapor, who died at age 6, likely in the 2nd century CE, and mentions several other individuals.

EDCS-27800456.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Puplio sic filio Magni io patri et Iuliae Cocceiae matri et Iulio Antonio et Iulio Antonino fratribus Iulia Puplia sic et Iulius Cocceius fili parentibus suis pietatis gratia bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800456.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Puplia and Iulius Cocceius, her son, erected this for their parents, Magnus, father, and Iulia Cocceia, mother, and their brothers, Iulius Antonius and Iulius Antoninus, who deserved well, out of gratitude for their piety.
EDCS-27800456.summary: This is a funerary dedication by children for their parents and brothers, expressing filial piety, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800458.text_edition: \n EMMCL vixit annos VIII et Quirillus filius vixit annos VI Villatia Matrona coniunx marito suo bene merenti et natis suis maemoriam sic posuit quae et ipsa vivo sibi cum eis fecit\n
EDCS-27800458.translation: EMMCL lived for 8 years and Quirillus, her son, lived for 6 years. Villatia Matrona, wife, erected this memorial for her deserving husband and for her children; she herself also made it for herself while alive with them.
EDCS-27800458.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of Emmcl (8 years) and her son Quirillus (6 years), and was erected by Villatia Matrona for her husband and children, also providing for herself, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800460.text_edition: \n me fati munus supplevit Pia et Daciscus fratres et filii eius memoriae gratiam sic posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800460.translation: Pia and Daciscus, brother and sisters, his children, fulfilled the duty of fate and erected this in gratitude for memory.
EDCS-27800460.summary: This funerary inscription records the memory of a deceased individual, erected by their siblings and children, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800462.text_edition: \n filius patri bene merenti posuerunt V Idus Iunias ob diem Rosaliorum\n
EDCS-27800462.translation: A son erected this for his deserving father. May 18th, on the day of the Rosalii.
EDCS-27800462.summary: This brief inscription is a funerary dedication by a son for his father, mentioning a specific date in June and a group called the Rosalii, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800468.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27800468.text_edition: \n ------] pro Theodule coniuce sic mea quae fuit filia Patrici presbyteri ac titulum posui ad memoriam nestram sic me superstantem quod di[gne ------\n
EDCS-27800468.translation: [...], I placed this monument for Theodula, my wife, who was the daughter of Patricius the priest, and for our memory, which [...] worthy [...].
EDCS-27800468.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by a husband for his wife Theodula, daughter of a priest, likely from the 3rd century CE or later.

EDCS-27800469.text_edition: \n Aurelia Ianuaria Ianuari annorum iuncta pari Flavio Martino et amplius vixi menses V dies II pro commoda fuit spiritum deo redere et Aurelia Domna soror Ianuari annorumIII iuncta pari vixit menses X dies I fatum conplevit durus pro caritate coniugi sic et sorori sic ipsi vivite parentes et nestris sic providete filiis estote memores iterum Elysiis coventuri ave vale viator\n
EDCS-27800469.translation: Aurelia Ianuaria, daughter of Ianuarius, lived for years, joined in marriage to Flavius Martinus, and lived 5 months and 2 days more. She was well-suited to return her spirit to God. And Aurelia Domna, sister of Ianuarius, aged 3 years, joined in marriage, lived 10 months and 1 day, fulfilled her fate. Harsh was the fate for the sake of love for husband and sister. Live yourselves, parents, and provide for your children, be mindful of your own. You will meet again in the Elysian Fields. Hail, traveler, farewell.
EDCS-27800469.summary: This complex funerary inscription, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, records the lives and deaths of Aurelia Ianuaria and her sister Aurelia Domna, offering reflections on life, death, and remembrance.

EDCS-27800470.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Titus Flavius Sabinus posuit pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Antonini\n
EDCS-27800470.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno Regina, Titus Flavius Sabinus erected this for the health of the Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus.
EDCS-27800470.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno by Titus Flavius Sabinus for the emperor's health, likely from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-27800476.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius Quirina Priscus Hemesa ex custode armorum vixit annis L militavit nnis XXVI post missionem vixit nnis II conveterani qui et heredes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800476.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Priscus, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, formerly a guard of arms, lived 50 years, served 26 years, and lived 2 years after his discharge; his fellow veterans and heirs erected this for the well-deserving man.
EDCS-27800476.summary: A tombstone for Tiberius Claudius Priscus, a veteran who served 26 years, erected by his fellow veterans and heirs.

EDCS-27800477.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Titi filius Teretina Valens Amastri veteranus legionis V Macedonicae annorum LXV Valeria Fortunata uxor eius annorum LX Flavius Alexander frater annorum LXII Marcia Basilissa uxor eius annorum L in locum titulum posuerunt sibi\n
EDCS-27800477.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Flavius Valens, son of Titus, of the Teretina tribe, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, aged 65, his wife Valeria Fortunata, aged 60, and his brother Flavius Alexander, aged 62, and his wife Marcia Basilissa, aged 50, erected this monument in this place for themselves.
EDCS-27800477.summary: A tombstone for Titus Flavius Valens, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica, and his family members, who jointly erected the monument for themselves.

EDCS-27800478.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Ponticus vixit nnos XXXII militavit nnos XIV in legione V Macedonica natus Amastris Iulius Ponticus frater Sentius Ponticus veteranus Sentius Ponticus miles legionis V Macedonicae fratres conheredes bene merenti de suo posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800478.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulius Ponticus lived 32 years, served 14 years in the Legio V Macedonica, born in Amastris. His brother Sentius Ponticus, a veteran, and Sentius Ponticus, a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica, his brothers and co-heirs, erected this for the well-deserving man at their own expense.
EDCS-27800478.summary: A tombstone for Iulius Ponticus, a soldier of Legio V Macedonica, erected by his brothers who were also veterans or serving in the same legion.

EDCS-27800479.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publiciae Cyrillae domo Bithyna vixit annis L Caius Publicius Niger miles legionis V Macedonicae libertae et coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27800479.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publicia Cyrilla from Bithynia lived 50 years. Caius Publicius Niger, a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica, erected this for his deserving freedwoman and wife.
EDCS-27800479.summary: A tombstone for Publicia Cyrilla, a woman from Bithynia, erected by her husband, Caius Publicius Niger, a soldier of Legio V Macedonica.

EDCS-27800480.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Marciano veterano ex singulari consularis alae I Dardanorum pontifici IIviro quinquennali municipii Troesmensium Marcii Antistius Rufus pontifex et Antistius Domitius aedilis quaestor fratres optimo viro\n
EDCS-27800480.translation: For Marcus Ulpius Marcianus, a veteran, formerly a *singularis consularis* of the I Dardanorum cavalry wing, priest, *duumvir quinquennalis* of the municipality of Troesmes. Marcius Antistius Rufus, priest, and Antistius Domitius, *aedile* and *quaestor*, brothers, [erected this] for the most excellent man.
EDCS-27800480.summary: A funerary monument for Marcus Ulpius Marcianus, a distinguished veteran and local magistrate, erected by his brothers who held civic offices.

EDCS-27800481.text_edition: \n Titus Valerius Titi filius Pollia Marcianus castris veteranus legionis V Macedonicae ex beneficiario consularis militare coepit Imperatore Antonino IIII consule functus expeditione orientali sub Statio Prisco Iulio Severo Martio Vero clarissimis viris item Germanica sub Calpurnio Agricola Claudio Frontone clarissimis viris missus honesta missione in Dacia Cethego et Claro consulibus sub Cornelio Clemente clarissimo viro reversus at sic Lares suos et Marcia Basilissa matre dendrophororum enupta sibi Valeria Longa sorore pro salute sua suorumque\n
EDCS-27800481.translation: Titus Valerius Marcianus, son of Titus, of the Pollia tribe, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, formerly a *beneficiarius consularis*, began his military service under Emperor Antoninus IV, completed his service during the Eastern expedition under Statins Priscus Iulius Severus Martius Verus, men of highest distinction, and also during the German campaign under Calpurnius Agricola Claudius Fronto, men of highest distinction, and was honorably discharged in Dacia when Cetheus and Clarus were consuls, under Cornelius Clemens, a man of highest distinction; returning to his household gods, and with Marcia Basilissa, his mother, formerly a *dendrophorus*, and with Valeria Longa, his sister, for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-27800481.summary: A lengthy inscription detailing the military career of Titus Valerius Marcianus, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica, highlighting his service in various campaigns and commands, and concluding with a dedication for his family's well-being.

EDCS-27800482.text_edition: \n Tiberius Vitales ex b(ene)f(iciario) veteranus legionis V Macedonicae sacerdosque provinciae vixit nnos LX vivo se fecit reliquiarum curagente Tiberio Vitale filio et herede hic situs est\n
EDCS-27800482.translation: Tiberius Vitales, formerly a *beneficiarius*, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica and priest of the province, lived 60 years. He made this monument for himself while alive, with Tiberius Vitales, his son and heir, in charge of the remains. He is buried here.
EDCS-27800482.summary: A self-funded funerary monument for Tiberius Vitales, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica and provincial priest, erected by himself and his son.

EDCS-27800484.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Saturnino quaestori aedili quandam duumver(o)! vixit \n
EDCS-27800484.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Caius Iulius Saturninus, quaestor, aedile, and sometimes *duumvir*. He lived...
EDCS-27800484.summary: A fragmentary tombstone for Caius Iulius Saturninus, who held several important municipal offices.

EDCS-27800485.translation: Festus, augur of the municipality of Troesmes.
EDCS-27800485.summary: An inscription recording Festus as an augur of the municipality of Troesmes.

EDCS-27800488.text_edition: \n pro salute Titus Flavius Apollinaris praefectus alae I Dardanorum qui et domum a solo sumptibus suis fecit adventantibus collegiis feliciter\n
EDCS-27800488.translation: For the well-being of Titus Flavius Apollinaris, prefect of the First Wing of the Dardanians, who also built this house from the ground up at his own expense, arriving with the collegia, successfully.
EDCS-27800488.summary: This is a dedication made by Titus Flavius Apollinaris, a military officer, for his successful arrival with collegia, noting he funded the construction of a house.

EDCS-27800493.text_edition: \n Pii Felicis invicti Augusti Aurelius Castor \n
EDCS-27800493.translation: Aurelius Castor for the Pious, Fortunate, and Unconquered Emperor.
EDCS-27800493.summary: This inscription is a dedication or statement of loyalty by Aurelius Castor to the emperor, possibly associated with a monument or award.

EDCS-27800494.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Erucio Victori medico cohortis \n
EDCS-27800494.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, for Quintus Erucius Victor, physician of the cohort.
EDCS-27800494.summary: This is a funerary inscription (Dis Manibus) commemorating Quintus Erucius Victor, a physician.

EDCS-27800498.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaius Iulius Aemilianus veteranus legionis VII Claudiae vixit annis LX mensibus X diebus IIII Aemilianus Vitalis miles praetorianus filius et Dudis frater et Victor et Iulius et Aelius filii karissimo patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800498.translation: To the Spirits of the departed: Gaius Iulius Aemilianus, a veteran of the Seventh Claudian Legion, lived 60 years, 10 months, and 4 days. Aemilianus Vitalis, a praetorian guardsman, his son, and Dudis his brother, and his sons Victor, Iulius, and Aelius, erected this for their dearest, well-deserving father.
EDCS-27800498.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Aemilianus, a veteran of the Seventh Legion, set up by his sons and brother.

EDCS-27800499.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Domitio Narcisso qui vixit annos LXX hic situs est Caius Iulius Maximus ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus amico merito\n
EDCS-27800499.translation: To the Spirits of the departed: Lucius Domitius Narcissus, who lived 70 years, is buried here. Caius Iulius Maximus, honored with decurional ornaments, erected this for his well-deserving friend.
EDCS-27800499.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Domitius Narcissus, a friend of Caius Iulius Maximus, who was honored with civic decorations.

EDCS-27800500.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae Iulius Didalsus et Iulius Antonius pro salute sua votum posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800500.translation: To Diana Regina, Iulius Didalsus and Iulius Antonius made a vow for their well-being.
EDCS-27800500.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Diana Regina by Iulius Didalsus and Iulius Antonius for their safety.

EDCS-27800501.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto sacrum Lucius Valerius Domitianus\n
EDCS-27800501.translation: Sacred to Hercules Invictus, by Lucius Valerius Domitianus.
EDCS-27800501.summary: This is a dedication to the god Hercules Invictus by Lucius Valerius Domitianus.

EDCS-27800503.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Iuli Cai fili Collina Celeris veterani legionis V Macedonicae et Iuliae Tyche Caius Iulius Crescens |(centurio) legionis I Italicae parentibus piissimis fecit\n
EDCS-27800503.translation: To the Spirits of the departed: Caius Iulius Crescens, a centurion of the First Italian Legion, made this for his most dutiful parents, Caius Iulius Collina Celer, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, and Iulia Tyche.
EDCS-27800503.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Iulius Collina Celer and Iulia Tyche, funded by their son Caius Iulius Crescens, a centurion.

EDCS-27800505.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Aprio decurio coloniae et Valentilla eius vixit nnis XLV mensibus III diebus V et Aprionianus filius eius vixit nnis XVIIII mensibus II diebus VI et Aprilla vixit nnis VIIII mensibus V diebus III et Valentinus vixit nnis VI mense uno diebus III et Aper vixit nnis III diebus VI Aprio decurio <c>oloniae vivus sibi fecit et coniugi bene meritae et filiis enfelicissimis sic posuit\n
EDCS-27800505.translation: To the Spirits of the departed: Aurelius Aprio, a decurion of the colony, and his wife Valentilla, who lived 45 years, 3 months, and 5 days, and their son Aprionianus, who lived 19 years, 2 months, and 6 days, and Aprilla, who lived 9 years, 5 months, and 3 days, and Valentinus, who lived 6 years, 1 month, and 3 days, and Aper, who lived 3 years, and 6 days. Aprio, a decurion of the colony, made this while alive for himself, his well-deserving wife, and his most fortunate sons.
EDCS-27800505.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Aprio, his wife Valentilla, and their children, who all died at various ages, with Aprio himself commissioning it while alive.

EDCS-27800506.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Pompeio Eutychi liberto Quinti Pompei Falconis\n
EDCS-27800506.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, for Quintus Pompeius Eutychus, freedman of Quintus Pompeius Falconis.
EDCS-27800506.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Pompeius Eutychus, a freedman, commemorating his relationship with his former master.

EDCS-27800507.text_edition: \n Caius Ersidius Sexstus sic miles legionis I Italicae votum Hercule sic solvit merito\n
EDCS-27800507.translation: Gaius Ersidius Sextus, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, fulfilled his vow to Hercules as was deserved.
EDCS-27800507.summary: This inscription records a soldier of the First Italian Legion fulfilling a vow to Hercules.

EDCS-27800508.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maxsimo sic exs sic visu posuit Quintus Memmius\n
EDCS-27800508.translation: Quintus Memmius set this up for Jupiter Best and Greatest, as he saw fit.
EDCS-27800508.summary: This inscription records Quintus Memmius setting up a monument for Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-27800509.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Castianae in annis X Carusiae et filiis ex eadem progenitis Marcus Aurel(ius) Iulius ex protectoribus vivo sibi et supra dictis coniugi et filiis memoriae gratia titulum constituit vale viator\n
EDCS-27800509.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. In her tenth year, Carusia and her children born from the same union, Marcus Aurelius Iulius, formerly a protector, erected this tombstone for himself while alive and for his wife and children as mentioned above, for remembrance. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-27800509.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Marcus Aurelius Iulius for himself, his wife, and children, mentioning his wife Carusia and their children.

EDCS-27800510.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Cai filius Fabia Longinus Heraclea miles legionis I Italicae centuria Vaselli Marcelli militavit nnos XIIII vixit nnos XXXIIII Publius Valerius Germanus commanipularis et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-27800510.translation: Gaius Valerius Longinus, son of Gaius, of the Fabia tribe, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, served in the century of Marcus Vasellus for 14 years, lived for 34 years. Publius Valerius Germanus, a fellow soldier and heir, saw to the erection of this monument.
EDCS-27800510.summary: This funerary inscription records the service and lifespan of Gaius Valerius Longinus, a soldier, with his fellow soldier and heir Publius Valerius Germanus responsible for its erection.

EDCS-27800511.text_edition: \n Iulius Iero negotiator vixit annos LX hic situs est Iulia \n
EDCS-27800511.translation: Iulius Iero, a merchant, lived for 60 years. He is buried here. Iulia [set this up].
EDCS-27800511.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulius Iero, a merchant, recording his age and burial place.

EDCS-27800513.text_edition: \n Lucius Plinius Sexti filius Fabia domo Trumplia miles legionis XX annorum XLV stipendiorum XVII hic situs est testamento fieri iussit Secundus Luci Plini et Publi Mestri libertus fecit\n
EDCS-27800513.translation: Lucius Plinius, son of Sextus, of the Fabia tribe, from Trumpia, a soldier of the 20th Legion, aged 45, with 17 years of service, is buried here. Secundus, a freedman of Lucius Plinius and Publius Mestrius, ordered it to be made by will.
EDCS-27800513.summary: This funerary inscription records the details of Lucius Plinius, a soldier of the 20th Legion, and states that his freedman ordered the monument to be built according to his will.

EDCS-27800514.text_edition: \n optime annos XLV me et senecta mea Crescentilla socra tua et Sile coniux tua Bitilla curavit filia optimo viro\n
EDCS-27800514.translation: Your very good [husband], for 45 years. Your mother-in-law Crescentilla and your wife Bitilla Sile took care of [this monument] in my old age, my daughter, for the best husband.
EDCS-27800514.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely funerary, for a man of 45, with his mother-in-law and wife arranging the monument.

EDCS-27800516.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Bassus Cai filius Collina Massacis vixit annis XXXX ex voluntate testamenti Caius Iulius Ingenuus filius et Iulia Veneria liberta et coniunx bene de se merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-27800516.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Julius Bassus, son of Gaius, of the Collina tribe, from Massacis, lived for 40 years. Gaius Julius Ingenuus, his son, and Iulia Veneria, his freedwoman and wife, arranged for this monument to be made as per his will, for one who deserved well of them.
EDCS-27800516.summary: This tombstone was erected by the son and freedwoman/wife of Gaius Julius Bassus, commemorating his life and fulfilling his testamentary wishes.

EDCS-27800517.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus s Victorinus II menses VI us Victor MMV filio ca[rissimo ------\n
EDCS-27800517.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Victorinus, 2 months, 6 days. To Victor [his] most beloved son [---].
EDCS-27800517.summary: This fragmented tombstone records the short life of a child named Victorinus, dedicated to his father Victor.

EDCS-27800905.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Marcus Titius Maximus IIviralis coloniae voto libens posuit scripsit Aelianus\n
EDCS-27800905.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras. Marcus Titius Maximus, holding the office of duovir in the colony, willingly erected this vow. Written by Aelianus.
EDCS-27800905.summary: This inscription records Marcus Titius Maximus, a local magistrate, dedicating a votive offering to the god Mithras.

EDCS-27800906.text_edition: \n Titus Tettius Plotuus veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae felicis pater sacrorum dei invicti solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27800906.translation: Titus Tettius Plotuus, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, the father of the sacred rites of the unconquered sun god, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his duty.
EDCS-27800906.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion to the god Sol Invictus, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-27800907.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Papiria Rufiniano decurioni? C M\n
EDCS-27800907.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Titus Flavius Papirius Rufinianus, decurion (?). M. C.
EDCS-27800907.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Papirius Rufinianus, a man who held the rank of decurion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800908.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Calpurnius Marci filius heres fecit memoriae causa \n
EDCS-27800908.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Titus Calpurnius, son of Marci, made this in memory.
EDCS-27800908.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Titus Calpurnius erected a monument for himself or a relative, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800909.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonius Valens miles legionis XI Claudiae miletavit sic annos XXIII Natalis et Suro bene merenti pusuerunt sic\n
EDCS-27800909.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Antonius Valens, a soldier of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, served for 23 years. Natalis and Suro placed this for him, deserving of good treatment.
EDCS-27800909.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Antonius Valens, a soldier of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, who served for 23 years and was honored by Natalis and Suro.

EDCS-27800910.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcianus veteranus ex ordinato vixit annis CIIII et Veneria Valentina qui et Pelma coniux carissima vivo suo sibi fecerunt opto sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-27800910.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Marcianus, a veteran of the 'ordinatus' rank, lived 104 years, and Veneria Valentina, who was also called Pelma, his dearest wife, made this for him while alive; may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-27800910.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Veneria Valentina for her husband Marcianus, a veteran who lived to be 104 years old, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800911.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Mucianus vixit nnos LX Valeria Firmina coniux vixit nnos L Aurelius Celsus M us Aurelius V\n
EDCS-27800911.translation: Publius Aelius Mucianus lived 60 years, Valeria Firmina, his wife, lived 50 years. Aurelius Celsus M us Aurelius V
EDCS-27800911.summary: This inscription records the ages at death of Publius Aelius Mucianus and his wife Valeria Firmina, with partial names of other individuals possibly mentioned.

EDCS-27800913.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Demetria Dam Zoili vivo suo Dizo vixit \n
EDCS-27800913.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Demetria daughter of Dam Zoilus, Dizo, lived while alive.
EDCS-27800913.summary: This is a funerary inscription mentioning Demetria and Dizo, likely a husband and wife, with Demetria being the daughter of Dam Zoilus.

EDCS-27800914.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dizias Bitipel vixit annos LXXX coniux Nene et filii bene merenti pusuerunt sic\n
EDCS-27800914.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Dizias Bitipel lived 80 years. His wife Nene and his sons placed this for him, deserving of good treatment.
EDCS-27800914.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Dizias Bitipel, who lived to be 80 years old, and was honored by his wife Nene and sons.

EDCS-27800916.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Zises Mucazenis et Bendina Bitua posuorunt sic titulum to filio suo Tarsanio vixit annos V\n
EDCS-27800916.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Zises Mucazenis and Bendina Bitua placed this marker for their son Tarsanius, who lived 5 years.
EDCS-27800916.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Tarsanius, a child who lived only 5 years, and was honored by his parents Zises Mucazenis and Bendina Bitua.

EDCS-27800919.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus HA IVO OFECL Maximo et Paterno consulibus Dis Manibus\n
EDCS-27800919.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, HA IVO OFECL. In the consulship of Maximo and Paterno. To the spirits of the underworld.
EDCS-27800919.summary: This is a funerary inscription that includes a reference to the consuls Maximo and Paterno, providing a date in the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-27800920.text_edition: \n Silvano Domino Titus Octavius Nonus posuit votum fecit T P\n
EDCS-27800920.translation: To the God Silvanus, Titus Octavius Nonus erected this, having made a vow. [---]
EDCS-27800920.summary: A private dedication to the god Silvanus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800921.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius Sergia Nicopoli Niger veteranus legionis I Italicae vixit annos LX Tiberius Claudius Zoticus libertus et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-27800921.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Nicopoli Niger, a veteran of the I Italica legion, lived 60 years. Tiberius Claudius Zoticus, his freedman and heir, saw to its construction.
EDCS-27800921.summary: A tombstone inscription for Tiberius Claudius Nicopoli Niger, a veteran, erected by his freedman and heir in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27800922.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Severo Severi filio buleutae vixit annos XLV Dentusucus Gerulonis filia coniunx et Silvanus et Marcus filii heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-27800922.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Severus, son of Severus, a member of the city council, lived 45 years. Dentusucus, daughter of Gerulo, his wife, and his sons Silvanus and Marcus, his heirs, saw to its construction.
EDCS-27800922.summary: A tombstone inscription for Severus, a council member, erected by his wife and sons, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800924.text_edition: \n Marcia Marci Demetriano karissimo coniux de suo sibi et filiis communibus et statuas posuit\n
EDCS-27800924.translation: Marcia to her dearest husband Demetrianus, son of Marcus, erected statues for himself and their common children, from her own funds.
EDCS-27800924.summary: A funerary monument erected by a wife, Marcia, for her husband Demetrianus and their children, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800925.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Miniciae Quintiae vixit annos et Caiis Miniciis Aquilae et Inpetrato filiis\n
EDCS-27800925.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Minicia Quintia lived [---] years, and to Gaius Minicius Aquila and Impetratus, her sons.
EDCS-27800925.summary: A tombstone inscription commemorating Minicia Quintia and her sons, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800926.text_edition: \n ------ p]udentissimo A CV APE C P M B\n
EDCS-27800926.translation: [---] most prudent [---] A CV APE C P M B
EDCS-27800926.summary: A fragmentary inscription that seems to record epithets and names, possibly part of a dedication or tombstone, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800927.text_edition: \n Quod bonum felix faustum Albino et Maximo consulibus nomina Bacchii vernaculorum per principes Caio sic Sentio sic Verano sic buleuta sic Tito sic Ulpio sic Her(ma sic sacerdos Caius Valerius Valens filii Valerianus et Valentianus arcarius Marcus Ulpius Iulianus frater Ulpius Dionysius librarius legionis archimystae Antonius Antoninus Titus Aelius Felix buleuta filius Titus Aelius Antonius ecdicus Valerius Festus filius Apollonius Lucius Geminius Herculanus buleuta pariator Aurelius Numisianus veteranus filius Aurelius Diophanes veteranus ex prae(toriano filius Aurelius Marcus decurio Montanus Herculani filius Montanus vixillarius sic Valerius Antonius filius Leo vixillarius sic Valerius Fronto filius Frontinus vixillarius sic Atronius Severus filius Aristo Valerianus Secundinus filius Aeternalis Arrius Herculanus buleuta filius Valentinus Aelius Rufinus filius Nausas Aemilius Crescens Balbius Valerianus Cocceius Superus filius Leontius Firmus Diophanti Iuvenis Herculani Aurelius Seneca filius Passar Rufinus Cauri Passer Prisci Claudius Herculanus Va Aelius Alexander qui et Pudens filius Augustalis Porcius Herculanus Trol Iulianus Crescens Antoni filius Aufidianus Aurelius Aurelianus Aelius Volussianus sic Numisius Felix Antonius Plotiani filius Plotianus Iulius Bonosus et Mucia Mucr Arrius Vitalis Luccius Herculanus Lup Valerius Longinianus et Manilius Senator Atronius Iuvinus et Varius Ulpianus Numisius Titi filius Numisianus Sabinianus Luci filius Sabinus Vivianus Gai Aurelius Apollinaris qui et Pistus Pistus Do Ianuarius Aureli Valens Crescentillionis Similis Valentis filius Proclus Titus Capitonis Troilus Margi Aurelius Diogenes Iulianus Mansueti filius ERGNIAE Marcianus Martini Aurelius Crescens Crescens Decebali Aurelius Alexander Aurelius Saturninus Valens Taranis Caius Sentinianus Marcus Antonius Secundus Victorinus Victoris Valerius Herculanus Thip Arrius Frontinus Primattia Marius Flavius Numisius Rufus Aelius Victor Aurelius Philinus Aurelius Priscus Arrius Rusticus Berilo Auluzeni Mucapor Menis Sabinus Alexandri Aurelius Varianus Aurelius Sulu Gemelli Castus Sabini Valerius cbusta Maximinus Flavi Mysiacus Bassi Valens Herotis Valerius Martialis Titianus Citti Valerius Alexander Valerius Cotria\n
EDCS-27800927.translation: May it be good, fortunate, and auspicious. In the consulship of Albinus and Maximus, the names of the native Bacchii, through their leaders Caius Sic Sentius, Sic Veranus, Sic Buleuta, Sic Titus, Sic Ulpius, Sic Her(ma), Sic the priest Caius Valerius Valens, his sons Valerianus and Valentianus, the treasurer Marcus Ulpius Iulianus, his brother Ulpius Dionysius, scribe of the Archimystae legion, Antonius Antoninus, Titus Aelius Felix, council member, his son Titus Aelius Antonius, the herald Valerius Festus, his son Apollonius, Lucius Geminius Herculanus, council member, pariator, Aurelius Numisianus, veteran, his son Aurelius Diophanes, ex-praetorian, his son Aurelius Marcus, decurion, Montanus, son of Herculanus, Montanus, vexillarius, Caius Valerius Antonius, son of Leo, vexillarius, Caius Valerius Fronto, son of Frontinus, vexillarius, Atronius Severus, son of Aristo, Valerianus Secundinus, son of Aeternalis, Arrius Herculanus, council member, his son Valentinus, Aelius Rufinus, son of Nausas, Aemilius Crescens, Balbius Valerianus, Cocceius Superus, son of Leontius, Firmus, son of Diophantus, Iuvenis, son of Herculanus, Aurelius Seneca, son of Passer, Rufinus Cauri, Passer Prisci, Claudius Herculanus, Va Aelius Alexander who is also Pudens, his son Augustalis, Porcius Herculanus, Trol Iulianus Crescens, son of Antonius, Aufidianus Aurelius Aurelianus, Aelius Volussianus, Sic Numisius Felix, Antonius Plotianus, son of Plotianus, Iulius Bonosus and Mucia Mucr, Arrius Vitalis, Luccius Herculanus, Lup Valerius Longinianus and Manilius Senator, Atronius Iuvinus and Varius Ulpianus, Numisius, son of Titus, Numisianus Sabinianus, son of Lucius, Sabinus Vivianus, Gaius Aurelius Apollinaris who is also Pistus, Pistus Do Ianuarius, Aurelius Valens, Crescentillionis Similis, son of Valentis, Proclus Titus, son of Capito, Troilus Margi, Aurelius Diogenes, Iulianus Mansueti, son of ERGNIAE, Marcianus Martini, Aurelius Crescens, Crescens, son of Decebalus, Aurelius Alexander, Aurelius Saturninus Valens, Taranis, Caius Sentinianus, Marcus Antonius Secundus, Victorinus, son of Victoris, Valerius Herculanus, Thip Arrius Frontinus, Primattia Marius Flavius Numisius Rufus, Aelius Victor, Aurelius Philinus, Aurelius Priscus, Arrius Rusticus, Berilo Auluzeni, Mucapor Menis, Sabinus Alexandri, Aurelius Varianus, Aurelius Sulu, Gemelli Castus, son of Sabinus, Valerius cbusta, Maximinus, son of Flavi, Mysiacus Bassi, Valens Herotis, Valerius Martialis, Titianus Citti, Valerius Alexander, Valerius Cotria.
EDCS-27800927.summary: A long list of names, including officials and individuals, recorded under the consulship of Albinus and Maximus, likely related to a civic or religious event or procession in the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-27800928.text_edition: \n Imperatores Caessares Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus et Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pii Felices invicti Auggusti et Flavius Valerius Constantius et Galeriusus Valerius Maximianus nobbilissimi Caessares Germanici maximi V Sarmatici maximi IIII Persici maximi II Brittannici maximi post debellatas hostium gentes confirmata orbi suo tranquillitate profuturum in aeternum rei publicae praesidium constituerunt\n
EDCS-27800928.translation: The Emperors and Caesars Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augusti, and Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximian, the Most Noble Caesars, greatest conquerors of the Germans, great conquerors of the Sarmatians twice over, greatest conquerors of the Persians, greatest conquerors of the Britons, after subduing the enemies' tribes and confirming peace throughout the world, have established a lasting protection for the Republic.
EDCS-27800928.summary: An imperial proclamation by Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius, issued after military victories to signify peace and good governance, dating to the Tetrarchy period (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-27800929.text_edition: \n Lucio Annio Luci filio Quirina Italico Honorato consuli sodali Hadrianali legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Moesiae inferioris curatori operum publicorum curatori Neapolis et Atellae praefecto aerarii militaris legato legionis XIII geminae iuridico per Flaminiam et Umbriam curatori viae Lavicanae sic et Latinae veteris praetori qui ius dixit inter civis sic et civis sic et peregrinos tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Achaiae seviro turmarum equitum quattuorviro viarum curandarum Flavius Severianus decurio alae I Atectorum Severianae candidatus eius\n
EDCS-27800929.translation: Flavius Severianus, a decurion of the I Atectorum Severiana ala, his candidate, to Lucius Annius Italicus Honoratus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, consul, companion of Hadrian, legate of the Emperor pro praetore of the province of Lower Moesia, curator of public works, curator of Neapolis and Atella, prefect of the military treasury, legate of the XIII Gemina legion, legal official for Etruria and Umbria, curator of the Via Labicana and the old Via Latina, praetor who administered justice among citizens and foreigners, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Achaia, member of the board of four for managing roads, etc.
EDCS-27800929.summary: An extensive career summary inscribed for Lucius Annius Italicus Honoratus, a high-ranking imperial official, likely erected by a subordinate, dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27800930.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Aureliae Valeriae virgini dextratae annis III mensibus VIIII diebus XVII filiae Aureli Herculani viri egregii ducenari qui vixit annis XLII mensibus VIII diebus XVI nepotiae pientissimae Ulpius Valerius Aurelianus vir egregius centenarius et Titinia Mansueta stolata femina viator resiste et lege nihil ultra crudelius hoc monumento cernere potes\n
EDCS-27800930.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ulpia Aurelia Valeria, a virgin dedicated [to the gods], 3 years, 9 months, and 17 days old, daughter of Aurelius Herculanus, a distinguished man of ducenary rank, who lived 42 years, 8 months, and 16 days; a most devoted grandchild. Ulpius Valerius Aurelianus, a distinguished man of centenarian rank, and Titinia Mansueta, a draped woman, arranged this. Traveler, stop and read: you can see nothing more cruel than this monument.
EDCS-27800930.summary: A tombstone inscription for a young girl, Ulpia Aurelia Valeria, and her grandfather Aurelius Herculanus, expressing profound grief, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27800931.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Catiliae Respectae domo Ratiaria ob memoriam Semproniae Rufinae filiae eius et Allidi Secundini nepotis eius Cai Allidi Rufinus et Rufus aviae suae et matri et fratri dulcissimis per Allidium Rufum patrem suum faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-27800931.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For the memory of Catilia Respecta, of Ratiaria by birth, Sempronia Rufina her daughter, and Allidius Secundinus her grandson. Caius Allidius Rufinus and Rufus had this erected by their father, Allidius Rufus, to their most beloved grandmother, mother, and brother.
EDCS-27800931.summary: A funerary monument, likely a tombstone, set up by sons to commemorate their mother, grandmother, and brother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800932.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Martiali et Ulpiae Valentinae Titus Flavius Mansuetus parentibus posuit\n
EDCS-27800932.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Titus Flavius Mansuetus erected this for his parents, Titus Flavius Martialis and Ulpia Valentina.
EDCS-27800932.summary: A funerary monument, likely a tombstone, dedicated by a son to his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800934.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae sacrum pro salute Imperatoris [Marci] Aureli Severi [Al]exandri Pii Felicis A[ugusti] []C EresiusEresiussianus sing(ularis)? co(n)s(ularis) votum solvit posuit Maximo et Aeliano consulibus\n
EDCS-27800934.translation: Sacred to Diana Regina. For the safety of the Emperor [Marcus] Aurelius Severus [Al]exander Pius Felix Au[gustus]. C. Eresius Eresiussianus, singularis consularis, fulfilled his vow and dedicated this under the consulship of Maximinus and Aelianus.
EDCS-27800934.summary: A dedication to the goddess Diana, likely on a shrine or altar, made by a high-ranking official for the emperor's well-being, dating to the reign of Severus Alexander (222-235 AD).

EDCS-27800935.text_edition: \n Dianae aeternae pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Flavius Antonius Romanus beneficiarius consularis legionis XI Claudiae Antoninianae Imperatore domino nostro Antonino et Advento II consulibus\n
