EDCS-27800935.translation: To eternal Diana. For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus. Flavius Antonius Romanus, a beneficiarius of a consular, of the XI Claudia Antoniniana legion, during the consulship of our lord Antoninus and Adventus, the second time.
EDCS-27800935.summary: A dedication to Diana, likely on a shrine or altar, made by a soldier for the emperor's well-being, dating to the reign of Severus Alexander (as Marcus Aurelius Antoninus) and the consulship of 224 AD.

EDCS-27800936.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii et Marci Aureli Veri Caesaris Publius Valerius Clemens et Lucius Cominius Valens veterani legionis V Macedonicae magistri et Lucius Valerius Crispus aedilis de suo posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800936.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and Caesar Marcus Aurelius Verus. Publius Valerius Clemens and Lucius Cominius Valens, veterans of the V Macedonica legion, as masters, and Lucius Valerius Crispus, as aedile, set this up at their own expense.
EDCS-27800936.summary: A dedication to Jupiter, likely on a public monument or shrine, made by veterans and officials for the well-being of emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Verus, dating to their co-reign (c. 161 AD).

EDCS-27800940.text_edition: \n I(ovi)? O(ptimo)? M(aximo) pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti Caio Valerio Pudente veterano legionis V Macedonicae et Marco Ulpio Leontio magistris canabensium et Tuccio Aeliano aedile donum dant veterani et cives Romani consistentes ad canabas legionis V Macedonicae\n
EDCS-27800940.translation: To [Jupiter] Optimus Maximus. For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus. Caio Valerio Pudente, veteran of the V Macedonica legion, and Marco Ulpio Leontio, masters of the camp followers, and Tuccio Aelianus, aedile, the veterans and Roman citizens residing at the camp followers' settlement of the V Macedonica legion give this gift.
EDCS-27800940.summary: A dedication to Jupiter, likely on a shrine within a military settlement, made by veterans and civilians for the emperor's well-being and naming local officials, dating to the reign of Hadrian (117-138 AD).

EDCS-27800942.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae Tiberius Claudius Celsus primus pilus legionis V Macedonicae\n
EDCS-27800942.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country. Tiberius Claudius Celsus, primus pilus of the V Macedonica legion.
EDCS-27800942.summary: A dedication, likely a statue base or honorary monument, to Emperor Antoninus Pius, set up by a high-ranking centurion, dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-27800943.text_edition: \n I(ovi)? O(ptimo)? M(aximo) pro salute Imperatorum Antonini et Veri Augustorum legionis V Macedonicae Ialli Bassi legati Augusti pro praetore Marti Veri legati Augusti Publius Aelius Quintianus Magni filius |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae Aelius Quintianus OTSC CIM et posuit\n
EDCS-27800943.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. For the safety of the Emperors Antoninus and Verus Augusti. From the V Macedonica legion. Iallus Bassus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, and Martius Verus, imperial legate. Publius Aelius Quintianus, son of Magnus, centurion of the V Macedonica legion. Aelius Quintianus, son of Magnus, and others placed this.
EDCS-27800943.summary: A dedication to Jupiter, likely on a shrine, made by high-ranking military officers and a centurion for the well-being of the co-emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, dating to their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-27800949.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Pompeiano clarissimo viro bis consuli Caius Valerius Firmus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae\n
EDCS-27800949.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Pompeianus, most distinguished man, twice consul. Gaius Valerius Firmus, centurion of the I Italica legion.
EDCS-27800949.summary: An honorary dedication, likely a statue base, to a prominent senator and general, Tiberius Claudius Pompeianus, by a centurion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800951.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XVIII consuli III patri patriae veterani qui militare coeperunt Annio et Atilio consulibus et Palma et Tullo consulibus missi honesta missione sub Iulio Maiore legato Augusti pro praetore et Plotio Iuliano legato Augusti cohors I Sempronius Valens ex architecto Pescennius I Flavius Antoninus Valerius Valens Iulius Proculus ex immuni Vibius Priscus Antistius Vetus Iulius Imu-- tenius Lupus Sabinus Valerius Valens ex imaginifero Iulius Longinus Publius Meaas Valerius Proculus Attius Alexander ex optione Tiberius Capito Pomponius Maximus <C=G>aesonius Niger Iulius Valens Iulius Valens Septimius Celer Antonius Silvanus ex corniculario Fonteius Capito Claudius Maximus Valerius Firmus Cassius Longinus Claudius Maximus Memmius Valens Valerius Ianuarius Cassius Valens Iulius Maximus Iulius Philippus Valens l Marcus Pontianus cohors II Sosius Longinus Fera Longinus Cornelius Bassus Valerius Victor Valerius Longinus Iulius Macrinus Publius Atalus ex beneficiario Memmius Capito Naevius Pudens Iulius Roscius Valerius Maximus Pomponius Iu Iulius Severus ex equite Cassius Longinus Valerius Libo Egnatius Valens Barbius Cantaber cohors III Lucilius Pu Iulius Licinianus Valerius Vale Helvius Cantaber Domitius Do Iulius Fi Caenius Nemonius Valens Valerius Rufus ex equite Valerius Valens ex Valerius Maximus Baebius Severus Sempronius Rufus Iulius Valens cohors IIII Iulius Aquila Cassius Germanus pe Valens Gellius Germanus ex equite Claudius Crispus Publius Maius Severus ex corniculario Verus Bassus Leontianus Velleius Capito Aufonius Valentinus cohors V ong Secundus V Alexander us Valens ex beneficiario procuratoris Crispus ex beneficiario legati Philippus Saturninus ex equite lus us ex beneficiario Secundus s ex equite nus s Valens Valens s ex actario Saturninus Saturninus ter ex beneficiario gnel Intimus ex beneficiario procuratoris Varianus us us ex signifero ont Pror niao Antonius Rex optione Iulius Gemellus ex beneficiario legati Licinius s Valerius Mo--- ex? immuni Flavius Caeler n s es Iulius Flavius Pont Valerius Ism Iulius Vibianus Publius Anconteius Antonius Antonius Statorius No Aemilius Servilius Aulus Vegetus Valerius Mars Titus Flavius Un Petronius ex beneficiario legati Flavius EI Valerius Flavius r Bul Ps Truccius Secundus Atisius Valens Iulius Valens Sulpicius Valentinus Iulius Balens sic Iulius Severus Iulius Longinus Iulius Genianus cohors VIIII Iulius v Valerius Longinus Antonius Pro Aemilius Geminus at Philippus ex beneficiario Valerius Severus Iulius Sabinus Iulius Proculus Catonius Secundus Iulius Longinus Iulius Vitulus Titus Flavius Magnus cohors X Lucretius Statutus Valerius Fronto Flavius Valens Messius Valens Publicius Tertius Aponius Moesicus Valerius Ma Papirius Mes Valerius Fronto Iulius Alexander Valerius Maximus Iulius Severus AVII Iulius Iub Mees Maeonius Val Severus Am Leonatus Iulius Valens Valentinus Iulius Candidus Iulius Nobilis D Geminus reliqui Antonius Eleuther Grattius Italicus Valerius Priscus Aufidius Iulianus Antonius Geminus alis optio retentus ad spem c ex aquilifero nD ex imaginifero Aquila ex bucinatore Pavius Felix s Pudens on Rufus us Valens Fronto Geta beneficiarius nq Valens e ex immuni genus ex equite us Proculus nus n Gemellus es ex equite us us ex beneficiario ex beneficiario legati tus us ex beneficiario procuratoris Mussianus ica Cassius Vi Terentius Gir Iulius Ponticus a Caesius Cabellius L Calpurnius ex equite Iulius Sap Antonius Crispus Antonius Proculus Iulius Germanus Valerius Pollio Claudius Secundus ex actario Marcius Silvanus <T=I>(itus) <F=E>lavius Valens ex beneficiario legati Valerius Clemens Numitorius Severus Valerius Aternus Nonius Saturninus Valerius Maximus Claudius Me Decimus Sextus Be Ruf Sext \n
EDCS-27800951.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country. The veterans who began their service under Annius and Atilius, consuls, and under Palma and Tullus, consuls, were honorably discharged under Julius Maior, imperial legate with praetorian authority, and Plotus Iulianus, imperial legate. Cohort I: Sempronius Valens, ex architecto; Pescennius I; Flavius Antoninus; Valerius Valens; Iulius Proculus, ex immuni; Vibius Priscus; Antistius Vetus; Iulius Imutenius Lupus; Sabinus Valerius Valens, ex imaginifero; Iulius Longinus; Publius Meaas; Valerius Proculus; Attius Alexander, ex optione; Tiberius Capito; Pomponius Maximus; Gaesonius Niger; Iulius Valens; Iulius Valens; Septimius Celer; Antonius Silvanus, ex corniculario; Fonteius Capito; Claudius Maximus; Valerius Firmus; Cassius Longinus; Claudius Maximus; Memmius Valens; Valerius Ianuarius; Cassius Valens; Iulius Maximus; Iulius Philippus Valens; Marcus Pontianus. Cohort II: Sosius Longinus; Fera Longinus; Cornelius Bassus; Valerius Victor; Valerius Longinus; Iulius Macrinus; Publius Atalus, ex beneficiario; Memmius Capito; Naevius Pudens; Iulius Roscius; Valerius Maximus; Pomponius; Iulius Severus, ex equite; Cassius Longinus; Valerius Libo; Egnatius Valens; Barbius Cantaber. Cohort III: Lucilius Pu; Iulius Licinianus; Valerius Vale; Helvius Cantaber; Domitius Do; Iulius Fi; Caenius Nemonius Valens; Valerius Rufus, ex equite; Valerius Valens; Valerius Maximus; Baebius Severus; Sempronius Rufus; Iulius Valens. Cohort IIII: Iulius Aquila; Cassius Germanus; Valens Gellius Germanus, ex equite; Claudius Crispus; Publius Maius Severus, ex corniculario; Verus Bassus; Leontianus; Velleius Capito; Aufonius Valentinus. Cohort V: Long Secundus; Alexander; Valens, ex beneficiario procuratoris; Crispus, ex beneficiario legati; Philippus Saturninus, ex equite; Valens; Valens, ex actario; Saturninus Saturninus; ex beneficiario; Intimus, ex beneficiario procuratoris; Varianus. Antonius Rex, optione; Iulius Gemellus, ex beneficiario legati; Licinius; Valerius Mo; Flavius Caeler. Cohort VI: Iulius Flavius Pont; Valerius Ism; Iulius Vibianus; Publius An; Antonius; Antonius Statorius; No; Aemilius Servilius; Aulus Vegetus; Valerius Mars; Titus Flavius; Petronius, ex beneficiario legati; Flavius EI; Valerius Flavius; Truccius Secundus; Atisius Valens; Iulius Valens; Sulpicius Valentinus; Iulius Balens; Iulius Severus; Iulius Longinus; Iulius Genianus. Cohort VIIII: Iulius; Valerius Longinus; Antonius Pro; Aemilius Geminus; Philippus, ex beneficiario; Valerius Severus; Iulius Sabinus; Iulius Proculus; Catonius Secundus; Iulius Longinus; Iulius Vitulus; Titus Flavius Magnus. Cohort X: Lucretius Statutus; Valerius Fronto; Flavius Valens; Messius Valens; Publicius Tertius; Aponius Moesicus; Valerius Ma; Papirius Mes; Valerius Fronto; Iulius Alexander; Valerius Maximus; Iulius Severus. AVII; Iulius Iub; Mees Maeonius; Val Severus; Am Leonatus; Iulius Valens; Valentinus; Iulius Candidus; Iulius Nobilis; Geminus; reliqui Antonius Eleuther; Grattius Italicus; Valerius Priscus; Aufidius Iulianus; Antonius Geminus; alis optio retentus ad spem; ex aquilifero; ex imaginifero; Aquila, ex bucinatore; Pavius Felix; Pudens; Rufus; Valens; Fronto Geta, beneficiarius; Valens, ex immuni; genus, ex equite; Proculus; Gemellus; ex equite; ex beneficiario; ex beneficiario legati; ex beneficiario procuratoris; Mussianus; Cassius Vi; Terentius Gir; Iulius Ponticus; Caesius Cabellius; L. Calpurnius, ex equite; Iulius Sap; Antonius Crispus; Antonius Proculus; Iulius Germanus; Valerius Pollio; Claudius Secundus, ex actario; Marcius Silvanus. Titus Flavius Valens, ex beneficiario legati; Valerius Clemens; Numitorius Severus; Valerius Aternus; Nonius Saturninus; Valerius Maximus; Claudius Me; Decimus Sextus; Rufus.
EDCS-27800951.summary: A large honorary monument dedicated to Emperor Hadrian by veterans of the V Macedonica legion, listing numerous soldiers from multiple cohorts who were granted an honorable discharge, dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-27800952.text_edition: \n LNVS PIVS EVOrius Valens Iuli(us) Valens \n
EDCS-27800952.translation: Lucius Pius Evorius Valens. Julius Valens.
EDCS-27800952.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucius Pius Evorius Valens, likely set up by Julius Valens, with no precise dating information available.

EDCS-27800954.text_edition: \n Lucio Antonio Luci filio Arnensi Felici Karthagine |(centurioni) legionis III Augustae |(centurioni) legionis X geminae |(centurioni) legionis I Italicae vixit annis LVIIII Didia Marcellina coniunx cum Antoniis Marcellina et Didiano filiis et heredibus bene merenti facere curaverunt\n
EDCS-27800954.translation: To Lucius Antonius Felix, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, centurion of the III Augusta legion, centurion of the X Gemina legion, centurion of the I Italica legion, lived 59 years. Didia Marcellina, his wife, with their children Marcellina and Didianus Antonius, and heirs, took care to erect this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-27800954.summary: This tombstone honors Lucius Antonius Felix, a centurion who served in three legions and lived to be 59; it was erected by his wife and children. Dating is not precisely inferable but likely Imperial period.

EDCS-27800955.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius Quirina Ulpianus domo Laodicea Syriae |(centurio) legionis X geminae et IIII Flaviae et XII fulminatae et III Cyrenaicae et X fretensis et II adiutricis et V Macedonicae vixit annis LVI heres sepulcrum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-27800955.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Ulpianus, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, from Laodicea in Syria, centurion of the X Gemina, IIII Flavia, XII Fulminata, III Cyrenaica, X Fretensis, II Adjutricis, and V Macedonica legions, lived 56 years. His heir took care to erect the tomb.
EDCS-27800955.summary: This epitaph commemorates Tiberius Claudius Ulpianus, a centurion who served in multiple legions and lived to 56, with his heir responsible for the tomb's construction. It is undated but likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800956.text_edition: \n filius Claudia Decimus ria |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae hic situs est Valerius Pudens |(centurio) legionis eiusdem heres et DVrius faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-27800956.translation: Here lies Claudius Decimus, son of Decimus, centurion of the V Macedonica legion. Valerius Pudens, centurion of the same legion, and his heir DVrius, took care to erect this.
EDCS-27800956.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Claudius Decimus, a centurion of the V Macedonica legion, with the monument erected by Valerius Pudens, also a centurion of the same legion and his heir. It is undated but likely Imperial period.

EDCS-27800957.text_edition: \n Caius Egnatius Cai filius Fabia Valens Ancyra vet decurio municipii Troesmensium Caius Egnatius Valens IV legionis eiusdem filius patri piissimo bene merenti fecit hic situs est\n
EDCS-27800957.translation: Caius Egnatius Valens, son of Caius, of the Fabia tribe, from Ancyra, former decurion of the municipium of Troesmensium. Caius Egnatius Valens, son of Caius, of the IV legion, erected this for his most dutiful father, who deserved well. Here he lies.
EDCS-27800957.summary: This monument was erected by Caius Egnatius Valens for his father, also named Caius Egnatius Valens, a former decurion and legionary, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800958.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Dizzace posuit filio pientissimo Valerio Valenti militi legionis V Macedonicae defuncto in expeditione Parthica militavit annis V vic(sit!) annis XXV et matri Attiae A-EA PF\n
EDCS-27800958.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Dizzace set this up for his most dutiful son, Valerius Valens, a soldier in the V Macedonica legion, who died on the Parthian expedition, served 5 years, lived 25 years, and for his mother Attia, who is alive and well.
EDCS-27800958.summary: This tombstone is dedicated by Julius Dizzace to his son Valerius Valens, a soldier of the V Macedonica legion who died during a Parthian campaign at the age of 25, and also honors his living mother Attia. The mention of the Parthian expedition suggests a dating within the context of Roman campaigns in that region.

EDCS-27800959.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Saturninus domo Oesci ex optione veteranus legionis V Macedonicae vivo se posuit cum Scribonia Melitine coniuge\n
EDCS-27800959.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Julius Saturninus, from Oesci, a veteran of the V Macedonica legion, from the rank of optio, set this up for himself while alive, along with his wife Scribonia Melitina.
EDCS-27800959.summary: This is a self-dedicated tombstone for Caius Julius Saturninus, a veteran opto of the V Macedonica legion, and his wife Scribonia Melitina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800960.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Urbicae filiae Gai Iuli Antonini |(centurionis) legionis XII fulminatae Gaius Iulius Theseus libertus Antonini o<b=P> pietatem facere curavit \n
EDCS-27800960.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Julius Antoninus, son of Gaius, centurion of the XII Fulminata legion. Gaius Julius Theseus, freedman of Antoninus, took care to provide this pious monument.
EDCS-27800960.summary: This epitaph is for Julia Urbica, daughter of Gaius Julius Antoninus, a centurion of the XII Fulminata legion, and was erected by Antoninus' freedman, Gaius Julius Theseus. It is undated but likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27800961.text_edition: \n Traianae legionis I Italicae legionis X fretensis legionis V Macedonicae vixit nnos L Petronia filia et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-27800961.translation: Lived 50 years. Petronia, his daughter and heir, took care to erect this for [him/her], [associated with the] Traiana [legion?], the I Italica legion, the X Fretensis legion, and the V Macedonica legion.
EDCS-27800961.summary: This is a tombstone for an individual who lived 50 years and was associated with multiple legions, erected by his daughter and heir Petronia. The legionary affiliations suggest an Imperial Roman date.

EDCS-27800962.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Thiumpo qui militavit in legione XI Claudia lectus in sacro comitatu lanciarius deinde protexit annis V missus praefectus legionis II Herculiae egit nnis II semissem et decessit vixit nnis XXXXV mensibus III diebus XI Aurelius \n
EDCS-27800962.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Thiumpus, who served in the XI Claudia legion, was selected for the sacred cohort as a lance-bearer, then served as prefect of the II Herculia legion for 2.5 years, and died, lived 45 years, 3 months, and 11 days. Aurelius [erected this].
EDCS-27800962.summary: This epitaph honors Valerius Thiumpus, who served in the XI Claudia legion, was a lance-bearer, and then prefect of the II Herculia legion, dying at age 45. The mention of specific legions and ranks points to the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-27800963.text_edition: \n ------ u]xor GEN ------ le]gionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis \n
EDCS-27800963.translation: Wife of GEN... of the Legio XI Claudia Pia Fidelis.
EDCS-27800963.summary: This is a funerary inscription for the wife of a soldier of the Legio XI Claudia Pia Fidelis, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800966.text_edition: \n MCO Lo decurioni quaestori municipii Troesmensium Iulius Saturninus quaestor municipii eiusdem optimo collegae\n
EDCS-27800966.translation: To Julius Saturninus, quaestor of this same municipality, the optimus colleague, Julius Saturninus, decurion and quaestor of the municipality of Troesmensium.
EDCS-27800966.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Julius Saturninus, a decurion and quaestor of the municipality of Troesmensium, by his colleague.

EDCS-27800967.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberia Claudia vixit nnos L Publiciius Viator augur municipii coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-27800967.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberia Claudia lived 50 years. Publicius Viator, augur of the municipality, for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-27800967.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberia Claudia, set up by her husband, Publicius Viator, an augur of the municipality, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800968.text_edition: \n Aulus Antonius Auli filius Papiria Valens Oesci vixit annis XXXX Antonia Tyrannis liberta et heres patrono bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27800968.translation: Aulus Antonius Valens, son of Aulus, of the Papiria tribe, from Oescus, lived 40 years. Antonia Tyrannis, freedwoman and heir, erected this for her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-27800968.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aulus Antonius Valens, erected by his freedwoman and heir, Antonia Tyrannis, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800969.text_edition: \n us Titi filius Velina Claudianus Planina vixit annum I mensibus sic IIII diem I \n
EDCS-27800969.translation: Claudianus, son of Titus, of the Velina tribe, from Planina, lived 1 year, 4 months, and 1 day.
EDCS-27800969.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudianus, recording his very young age at death, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800970.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Rascanius Fortunatus Pollia Faventia medicus annorum L hic situs est cui monumentum Rascania Phoebe et Titus Rascanius Eutychus heredes faciendum curaverunt hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-27800970.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Rascanius Fortunatus, doctor, aged 50, is buried here. Rascania Phoebe and Titus Rascanius Eutychus, his heirs, saw to it that this monument was built for him. This monument will not be inherited by an heir.
EDCS-27800970.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Rascanius Fortunatus, a doctor, erected by his heirs, with a clause stating the monument is not to be inherited further.

EDCS-27800971.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius F Teretina \n
EDCS-27800971.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius F. [from the] Teretina [tribe].
EDCS-27800971.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius F., identifying his tribe, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800974.text_edition: \n Antistius Zoticus vixit nnos XXXVI hic situs est Antistia Antonina marita eius et Stelea Atilia Fortunata mater heredes primi\n
EDCS-27800974.translation: Antistius Zoticus lived 36 years. He is buried here. Antistia Antonina, his wife, and Stelea Atilia Fortunata, his mother, the primary heirs.
EDCS-27800974.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Antistius Zoticus, erected by his wife and mother, who are identified as his heirs, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800975.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus memoriae Claudiae Hedistes Claudia Aglais mater\n
EDCS-27800975.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. In memory of Claudia Hedistes. Claudia Aglais, mother.
EDCS-27800975.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Hedistes, set up by her mother, Claudia Aglais, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800976.text_edition: \n QII Claudiae coniugi suae vixit annis XXX et Claudiae Iulianae filiae suae vixit annis V et Domitiae Matronae filiae suae vixit annis III hic sita est\n
EDCS-27800976.translation: To Claudia, his wife, lived 30 years, and to Claudia Juliana, his daughter, lived 5 years, and to Domitia Matrona, his daughter, lived 3 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-27800976.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a man's wife and two daughters, all deceased at young ages, likely set up by the husband/father, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27800979.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Iuventio Luci liberto Marcello qui et Faustus vixit annos LX\n
EDCS-27800979.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Iuventius Marcellus, freedman of Lucius, who was also called Faustus, lived 60 years.
EDCS-27800979.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Lucius Iuventius Marcellus, a freedman, who lived to be 60 years old.

EDCS-27800980.text_edition: \n LAR V Claudius MAus et Ulpius Claudius Auctus patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800980.translation: Claudius Maus and Ulpius Claudius Auctus dedicated this to their well-deserving father.
EDCS-27800980.summary: This dedication was set up by Claudius Maus and Ulpius Claudius Auctus for their father, likely an altar or funerary monument.

EDCS-27800984.text_edition: \n SICNATV ITE SVM pontifici maximo patri patriae P\n
EDCS-27800984.translation: To Pontifex Maximus, Father of the Fatherland. Go and fulfill your destiny.
EDCS-27800984.summary: This fragment seems to be part of a dedication or honorific inscription to a Pontifex Maximus and Father of the Fatherland, with an obscure concluding phrase.

EDCS-27800985.text_edition: \n vixit annos Caius Iulius Pr decurio alae II Aravacorum et Caius Iulius Primus beneficiarius procuratoris patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800985.translation: Caius Iulius Pr, decurion of the II Aravacorum cavalry ala, and Caius Iulius Primus, beneficiarius of a procurator, set this up for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-27800985.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by two individuals, Caius Iulius Pr and Caius Iulius Primus, to their father, possibly a military man.

EDCS-27800987.text_edition: \n Imperatori Tito Caesari pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae\n
EDCS-27800987.translation: To the Emperor Titus Caesar, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power, Father of the Fatherland.
EDCS-27800987.summary: This is an honorific inscription or title for Emperor Titus Caesar, listing his important offices.

EDCS-27800988.text_edition: \n Bono Eventui legionis I Italicae Marcus Maesius Geminus Bononia primus pilus donum dedit Mamertino et Rufo\n
EDCS-27800988.translation: Marcus Maesius Geminus, primus pilus of the I Italica legion, from Bononia, gave this gift to Mamertinus and Rufus.
EDCS-27800988.summary: This is a dedication of a gift by Marcus Maesius Geminus, a senior centurion (primus pilus) of the I Italica legion, to two individuals, Mamertinus and Rufus.

EDCS-27800989.text_edition: \n Dis Militaribus Genio Virtuti Aquilae Sanctae Signisque legionis I Italicae Severianae Marcus Aurelius Iustus domo Horrei sic Margensis sic municipii Moesiae superioris ex CCCtrecenario primus pilus donum dedit dedicatum XII Kalendas Octobres Iuliano II et Crispino consulibus per Annium Italicum legatum Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-27800989.translation: To the Divine Soldiers, to Virtue, to the Sacred Eagle, and to the Standards of the I Severiana Italica Legion. Marcus Aurelius Iustus, from Horreum Margi in the province of Moesia Superior, a primus pilus from the CCC (trecenarium?) unit, gave this gift, dedicated on the twelfth day before the Kalends of October, during the consulship of Iulianus II and Crispinus, through Annius Italicvs, legatus Augusti pro praetore.
EDCS-27800989.summary: This is a complex dedication by Marcus Aurelius Iustus, a primus pilus and soldier from Moesia Superior, to various military deities and standards, dated to the consulship of Iulianus II and Crispinus.

EDCS-27800991.text_edition: \n Donatus Gal miles legionis I Italicae vixit nnos XXXII militavit nnos XIIII hic situs est Lucius Attius Maxsimus sic centuria Iuli Verecundi heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-27800991.translation: Donatus Gal, a soldier of the I Italica legion, lived 32 years and served 14 years; he is buried here. Lucius Attius Maxsimus, heir of the century of Iulius Verecundus, saw to its construction.
EDCS-27800991.summary: This tombstone records the life and service of Donatus Gal, a soldier of the I Italica legion, and its construction by his heir, Lucius Attius Maxsimus.

EDCS-27800992.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Victor beneficiarus semestris stipendiorum XXIIII vivus sibi fecit et Flaviae Viviae filiae et Iuliae Longinae coniugi et Flaviis Vero et Valerio et Vitali filiis bene merentibus\n
EDCS-27800992.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Flavius Victor, a beneficiarius who served 22 years and 6 months, made this for himself while alive, and for his daughter Flavia Vivia, his wife Iulia Longina, and his sons Flavii Verus, Valerius, and Vitalis, who were well-deserving.
EDCS-27800992.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Flavius Victor for himself and his family, recording his military service as a beneficiarius.

EDCS-27800993.text_edition: \n Honori Caio Valerio Longiniano flamini quaestori aedili IIviro municipii Troesmensium Iulius Herculanus aedilis optimo socro\n
EDCS-27800993.translation: To the honored Caius Valerius Longinianus, flamen, quaestor, aedile, and duovir of the municipality of Troesmensium. Iulius Herculanus, aedile, [set this up for] his excellent father-in-law.
EDCS-27800993.summary: This inscription honors Caius Valerius Longinianus, a municipal official, and was dedicated by his son-in-law, Iulius Herculanus.

EDCS-27800994.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto V Imperatori Caesari Lucio Domitio Aureliano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo Germanico maximo bis consuli patri patriae proconsuli mille passus\n
EDCS-27800994.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, twice conqueror of Germany, twice consul, father of the fatherland, [this road measures] a thousand paces.
EDCS-27800994.summary: This inscription records the completion of a road, likely commissioned by Emperor Gordian III and possibly dedicated or measured during the reign of Emperor Aurelian.

EDCS-28500114.text_edition: \n Iuliae Domnae Augustae matri castrorum nautae universi Danuvii ex re publica sua sub cura Luci Iuli Faustiniani legati Augustorum nnostrorum numini m(aiestatique)? e(ius)? ---\n
EDCS-28500114.translation: To Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camps, from all the boatmen of the Danube, at their own expense, under the supervision of Lucius Julius Faustinianus, legate of our Emperors.
EDCS-28500114.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Julia Domna, mother of the camps, by the boatmen of the Danube, likely during the reign of Caracalla or Severus.

EDCS-28500118.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Valens veteranus ex ala ex singularibus vixit annis LXV Iulia Mansueta marito bene mernti posuit\n
EDCS-28500118.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Julius Valens, a veteran from an auxiliary cavalry unit, served among the elite guards, lived for 65 years. Julia Mansueta set this up for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-28500118.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julius Valens, a veteran, set up by his wife Julia Mansueta, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500119.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus \xce\x93 Cethithis Didi viva suRTO ET \n
EDCS-28500119.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Didi, daughter of Cethithis, while alive...
EDCS-28500119.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a woman named Didi, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28500120.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Augusti et Iuliae Dominae matri castrorum Polydeuces Theophili et Lucius Kapito et Flavius Reginus sacerdotes Iovis Dolicheni votum posuerunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-28500120.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and Julia Domna, mother of the camps. Polydeuces son of Theophilus, Lucius Kapito, and Flavius Reginus, priests of Jupiter Dolichenus, willingly and deservedly set up this votive offering.
EDCS-28500120.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus by priests for the safety of Emperor Caracalla and Julia Domna, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500121.text_edition: \n DOMND vixit nnos LX Gaudentius filius vixit nnos XVIII Theodora filia vixit nnos XX Maximina filia vixit nnos V Bona carissima coniux cum natibus sic suis Vitalem Valentino Cumenio Valente et Cyrilla bene merito pari et patri titulo sic posuerunt\n
EDCS-28500121.translation: DOMND lived 60 years. Gaudentius, son, lived 18 years. Theodora, daughter, lived 20 years. Maximina, daughter, lived 5 years. Bona, dearest wife, with her children, set this tomb for their vital father Valentino Cumeni Valens and for herself, deservedly and equally, with this inscription.
EDCS-28500121.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a family, recording the ages of the deceased wife Bona and three children, set up by the husband Valentino Cumeni Valens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500122.text_edition: \n EF fecit et statuam posuit voto suscepto\n
EDCS-28500122.translation: He made and set up a statue, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28500122.summary: This is a brief inscription recording the erection of a statue in fulfillment of a vow, the dedicant and deity are not specified.

EDCS-28500125.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Nervae filio Nervae Traiano optimo Augusto Germanico Dacico Parthico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXI imperatori XII consuli VI patri patriae res publica Tomitanorum Quinto Roscio Murena Coelio Pompeio Falcone legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-28500125.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Trajan Optimus Augustus, Germanicus, Dacicus, Parthicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 21st time, acclaimed emperor for the 12th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the fatherland. The community of Tomis, under Quintus Roscius Murena Coelius Pompeius Falco, legate of the emperor with praetorian power.
EDCS-28500125.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Trajan, recording his titles and achievements, set up by the community of Tomis under the governor Quintus Roscius Murena Coelius Pompeius Falco, likely from around 117 CE.

EDCS-28500127.text_edition: \n Flavius Ursus filius Quinti Mestri quondam Bonouci praefectus a patre anno II factus annos XVI provitus miles officialis Ladici praefecti stipendiorum IIII vixit annos XX et sinmestrum sic et Cucemene sic matronam\n
EDCS-28500127.translation: Flavius Ursus, son of Quintus Mestrius Bonoucus, appointed prefect by his father in his second year, served for 16 years, served as an aide to the prefect of supplies, Ladicus, for 4 years, lived for 20 years and six months. And Cucemene, the matron...
EDCS-28500127.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph for Flavius Ursus, recording his military career and age, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28500131.text_edition: \n nata T<i=L>o Ulpi Filippi filia vixit nnos XLIX Marcus Aurelius Pretiosus vivus coniugi pientissimae hoc sepulc<r>hum sic emit \n
EDCS-28500131.translation: Born... daughter of Ulpius Philippus, lived 49 years. Marcus Aurelius Pretiosus bought this tomb while alive for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-28500131.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Marcus Aurelius Pretiosus purchased a tomb for his wife, who lived to be 49, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500134.text_edition: \n ------] a Vari Trophimi coniunx Claudius Trophimus filius eorum hic siti sunt Claudius Varius Trophimus infelix pater\n
EDCS-28500134.translation: Here lie Vari Trophimi's wife, Claudius Trophimus, their son; Claudius Varius Trophimus, the unhappy father.
EDCS-28500134.summary: This is a funerary inscription for members of the family of Claudius Varius Trophimus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500143.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XI pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate II<I> et [Publius Septimius Geta] [Caesar Augustus] restituerunt per Caium Ovinium Tertullum legatum pro praetore\n
EDCS-28500143.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 11th time, Father of the Country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, and [Publius Septimius Geta] Caesar Augustus restored [this] through Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, legate with praetorian authority.
EDCS-28500143.summary: This is a building inscription recording the restoration of a public work by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, with Geta mentioned, under the administration of the legate Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500146.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus consul II tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XI pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Anton<in>us Augustus tribunicia potestate II et Publius Septimius [Ge]ta Caesar Augustus restituerunt per Caium Ovinium Tertullum legatum pro praetore milia passuum X\n
EDCS-28500146.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, consul for the 2nd time, with tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 11th time, Father of the Country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, and Publius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus restored [this] through Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, legate with praetorian authority, [for] 10 miles.
EDCS-28500146.summary: This building inscription documents the restoration of 10 miles of road by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, through the legate Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500147.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar [Caius Iulius] Verus M[aximi][nus] Pius Felix Invictus Augustus et Caius Iulius Verus Maxi[mu]s nobilissimus Caesar restituerunt per Flavium Lucillianum legatum pro praetore milia passuum\n
EDCS-28500147.translation: Emperor Caesar [Gaius Julius] Verus Maxi[minus] Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Gaius Julius Verus Maxi[mu]s, the most noble Caesar, restored [this] through Flavius Lucillianus, legate with praetorian authority, [for] ... miles.
EDCS-28500147.summary: This building inscription records the restoration of a road over a specified distance by Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximus, through the legate Flavius Lucillianus, likely dating to the period 235-238 CE.

EDCS-28500150.text_edition: \n ------ tri]bunicia potestate patri patriae proconsuli Caio Pe[[]] [[]] legato Augusti pro praetore milia passuum\n
EDCS-28500150.translation: ...with tribunician power, Father of the Country, proconsul, through Gaius Pe[...], legate of Augustus with praetorian authority, for ... miles.
EDCS-28500150.summary: This is a fragment of a building inscription, possibly recording the completion of a road for a certain distance by a proconsul and a legate of Augustus, the date is indeterminate.

EDCS-28500151.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Valeriano Pio Felici invicto Augusto et G Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Gallieno Pio Felici invicto Augusto et Publio Licinio Incnatio sic Cornelio Valeriano Caesari Pio Felici invicto Augusto milia passuum XVIII\n
EDCS-28500151.translation: For Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and for Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and for Publius Licinius Incnatio sic Cornelius Valerianus Caesar Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, 18 miles.
EDCS-28500151.summary: This inscription records the construction or repair of 18 miles of road, dedicated to Emperors Valerian I, Gallienus, and their son Saloninus (possibly denoted by Incnatio sic), dating to the period 253-260 CE.

EDCS-28500153.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Caessaribus Caio Aurelio Valerio Diocletiano et Marco Aurelio Valerio Maximiano et Flavio Valerio Constantio et Galerio Valerio Maximiano milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-28500153.translation: For the Emperors Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian and Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximian, 4 miles.
EDCS-28500153.summary: This inscription records the construction or repair of 4 miles of road, dedicated to the Tetrarchic emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius I, and Galerius, likely dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-28500155.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius [Iulius] [Verus Maximi][nus] Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus maximus Dacicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III imperator V consul proconsul et [Caius Iulius Verus] [Maximus nobilis]simus Caesar Germanicus maximus Dacicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus filius eius miliaria nova et vias et pontes distruct[as! per ------\n
EDCS-28500155.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius [Julius] [Verus Maximi][nus] Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, Imperator for the 5th time, consul, proconsul, and [Gaius Julius Verus] [Maximus, the most nobilis]simus Caesar, Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, his son, [built] new milestones and roads and destroyed bridges, through ------
EDCS-28500155.summary: This building inscription details the construction of new milestones and roads and the repair of bridges by Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximus, likely dating to 236-238 CE.

EDCS-28500156.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII pater patriae consul III ab III Protomis milia passuum XXVII\n
EDCS-28500156.translation: For Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, Father of the Country, consul for the 3rd time, from the 3rd Protomis, 27 miles.
EDCS-28500156.summary: This inscription records the construction or repair of 27 miles of road, dedicated to Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to the period 133-134 CE.

EDCS-28500161.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus II imperator Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis XVI cons(ul) III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Armeniacus Augustus tribuniciae potestatis II consul II divi Pii Titi Antonini fili divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes a Callatide milia passuum VII\n
EDCS-28500161.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Imperator for the 2nd time, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Armeniacus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, from Callatida, 7 miles.
EDCS-28500161.summary: This building inscription records the construction or repair of 7 miles of road by Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, dating to the period 163-164 CE.

EDCS-28500176.text_edition: \n Herculi pro salute Titi Vitrasi Pollionis legati Augusti pro praetore Lucius Messius Primus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae frumentarius\n
EDCS-28500176.translation: To Hercules, for the health of Titus Vitrasius Pollio, imperial legate pro praetore, Lucius Messius Primus, a quartermaster of the I Italica Legion.
EDCS-28500176.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hercules by a legionary quartermaster for the health of a provincial governor, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28500177.summary: This is a simple epitaph for a veteran named Vinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500178.text_edition: \n Dianae Invictae Apollonius Diomedis votum solvit libens laetus\n
EDCS-28500178.translation: Apollonius son of Diomedes willingly and joyfully fulfilled his vow to Diana Invicta.
EDCS-28500178.summary: This is a votive inscription by Apollonius to Diana Invicta, recording the fulfillment of a vow, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500179.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Titus Flavius Felix singularis consularis cum Aelia Antonia coniuge aram cum sigillo ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-28500179.translation: Titus Flavius Felix, a singularis (bodyguard) of a consular official, with his wife Aelia Antonia, set up this altar with a small image, according to their vow.
EDCS-28500179.summary: This inscription records a family's votive offering of an altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500180.text_edition: \n ------ Titi Flavi] Longini legati Augusti pro praetore vexillatio legionis XI Claudiae sub cura Flavi Maximi |(centurionis) legionis eiusdem Severo et Sabiniano beneficarius consularis Ulpius consulibus Alexander principales tesserarius Iulius Aeternalis tubicen Aurelius Postumus cornice<n=M> Valerius Rufus medicus Aurelius Artemo immunes venatores Iulius Longinus et Flavius Valerius cohortis I Calpurnius Tertianus Valerius Felix Flavius Valens Aurelius Pedo Flavius Hercla Aurelius Tiberinus Iulius Marcus Aurelius Cerfonius cohortis II Aurelius Appianus Flavius Reginus Aurelius Chaireas Valerius Fronto Iulius Claudianus cohortis III Iulius Hortensis Aelius Martial<i=E>s Valerius Valens Antonius Valens Pontius Pontianus Valerius Antonius Valerius Rufus Claudius Ianuarius cohortis IIII Aelius Paulus Aurelius Germanus Aurelius Sanctus Valerius Maximus Antonius Valens Iulius Valens Valerius Longus Ulpius Bassus Flavius Primus cohortis V Aelius Apollodorus Aelius Sabinus Iulius Flaccus Aurelius Helenus Aurelius Artemo Cocceius Longus Atilius Crispus Aurelius Quadratus Aelius Apelles Flavius Alexander Iulius Nigrinus Aelius Antullinus Aelius Victorinus Cervius Maximus Valerius Firmus Aelius Flavinus Petronius Valens Aurelius Longinus Valerius Valens Iulius Alexander Aurelius Agathocles Iulius Capito Umidius Quadratus Mummius Celer Tri Valens Flavius Tertius Aurelius Tertullinus Flavius Pontianus Mummius Niger Valerius Valens Flavius Longinus Iulius V Aurelius Vindex Aurelius Decimus Quintus Valerius E decurio Pomponius Herculanus ex equite legionis XI Claudiae \n
EDCS-28500180.translation: ------ of Titus Flavius Longinus, imperial legate pro praetore. A detachment of the XI Claudia Legion, under the supervision of Flavius Maximus, (centurion) of the same legion, Severus and Sabinianus, beneficiaries of the consular official, Ulpius and Alexander being consuls, the principales Alexander, the tesserarius Julius Aeternalis, the tubicen Aurelius Postumus, the cornice(n) Valerius Rufus, the medicus Aurelius Artemo, the immunes, hunters: Julius Longinus and Flavius Valerius of the I Cohort; Calpurnius Tertianus, Valerius Felix, Flavius Valens, Aurelius Pedo, Flavius Hercla, Aurelius Tiberinus, Julius Marcus, Aurelius Cerfonius of the II Cohort; Aurelius Appianus, Flavius Reginus, Aurelius Chaireas, Valerius Fronto, Julius Claudianus of the III Cohort; Julius Hortensis, Aelius Martialis, Valerius Valens, Antonius Valens, Pontius Pontianus, Valerius Antonius, Valerius Rufus, Claudius Ianuarius of the IIII Cohort; Aelius Paulus, Aurelius Germanus, Aurelius Sanctus, Valerius Maximus, Antonius Valens, Julius Valens, Valerius Longus, Ulpius Bassus, Flavius Primus of the V Cohort; Aelius Apollodorus, Aelius Sabinus, Julius Flaccus, Aurelius Helenus, Aurelius Artemo, Cocceius Longus, Atilius Crispus, Aurelius Quadratus, Aelius Apelles, Flavius Alexander, Julius Nigrinus, Aelius Antullinus, Aelius Victorinus, Cervius Maximus, Valerius Firmus, Aelius Flavinus, Petronius Valens, Aurelius Longinus, Valerius Valens, Julius Alexander, Aurelius Agathocles, Julius Capito, Umidius Quadratus, Mummius Celer, Tri Valens, Flavius Tertius, Aurelius Tertullinus, Flavius Pontianus, Mummius Niger, Valerius Valens, Flavius Longinus, Julius V, Aurelius Vindex, Aurelius Decimus, Quintus Valerius E, decurio Pomponius Herculanus, ex equite of the XI Claudia Legion.
EDCS-28500180.summary: This extensive inscription lists personnel from a detachment of the XI Claudia Legion, including various ranks and roles, under the command of an imperial legate, likely dating to the consulship of Ulpius and Alexander (around 200-220 CE).

EDCS-28500181.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Antoninus cohortis III collectae portam praetoriam cum turre a fundamento sumptibus suis et instantia fabricavit Tusco et Basso consulibus\n
EDCS-28500181.translation: Publius Aelius Antoninus of the III Cohort, raised from collected troops, built the praetorian gate with its tower from the foundation at his own expense and under his supervision, when Tuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-28500181.summary: This inscription records the construction of a praetorian gate and tower by a soldier of the III Cohort at his own expense during the consulship of Tuscus and Bassus, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500182.text_edition: \n Lucius Antonius Proclus decurio Montanensium vixit annis L mensis sic tres Lucius Antonius Proclinus et Lucius Antonius Seneca et Lucius Antonius Cassianus et Lucius Antonius Mellior sic filii patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-28500182.translation: Lucius Antonius Proclus, a decurion of the Montanensii, lived 50 years and three months. Lucius Antonius Proclinus, Lucius Antonius Seneca, Lucius Antonius Cassianus, and Lucius Antonius Mellior, his sons, set this up for their deserving father.
EDCS-28500182.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by four sons for their father, Lucius Antonius Proclus, a decurion, who lived to be 50 years old, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500185.text_edition: \n Reste viator et lege Varsudicintis militavit IX vixit annis XL qui militavit in numero scutariorum cuius cara mater Susa fecit titulom sic viator\n
EDCS-28500185.translation: Stop, traveler, and read. Varsudicintis served for 9 years, lived for 40 years. He served in the unit of the scutarii (shield-bearers). His dear mother Susa made this inscription for the worthy traveler.
EDCS-28500185.summary: This epitaph honors a soldier named Varsudicintis who served for 9 years and lived for 40, erected by his mother, and is likely from a frontier province during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500186.text_edition: \n ------] Flavius Flavianus et Flavius Cassianus et Flavia Cara coniunx titulo BE vivo patres S merentibus inscribserunt sic\n
EDCS-28500186.translation: ------ Flavius Flavianus and Flavius Cassianus and Flavia Cara, his wife, inscribed this for their deserving parents while they were alive.
EDCS-28500186.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by children and a spouse for their parents during their lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500188.text_edition: \n Apollini sancto Antonius Zinenis princeps pro se et suis votum\n
EDCS-28500188.translation: To the holy Apollo, Antonius Zinenis, princeps, for himself and his family, a vow.
EDCS-28500188.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Apollo by Antonius Zinenis, a princeps, for himself and his household, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500189.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Myrizmo alumno Cosconius Ingenuus decurio posuit\n
EDCS-28500189.translation: To the divine spirits. Cosconius Ingenuus, a decurion, dedicated this to his pupil Myrizmus.
EDCS-28500189.summary: A funerary dedication from a decurion to his pupil, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500190.translation: Marcianus Lecti fulfilled his vow to Heron.
EDCS-28500190.summary: A votive dedication by Marcianus Lecti to Heron, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500191.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Magni Antonini Pii filio [divi Severi Pii nepoti Marco Aurelio Severo] [Alexandro Pio Felici Augusto pon]tifici maximo tribunicia potestate IX consuli III patri patriae [et Iuliae] [Mamaeae matri] Augusti nostri et castrorum balna cohortis II Flaviae Brittonum Alexandrianae a so<l=I>o restitutae sub Anicio Fausto Paulino legato Augusti pro praetore per Septimium Agathonicum praefectum \n
EDCS-28500191.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Pius Magnus Antoninus, [son of the deified Pius Severus, Marcus Aurelius Severus] [Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontiff supreme, with tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, and to Julia] [Mamaea, mother] of our Augustus, and of the camp bath of the 2nd Cohort of Flavians of the Britons of Alexandria, restored by the sun, under Anicius Faustus Paulinus, imperial legate with praetorian powers, by Septimius Agathonicus, prefect.
EDCS-28500191.summary: A dedication honouring Emperor Alexander Severus and his mother Julia Mamaea, detailing their titles and a restoration project, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500192.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii et Veri Caesaris templum et statuam civibus Romanis et consisstentibus in canabis Aelis legionis XI Claudiae Cnaeus Oppius Soterichus et Oppius Severus filius eius de suo fecerunt dedicatum est per Tiberium Claudium Saturninum legatum Augusti pro praetore Tiberio Claudio Iuliano legato Augusti\n
EDCS-28500192.translation: For Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and Caesar Verus, Cnaeus Oppius Soterichus and his son Oppius Severus built a temple and a statue at their own expense for the Roman citizens and those residing in the canabae of the 11th Claudian Legion; it was dedicated by Tiberius Claudius Saturninus, imperial legate with praetorian powers, and Tiberius Claudius Julianus, imperial legate.
EDCS-28500192.summary: A dedication of a temple and statue to Jupiter for the emperors' safety, built by two Roman citizens for the community of the 11th Legion's canabae, likely mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-28500193.text_edition: \n <In=M>victo Mithrae Cornelius Faustus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae \n
EDCS-28500193.translation: To Invictus Mithras. Cornelius Faustus, centurion of the 11th Claudian Legion.
EDCS-28500193.summary: A dedication to the god Mithras by a centurion of the 11th Legion, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500194.text_edition: \n Q(uintus)? Carmaeus Laetus Aquileia cornicularius tribuni legionis XI CLaudiae Quintus Carmaeus Felix patrono bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28500194.translation: Quintus Carmaeus Felix dedicated this to his patron Quintus Carmaeus Laetus, a cornicularius (an officer in the staff) of a tribune of the 11th Claudian Legion, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-28500194.summary: A dedication by a soldier to his patron, a cornicularius of the 11th Legion, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500195.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Marco militi legionis XI Claudiae vixit nnos XLV stipendiorum XXVI Aurelia Faustina coniux et heres una cum Valeriis Decibal M et Seicipere et Mamutzim et Macaria et Matidia filiis suis marito pientissimo sepulcrum \n
EDCS-28500195.translation: To the divine spirits. For Valerius Marcus, soldier of the 11th Claudian Legion, who lived 45 years and served 26 years. Aurelia Faustina, his wife and heir, together with their children Valerius Decibal, Seicipere, Mamutzim, Macaria, and Matidia, placed this tomb for their most devoted husband.
EDCS-28500195.summary: A funerary inscription for Valerius Marcus, a soldier of the 11th Legion, set up by his wife and children, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500196.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonio Valerio |(centurioni) classis II Flaviae Brittanniae atus A M\n
EDCS-28500196.translation: To the divine spirits. For Antonius Valerius, centurion of the 2nd Flavian Cohort of the fleet of Britannia, from M. A.
EDCS-28500196.summary: A funerary dedication to a centurion of a naval cohort stationed in Britain, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500199.text_edition: \n vixit annos LXXX NN princeps filius eius bene merenti posuit uxor Zudecitulp \n
EDCS-28500199.translation: He lived 80 years. NN, his son, dedicated this to the well-deserving deceased; his wife Zudecitulp.
EDCS-28500199.summary: A funerary inscription recording the age of the deceased and dedicated by his son and wife, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500200.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Bonitae vixit annos XIX Iulius Vitalis pater et Claudius Arbusculus et Iulius Iucundus fratres et Claudia Placida soror bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-28500200.translation: To the divine spirits. For Claudia Bonita, who lived 19 years. Iulius Vitalis, her father, and her brothers Claudius Arbusculus and Iulius Iucundus, and her sister Claudia Placida, placed this for the well-deserving deceased.
EDCS-28500200.summary: A funerary dedication for Claudia Bonita, erected by her family members, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28500201.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto pro salute Imperatoris Marci Antonini Veri Annius Saturninus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28500201.translation: To the Unconquered God, for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Verus, Annius Saturninus, centurion of the XI Claudia legion, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-28500201.summary: A centurion of the XI Claudia legion dedicates an offering to the Unconquered God for the emperor's safety, likely from the Antonine period.

EDCS-28500202.text_edition: \n Ordo splendidissima sic municipii Tropaei per Tiberium Claudium Germanum et Marcum Aurelium Flavium duumveros sic aediles Valerius Valerianus et Iulius Quirillus quaestores Ulpius Herculanus et Iulius Herculanus scriba Cocceius posuerunt\n
EDCS-28500202.translation: The council of the most distinguished municipality of Tropea, through Tiberius Claudius Germanus and Marcus Aurelius Flavius, duumvirs, and Valerius Valerianus and Julius Quirillus, quaestors, and Valerius Valerianus and Julius Quirillus, quaestors, and Ulpius Herculanus and Julius Herculanus, scribes, Cocceius erected this.
EDCS-28500202.summary: The local governing body of the municipality of Tropea, listing its officials including duumvirs and quaestors, erected a monument, likely public, during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000002.text_edition: \n Genio Iuliorum Ianuari Capitonis Epaphroditi nostrorum conductorum portorii publici Hermes servus vilicus posuit\n
EDCS-29000002.translation: Hermes, the overseer slave of our tax collectors for the public revenue of the Iulii, erected this.
EDCS-29000002.summary: A slave overseer of tax collectors dedicated a monument to the genius of the Iulii family, likely related to publican activities in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Annius Superstes vixit annos XXX hic situs est Titus Iulius Kapito conductor portorii publici et Iulia Cynegis heredes clienti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29000003.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Titus Annius Superstes lived for 30 years; he is buried here. Titus Julius Kapito, tax collector for the public revenue, and Iulia Cynegis, his heirs, had this made for their client.
EDCS-29000003.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life and burial of Titus Annius Superstes, with his tomb erected by his heirs who were involved in tax collection, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000004.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Annius Verus buleuta vixit annos XXXV Aurelia Flavia coniugi piissimo fecit\n
EDCS-29000004.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Caius Annius Verus, a council member, lived for 35 years. Aurelia Flavia made this for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-29000004.summary: A funerary inscription for Caius Annius Verus, a member of the local council, erected by his wife, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29100001.text_edition: \n Veneria Castoris votum ponet sic Dominae Reginae Publius Iulius scripsit\n
EDCS-29100001.translation: Veneria Castoris will place her vow, thus. Publius Julius wrote this for the Lady Queen.
EDCS-29100001.summary: A dedication to a female deity or powerful woman, possibly an empress, by Publius Julius who wrote the inscription for Veneria Castoris.

EDCS-29100002.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Capitoni ex sesquiplicario veterano vixit annos LXV militavit nnos XXV hic situs est Iulius Capito filius et heres eius bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-29100002.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Caius Iulius Capitoni, former sesquiplicarius veteran. He lived for 65 years and served for 25 years. He is buried here. Iulius Capito, his son and heir, erected this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-29100002.summary: A funerary inscription for a veteran soldier, Caius Iulius Capitoni, detailing his service and age, erected by his son and heir.

EDCS-29100003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Hermes paganus vixit annis LXX dies XI et Aurelia Meliti sic coniux eiusdem vixit annis LX menses duobus sic dies VII qui reliquerunt heredes bonos filios superstantes VII sex quidem filios et unam filiam bene merentibus pientissimi nati memoriam posuerunt ave vale viator\n
EDCS-29100003.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aurelius Hermes, a villager, lived for 70 years and 11 days, and Aurelia Meliti, his wife, lived for 60 years, 2 months, and 7 days. Their good children, seven sons and one daughter, who survived them, erected this memorial for their most devoted parents. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-29100003.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Hermes and his wife Aurelia Meliti, detailing their ages and listing their seven sons and one daughter who erected the memorial.

EDCS-29100004.text_edition: \n Genio vici Verobrittiani Caius Iulius Valens veteranus legionis V Macedonicae magister vici votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29100004.translation: To the genius of the Verobrittian district, Caius Iulius Valens, veteran of the V Macedonica legion and local official, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-29100004.summary: A veteran of the V Macedonica legion and local official dedicates an offering to the genius of his district, likely during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29100005.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Domitio Anthio veterano cohortis Lusitanorum ex pedite militavit annis XXVIII vixit annis LXXXIII Iulia coniux posuit marito bene merenti\n
EDCS-29100005.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Marcus Ulpius Domitius Anthus, a veteran of the Lusitanian cohort, who served as an infantryman for 28 years and lived for 83 years. Iulia his wife erected this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-29100005.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Domitius Anthus, a veteran infantryman, detailing his service and age, erected by his wife.

EDCS-29100006.text_edition: \n Marcus Ulpius Polycarpus votum solvit et templum \n
EDCS-29100006.translation: Marcus Ulpius Polycarpus fulfilled his vow and dedicated the temple.
EDCS-29100006.summary: This inscription records Marcus Ulpius Polycarpus fulfilling a vow and dedicating a temple, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100007.text_edition: \n Imppperatores Caesssares Flavius Claudius Constantinus Alamannicus maximus Germanicus maximus et Flavius Iulius Constantius Sarmaticus Persicus maximus et Flavius Iulius Constans Sarmaticus Pii Felices Augg<g>(usti) locum in parte limitis positum gentilium Gothorum temeritati semper aptissimum ad confirmandam provincialium suorum aeternam securitatem erecta istius fabricae munitione clauserunt latrunculorumque impetum perennis muniminis dispositione tenuerun<t=I> adcurante Sappone viro perfectissimo duce limitis Scythiae \n
EDCS-29100007.translation: The Emperors, Caesars Flavius Claudius Constantinus Alamannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus and Flavius Iulius Constantius Sarmaticus Persicus Maximus and Flavius Iulius Constans Sarmaticus, Pious, Fortunate Augusts, have closed off a location on the frontier, always most prone to the rashness of the Goths, to confirm the eternal security of their provincials by the erection of this fortification, and have repelled the attacks of brigands by the perpetual disposition of defenses, under the administration of Sappo, a most accomplished man, duke of the Scythian frontier.
EDCS-29100007.summary: This inscription records the fortification of a frontier location by Emperors Constantinus, Constantius, and Constans to secure the province against Goths, under the command of Duke Sappo, dated to the 4th century AD.

EDCS-29100011.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salutem sic Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Commmodi Augusti Pii Felicis et Crispinae Augustae -- Maximus -- magister vici ex viso libens merito posuit Crispino et Aeliano consulibus\n
EDCS-29100011.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Commodus Augustus Pius Felix and the Empress Crispina Augusta -- Maximus, magistrate of the district, set this up willingly and deservedly, having seen it, under the consulship of Crispino and Aelianus.
EDCS-29100011.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the well-being of Emperor Commodus and Empress Crispina, set up by a district magistrate named Maximus during the consulship of Crispino and Aelianus in the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-29100013.text_edition: \n Dis Inferis Manibus Lucius Pompeius Valens natus Fabia Anquira sic consistens regione Histri muneraque fecit Histro in oppido archontium et aedilicium et sacerdotium at sic Liberum et sepulcrum se vivo ME ipse sibi fecit et aram posuit cum fuit annorum LXXVI et Mansuetae coniugi suae piae bene merentique vixit annos L et Donato filio suo qui nunc est annorum XX dierum minus plus V Marcus maiorarius rerum supra scripti et supra scripte puosuit sic quem commendo tus cum libertis meis scriptum XIII Octobribus Barbaro consule\n
EDCS-29100013.translation: To the Gods of the Underworld, the Manes. Lucius Pompeius Valens, born in the Fabia tribe, while living in the Histri region, made offerings and served as archon, aedile, and priest in the town of Histros. He made this tomb and sepulchre for himself while alive, and set up an altar. When he was 76 years old, and for his deserving wife Mansueta, with whom he lived for 50 years, and for his son Donatus, who is now 20 years old less more than 5 days. Marcus Maiorarius, relative of the aforementioned man and woman, set this up. Whom I commend to you, along with my freedmen. Inscribed on the 13th of October, in the consulship of Barbarus.
EDCS-29100013.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Pompeius Valens, who built his own tomb and altar at age 76, along with his wife Mansueta and son Donatus, in Histros, and was inscribed in October during the consulship of Barbarus, likely in the late Roman period.

EDCS-29100014.text_edition: \n Memoriae Gaius Iulius Sergius vivo suo posuit sibi et coniugi suae Theageniae qui vixit annis XXXXV et filio suo Theodoriano qui vixit annis XXVI et filiae sic suae sic Cocceiani sic qui vixit annis XXII et filiae sic suae sic Sergiani sic qui vixit annis IIII bene merentibus posuit ave viator et vale\n
EDCS-29100014.translation: For the memory of himself, Gaius Iulius Sergius set this up while alive for himself, his wife Theagenia, who lived 45 years, his son Theodorianus, who lived 26 years, and his daughters Cocceiana, who lived 22 years, and Sergiana, who lived 4 years. He set this up for those who deserved well. Hail, traveler, and farewell.
EDCS-29100014.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Gaius Iulius Sergius for himself and his deceased wife and children, listing their ages and expressing well wishes to passersby, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100015.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Quadratus memoriam sibi et Iuliae Terentiae coniugi suae se vivo per liberos suos fecit loci princeps quinquennalis territorii Capidavensis\n
EDCS-29100015.translation: Caius Iulius Quadratus, son of Caius, by his children, made this memorial for himself and his wife Iulia Terentia while he was alive. He was the chief quinquennial official of the territory of Capidava.
EDCS-29100015.summary: This inscription records Caius Iulius Quadratus, a quinquennial official in Capidava, commissioning his own and his wife's memorial while alive, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100017.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Cocceius Hilus posuit pro salutem sua et suorum ci H I de suo posuit aram\n
EDCS-29100017.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Marcus Cocceius Hilus set this up for the safety of himself and his family. He set up an altar from his own resources.
EDCS-29100017.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Cocceius Hilus, who erected an altar for the well-being of himself and his household, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100018.text_edition: \n Cuspio Rufino et Statio Codrato sic consulibus Cla<u>dius Posidonius consacravit\n
EDCS-29100018.translation: Under the consulship of Cuspius Rufus and Sthenius Quadratus, Claudius Posidonius consecrated [this].
EDCS-29100018.summary: This inscription records the consecration by Claudius Posidonius during the consulship of Cuspius Rufus and Sthenius Quadratus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100019.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Baebio Proculo veterano legionis XIII geminae vixit annos LX Germinia Valentina coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-29100019.translation: To the Gods of the Underworld, the Manes. For Quinto Baebio Proculo, a veteran of the 13th Gemina legion, who lived 60 years. His wife, Germinia Valentina, set this up for her deserving husband.
EDCS-29100019.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quinto Baebio Proculus, a veteran of the 13th Legion, erected by his wife Germinia Valentina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100020.text_edition: \n Titus Valerius Titi filius Collina Germanus Pessennunto sic immaginifer sic legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis donis II donatus vixit annos LVII hic situs est heredes Titus Valerius Iulianus et Lucius Valerius Ge<r=E>manus et Valeria Germana et Claudia Iulia coniunx eius nobilissima bene merenti posuerunt \n
EDCS-29100020.translation: Titus Valerius Collina Germanus, son of Titus, a standard-bearer of the 7th Claudian legion, pious and faithful, honored twice with gifts, who lived 57 years, lies here. His heirs, Titus Valerius Iulianus, Lucius Valerius Germanus, Valeria Germana, and his wife Claudia Iulia, most noble, set this up for the deserving deceased.
EDCS-29100020.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Titus Valerius Collina Germanus, a decorated standard-bearer of the 7th Legion, who lived 57 years, and was set up by his heirs and wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100023.text_edition: \n ------ di]ebus II Manilla <a>nnis VIII Matrona annis LIII filias sic quibus Valeria Heliodora coniunx bene merentibus ponendum curavit ave vale viator\n
EDCS-29100023.translation: To Manilla, aged 8 years, and Matrona, aged 53 years, daughters. Their wife Valeria Heliodora took care to set this up for them, well-deserving. Hail and farewell, traveler.
EDCS-29100023.summary: This is a tombstone commemorating two daughters, Manilla and Matrona, erected by the wife and mother, Valeria Heliodora, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100029.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani nepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXII consul IIII pater patriae Tito Statilio Iulio Severo legato Augusti pro praetore milia passuum XVIIII\n
EDCS-29100029.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 22nd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, [records] 19 Roman miles for Titus Statilius Severus, his legate with the rank of Augustal governor.
EDCS-29100029.summary: This milestone records an imperial construction project of 19 Roman miles undertaken by Emperor Antoninus Pius for his legate Titus Statilius Severus, likely dating to his 22nd year of tribunician power (AD 158/159).

EDCS-29100030.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVI consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus tribunicia potestate II consul II divi fili Pii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Nervae adnepotes Marcus Servilius Fabianus Maximus legatus Augusti pro praetore milia passuum XVIIII\n
EDCS-29100030.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 16th time, Consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, Consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Pius, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, [record] 19 Roman miles for Marcus Servilius Fabianus Maximus, their legate with the rank of Augustal governor.
EDCS-29100030.summary: This milestone indicates road construction of 19 Roman miles attributed to Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, overseen by their legate Marcus Servilius Fabianus Maximus, likely dating to AD 166.

EDCS-29100035.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix sanctissimus Augustus tribuniciae potestatis XIII consul III pater patriae proconsul pontes derutos et vias conlapsas restituit curante Quinto Decio pro praetore legato suo milia passuum \n
EDCS-29100035.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix sanctissimus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, restored ruined bridges and collapsed roads, under the care of Quintus Decius, his legate with the rank of governor, [over a distance of] Roman miles...
EDCS-29100035.summary: This inscription records Emperor Severus Alexander's restoration of infrastructure, including bridges and roads, managed by his legate Quintus Decius, likely dating to AD 234.

EDCS-29100036.text_edition: \n DDominis nnostris Constantino Augg(usto) Crispo Constantino et Constantio nnobbilissimis Caessaribus milia passuum I\n
EDCS-29100036.translation: To our Lords Constantine Augustus, Crispus Caesar, Constantine Caesar, and Constantius Caesar, the most noble, 1 Roman mile.
EDCS-29100036.summary: This milestone records a distance of 1 Roman mile, dedicated to the Augusti Constantine and Crispus, and the Caesars Constantine and Constantius, likely dating to the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-29100147.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Petronius Sentius sacerdotalis se vivo sibi et Flaviae Margaritae coniugi suae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29100147.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Petronius Sentius, a priest, took care to have this made for himself while alive, and for his wife Flavia Margarita.
EDCS-29100147.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up during his lifetime by Lucius Petronius Sentius for himself and his wife Flavia Margarita, dedicated to the underworld deities, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100148.text_edition: \n Iovi Sabadio sic et Mercurio Marcus Servilius Verecundus pro se et suos sic votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-29100148.translation: To Jupiter Sabadius, and also to Mercury. Marcus Servilius Verecundus willingly set up this vow for himself and his family, as deserved.
EDCS-29100148.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a vow made by Marcus Servilius Verecundus to the gods Jupiter Sabadius and Mercury for himself and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100149.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sippiae Paezusae Marcus Antonius Marcellus coniugi pientissimae posuit have\n
EDCS-29100149.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Antonius Marcellus set this up for his most dutiful wife, Sippia Paezusa. Hail.
EDCS-29100149.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Marcus Antonius Marcellus for his wife Sippia Paezusa, dedicated to the underworld deities, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100151.text_edition: \n itus civis Romanus vivo suo posuit Iuliae Dometiae sic maritus coniugi suae karissimae\n
EDCS-29100151.translation: To Titus, a Roman citizen, he set this up for his dearest wife Julia Dometia while he was alive.
EDCS-29100151.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up during his lifetime by Titus, a Roman citizen, for his wife Julia Dometia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100152.text_edition: \n Martinae et Autronio Martino et Do Martino et domui et familiae pro salute eorum botum sic posuit Hermadion Phrygillio\n
EDCS-29100152.translation: For Martina, and for Autronius Martinus, and for Do Martinus, and for the household and family, for their well-being, Hermadion Phrygillius set up this vow.
EDCS-29100152.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a vow made by Hermadion Phrygillius for the well-being of Martina, Autronius Martinus, Do Martinus, and their household and family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Cai liberto Epagatho aedili Nicopolis Caius Valerius Castus patri suo piissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29100154.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Valerius Castus erected this for his most devoted father, Gaius Valerius Epagathus, freedman of Gaius, an aedile of Nicopolis.
EDCS-29100154.summary: A funerary monument erected by a son for his father, a former aedile of Nicopolis.

EDCS-29100155.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Clementilla vixit annos XVIII Quintus Caurisinius Ingenuus maritus eius miles et sibi vivo memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-29100155.translation: To the Divine Shades. Claudia Clementilla lived for 18 years. Quintus Caurisinius Ingenuus, her husband, a soldier, erected this memorial for himself and his wife while alive.
EDCS-29100155.summary: A joint funerary monument erected by a soldier for himself and his deceased wife, Claudia Clementilla.

EDCS-29100156.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus si modo sunt Manes sentiant spes vitae nostrae fueras nunc quis ubi iaceas indicat iste lapis Lucius Spurennius Herculanus frater et Flavia Paula mater Lucio Spurennio Rufo bucinatori cohor(tis) Mattiacorum militante sic annos XX bene merenti posuerunt valete viatores\n
EDCS-29100156.translation: To the Divine Shades. If only the shades exist and feel the hopes of our life. You were once alive, now this stone indicates where you lie. Lucius Spurennius Herculanus, his brother, and Flavia Paula, his mother, erected this for Lucius Spurennius Rufus, a bugler in the cohort of the Mattiaci, who served for 20 years with merit. Farewell, travelers.
EDCS-29100156.summary: A funerary monument for Lucius Spurennius Rufus, a bugler, erected by his brother and mother, with a philosophical reflection on death and remembrance.

EDCS-29100157.text_edition: \n Lucio Valerio Pudenti veterano ex signifero et Valeriae Restutae et Valeriae Pudentillae et Valerio Pudentiano Kalpurnia Procula marito dulcissimo mater natis memoriam maerens posuit\n
