EDCS-29100157.translation: Kalpuria Procula, the grieving mother, erected this memorial for her dearest husband, Lucius Valerius Pudentus, a veteran and former standard-bearer, and for Valeria Restuta, Valeria Pudentilla, and Valerius Pudentianus.
EDCS-29100157.summary: A funerary monument erected by a mother for her deceased husband and children, who include a veteran.

EDCS-29100160.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae Alexandrae quae vixit annos XXIIII menses III Titus Flavius Marcianus praefectus equitum alae Atectorigianae uxori piissimae posuit\n
EDCS-29100160.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titus Flavius Marcianus, prefect of cavalry in the Atectorigiana ala, erected this for his most devoted wife, Cornelia Alexandra, who lived for 24 years and 3 months.
EDCS-29100160.summary: A funerary monument erected by a cavalry prefect for his wife, Cornelia Alexandra.

EDCS-29100166.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Severus miles legionis primae Italicae coniugi suo Volumnia Marciana posuit memoriam vixit annis XXV pos(uit) titulum feliciter\n
EDCS-29100166.translation: To the Divine Shades. Volumnia Marciana erected this memorial for her husband, Gaius Valerius Severus, a soldier in the First Italian Legion, who lived for 25 years. May the inscription be successful.
EDCS-29100166.summary: A funerary monument erected by a wife for her husband, a soldier in the First Italian Legion.

EDCS-29100167.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque diis deabusque Giridavensibus Memor Auggustorum nnnostrorum servus |contrascriptor stationis Dimensis gravi valetudine liberatus\n
EDCS-29100167.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to all the other gods and goddesses of Giridava. Memor, slave of our Augusti, a contrascriptor of the Dimensis station, freed from a serious illness...
EDCS-29100167.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter and local deities by Memor, a slave and scribe, after recovering from illness.

EDCS-29100169.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Iustinus quaestionarius legionis XI Claudiae qui militavit annis XXIIII vixit annis XXXXV Aelius Ingenuus beneficiarius legati legionis heres bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-29100169.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelius Iustinus, an investigator of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, who served for 24 years and lived for 35 years. Aelius Ingenuus, a beneficiarius of the legionary legate, erected this for his well-deserving heir.
EDCS-29100169.summary: A funerary monument erected by Aelius Ingenuus for his heir, Aelius Iustinus, an investigator in the Eleventh Claudian Legion.

EDCS-29100170.text_edition: \n ------ e]t Ulpiae Luciae matri et Ulpio Marco canalicario et Ulpio Hirto librario fratribus Ulpius Marcellus frumentarius parentibus degnissimis sic\n
EDCS-29100170.translation: ... and for Ulpia Lucia, his mother, and for Ulpius Marcus, a canal worker, and Ulpius Hirto, a scribe, his brothers. Ulpius Marcellus, a grain official, erected this for his most worthy parents.
EDCS-29100170.summary: A funerary monument erected by Ulpius Marcellus for his mother and two brothers, who held professions related to administration and trade.

EDCS-29100171.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et dis Samariae Geminius Herodianus ex voto posuit C\n
EDCS-29100171.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the gods of Samaria. Geminius Herodianus erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-29100171.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter and the gods of Samaria by Geminius Herodianus.

EDCS-29100173.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo T pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii et Marci Aureli Veri Caesaris \n
EDCS-29100173.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar.
EDCS-29100173.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, likely dating to the joint reign of Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius, requesting divine protection for the emperors.

EDCS-29100176.text_edition: \n exstruxit sibi vivus amicus qua nunc Marcus Aurelius Mucianus veteranus ex beneficiario legati legionis I Italicae Mucianae filiae eius et Aurelio Dizze sic veterano fratri fecit Mucianus fecit\n
EDCS-29100176.translation: Marcus Aurelius Mucianus, a veteran and former beneficiarius of a legate of the I Italica legion, built this for himself while alive, as a gift for his daughter Muciana and his brother Aurelius Dizze, a veteran. Mucianus built this.
EDCS-29100176.summary: This funerary monument records a veteran, Marcus Aurelius Mucianus, who built it for himself and his family, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29100177.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Pompeio Publi filio Aemilia Magno Stobis veterano legionis I Italicae vixit annos LX hic situs est Caius Pompeius Magnus filius et heres patri bene merito et Pompeia Marcia coniunx honoris et facultatis causa posuerunt\n
EDCS-29100177.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Pompeius Magnus, son of Publius, a veteran of the I Italica legion, lived 60 years and is buried here. Caius Pompeius Magnus, son and heir, and his wife Pompeia Marcia, erected this monument out of respect and affection for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-29100177.summary: This epitaph records the death of Publius Pompeius Magnus, a veteran of the I Italica legion, and was erected by his son and wife, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29100178.text_edition: \n Pompeius Magnus buleuta Cai Pompei Magni filius \n
EDCS-29100178.translation: Pompeius Magnus, council member, son of Caius Pompeius Magnus.
EDCS-29100178.summary: This brief inscription identifies Pompeius Magnus as a council member (buleuta) and records his parentage, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100179.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Luci filius Proclus miles legionis V Macedonicae beneficiarius legati optio ad spem ordinis |(centurio) legionis eiusdem donatus torquibus armillis phaleris bello Dacico |(centurio) legionis I Italicae |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae |(centurio) legionis XX Valeriae victricis |(centurio) legionis VIIII Hispanae missus honesta missione vixit annos LXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-29100179.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Valerius Proclus, son of Lucius, a soldier of the V Macedonica legion, a beneficiarius of the legate, optio ad spem ordinis (centurion) of the same legion, awarded torques, armillae, and phalerae during the Dacian War, centurion of the I Italica legion, centurion of the XI Claudiae legion, centurion of the XX Valeria Victrix legion, centurion of the VIIII Hispana legion, discharged with an honorable release, lived 75 years and is buried here.
EDCS-29100179.summary: This epitaph honors Lucius Valerius Proclus, a decorated soldier who served in multiple legions and achieved the rank of centurion, likely during or after the Dacian Wars in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29100184.text_edition: \n Vipsania Luci filia Maxima vixit nnos XXXV hic sita est Vipsani Clemens miles legionis VII et Valens et Martialis {Vipsani Valens et Vipsani Martialis} et Modeistus et Frontoto fratres pientissimae sorori et Visentia Modesta mater faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29100184.translation: Vipsania Maxima, daughter of Lucius, lived 35 years and is buried here. Her brothers Vipsanius Clemens, a soldier of the VII legion, Valens, Martialis, Modeistus, and Frontoto, and their mother Visentia Modesta, arranged for this to be made for their most devoted sister.
EDCS-29100184.summary: This epitaph records the death of Vipsania Maxima, aged 35, and was erected by her brothers and mother, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29100186.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Firmus vixit annis XXX <V=N>aleria Pieris mater et Ulpia Marcia coniux et Marcia soror et Ingenua posuerunt \n
EDCS-29100186.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Iulius Firmus lived 35 years. Valeria Pieris, his mother, Ulpia Marcia, his wife, Marcia, his sister, and Ingenua, erected this.
EDCS-29100186.summary: This epitaph records the death of Caius Iulius Firmus, aged 35, and was erected by his mother, wife, and sister, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100187.text_edition: \n Herculanus actor Flavi Gemelli votum solvit\n
EDCS-29100187.translation: Herculanus, agent of Flavius Gemellus, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29100187.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Herculanus, an agent for Flavius Gemellus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100190.text_edition: \n Lucius Aurelius Surus buleuta civitatis IVS Lucium Aurelium Surum veteranum ex signifero patrem et Claudiam Gaillam matrem et Domitiam Valentiam coniugem secum ita haec memoriae consilio suo usus de suo dignatus est\n
EDCS-29100190.translation: Lucius Aurelius Surus, a council member of the civitas IVS, honored his father Lucius Aurelius Surus, a veteran and standard-bearer, his mother Claudia Gailla, and his wife Domitia Valentia by including them in this memorial, as he decided by his own counsel and out of his own resources.
EDCS-29100190.summary: This memorial records Lucius Aurelius Surus, a council member, who established a family burial plot for himself and his parents and wife, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29100194.summary: This fragmentary inscription begins with a father's name, Aurelius Tauriscus, and indicates it pertains to his daughter, likely a funerary monument from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100195.text_edition: \n ER eT Apronia Respecta uxor avo vivo suo titulum et memoriam posuerunt\n
EDCS-29100195.translation: Apronia Respecta and her mother, her living grandmother, erected this monument and memorial.
EDCS-29100195.summary: A funerary monument erected by Apronia Respecta and her mother for her living grandmother, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-29100200.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Rufus veteranus legionis I Italicae meruit annos XX vixit annos LX Iulia Verecunda coniux coniugi et Valerius Rufus filius faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29100200.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Valerius Rufus, a veteran of the First Italic Legion, served for 20 years and lived for 60 years. His wife, Iulia Verecunda, and his son, Valerius Rufus, took care of the construction.
EDCS-29100200.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Valerius Rufus, a veteran of the I Italica legion, who served for 20 years and lived for 60, erected by his wife and son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29100201.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Quinta cum filio Licinio posuit viro su[o ------\n
EDCS-29100201.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Quinta erected this with her son for her husband [------]
EDCS-29100201.summary: A funerary inscription for an unnamed husband, set up by his wife Iulia Quinta and son, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-29100202.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Valenti statori aequiti sic ex singularibus vixit annis XXXVIII Coelia Maxima marito\n
EDCS-29100202.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Valens, a standard-bearer (stator) and cavalryman (eques), who served as a special trooper (singularis), lived for 38 years. Coelia Maxima dedicated this to her husband.
EDCS-29100202.summary: A tombstone for Aurelius Valens, a cavalryman and standard-bearer who served in a special unit, erected by his wife Coelia Maxima, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29100203.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Ni et Orfia Vitilla Domitiae Iulianae filiae suae qui vixit annis plus minus novem mensses sic duos \n
EDCS-29100203.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Ni and Orfia Vitilla placed this for their daughter Domitia Iuliana, who lived for approximately nine years and two months.
EDCS-29100203.summary: A tombstone for Domitia Iuliana, daughter of Aurelius Ni and Orfia Vitilla, who died at approximately 9 years and 2 months old, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-29100204.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemili Burozi vixit nnos XLV Bdutua marito pientissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29100204.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aemilius Burozi lived for 45 years. Bdutua arranged for this to be made for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-29100204.summary: A funerary inscription for Aemilius Burozi, who lived 45 years, erected by his wife Bdutua, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-29100205.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Victor veteranus alae II Aravacorum militavit annos XXIX vixit annos LX et Claudia Procula vixit annos hic siti sunt Restitutus secundum testamentum? faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29100205.translation: Tiberius Claudius Victor, a veteran of the Second Wing of the Aravaci, served for 29 years and lived for 60 years. Claudia Procula lived for [unknown age]. They are buried here. Restitutus arranged for this to be made, possibly according to a will.
EDCS-29100205.summary: A tombstone for Tiberius Claudius Victor, a veteran of the ala II Aravacorum, and Claudia Procula, erected by Restitutus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29100206.text_edition: \n Sulpicius Massa veteranus alae Hispanorum natione Tunger vixit nnos LX meruit annos XXXV hic situs est coniux Pieris faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29100206.translation: Sulpicius Massa, a veteran of the Wing of the Hispani, of Tungrian origin, lived for 60 years and served for 35 years. He is buried here. His wife Pieris arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-29100206.summary: A tombstone for Sulpicius Massa, a Tungrian veteran of the ala Hispanorum, who lived 60 years and served 35, erected by his wife Pieris, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29100207.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus DVITVOR MET VEZVI vixit annos IX Nuidasia vixit annos IIIX Doiodus vixit annos XX Etzozi Flegitsana Et Zito pro pietate bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-29100207.translation: To the spirits of the departed. DVITVOR MET VEZVI lived for 9 years, Nuidasia lived for 8 years, Doiodus lived for 20 years. Etzozi Flegitsana and Et Zito placed this for them out of dutiful affection for those who deserved well.
EDCS-29100207.summary: A funerary inscription for DVITVOR MET VEZVI, Nuidasia, and Doiodus, with ages 9, 8, and 20 respectively, erected by Etzozi Flegitsana and Et Zito, likely from the Roman period and possibly belonging to a non-Romanized population.

EDCS-29100210.text_edition: \n Acutia Liberalis pro se et suis Dianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29100210.translation: Acutia Liberalis willingly and deservedly paid her vow to Diana for herself and her family.
EDCS-29100210.summary: A votive inscription recording a vow made by Acutia Liberalis to the goddess Diana for herself and her family, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-29100211.text_edition: \n Tauriscum ex |(centurione) veterano avom suum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29100211.translation: Tauriscus, a veteran centurion, took care to have this made for his grandfather.
EDCS-29100211.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a grandfather, commissioned by his grandson Tauriscus, a veteran centurion.

EDCS-29100212.text_edition: \n Deanae Reginae pro salute Cai Ovini Tertulli consularis Quintus V cum filio suo Victorino Raesius Pudens primiscrinius officinae nostrae cum suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29100212.translation: Quintus V with his son Victorino, Raesius Pudens, chief clerk of our workshop, with his own people, willingly and deservedly paid their vow to Deana Regina for the well-being of Caius Ovinius Tertullus, the consular.
EDCS-29100212.summary: This votive inscription records the fulfillment of a vow made by Quintus V, his son Victorino, and Raesius Pudens for the well-being of the consular Caius Ovinius Tertullus.

EDCS-29100213.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae et Apollini pro salute Luci Iu<l=I>i Statilii Severi legati Augusti pro praetore et liberorum eius Aelius filius Artemidorus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae frumentarius \n
EDCS-29100213.translation: For Diana Regina and Apollo, for the well-being of Lucius Iulius Statilius Severus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, and his children, Aelius Artemidorus, son of Aelius, a centurion of the I Italica legion, a quartermaster, dedicated this.
EDCS-29100213.summary: This dedicatory inscription is for Diana Regina and Apollo, made by Aelius Artemidorus, a centurion, for the well-being of the imperial legate Lucius Iulius Statilius Severus and his children.

EDCS-29100215.text_edition: \n Deo Sancto Invicto Lucaius ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-29100215.translation: Lucaius set this up in fulfillment of a vow to the Holy Unconquered God.
EDCS-29100215.summary: This is a votive inscription to the 'Holy Unconquered God', dedicated by Lucaius.

EDCS-29100217.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Crescentio ex praepositis et Vincentia coniunx eius Aureliae Vericiae bruti suae qui sic vixit annis XXIIII bene meritae titulo sic posuerunt\n
EDCS-29100217.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Crescentius, formerly prefect, and his wife Vincentia, erected this monument to their well-deserving Aurelia Vericia, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-29100217.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Aurelia Vericia, erected by her husband Aurelius Crescentius and wife Vincentia.

EDCS-29100218.text_edition: \n Cereri sacrum Dionysius Serapiaci Augusti nostri dispensatoris vikarius sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29100218.translation: Dionysius, deputy for Serapiacus, steward of our Emperor, willingly and deservedly paid this vow, sacred to Ceres.
EDCS-29100218.summary: This is a votive inscription to Ceres, paid by Dionysius, a deputy steward.

EDCS-29100220.text_edition: \n m cum filiis Aurelios Martialem et Salvianum perfectissimos viros iuniores Aulogenem Ziepyrum opus perfectum manet perpetua divinitas\n
EDCS-29100220.translation: to his sons Aurelius Martialis and Aurelius Salvinianus, most accomplished young men. Aulogenes and Ziepyrus completed the work; eternal divinity awaits.
EDCS-29100220.summary: This inscription seems to commemorate the completion of a work by Aulogenes and Ziepyrus, possibly for their sons or patrons, and implies a divine reward.

EDCS-29100221.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatorum Firmus Antigoni et Nicostratus Demosthenis et Zoilus Gorgi lapidarii per magistros Bellicionem Valerium Mnaseam Pollionem vicani Trullenses donum dederunt dedicaverunt posuerunt\n
EDCS-29100221.translation: The villagers of Trullae, through their magistrates Bellicio, Valerius, Mnaseas, and Pollio, gave and dedicated this offering, sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the well-being of the Emperors.
EDCS-29100221.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, dedicated by the villagers of Trullae for the well-being of the Emperors.

EDCS-29100222.text_edition: \n Sancto Heroni Iulius AuICII NCI TC M votum posuerunt SCRIBA-VIBITHI\n
EDCS-29100222.translation: Iulius [Auic]i[i] NCI TCI M [set up this vow] to Saint Heron. [---]
EDCS-29100222.summary: This is a fragmented votive inscription to Saint Heron, initiated by Iulius.

EDCS-29100223.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ael(ium) sic Mucianum sic militem sic legionis deceptum sic a barbaris Aelius Durazis frater eius memoriam sic causam sic posuit valete viatores\n
EDCS-29100223.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Durazis, his brother, erected this memorial for Aelius Mucianus, a soldier of the [---] legion, fallen at the hands of barbarians, setting forth the circumstances. Farewell, travelers.
EDCS-29100223.summary: This funerary inscription is for Aelius Mucianus, a soldier killed by barbarians, erected by his brother Aelius Durazis.

EDCS-29100225.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Marcus Aurelius Maximus eques legionis I Italicae Antoniniaanae quod vover<a=N>t Imperatore Antonin<o=I> Augusto II consule \n
EDCS-29100225.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, Marcus Aurelius Maximus, equestrian of the I Italica Legion Antoniniana, concerning what they vowed when Emperor Antoninus Augustus II was consul.
EDCS-29100225.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter and Juno, made by Marcus Aurelius Maximus, an equestrian of the I Italica Legion, during the consulship of Antoninus Augustus II (likely Caracalla) around 205 AD.

EDCS-29100226.text_edition: \n Densela Drulentis vixit annis LX Bitus Ep coniiugi posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-29100226.translation: Densela, wife of Drulentis, lived 60 years. Bitus dedicated this to his deserving wife.
EDCS-29100226.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Densela, wife of Drulentis, erected by her husband Bitus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100228.text_edition: \n Lucius Baebius Luci filius Papiria domo Nicopoli vixit nnos LMN Lucius Baebius Clemens iunior frater bene merenti posuit hic situs est\n
EDCS-29100228.translation: Lucius Baebius Lucius son, of the Papiria tribe, from Nicopolis, lived 50 years. Lucius Baebius Clemens the younger erected this for his deserving brother; he is buried here.
EDCS-29100228.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Baebius, son of Lucius, from Nicopolis, erected by his brother Lucius Baebius Clemens the younger, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100319.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Falcius Quinti filius Aniensi Constans Arimini veteranus legionis I Italicae vixit annos L heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29100319.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Falcius Constans, son of Quintus, of the Aniensis tribe, a veteran of the I Italica Legion from Ariminum, lived 50 years. His heir had this made.
EDCS-29100319.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Falcius Constans, a veteran of the I Italica Legion from Ariminum, whose heir commissioned the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100323.text_edition: \n Caius Vibius Cai filius Fabia Fronto domo Brixia veteranus legionis V Macedonicae vixit annos \n
EDCS-29100323.translation: Caius Vibius Fronto, son of Caius, of the Fabia tribe, from Brixia, a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion, lived [X] years.
EDCS-29100323.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Vibius Fronto, a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion from Brixia, with the years of his life incomplete, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100437.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolycheno sic pro salutem sic Imperatoris domini nostri Pii Felicis Augusti S<e>veri Alexandrus sic Luginus Dometi sic et Aquila Barsemon et Flavius Damas sacerdotes templum a solo f<e>cerunt\n
EDCS-29100437.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus. For the safety of our lord Emperor Pius Felix Augustus Severus. Alexander Luginus son of Dometius, and Aquila son of Barsemon, and Flavius Damas, priests, built this temple from the ground up.
EDCS-29100437.summary: This is a dedication of a temple built from scratch to Jupiter Dolichenus by three priests, including Alexander Luginus and Aquila Barsemon, for the well-being of Emperor Severus Alexander, dating to his reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-29100441.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Iulius as a priest of Jupiter Dolichenus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100442.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Aurelius Antiochianus sacerdos et Flavius\n
EDCS-29100442.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus. Aurelius Antiochianus, priest, and Flavius [also priest?].
EDCS-29100442.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Aurelius Antiochianus and Flavius, who are identified as priests, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601655.text_edition: \n Valerius Antoninus speculator legionis I Italicae Severianae votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-29601655.translation: Valerius Antoninus, a speculator of the I Italica Legion Severiana, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-29601655.summary: This is a votive inscription by Valerius Antoninus, a speculator (military police/messenger) of the I Italica Legion Severiana, who gladly fulfilled a vow, likely from the Severan period (193-235 AD).

EDCS-29601657.text_edition: \n Dianae Scoptitiae pro salute domini nostri Severi Alexandri Pii Augusti Domitianus eius vilicus voti compos solvit libens posuit\n
EDCS-29601657.translation: To Diana Scoptitia. For the safety of our lord Severus Alexander Pius Augustus. Domitianus, his steward, fulfilled his vow and willingly dedicated this.
EDCS-29601657.summary: This is a dedication to Diana Scoptitia made by Domitianus, a steward, for the safety of Emperor Severus Alexander, likely dating to his reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-29601660.text_edition: \n Apronius Maximus tribunus cohortis prata publica terminis circumclusit\n
EDCS-29601660.translation: Apronius Maximus, tribune of the cohort, enclosed the public lands with boundary stones.
EDCS-29601660.summary: This inscription records the marking of public land boundaries by a military officer, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29601662.text_edition: \n cum Iuli sibi et Muco et Z coniugi vixit annos quinquaginta ex in summum ponioni diem ab heredibus suis digI\n
EDCS-29601662.translation: He lived with his wife Iulia, Muco, and Z for fifty years; his heirs placed him on his final pyre.
EDCS-29601662.summary: This fragmented epitaph commemorates a person who lived with their wife and others for fifty years, with the burial arranged by their heirs, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29601663.text_edition: \n Vivus sibi fecit Caius Iulius Valens optio legionis I Italicae et Ireni coniugi et Iuliae Valentinae filiae vixit annos \n
EDCS-29601663.translation: Caius Iulius Valens, a optio of Legio I Italica, made this for himself, his wife Irena, and his daughter Iulia Valentina, and lived for...
EDCS-29601663.summary: This epitaph, likely from the Roman imperial period, was set up by a soldier for himself and his family.

EDCS-29601664.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aemilio Publi filio Montano vixit annos <L=I> et filio eiusdem Aemiliano Vibia Rufina coniugi et filio bene merentibus et sibi vivae faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-29601664.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Vibia Rufina had this made while alive for her deserving husband Publius Aemilius Montanus, son of Publius, who lived 51 years, and for her son Aemilianus, and for herself.
EDCS-29601664.summary: This funerary inscription, probably from the Roman imperial period, honors a family, with the monument erected by the wife for her husband, son, and herself.

EDCS-29601665.text_edition: \n Romanae securitatis libertatisque vindicibus ddominis nnostris Flavio Valerio Constantino et [Liciniano] [Licinio] Piis Felicibus aeternis Auggustis quorum virtute et providentia edomitis ubique barbararum gentium populis ad confirmandam limitis tutelam etiam Tropaeensium civitas auspicato a fundamentis feliciter opere constructa est Petronius Annianus vir clarissimus et Iulius Iulianus vir eminentissimus praefecti praetorio numini eorum semper dicatissimi\n
EDCS-29601665.translation: To our lords, Flavius Valerius Constantinus and Licinius, the Pious, Fortunate, Eternal Augusti, vindicators of Roman security and liberty, by whose valor and foresight, after barbarous peoples were subdued everywhere, the city of Tomis was happily founded from its foundations for the strengthening of the border defense. Petronius Annianus, most distinguished man, and Iulius Iulianus, most eminent man, prefects of the praetorium, always most devoted to their divine majesty.
EDCS-29601665.summary: This lengthy inscription from the late Roman period praises Emperors Constantine and Licinius for their victories and the construction of fortifications at Tomis, commissioned by two high-ranking prefects.

EDCS-29601666.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cocceius Vitales sic vixsit sic annis L et Cocceia Iulia coniunxs sic eius vixsit sic annis XL obiti ad villam suam Cocceius Clemens et Cocceius Helius filii patri et matri bene merentibus titulum posuerunt\n
EDCS-29601666.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Cocceius Vitales lived 50 years, and his wife Cocceia Iulia lived 40 years. Their sons Cocceius Clemens and Cocceius Helius placed this monument for their deserving father and mother at their villa.
EDCS-29601666.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman imperial period records the lives of a couple and was erected by their sons.

EDCS-29601667.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus memoriae karissimorum filiorum Titi Flavi Flaviani et Titi Flavi Mo[desti ------\n
EDCS-29601667.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, to the memory of his dearest sons, Titus Flavius Flavianus and Titus Flavius Modestus...
EDCS-29601667.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription, likely from the Roman imperial period, is dedicated to the memory of two sons.

EDCS-29601668.text_edition: \n Artemidora coniunx cum filio suo et filia sua Viventia patri eorum titulum posuerunt Dis Manibus memoria Nepotiani principalis vixit annis XXX|VII Victor filius vixit nnis II Iuliana filia vixit annis III Heraclius frater vixit annis III Vitalis frater vixit annis V valete lectores\n
EDCS-29601668.translation: Artemidora, his wife, with their son and their daughter Viventia, placed this monument for their father. To the spirits of the underworld. In memory of Nepotianus Principalis, who lived 37 years. Victor, their son, lived 2 years. Iuliana, their daughter, lived 3 years. Heraclius, brother, lived 3 years. Vitalis, brother, lived 5 years. Farewell, readers.
EDCS-29601668.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman imperial period honors Nepotianus and his family, with ages provided for his children and brothers, and a plea to readers.

EDCS-29601669.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Daleni ex vico Amlaidina et quondam praetorianorum Aurelia Uthis uxor eius titulum de suo merenti ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-29601669.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aurelia Uthis, wife of Aurelius Dalenus from the village of Amlaidina, and formerly of the praetorian guard, saw to it that this deserved monument was erected at her own expense.
EDCS-29601669.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman imperial period honors Aurelius Dalenus, a former praetorian, and was set up by his wife.

EDCS-29601670.text_edition: \n Aburia Auli filia Primigenia vixit annos L vixit con sic Gaio Auleno Proclo annos XXV hic sita est coniux bene meritae posuit\n
EDCS-29601670.translation: Aburia Primigenia, daughter of Aulus, lived 50 years. She lived with Gaius Aulenus Proclus for 25 years. Here she rests. Her deserving wife placed this.
EDCS-29601670.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman imperial period records the life of a woman and her marriage, with the monument erected by her husband.

EDCS-29601671.text_edition: \n Aurelia Grata filia patri bene merenti \n
EDCS-29601671.translation: Aurelia Grata to her deserving father.
EDCS-29601671.summary: A funerary dedication by a daughter to her father.

EDCS-29601672.text_edition: \n LS Aureli- S Gai supra scripti et Co patrono bene merenti \n
EDCS-29601672.translation: LS Aureli- S Gai, above inscribed, and Co, to their deserving patron.
EDCS-29601672.summary: A dedication by freedmen to their patron, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29900003.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Aureli Veri Augusti Marcus Servilius Fabianus legatus Augusti pro praetore templum vetustate corruptum a solo per regionem Montanensium restituit\n
EDCS-29900003.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, Marcus Servilius Fabianus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, restored this temple, corrupted by age, from its foundations through the region of the Montanenses.
EDCS-29900003.summary: An imperial dedication for the well-being of co-emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, recording the restoration of a temple by a provincial governor, likely mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-29900006.text_edition: \n In honorem domus divinae Soli Invicto sacrum Quintus Lucilius Piscinus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29900006.translation: In honor of the divine house, sacred to Sol Invictus, Quintus Lucilius Piscinus, centurion of the I Italica legion, willingly paid his vow, well deserved.
EDCS-29900006.summary: A dedication to the god Sol Invictus, made by a centurion of the I Italica legion, likely during the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29900009.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Valeriano vixit annos Flavi- Res vixit anos \n
EDCS-29900009.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Valerian lived X years. Flavi- Res lived X years.
EDCS-29900009.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription recording the ages of two individuals, Flavius Valerian and Flavi Res, with missing dates.

EDCS-29900505.text_edition: \n Flavius Tethianus sic quandam sic circetor sic de nomero sic catafractariorum annorum XXXVIII decessit de humana vita et demisit coniugem Nonnosa(m)? --vapir et Flavio! --- et Valerio sic\n
EDCS-29900505.translation: Flavius Tethianus, an armored cavalryman, died at the age of 38. He left behind his wife Nonnosa(?), Vapir, Flavio, and Valerio.
EDCS-29900505.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavius Tethianus, a 38-year-old member of the armored cavalry, mentioning his surviving family, likely late Roman period.

EDCS-29900509.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli III patri patriae ddominis nnnostris Valentiniano Theodosio et Arcadio \n
EDCS-29900509.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the third time, father of the country, to our lords Valentinian and Theodosius and Arcadius.
EDCS-29900509.summary: A long honorific inscription listing imperial titles for Trajan Hadrian Augustus and mentioning later emperors Valentinian, Theodosius, and Arcadius, suggesting a late antique dating.

EDCS-29900515.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Victorinus veteranus vixit annus sic L militavit annos XXV in vixillatione sic secondo sic Iscutariorom sic et pusuvit sic hunc sic Rebicithrioen sic cuniugen sic sua Antiochian sic gui sic ficit sic memuriam sic titulum in eo sic vonas sic vias viatur sic geras\n
EDCS-29900515.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Victorinus, a veteran, lived 50 years and served 25 years in the auxiliary detachment of the second Iscutariorum. He placed this memorial and inscription for his wife Antiochiana, who made it. May the roads you travel be good.
EDCS-29900515.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavius Victorinus, a veteran who served in the Iscutariorum auxiliary unit, and his wife Antiochiana, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-29900518.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely part of a name or epithet, with a significant lacuna.

EDCS-29900563.text_edition: \n Tu viator qui transis resta lege titulum obiter lege et repete sit Dis Manibus tuis terra levis Publius Valerius Tzita qui et Vitalis veteranus et Florentius filius miles et Vitalis filius miles Tzita pater posuit Laurentzio sic filio suo carissimo\n
EDCS-29900563.translation: You who pass by, stop and read this inscription, and read it again. May the earth be light on your Spirits of the Departed. Publius Valerius Tzita, who is also Vitalis, a veteran, and his son Florentius, a soldier, and Vitalis, his son, a soldier, and Tzita the father placed this for their dearest son Laurentius.
EDCS-29900563.summary: A funerary epitaph for Laurentius, a soldier, by his father Tzita and other relatives, including a veteran, urging passersby to remember him, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-30000418.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Ulpius Avediurnus vixit annis I\n
EDCS-30000418.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Ulpius Avediurnus lived for 1 year.
EDCS-30000418.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Ulpius Avediurnus, likely a child, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000420.text_edition: \n vixit annos IIXX Aelius Ingenuus civis Romanus defunctae coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30000420.translation: Aelius Ingenuus, Roman citizen, erected this for his deceased, well-deserving wife, who lived 20 years.
EDCS-30000420.summary: A funerary epitaph placed by Aelius Ingenuus for his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000421.text_edition: \n Marco Titio Marci filio Papiria Maximo IIvirali iterum quinquennalis coloniae flamini perpetuo praefecto saltus patrono fabrum Narcissus actor\n
EDCS-30000421.translation: To Marcus Titius Maximus, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, twice chief magistrate, quinquennial magistrate, perpetual priest of the colony, prefect of the estate, patron of the guilds. Narcissus, the agent, [erected this].
EDCS-30000421.summary: A dedication to Marcus Titius Maximus, a prominent local official in a Roman colony, likely erected by his agent, Narcissus, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000422.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ampi Victorini vixit nnos II menses VI faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30000422.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Built by [---], who lived 2 years and 6 months.
EDCS-30000422.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the short lifespan of an unnamed individual, likely a child, for whom the monument was constructed, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000423.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Servilio Valentino vixit nnos XXV Servilius Valens sibi et filio vivus fecit\n
EDCS-30000423.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Servilius Valens made this while alive for himself and his son, Servilio Valentino, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-30000423.summary: A funerary monument established by Servilius Valens for himself and his son, Servilio Valentino, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000424.translation: For Ulpius Pollia, the well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-30000424.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpius Pollia, likely a daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000427.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caius Attius lived [---].
EDCS-30000427.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the name Caius Attius and his lifespan, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000428.text_edition: \n Sabiniae Tranquillinae sanctissimae Augustae coniugi domini nostri Gordiani Pii Felicis Augusti cohors gemina Dacorum Gordiana X devota numini maiestati eius\n
EDCS-30000428.translation: To Sabinia Tranquillina, most holy wife of our lord Gordian Pius Felix Augustus, from the Second Dacian Cohort, Gordiana X, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-30000428.summary: A dedication by the Second Dacian Cohort to Sabinia Tranquillina, wife of Emperor Gordian III, dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-30000429.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Herculano Valeri Silvani veterani filio vixit annis XII mensibus VI Claudia Secundina mater filio bene merenti Firmi vicarii f(ilia) posuit\n
EDCS-30000429.translation: Claudia Secundina, daughter of Firmius Vicarius, erected this for her father, Valerio Herculano, son of Valerius Silvanus the veteran, who lived 12 years and 6 months, a well-deserving son.
EDCS-30000429.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Claudia Secundina for her son, Valerio Herculano, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000430.text_edition: \n patri bene merenti filius posuit Annius pro Flavia Ingenuae vixit annis \n
EDCS-30000430.translation: A son erected this for his well-deserving father. Annius [erected this] for Flavia Ingenua, who lived [---] years.
EDCS-30000430.summary: A funerary epitaph likely erected by a son for his father, also mentioning Flavia Ingenua and her lifespan, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000431.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) Aeliae Aviliae quae vixit annis XXXV Iulius Iulianus |(centurio) legionis fieri [iussit ------\n
EDCS-30000431.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelia Avilia, who lived 35 years. Iulius Iulianus, centurion of the legion, ordered this to be made.
EDCS-30000431.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Avilia, set up by her husband Iulius Iulianus, a centurion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100214.text_edition: \n Invicto Domiino Titus Claudius Cassianus votum libens animo posuit\n
EDCS-30100214.translation: To the Unconquered Lord. Titus Claudius Cassianus willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-30100214.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus by Titus Claudius Cassianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100799.text_edition: \n Geminius Severus evocatus legionis I Italicae Domitius Celerinus optio Aurelius Dionysius imaginifer Petronius Ingenuus tesserarius Petronius SN cornicen Mettius Aquila imaginifer Valerius Min cornicen nnus Celer optio Papirius Vale tubicen Pomponius n tibicen Aurelius Cornelianus custos armorum Iulius Felix custos armorum Cocceius Urb cornicen Perennius Primus cornicen Sal Valens cornicen Iulius Rusticus evocatus\n
EDCS-30100799.translation: Geminius Severus, a recalled soldier of the I Italica legion; Domitius Celerinus, optio; Aurelius Dionysius, imaginifer; Petronius Ingenuus, tesserarius; Petronius SN, cornicen; Mettius Aquila, imaginifer; Valerius Min[...], cornicen; nnus Celer, optio; Papirius Vale, tubicen; Pomponius n, tibicen; Aurelius Cornelianus, custos armorum; Iulius Felix, custos armorum; Cocceius Urb[...], cornicen; Perennius Primus, cornicen; Sal Valens, cornicen; Iulius Rusticus, a recalled soldier.
EDCS-30100799.summary: This inscription lists the names and ranks of numerous soldiers from the I Italica legion, including recalled soldiers, an optio, and various signalmen, likely marking a military building or dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100800.text_edition: \n Gaius Plotius Maro aram Invicto posuit pro se et suis \n
EDCS-30100800.translation: Gaius Plotius Maro set up an altar to the Unconquered one for himself and his family.
EDCS-30100800.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicating an altar to Sol Invictus by Gaius Plotius Maro for the benefit of his family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100807.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonio Floro beneficiario consularis militavit nnos XXIII vixit nnos XL Antonius Florianus optio legionis I Italicae frater posuit\n
EDCS-30100807.translation: To the departed spirits. Antonio Florus, beneficiarius of the consular governor, served for 23 years and lived for 40 years. Antonius Florianus, optio of the I Italica legion, his brother, set this up.
EDCS-30100807.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Antonio Florus, a beneficiarius of a consular governor who served for 23 years and lived for 40, erected by his brother Antonius Florianus, an optio of the I Italica legion, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100808.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cocceius Helius posuit vivo suo sibi et Titiae Matrinae coniugi suae bene merenti quae vixit nnos XXX obita ad villa sic sua sic titulum posuit\n
EDCS-30100808.translation: To the departed spirits. Cocceius Helius set this up for himself while alive, and for his wife Titia Matrina, who deserved well and lived 30 years; having died at his villa, he set up this tombstone. Such was his life, such is the inscription. Greetings, traveler, and farewell.
EDCS-30100808.summary: This is a self-funded funerary inscription by Cocceius Helius for himself and his deserving wife Titia Matrina, who lived 30 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100809.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus vilicus et coniugi ssimae bene merenti rus ro vixit \n
EDCS-30100809.translation: To the departed spirits. For the most deserving farm manager and his wife, who lived [duration unknown].
EDCS-30100809.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a farm manager and his deserving wife, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100813.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Dusia vixit annos Aurelii Cocceius et Genialis et Claudius et Vindix sic fili Herculani et heredes matri pientissimae vivo titulum posuerunt bene meritae memoriae causae ave viator et vale\n
EDCS-30100813.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Dusia lived for [duration unknown]. Aurelii Cocceius and Genialis and Claudius and Vindix, sons of Herculanus, and their heirs, set up this tombstone for their most dutiful mother while alive, for her well-deserved memory. Greetings, traveler, and farewell.
EDCS-30100813.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by the sons and heirs of Claudia Dusia for their dutiful mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100814.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Ulmeto pro salute Imperatoris Aeli Antonini Caesaris per magistrum Lucium Valerium Maxellius sic possuit sic de suo votum solvit libens Imperatore Antonino III consule\n
EDCS-30100814.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina. The Roman citizens and the Bessi living in the village of Ulmeto, for the safety of the Emperor Aelius Antoninus Caesar, through the magistrate Lucius Valerius Maxellius, willingly paid this vow from his own funds. Such was his dedication, such was the payment. During the consulship of Emperor Antoninus III.
EDCS-30100814.summary: This is a public votive inscription by Roman citizens and the Bessi tribe dedicated to Jupiter and Juno for the safety of Emperor Antoninus Pius, dated to his third consulship (circa 139-161 AD).

EDCS-30100815.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Ursinianus miles officii praesidis vixit nnos XXV militavit nnum unum menses VIII et soror nnorum IIII posuit Martinus veteranus ex quaestionario pater eorum estote t vale viator\n
EDCS-30100815.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavius Ursinianus, soldier in the office of the governor, lived 25 years, served for one year and 8 months. And his sister, 4 years old, set this up. Martinus, a veteran from the quaestorship, is their father. Be well, and farewell, traveler.
EDCS-30100815.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavius Ursinianus, a soldier and his infant sister, erected by their veteran father Martinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100822.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Bussumario Gaius Atilius Eutyches Augustalis coloniae Apulensis pro salute sua suorumque omnium exedram longam pedes XXX latam pedes XXV cum arcu pecunia suae f<e>cit\n
EDCS-30100822.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Atilius Eutyches, an Augustalis from the colony of Apula, built this long hall, 30 feet wide and 25 feet broad with an arch, from his own money for his own health and that of all his family.
EDCS-30100822.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter recording the construction of a public building by a local official in Apula, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30100830.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-30100830.text_edition: \n Ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias Philippo Augusto II et Philippo consulibus cohortis V praetoriae Philippianae piae vindicis Marco Aurelio Marci filio Ioviniano Ulpia Serdica descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea qu(a)e fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Publi Aeli Marcelli Marci Aureli Dizes Marci Ulpi Longini Marci Aureli Prisci Publi Aeli Crescentis Marci Aureli Heraclidae Marci Aureli Victoris\n
EDCS-30100830.translation: On the seventh day before the Ides of January, during the consulship of Philippus Augustus II and Philippus, Marcus Aurelius Iovinianus, son of Marcus, of the Julian tribe, from the Philippian Praetorian cohort, pious and victorious, a copy and recognition from a bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of the divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva, by Publius Aelius Marcellus, Marcus Aurelius Dizes, Marcus Ulpius Longinus, Marcus Aurelius Priscus, Publius Aelius Crescens, Marcus Aurelius Heraclidas, Marcus Aurelius Victor.
EDCS-30100830.summary: This is an official copy of a bronze tablet, likely a grant of citizenship or discharge, dated to 163 CE, related to soldiers and officials serving in Rome.

EDCS-30100833.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-30100833.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XI imperator XXI censor perpetuus consul XVI pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in alis septem et cohortibus decem et quinque quae appellantur I Vespasiana Dardanorum et I Flavia Gaetulorum et I Pannoniorum et II Claudia Gallorum et Gallorum Flaviana et Gallorum Atectorigiana et Hispanorum et I Raetorum et I Bracaraugustanorum et I Lusitanorum Cyrenaica et I Flavia Commagenorum et I Sugambrorum tironum et I Sugambrorum veterana et II Chalcidenorum et II Lucensium et II Bracaraugustanorum et II Flavia Bessorum et II et III et IIII et VII Gallorum et Ubiorum et sunt in Moesia inferiore sub Sexto Octavio Frontone qui quina vicena plurave stipendia meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XVIII Kalendas Iulias Tiberio Iulio Celso Polemaeano Lucio Stertinio Avito consulibus cohortis VII Gallorum cui praeest Caius Iulius Cai filius Collina Capito equiti Macrino Acresionis filio Apameno et Macro filio eius et Saturnino filio eius et Augustae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XI imperator XXI censor perpetuus consul XVI pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in alis septem et cohortibus decem et quinque quae appellantur I Vespasiana Dardanorum et I Flavia Gaetulorum et I Pannoniorum et II Claudia Gallorum et Gallorum Flaviana et Gallorum Atectorigiana et Hispanorum et I Raetorum et I Bracaraugustanorum et I Lusitanorum Cyrenaica et I Flavia Commagenorum et I Sugambrorum tironum et I Sugambrorum veterana et II Chalcidenorum et II Lucensium et II Bracaraugustanorum et II Flavia Bessorum et II et III et IIII et VII Gallorum et Ubiorum et sunt in Moesia inferiore sub Sexto Octavio Frontone qui quina vicena plurave stipendia meruerunt item Dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XVIII Kalendas Iulias Tiberio Iulio Celso Polemaeano Lucio Stertinio Avito consulibus cohortis VII Gallorum cui praeest Caius Iulius Cai filius Collina Capito equiti Macrino Acresionis filio Apameno et Macro filio eius et Saturnino filio eius et Augustae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae Auli Lappi Polliani Cai Iuli Helini Marci Galli Fortis Cnaei Matici Barbari Quinti Orifi Cupiti Luci Pulli Sperati Luci Pulli Verecundi\n
EDCS-30100833.translation: Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the divine Vespasian, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 11th time, Imperator for the 21st time, perpetual censor, consul for the 16th time, father of the country, to the equites and infantry serving in seven alae and fifteen cohorts, which are called I Vespasiana Dardanorum, I Flavia Gaetulorum, I Pannoniorum, II Claudia Gallorum, Gallorum Flaviana, Gallorum Atectorigiana, Hispanorum, I Raetorum, I Bracaraugustanorum, I Lusitanorum Cyrenaica, I Flavia Commagenorum, I Sugambrorum tironum, I Sugambrorum veterana, II Chalcidenorum, II Lucensium, II Bracaraugustanorum, II Flavia Bessorum, II, III, IIII, and VII Gallorum, and Ubiorum, and are in Lower Moesia under Sextus Octavius Fronto, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, likewise those discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, each only one wife, on the eighteenth day before the Kalends of July, during the consulship of Tiberius Julius Celsus Polemaeanus and Lucius Stertinius Avitus, to the knight Macrinus, son of Acresio, from Apamea, and his son Marcus, and his son Saturninus, and his daughter Augusta, of the 7th Gallorum cohort, commanded by Gaius Julius Capito, son of Gaius, of the Collina tribe. A copy and recognition from a bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of the divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva, by Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the divine Vespasian, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 11th time, Imperator for the 21st time, perpetual censor, consul for the 16th time, father of the country, to the equites and infantry serving in seven alae and fifteen cohorts, which are called I Vespasiana Dardanorum, I Flavia Gaetulorum, I Pannoniorum, II Claudia Gallorum, Gallorum Flaviana, Gallorum Atectorigiana, Hispanorum, I Raetorum, I Bracaraugustanorum, I Lusitanorum Cyrenaica, I Flavia Commagenorum, I Sugambrorum tironum, I Sugambrorum veterana, II Chalcidenorum, II Lucensium, II Bracaraugustanorum, II Flavia Bessorum, II, III, IIII, and VII Gallorum, and Ubiorum, and are in Lower Moesia under Sextus Octavius Fronto, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, likewise those discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, each only one wife, on the eighteenth day before the Kalends of July, during the consulship of Tiberius Julius Celsus Polemaeanus and Lucius Stertinius Avitus, to the knight Macrinus, son of Acresio, from Apamea, and his son Marcus, and his son Saturninus, and his daughter Augusta, of the 7th Gallorum cohort, commanded by Gaius Julius Capito, son of Gaius, of the Collina tribe, from a bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall of Aulus Lappius Pollianus, Gaius Julius Helinus, Marcus Gallus Fortis, Cnaeus Meticus Barbarus, Quintus Orifius Cupitus, Lucius Pullius Speratus, Lucius Pullius Verecundus.
EDCS-30100833.summary: This is a significant imperial rescript issued by Emperor Domitian in 93 CE, granting Roman citizenship and the right to legal marriage to a specific group of auxiliary soldiers serving in Moesia and their families.

EDCS-30100836.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cives Romani consistentes vico classicorum curam agente magistro \n
EDCS-30100836.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Roman citizens residing in the Vicus Classicorum, with the magistrate in charge.
EDCS-30100836.summary: This is a brief dedication to Jupiter by Roman citizens living in a specific neighborhood, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-30100837.text_edition: \n Apollini Auluzelo Titus Salvius Chresimus ex imperio posuit\n
EDCS-30100837.translation: Titus Salvius Chresimus set this up to Apollo Auluzelus by order.
EDCS-30100837.summary: This is a votive offering dedicated to Apollo Auluzelus by Titus Salvius Chresimus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30100838.text_edition: \n Lucius Cultius Iovi Optimo Maximo votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100838.translation: Lucius Cultius gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-30100838.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by Lucius Cultius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100840.translation: Valerius Ingenuus set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-30100840.summary: This is a simple votive inscription by Valerius Ingenuus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200644.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Dinnio praefecto vehiculorum vixit annos LX militavit annos XXXV et Aureliae Salviae vixit annos LXX hic sepulti sunt quorum memoriae filii bene meritis patri et matri Flavius Bitianus protector domesticus fecit\n
EDCS-30200644.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Dinnus, prefect of the transport service, who lived 60 years and served 35 years, and Aurelia Salvia, who lived 70 years, are buried here. Their sons made this memorial for their well-deserving father and mother: Flavius Bitianus, a domestic guard.
EDCS-30200644.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Flavius Dinnus, a prefect of the transport service, and his wife Aurelia Salvia, set up by their sons, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-30200647.text_edition: \n Deanae sacrum Liburnia Valentina ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-30200647.translation: Sacred to Diana. Liburnia Valentina set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-30200647.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the goddess Diana by Liburnia Valentina, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200649.text_edition: \n Caius Annius Cai filius Aniensi Milo Luca veteranus legionis V Macedonicae ex testamento \n
EDCS-30200649.translation: Gaius Annius Milo, son of Gaius, of the Aniensis tribe, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, by his will.
EDCS-30200649.summary: This inscription records a posthumous dedication or memorial for Gaius Annius Milo, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica, established by his will, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200650.text_edition: \n Tito Aurelio Titi filio Papiria Flavino primipilari et principi ordinis coloniae Oescensium et buleutae civitatium Tyranorum Dionysiopolitanorum Marcianopolitanorum Tungrorum et Aquincensium patrono collegii fabrum honorato a divo Magno Antonino Augusto HS L milia nummum et XXV gradum promotionis ob alacritatem virtutis adversus hostes Getas et res prospere Tyrae gestas Claudius Nicomedes buleuta civitatis Tyranorum amico dignissimo locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-30200650.translation: To Titus Aurelius Flavius, son of Titus, of the Papiria tribe, former centurion and chief of the order of the colony of Oescus, and councilor of the cities of the Tyrants, Dionysiopolitans, Marcianopolitans, Tungri, and Aquincenses, patron of the guild of craftsmen, honored by the Divine Magn Antoninus Augustus with 75,000 sesterces and promotion to the 25th rank for his zealous valor against the Getae enemies and for successfully managing affairs at Tyras. A place was granted by decree of the decurions to Claudius Nicomedes, councilor of the Tyrants, his most worthy friend.
EDCS-30200650.summary: This inscription records a public honor bestowed upon Titus Aurelius Flavius, a prominent local official and patron, by the city of Oescus and its allies, likely dating to the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-30200651.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Firmio Luci filio Narbone Valentino veterano vixit nnos LXX Firmia Secunda liberta patrono bene merenti\n
EDCS-30200651.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Firmis Valens of Narbo, a veteran, lived 70 years. Firmia Secunda, his freedwoman, erected this for her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-30200651.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Firmis Valens, a veteran, set up by his freedwoman, Firmia Secunda.

EDCS-30200652.text_edition: \n vius s veteranus cohortis IIII Gallorum vixit annos L militavit annos XX hic situs est Martialis heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30200652.translation: To the departed spirit of [name missing], a veteran of the Fourth Gallic Cohort, lived 50 years, served 20 years. Martialis, his heir, saw to the erection of this monument.
EDCS-30200652.summary: A funerary inscription for a veteran of the Fourth Gallic Cohort, erected by his heir, Martialis.

EDCS-30200653.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Iulianus vixit annis XXIII Ulpia Valentina mater Ulpius Crescens pater bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-30200653.translation: To the departed spirits. Ulpius Iulianus lived 23 years. Ulpia Valentina, mother, and Ulpius Crescens, father, erected this for him as he well deserved.
EDCS-30200653.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ulpius Iulianus, who died at the age of 23, set up by his parents.

EDCS-30200654.text_edition: \n Maria Maryllina et Marius Iustus annorum III Marius Agathopus liberis suis non libens fecit\n
EDCS-30200654.translation: Maria Maryllina and Marius Iustus, aged 3 years, and Marius Agathopus. Not willingly did they erect this for their children.
EDCS-30200654.summary: This inscription records the grief of parents, Maria Maryllina and Marius Iustus, for their deceased children, one of whom was named Marius Agathopus and was 3 years old.

EDCS-30200655.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Leubini et meorum omnium\n
EDCS-30200655.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the health of Leubinus and all my family.
EDCS-30200655.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter for the health of the dedicator, Leubinus, and his family.

EDCS-30200656.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Drigisa veteranus vixit annos L menses V dies Valeria coniux vixit annos XL Aelius Valeintinus filius vixit annos et Aelia Sissa vixit annos Aurelius Vitalis pater bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-30200656.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Drigisa, veteran, lived 50 years and 5 months. Valeria, his wife, lived 40 years. Aelius Valentinus, his son, lived [number of years missing]. Aelia Sissa lived [number of years missing]. Aurelius Vitalis, the father, erected this for the well-deserving deceased.
EDCS-30200656.summary: This funerary inscription for Aurelius Drigisa, a veteran, and his wife Valeria, also records the lifespans of their son Aelius Valentinus and daughter Aelia Sissa, and was erected by Aurelius Vitalis, likely the father of Aurelius Drigisa.

EDCS-30200658.text_edition: \n O LLIV veterano legionis Italicae Claudia Marcellina marito pientissimo et sibi vivo posuit\n
EDCS-30200658.translation: To [name missing], a veteran of the Italian Legion. Claudia Marcellina erected this for her most devoted husband and for herself while alive.
EDCS-30200658.summary: This funerary inscription for a veteran of the Italian Legion was set up by his wife, Claudia Marcellina, who also dedicated a part for herself.

EDCS-30200659.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris divi Traiani Parthici filio sic divi Nervae nepotis Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae pontificis maximo sic tribuniciae potestatis XX consulis III Marcus Antius Rufinus inter Moesos et Thraces fines posuit\n
EDCS-30200659.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar, son of the Divine Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the Divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus erected this at the border between the Moesians and Thracians.
EDCS-30200659.summary: This inscription, issued under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, records the establishment of a border marker by Marcus Antius Rufinus between Moesia and Thrace.

EDCS-30200660.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Claudio Valenti et Corinni Tiberius Claudius Secundus pater et Claudia Fortunata mater\n
EDCS-30200660.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Valens and Corinna. Tiberius Claudius Secundus, father, and Claudia Fortunata, mother.
EDCS-30200660.summary: This funerary inscription is for Tiberius Claudius Valens and Corinna, set up by their parents.

EDCS-30200661.text_edition: \n Hirone sic santo Flavius Mestrius Iustus? mil(es) cohortis II Lucensium votum posuit\n
EDCS-30200661.translation: Flavius Mestrius Iustus, a soldier of the II Lucensian cohort, set up this votive offering to the holy Heron.
EDCS-30200661.summary: A soldier of the II Lucensian cohort dedicated a votive offering to the god Heron, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30200662.translation: Messianus (dedicated this) to the holy Heron.
EDCS-30200662.summary: Messianus dedicated something to the god Heron, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30200663.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Annia Dentustaina vixit nnos LVIII Iustus Rescuporis buleuta coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30200663.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Annia Dentustaina lived 58 years. Iustus Rescuporis, a council member, set this up for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-30200663.summary: A funerary monument records the death of Annia Dentustaina, set up by her husband Iustus Rescuporis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200665.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VI imperatori XI consuli II patri patriae proconsuli dedicante Caio Ovinio Tertullo legato Auggustorum pro praetore Titus Aurelius Aquila tribunus cohortis II Mattiacorum devotus numini eius de suo posuit ante diem XIII Kalendas Augustas Saturnino et Gallo consulibus\n
EDCS-30200665.translation: Titus Aurelius Aquila, tribune of the II Mattiacorum cohort, devoted to his divine majesty, set up (this monument) at his own expense before the 13th day before the Kalends of August, during the consulship of Saturninus and Gallus, to the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, proconsul, dedicated by Gaius Ovinus Tertullus, imperial legate with praetorian authority.
EDCS-30200665.summary: The II Mattiacorum cohort, under the command of Titus Aurelius Aquila, erected a monument to Emperor Septimius Severus in 202 CE, during the governorship of Gaius Ovinus Tertullus.

EDCS-30200666.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Iulio Vero Maximino Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis consuli patri patriae cohors I Cispadensium Maximiniana devota numini maiestatique eius de pecunia quaesturae dedicante Domitio Antigono clarissimo viro legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-30200666.translation: The I Cispadensium Maximiniana cohort, devoted to his divine majesty and authority, set up (this monument) from the funds of the quaestorship, dedicated by Domitius Antigonus, a most distinguished man, imperial legate with praetorian authority, to the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul, father of the country.
EDCS-30200666.summary: The I Cispadensium Maximiniana cohort erected a monument to Emperor Maximinus Thrax, financed by the quaestorship and dedicated by legate Domitius Antigonus, likely in the 230s CE.

EDCS-30200667.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto patri patriae pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli II proconsuli cohors I Hispanorum Gordiana devota numini maiestatique eius dedicante C legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-30200667.translation: The I Hispanorum Gordiana cohort, devoted to his divine majesty and authority, set up (this monument) to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, father of the country, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, dedicated by C. [name missing], imperial legate with praetorian authority.
EDCS-30200667.summary: The I Hispanorum Gordiana cohort erected a monument to Emperor Gordian III, dedicated by a legate, during his reign (238-244 CE).

EDCS-30200669.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Diogati qui vixit annis LXX et Marinae Demetriae \n
EDCS-30200669.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Diogatus, who lived 70 years, and Marina Demetria.
EDCS-30200669.summary: A funerary inscription for Valerius Diogatus and Marina Demetria, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200672.text_edition: \n Termini positi territorii civitatis Ausdecensium adversus Dacos secundum civitatis acta C Vexarus terminavit opus hinc excessent Daci termini territorii civitatis obligati sint Messalla PROX terminos posuit territorii iussu Helvi Pertinacis consularis nostri per Anternium Antoninum tribunum cohortis I Cilicum\n
EDCS-30200672.translation: Boundary stones were placed for the territory of the Ausdecensian community against the Dacians, according to the community's decrees. C. Vexarus marked the work; let the Dacians depart from here. The boundaries of the community's territory shall be bound. Messalla PROX placed the boundaries of the territory by order of Helvius Pertinax, our consular governor, through Anternius Antoninus, tribune of the I Cilicum cohort.
EDCS-30200672.summary: Boundary stones defining the territory of the Ausdecensian community against the Dacians were set up in the Roman Imperial period under the supervision of a consular governor and a tribune.

EDCS-30200673.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Valenti filio vixit nnos XII et Iulio Vitali filio vixit nnos XVII Caius Iulius Hermes et Iulia Rhodope parentes se vivi faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-30200673.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Iulius Valens, who lived 12 years, and for Iulius Vitalis, who lived 17 years. Gaius Iulius Hermes and Iulia Rhodope, parents, had this made during their lifetime.
EDCS-30200673.summary: A funerary epitaph for two young sons, Iulius Valens and Iulius Vitalis, commissioned by their parents Caius Iulius Hermes and Iulia Rhodope, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200674.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Vettius vixit annos LXXXX Tiberii Claudii Ingenuus et Marcus et Florus \n
EDCS-30200674.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Claudius Vettius lived 90 years. (Also mentioned are) Tiberius Claudius Ingenuus, Marcus, and Florus.
EDCS-30200674.summary: A funerary inscription for Tiberius Claudius Vettius, who lived to 90, and mentions three other individuals, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200677.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris <L=P>(uci) Aeli cives Romani veterani vico Novioduni sub curam sic Silvi Casionis et Publi SOCVFVNI et quaestore Caio Alexandri Idibus Iunis Orf<i=A>to et Rufo consulibus \n
EDCS-30200677.translation: Sacred to Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aelius, Roman citizen, veteran, of the village of Noviodunum, under the care of Silvius Casionus and Publius Socofunius, and with Gaius Alexandrius as quaestor, on the Ides of June, with Orfitus and Rufus as consuls.
EDCS-30200677.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter, likely by a veteran in the Roman province of Dacia, dating to the consulship of Orfitus and Rufus (likely AD 240).

EDCS-30200679.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Capitoni decurioni veterano alae Pannoniorum donis donato ab Imperatore Vespasiano ob virtutem Titus Flavius Castus filius faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30200679.translation: Titus Flavius Castus, son of Titus Flavius Capito, decurion and veteran of the Pannonian cavalry, honored with gifts by the Emperor Vespasian for his valor, took care to have this made.
EDCS-30200679.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a son for his father, a decorated veteran of the Pannonian cavalry, likely during the Flavian dynasty (AD 69-96).

EDCS-30200682.text_edition: \n Venustae Marcellius Clemens Claudius Mucasius socrui et Claudiae Rufianae matri \n
EDCS-30200682.translation: Marcellius Clemens Claudius Mucasius for Venusta, his mother-in-law, and Claudia Rufiana, his mother.
EDCS-30200682.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcellius Clemens Claudius Mucasius for his mother-in-law and his mother.

EDCS-30200683.text_edition: \n IIIDIa mater bene merenti filio suo et sibi posuerunt vixit annis XXV pater IC annorum LX mater annorum XXXX\n
EDCS-30200683.translation: His mother erected this for her well-deserving son and for herself; he lived 25 years; father [lived] 60 years; mother 40 years.
EDCS-30200683.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the ages of a deceased son and his parents.

EDCS-30200687.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar restituerunt per Flavium Lucilianum legatum pro praetore milia passuum C\n
EDCS-30200687.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible, Augustus, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, restored 100 Roman miles through Flavius Lucilianus, governor and propraetor.
EDCS-30200687.summary: This inscription records the restoration of 100 miles of road by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, under the administration of the legate Flavius Lucilianus, likely around AD 236-238.

EDCS-30300737.summary: This inscription is a very short dedication or epitaph naming Aurelius Listar.

EDCS-30300739.translation: Figulus took care to have this made. Acinianus.
EDCS-30300739.summary: This inscription records that Acinianus commissioned Figulus to make something.

EDCS-30300741.summary: This inscription, likely from Rome, is dedicated to someone named Gordianus.

EDCS-30600104.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop of Flavius Nicanus, who was a maker of vessels.

EDCS-30600105.translation: Workshop of Flavius Nicanus, M. B. N.
EDCS-30600105.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop of Flavius Nicanus and includes possibly abbreviated names or titles.

EDCS-31300285.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Daizi Comozoi vixit annos L interfectus a Castabocis Iustus et Valerius patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-31300285.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Daizi Comozoi lived 50 years; killed by the Castaboces. Iustus and Valerius erected this for their deserving father.
EDCS-31300285.summary: A funerary epitaph for Daizi Comozoi, noting her age and violent death, erected by her sons Iustus and Valerius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400046.text_edition: \n annos L hic situs est Caius Oppius Varus eques alae Au[gustae ------\n
EDCS-31400046.translation: Caius Oppius Varus, a cavalryman of the ala Augusta, lived 50 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-31400046.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Oppius Varus, a cavalryman of the ala Augusta, recording his age and burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400048.text_edition: \n Silvano et Silvestribus Iulianus c<um=XXV> merito \n
EDCS-31400048.translation: To Silvanus and the woodland spirits. Iulianus, [with? given?] 25 [units?], [erected this] deservedly.
EDCS-31400048.summary: A dedication to the deities Silvanus and the Silvestres by Iulianus, likely a soldier or official, possibly recording a quantity or value, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400050.text_edition: \n -ios Vindillus iunior Gentiano et Basso consulibus\n
EDCS-31400050.translation: -ios Vindillus the younger, during the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus.
EDCS-31400050.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning Vindillus the younger and two consuls, Gentianus and Bassus, allowing for dating to their consular year, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400051.summary: A simple dedication to the goddess Diana Regina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400053.translation: Saturninus, a centurion, [erected this] from a vow.
EDCS-31400053.summary: A dedication by Saturninus, a centurion, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400054.text_edition: \n D(is) Manibus nus et Publius VIV NP coniugi se vivo fecerunt\n
EDCS-31400054.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Nus and Publius made this for their spouse [or child] while alive.
EDCS-31400054.summary: A funerary epitaph where Nus and Publius dedicated a monument to a deceased family member during their own lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400055.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XII consul II proconsul \n
EDCS-31400055.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, in his twelfth year of tribunician power, consul for the second time, proconsul.
EDCS-31400055.summary: A formal titulature of Emperor Caracalla, listing his various titles and powers, dating his reign to the period after his twelfth tribunician power grant and second consulship, likely between 208 and 211 CE.

EDCS-31400059.text_edition: \n LAS Aurelio Mica centurioni legionis XI Claudiae? et filio suo militavit annos IIII vixit annos XX titulum pater bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400059.translation: LAS [or L. Assius?] Aurelius Mica, centurion of the legion XI Claudia, and his son; he served for 4 years, lived for 20 years; his father erected this monument for the deserving man.
EDCS-31400059.summary: A funerary monument for Aurelius Mica, a centurion of the legion XI Claudia, who served for 4 years and lived to 20, erected by his father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400072.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Aurelius Valens miles legionis I Italicae ex votu sic posuit\n
EDCS-31400072.translation: To Juno Regina, Aurelius Valens, a soldier of the legion I Italica, erected this from a vow.
EDCS-31400072.summary: A dedication to Juno Regina by Aurelius Valens, a soldier of the legion I Italica, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400075.text_edition: \n ------ Claudio] Mansueto annorum Claudia M filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-31400075.translation: To Claudio Mansueto, aged [years], his most devoted son Claudia.
EDCS-31400075.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a boy named Claudio Mansueto, dedicated by his mother Claudia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400076.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Valerius Calius vixit \n
EDCS-31400076.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Valerius Calius lived [for X years/months/days].
EDCS-31400076.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Valerius Calius, a standard dedication to the underworld spirits, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400083.text_edition: \n uae form umo com ? lapidi situs ? Valens |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae CO\n
EDCS-31400083.translation: To the beautiful [name?], placed on this stone, Valens, a centurion of the eleventh Claudian legion, [erected this].
EDCS-31400083.summary: This inscription likely records the placement of a monument or tomb by Valens, a centurion of the Legio XI Claudia, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400085.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Heroni OILINI Sozomenus O et Valerius IO\n
EDCS-31400085.translation: To the holy god Heron. Sozomenus and Valerius [dedicated this] from [wealth?].
EDCS-31400085.summary: This is a dedication to the god Heron by Sozomenus and Valerius, possibly related to a cult or temple, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400086.text_edition: \n Heroni invicto Iaehetav vilicus Luci Aeli Marciani clarissimi viri servus e vivo suis posuit\n
EDCS-31400086.translation: To Heron the unconquered. Iaehetav, steward of the distinguished man Lucius Aelius Marcianus, placed this from his own means while alive.
