EDCS-31400086.summary: This is a dedication to the god Heron by a steward named Iaehetav, acting on behalf of the prominent official Lucius Aelius Marcianus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400088.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Inserus IIvir pro se et suorum sic ex votum sic posuit aram\n
EDCS-31400088.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Aelius Inserus, as a second-time magistrate, for himself and his family, fulfilled this vow and placed this altar.
EDCS-31400088.summary: This inscription records Aelius Inserus, a local magistrate, dedicating an altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus in fulfillment of a vow for his family, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400089.translation: Rufus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-31400089.summary: This is a brief votive inscription by Rufus, indicating the fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400092.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Valentina quae vixit nnos XI dies LVIIII Iulius Valentinus bis IIviralis municipii Tropaei pater eius cum Iulia Surilla avia eius posuit ne qui post hoc aperuerit dabet sic fisco |(denariorum) duo milia quin<g=C>entos Dis Manibus Iuliae Surillae quae vixit nnos LVIII Caius Iulius Valentinus IIviralis iterum IIvir <et=I> Iulii Valens et Caius Fuscinus Surus matri bene merenti posuerunt \n
EDCS-31400092.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Valentina, who lived 11 years and 59 days. Her father, Iulius Valentinus, a twice-elected magistrate of the municipality of Tropaeum, with her grandmother, Iulia Surilla, placed this; whoever opens this hereafter will pay a fine of two thousand five hundred denarii to the treasury. To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Iulius Valentinus, twice a magistrate, again a magistrate, and Iulii Valens and Caius Fuscinus Surus, placed this for their well-deserving mother.
EDCS-31400092.summary: This is a complex funerary monument for Iulia Valentina and her grandmother Iulia Surilla, erected by family members including two magistrates, and includes a penalty for disturbing the graves, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31400093.text_edition: \n liae mae Calpurni Macri Rufi ANIATA\n
EDCS-31400093.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription commemorating a woman named Calpurnia Macri Rufi, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32200941.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae pro salute Imperatoris Ma Augusti Caesidius Amandus beneficiarius viri clarissimi consularis voto posuit\n
EDCS-32200941.translation: To Nemesis Augusta, for the health of the Emperor Augustus. Caesidius Amandus, an official of a distinguished consular officer, placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-32200941.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to the goddess Nemesis, dedicated by Caesidius Amandus, an official, for the safety of the Emperor Augustus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32200943.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus VVBIREO CEHIAE Myrino HAPL IX OSIROR A XIII vixit Romae AI pater TICTIB AFRO R ES \n
EDCS-32200943.translation: To the departed spirits. Myrino Haplxios lived in Rome for 13 years. His father, Tiqtius Afro, placed this.
EDCS-32200943.summary: A funerary inscription for Myrino Haplxios, set up by his father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200944.text_edition: \n Pro salute d(omini)? n(ostri) Marci Antoni Gordiani P(ii) F(elicis) Aug(usti) pont(ificis) maximi tribuniciae potestatis patris patriae MV per ISCC IIvirum et Marcum Ulpium Antoninum et Caium Iulium Messium aediles et Iulii sic Marcus sic et Marcus sic f et Scribonios Respectum et Gaium Pio et Proculo consulibus\n
EDCS-32200944.translation: For the safety of our lord Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, through the duumvirate and Marcus Ulpius Antoninus and Gaius Julius Messius, aediles, and Iulii, namely Marcus and Marcus, and Scribonii Respectus and Gaius, and Proculus, in the consulship.
EDCS-32200944.summary: A dedication concerning the health of Emperor Gordian III and various officials, likely from a Roman provincial city, in the period of his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-32200948.text_edition: \n Lucius Memmius Valens decurio municipii ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-32200948.translation: Lucius Memmius Valens, a decurion of the municipality, placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-32200948.summary: A votive inscription set up by Lucius Memmius Valens, a local official, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200950.text_edition: \n Aelio Antonino Firmo duumvirali municipii Tropaei patri <et=I> Aeliae Quirillae sorori Aelius Antoninus Sabinus duumvir municipii supra sripti et Aelius Antoninus Aeternalis signifer legionis XI Claudiae bene merentibus posuerunt \n
EDCS-32200950.translation: Aelius Antoninus Sabinus, duumvir of the municipality of Tropaei, and Aelius Antoninus Aeternalis, standard-bearer of the XI Claudian legion, placed this for their father Aelius Antoninus Firmus, holder of duumviral office in the municipality, and for their sister Aelia Quirilla, who deserved well of them.
EDCS-32200950.summary: A funerary inscription for a father and sister, set up by two sons, one a local magistrate and the other a legionary standard-bearer, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200951.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Valerius IVIIN Publius Aelius ICRO Aemilius Priscus frater eius et Publius Aelius Apollinaris filius eius MILIT\n
EDCS-32200951.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Aelius Valerius IVIIN, Publius Aelius ICRO, his brother, and Publius Aelius Apollinaris, his son, MILIT.
EDCS-32200951.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Valerius IVIIN, set up by his brother and son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200952.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Aemilius Severus |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae vixit annis LXVIII Aelia Marcellina coniux et Aemilius Modestus et Aemilius Proculus et Aemilius Severus filii et herdes patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-32200952.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Aemilius Severus, centurion of the XIII Gemina legion, lived 68 years. Aelia Marcellina, his wife, and Aemilius Modestus, Aemilius Proculus, and Aemilius Severus, his sons and heirs, placed this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-32200952.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Aemilius Severus, a centurion, set up by his wife and sons, likely from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-32200953.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Artorius Saturninus Siscia ex duplicario veteranus cohortis I Lusitanorum vixit annos XL militavit annos XX hic situs est Caius Artorius et Roscia Saturnina et Artorius Saturninus filii patri pientissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-32200953.translation: To the departed spirits. Artorius Saturninus of Siscia, a veteran ex-duplicarius of the I Cohort of Lusitanians, lived 40 years, served 20 years, is buried here. Caius Artorius, Roscia Saturnina, and Artorius Saturninus, his sons, placed this for their most devoted father.
EDCS-32200953.summary: A funerary inscription for Artorius Saturninus, a veteran soldier, set up by his children, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200954.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Cai filius Valens |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae domo Amasia vixit annos L militavit annos XXX Valeria Frontina coniugi et Iulia Hadriana Caius Iulius Iulianus Caius Iulius Valentinus filii patri pientissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-32200954.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Iulius Valens, son of Caius, centurion of the V Macedonica legion, from Amasia, lived 50 years, served 30 years. Valeria Frontina, his wife, and Iulia Hadriana, Caius Iulius Iulianus, Caius Iulius Valentinus, his sons, placed this for their most devoted father.
EDCS-32200954.summary: A funerary inscription for Caius Iulius Valens, a centurion from Amasia, set up by his wife and sons, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200955.text_edition: \n Crescenti Rigozi vixit annos LV OI Helpis liberta et Cornelius Vitalis I faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-32200955.translation: Crescentius Rigozi lived 55 years. Helpis, his freedwoman, and Cornelius Vitalis arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-32200955.summary: A funerary inscription for Crescentius Rigozi, arranged by his freedwoman and another individual, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200956.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Scoris Mucapori vicsit sic annos LX Aurelius filius vicsit sic annis XXXI Sabina filia vicsit sic annis XXX Aurelia Ettepir ucsor sic et Valens et Sabinianus filii superstantes posuerunt\n
EDCS-32200956.translation: To the departed spirits. Scoris Mucapori lived 60 years. Aurelius, his son, lived 31 years. Sabina, his daughter, lived 30 years. Aurelia Ettepir, his wife, and Valens and Sabinianus, his surviving sons, placed this.
EDCS-32200956.summary: A funerary inscription for Scoris Mucapori, set up by his wife and children, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200957.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sozusa Hermogenis vixit annos LXV et Chrestio filius eius vixit annos XXII et Iulia Glyconis filia eius vixit annos XXXX Pylades filius Glyconidis vixit annos XVIII Pompeia Privata parentibus bene merentibus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32200957.translation: To the divine shades. Sozusa daughter of Hermogenes lived 65 years, and her son Chrestio lived 22 years, and her daughter Julia lived 40 years. Pylades son of Glyconis lived 18 years. Pompeia Privata had this made for her well-deserving parents.
EDCS-32200957.summary: A funerary monument recording the lives of several family members, with the daughter Pompeia Privata as the dedicant, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200958.text_edition: \n qui natus fuit Axiopoli vixit nnos XXVIT \n
EDCS-32200958.translation: who was born at Axiopolis lived 26 years.
EDCS-32200958.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the lifespan of an individual born in Axiopolis, likely a funerary text from the Roman period.

EDCS-32200975.summary: A brief inscription indicating that Filemon created something in Arretium, possibly an artwork or monument, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-32500042.text_edition: \n OM FNIVN TIINOSVNROE vixit nnos XIII heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32500042.translation: [---] lived 13 years. The heir had this made.
EDCS-32500042.summary: A funerary inscription recording the short life of an individual, with their heir commissioning the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500053.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli et Marci Aureli Caesaris cives consistentes vico I Urb cur(am) a<g=C>(entibus) magistris Iulio et Domitio Anibal C votum solverunt libentes merito D V \n
EDCS-32500053.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperor Titus Aelius and Caesar Marcus Aurelius. The citizens residing in the 1st Street precinct, with Iulius and Domitius Anibal acting as magistrates, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-32500053.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of Emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius, set up by citizens and magistrates of the 1st Street precinct, from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-32500054.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Antonini Hadriani Augusti Pii et Veri Caesaris veterani et cives Romani consistentes vico V \n
EDCS-32500054.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Hadrianus Augustus Pius and Verus Caesar. Veterans and Roman citizens residing in the 5th Street precinct.
EDCS-32500054.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of Emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius, set up by veterans and citizens of the 5th Street precinct, likely from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-32500055.text_edition: \n legionis V Macedonicae et Coccceiae Dorinae coniugi eius Antonia Crispina et Alexander et \n
EDCS-32500055.translation: of the Legio V Macedonica, and to Coccceia Dorina, his wife. Antonia Crispina and Alexander and
EDCS-32500055.summary: A fragmentary inscription that appears to be a dedication or funerary notice related to a soldier of Legio V Macedonica and his family, with dedicants Antonia Crispina and Alexander.

EDCS-32500057.text_edition: \n Imperator- Caesar- divi Ne[rvae fili- Nerva- Traian------\n
EDCS-32500057.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Nerva Trajan...
EDCS-32500057.summary: A heavily damaged inscription that begins with the titles of an emperor, likely Trajan, indicating a public monument or honorific text.

EDCS-32500058.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari L(ucio) Domitio Aurelianus sic Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae legati Augusti pro praetore mille passuum\n
EDCS-32500058.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, pious, happy, unconquered, Augustus, pontiff, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland. To the legates of the Emperor, proconsul, a milestone.
EDCS-32500058.summary: A milestone inscription marking distance, dedicated to Emperor Aurelian, likely from the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32500059.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XI consul II pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate II<I> et Lucius Sept[imius] Geta Caesar restituerunt per Caium Ovinium Tertullum legatum pro praetore\n
EDCS-32500059.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontiff, holder of tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 11th time, Consul II, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holder of tribunician power for the 3rd time, and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar restored this through Caius Ovinius Tertullus, legate proconsul.
EDCS-32500059.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a public work by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, overseen by the legate Caius Ovinius Tertullus, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-33500440.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-33500440.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator IIII consul V pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in alis tribus et cohortibus septem quae appellantur I Flavia Gaetulorum et I Asturum et II Hispanorum et Arvacorum et I Lepidiana civium Romanorum et I Bracaraugustanorum et I Sugambrorum tironum et II Mattiacorum et II Chalcidenorum et II Flavia Brittonum et Ubiorum et sunt in Moesia inferiore sub Aulo Caecilio Faustino qui quina et vicena plurave stipendia meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem III Idus Maias Caio Iulio Basso Cnaeo Afranio Dextro consulibus alae II Hispanorum et Arvacorum cui praeest Lucius Fabius Luci filius Palatina Fabullus gregali Atrecto Capitonis filio Nemeti descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator IIII consul V pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in alis tribus et cohortibus septem quae appellantur I Flavia Gaetulorum et I Asturum et II Hispanorum et Arvacorum et I Lepidiana civium Romanorum et I Bracaraugustanorum et I Sugambrorum tironum et II Mattiacorum et II Chalcidenorum et II Flavia Brittannorum et Ubiorum et sunt in Moesia inferiore sub Aulo Caecilio Faustino qui quina et vicena plurave stipendia meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione\n
EDCS-33500440.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 5th time, Father of the Fatherland, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who serve in the three alae and seven cohorts named the I Flavia Gaetulorum, I Asturum, II Hispanorum et Arvacorum, I Lepidiana of Roman citizens, I Bracaraugustanorum, I Sugambrorum tironum, II Mattiacorum, II Chalcidenorum, II Flavia Brittonum, and Ubiorum, and are stationed in Lower Moesia under Aulus Caecilius Faustinus, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and likewise those discharged with honorable release, to them and their children and descendants, granted citizenship and the right to marry women they then had (at the time citizenship was granted), or if they were unmarried, to marry those they would subsequently take, provided each man took only one wife, on the third day before the Ides of May, under the consulship of Gaius Julius Bassus and Gnaeus Afranius Dexter. For the II Ala of Spaniards and Arverni, commanded by Lucius Fabius Fabullus, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe, for the rank-and-file soldier Atrectus, son of Capito, of the Nemetes tribe. Recorded and verified from the bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the Divine Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. [The text is repeated here with the emperor's titles and the units listed.]
EDCS-33500440.summary: This inscription records a grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Trajan to auxiliary soldiers serving in Moesia, dated to May 13, 106 AD.

EDCS-33500441.text_edition: \n IL S occisus in pugna vixit annis triginta L Mucianus miles cohortis Lusitanorum posuit titulum fratri genitivo\n
EDCS-33500441.translation: Mucianus, a soldier of the Lusitanian cohort, who was killed in battle, lived for thirty years; he erected this monument to his brother.
EDCS-33500441.summary: This is a tombstone erected by a soldier for his brother, who died in battle.

EDCS-33500442.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Muciano discente sic mensore sic legionis I Italicae vixit nnos XXVI T \n
EDCS-33500442.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Mucianus, a student, a measurer, of the I Italica Legion, lived 26 years.
EDCS-33500442.summary: This is a tombstone for Aurelius Mucianus, a student and measurer in the I Italica Legion, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-33500444.text_edition: \n Tito Aelio Damanaeo veterano Atticus et Amaranthus liberti et heredes ex testamento fecerunt\n
EDCS-33500444.translation: Atticus and Amaranthus, freedmen and heirs, established this for Titus Aelius Damanaeus, a veteran, in accordance with his will.
EDCS-33500444.summary: This monument was erected by freedmen and heirs for Titus Aelius Damanaeus, a military veteran.

EDCS-33500445.text_edition: \n Monius Valens veteranus legionis V Macedonicae vixit annis LX\n
EDCS-33500445.translation: Monius Valens, a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-33500445.summary: This is a tombstone for Monius Valens, a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-33500446.text_edition: \n Claudia Chione Perintho vixit annos XXX Flavius Iulianus maritus eius bene merenti titulum posuit\n
EDCS-33500446.translation: Claudia Chione of Perinthus lived for 30 years. Her husband, Flavius Iulianus, erected this monument to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-33500446.summary: This is a tombstone erected by a husband for his wife, Claudia Chione, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-34100006.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Primus Cari vixit annos XXX Primanus Primi vixit annos V Acutus Gemini vixit annos IX Tertia Primigeni vixit annos L Carianus Cari vixit annos XXV fratri et pientissimis parentibus bene merentibus\n
EDCS-34100006.translation: To the departed spirits. Primus Cari lived 30 years; Primanus Primi lived 5 years; Acutus Gemini lived 9 years; Tertia Primigeni lived 50 years; Carianus Cari lived 25 years. For a brother and most dutiful parents, well-deserving.
EDCS-34100006.summary: This monument honors multiple family members who have died, including parents and a brother.

EDCS-34100007.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Domitius Valens codicarius quaestoris provinciae Iuliae Decuminae coniugi pientissimae Marcus Domitius Valentinus et Domitius Valens \n
EDCS-34100007.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Domitius Valens, scribe of the quaestor of the province, to his most dutiful wife, Iulia Decumena. Marcus Domitius Valentinus and Domitius Valens [erected this].
EDCS-34100007.summary: This monument is dedicated by sons to their mother, Iulia Decumena, wife of a provincial quaestor's scribe.

EDCS-34100008.text_edition: \n decurioni Aurelius patrono bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-34100008.translation: For Aurelius, a well-deserving decurion and patron.
EDCS-34100008.summary: This is a dedication to Aurelius, recognized for his service as a decurion and patron.

EDCS-34100009.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris divi Traiani Parthici fili divi Nervae nepotis Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate XX consulis III Marcus Antius Rufinus inter Thraces et Moesos fines posuit\n
EDCS-34100009.translation: By the authority of Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 20th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Marcus Antius Rufinus erected this boundary marker between the Thracians and Moesians.
EDCS-34100009.summary: This inscription records the erection of a boundary marker by Marcus Antius Rufinus under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, likely during his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-34100010.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Nymphidius Publi filius Valens buleuta Nicopolis vixit annos XXX hic situs est Nymphidia mater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-34100010.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Nymphidius Valens, son of Publius, a council member of Nicopolis, lived 30 years; he is buried here. Nymphidia, his mother, saw to its construction.
EDCS-34100010.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Nymphidius Valens, erected by his mother, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100031.text_edition: \n vixit annos XXII meruit annos II Quintus C Sura patronus pius curavit faciendum \n
EDCS-34100031.translation: He lived 22 years, served 2 years. Quintus C. Sura, a dutiful patron, saw to its construction.
EDCS-34100031.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and service of an unnamed individual, erected by his patron Quintus C. Sura, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100033.text_edition: \n Marcellina soror socra gener VLVRLI VME \n
EDCS-34100033.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely funerary, mentioning family relationships such as sister, mother-in-law, and son-in-law.

EDCS-34100035.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Antonio Marci filio Papiria Ianuario domo Oesci O vixit annosTI \n
EDCS-34100035.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Antonius Ianuarius, son of Marcus, of the Papirian tribe, from Oesci, lived [---] years.
EDCS-34100035.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Antonius Ianuarius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100036.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria vixit annos VIIII et Quintia Pupa vixit annos XIX Iulia Veneria mater f(iliis) suis posuit\n
EDCS-34100036.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Valeria lived 9 years and Quintia Pupa lived 19 years. Iulia Veneria, their mother, placed this for her sons.
EDCS-34100036.summary: This funerary epitaph records the deaths of Valeria and Quintia Pupa, erected by their mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100037.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attia Dionysia se viva titulum posuit sibi et Attiae vernaculae suae @(obitae) annorum III\n
EDCS-34100037.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Attia Dionysia set up this monument to herself while alive, and for her slave Attia Vernacula, who died at 3 years old.
EDCS-34100037.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Attia Dionysia for herself and her deceased young slave, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100038.text_edition: \n Publio Septimio Getae legato Auggustorum pro praetore patrono coloniae coloni coloniae eiusdem\n
EDCS-34100038.translation: To Publius Septimius Geta, legate of the Augusti pro praetore and patron of the colony, from the colonists of the same colony.
EDCS-34100038.summary: This is a dedication set up by the colonists of a colony in honor of their patron, Publius Septimius Geta, who served as imperial legate, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-34100041.summary: This is likely an inscription honoring Emperor Septimius Severus, possibly erected during his reign.

EDCS-34100043.translation: To the genius of the century of Egnatius Ven, the commander.
EDCS-34100043.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the 'genius' of a military unit, specifically the century of Egnatius Ven, from the Roman military context.

EDCS-34100044.text_edition: \n Iovi et Dianae Bu Caius Curtius Vegetus posuit veteranus legionis I Italicae\n
EDCS-34100044.translation: To Jupiter and Diana. Caius Curtius Vegetus, a veteran of the I Italica Legion, placed this.
EDCS-34100044.summary: This is a dedication made by Caius Curtius Vegetus, a veteran of the I Italica Legion, to Jupiter and Diana.

EDCS-34100046.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Charagoniae Arche i vixit annos XXVIII Domitius Timocrates \n
EDCS-34100046.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Charagonia Arche lived 28 years. Domitius Timocrates [erected this].
EDCS-34100046.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Charagonia Arche, who lived 28 years, set up by Domitius Timocrates, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100047.text_edition: \n filius missicius cohortis I Cilicum vixit nnis LXV militavit annis \n
EDCS-34100047.translation: Son, a dismissed soldier of the 1st Cohort of Cilicia, lived 65 years, served for [missing duration].
EDCS-34100047.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life and military service of a dismissed soldier from the 1st Cohort of Cilicia, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100048.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri <p=M>(atriae) consuli \n
EDCS-34100048.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul.
EDCS-34100048.summary: This is a titulature inscription for Emperor Trajan Decius, listing his official titles and honors, dating to his reign (249-251 AD).

EDCS-34100049.summary: This is a dedication or ownership marker for Gaius Soccatius Capitolinus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100050.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aurelius Coxxoris sic Salbio sic Coxxoris sic filio suo titulo sic posuit vivus vivo\n
EDCS-34100050.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aurelius Coxxoris placed this tombstone for his son Salbius, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-34100050.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aurelius Coxxoris, dedicating the tomb to his son Salbius and also to himself as a living memorial, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100051.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Memmio Aquilae veterano legionis V Macedonicae vixit nnos LII Lucius Valerius Valens miles legione eadem et Valerius Rusticus fratri fecerunt\n
EDCS-34100051.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Memmius Aquila, a veteran of the 5th Macedonian Legion, lived 52 years. Lucius Valerius Valens, a soldier of the same legion, and Valerius Rusticus erected this for him.
EDCS-34100051.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Memmius Aquila, a veteran of the 5th Macedonian Legion, erected by a fellow soldier and another individual, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100052.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hic <e=I>go qui sic iacior Valeria dicta fuisse ter denos annos <q=C>uia pariblandum si nec alios nisi quot fata tribuerunt cum tribent sic nec Iuppiter ipse l datarum et CI vitam nam dum eramus! iuvenes sic amata fuisse illo caro me s mihi et sibi \n
EDCS-34100052.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. I, who lie here, was called Valeria; I lived for thirty years. It is not permitted to flatter others, only what the fates grant, and Jupiter himself gives not what is fated, for while we were young! I was loved by that dear one to me and to himself.
EDCS-34100052.summary: This is a funerary inscription in verse for Valeria, reflecting on her life and love, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100054.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Iulianus veteranus ex equite legionis XI Claudiae annorum LX Valeria Crispina coniux et heres posuit\n
EDCS-34100054.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Julianus, a veteran formerly of the 11th Claudian Cavalry, aged 60. Valeria Crispina, his wife and heir, placed this.
EDCS-34100054.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius Julianus, a veteran of the 11th Claudian Cavalry, erected by his wife and heir, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpio Archidamo patri pient(issimo) vixit annos LXV et Ulpiae Helpidi matri vixit annos \n
EDCS-34100056.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ulpius Archidamus, a most devoted father, lived 65 years, and for Ulpia Helpidius, mother, lived [missing duration].
EDCS-34100056.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Ulpius Archidamus and Ulpia Helpidius, parents, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100057.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atticus Cecitias vixit nnos LXX et Sarbis Celsi marita eius vixit annos LX bene merentibus patri et matri Castus Attici |(centurio) legionis et Marcus et Caelsus sic miles legionis XI Claudiae et Valens fratres posuerunt\n
EDCS-34100057.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Atticus Cecitias lived 70 years, and Sarbis, wife of Celsus, lived 60 years. Castus, son of Atticus, a centurion of the legion, and Marcus and Celsus, soldiers of the 11th Claudian Legion, and Valens, brothers, erected this for their well-deserving father and mother.
EDCS-34100057.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Atticus Cecitias and his wife Sarbis, erected by their sons who were military men, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100058.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Vesius Philetus Iuliae Olimpiae sic coniugi bene merenti posuit vixit annos XXV avete viatores\n
EDCS-34100058.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Vesius Philetus erected this for his deserving wife, Julia Olympia. She lived 25 years. Farewell, travelers.
EDCS-34100058.summary: A tombstone inscription recording the death of Julia Olympia, erected by her husband, Caius Vesius Philetus, and dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100059.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Crispus Claudi Terentius Iulianus VIB XIII AO CI CE IIA \n
EDCS-34100059.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Valerius Crispus. Claudius Terentius Julianus: 13 years of military service, 101 days of service, 2 years of service.
EDCS-34100059.summary: A tombstone inscription honoring Claudius Terentius Julianus, likely a soldier, by Caius Valerius Crispus, indicating specific lengths of service, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mucaporis Derzitralo filio suo qui vixit annis XXV memoriae causa posuit\n
EDCS-34100060.translation: To the departed spirits. Mucapor, son of Derzitalos, who lived 25 years, erected this in memory.
EDCS-34100060.summary: A tombstone inscription commemorating Mucapor, son of Derzitalos, who died at the age of 25, erected by himself, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100062.translation: [---] Mercu[rialis] erected this for his living son.
EDCS-34100062.summary: A tombstone inscription fragment recording the erection of a monument by Mercurialis for his son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34801105.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-34801105.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Trajan Augustus, Germanicus, Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 9th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 5th time, Father of the Fatherland, to the cavalry and infantry serving in the three alae and seven cohorts named I Pannoniorum, and Hispanorum, and Alectorigiana, and I Augusta Nerviana Pacensis miliaria Brittonum, and I Sugambrorum veterana, and I Tyriorum sagittaria, and I Hispanorum veterana, and I Flavia Numidarum, and II Brittonum Augusta Nerviana Pacensis miliaria, and VII Gallorum, and who are in Lower Moesia under Aulus Caecilius Faustinus, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and also those discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed below, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they would later marry, provided each man only one wife, on the third day before the Ides of May, with Gaius Julius Bassus and Gnaeus Afranius Dexter as consuls. From the cohort I Tyriorum sagittariorum, commanded by Lucius Rutilius Ravonianus, to the infantryman Tarsas, son of Tarsas, from Bessa. Recorded and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus near the Minerva.
EDCS-34801105.summary: An imperial grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights issued by Emperor Trajan on May 13, 105 AD, to auxiliary soldiers and veterans serving in Lower Moesia, recorded on a bronze tablet.

EDCS-36600010.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-36600010.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator XII consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate VIII consul II equitibus qui inter singulares militaverunt castris prioribus quibus praeest Octavius Piso tribunus quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis singulas ante diem III Idus Martias domini sic nostri sic Antonini sic Augusti! II et Getae sic Caesare consulibus Caio Valerio Dolentis filio Valenti Serdica ex Thracia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator XII consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate VIII consul II equitibus qui inter singulares militaverunt quibus praeest castris perioribus Octavius Piso tribunus quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum iis data quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis singulas\n
EDCS-36600010.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius, Germanicus, Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-nephew of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed Imperator 12 times, Consul 3 times, Father of the Fatherland; and Emperor Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, nephew of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurellius Antoninus Pius Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, Consul 2 times, to the cavalrymen who served among the singulars in the prior camps, under the command of the tribune Octavius Piso, having completed twenty-five or more years of service and honorably discharged, for those whose names are listed below, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would afterwards marry, only one each; on the third day before the Ides of March, in the consulship of our lords Antoninus Augustus II and Geta Caesar; inscribed and confirmed from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius, Germanicus, Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-nephew of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed Imperator 12 times, Consul 3 times, Father of the Fatherland; and Emperor Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, nephew of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurellius Antoninus Pius Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, Consul 2 times, to the cavalrymen who served among the singulars in the prior camps, under the command of the tribune Octavius Piso, having completed twenty-five or more years of service and honorably discharged, for those whose names are listed below, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with those whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those whom they would afterwards marry, only one each.
EDCS-36600010.summary: This lengthy inscription, dated to March 13th in the year 205 AD, records the grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla to veteran cavalrymen of the legionary guard.

EDCS-36600011.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-36600011.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Marci Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator XIII consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini <P=D>ii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate X imperator II consul II equitibus qui inter singulares militaverunt castris novis quibus praeest sic Valerius Herculanus et Obulcius Verus tribuni quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem XIII Kalendas Novembres Caio Septimio Apro Lucio Annio Maximo consulibus Marco Valerio Marci filio Apollinari Antiochia ex Syria descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator XIII consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filius divi Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate X consul II equitibus qui inter singulares militaverunt castris novis quibus praesunt Valerius Herculanus et Obulcius Verus tribuni quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas poste[a duxissent ------ \n
EDCS-36600011.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, nephew of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius, grandnephew of the deified Hadrian, great-grandnephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-grandnephew of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 15th time, Imperator for the 13th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, and Emperor Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, nephew of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandnephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandnephew of the deified Hadrian, and great-great-grandnephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, with tribunician power for the 10th time, Consul for the 2nd time, to the cavalrymen who served among the Singulares of the new camps, commanded by tribunes Valerius Herculanus and Obulcius Verus, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed below, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would later marry, provided each man only one wife, on the 13th day before the Kalends of November, with Gaius Septimius Aper and Lucius Annius Maximus as consuls. From Antioch in Syria, to Marcus Valerius Apollinaris, son of Marcus. Recorded and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus near the Minerva.
EDCS-36600011.summary: An imperial grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights issued by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla on October 20, 207 AD, to cavalrymen of the Singulares, recorded on a bronze tablet.

EDCS-36600041.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-36600041.text_edition: \n ------ et sunt in Mauretania] Tin<g=C>(itana) sub U<tt=II>edio Honorato quinis et vicenis item classicis senis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat sin<g=C>ulis ante diem XI Kalendas Ianuarias Marco Calpurnio Lon<g=C>o Decimo V<el=II>io Fido consulibus cohortis V Delmatarum civium Romanorum cui praeest Publius Servilius Africanus Sabratha ex pedi<t=I>e Damanaeo Sri <f=I>(ilio) Daco descriptum et reco<g=C>(nitum) ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator II consul III designatus IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XI quae appellantur et V Delmatarum civium Romanorum et sunt in Mauretania Tin<g=C>(itana) sub Uttedio Honorato quinis et vicenis item classicis senis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dum[taxat ------ cohortis V Delmatarum civium Romanorum cui praeest Publius Servilius Africanus Sabratha ex pedite Damanaeo S Publi Atti Severi Luci Pulli Daphni Marci Servili Getae Luci Pulli Chresimi Marci Sentili Iasi Tiberi Iuli Felicis Cai Iuli Silvani \n
EDCS-36600041.translation: [---] and who are in Mauretania Tingitana under Uttedius Honoratus, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and also regular soldiers who have completed twenty-six or more years of service, and discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed below, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would later marry, provided each man only one wife, on the eleventh day before the Kalends of January, with Marcus Calpurnius Longus and Decimus Velius Rufus as consuls. From Sabratha, to Damanaeus, son of Sri, from Dacia, an infantryman of cohort V Delmatarum civium Romanorum, commanded by Publius Servilius Africanus. Recorded and verified from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus near the Minerva. Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 3rd time, designated Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, to the cavalry and infantry who served in the five alae and eleven cohorts named V Delmatarum civium Romanorum, and who are in Mauretania Tingitana under Uttedius Honoratus, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and also regular soldiers who have completed twenty-six or more years of service, and discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed below, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would later marry, provided [each man] [---] of cohort V Delmatarum civium Romanorum, commanded by Publius Servilius Africanus, from Sabratha, to Damanaeus, son of Publius Attius Severus, son of Lucius Pullius Chresimus, son of Marcus Servilius Geta, son of Lucius Pullius Chresimus, son of Marcus Sentilius Ias, son of Tiberius Julius Felix, son of Gaius Julius Silvanus.
EDCS-36600041.summary: An imperial grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights issued by Emperor Antoninus Pius on December 22, 144 AD, to soldiers of cohort V Delmatarum in Mauretania Tingitana, recorded on a bronze tablet.

EDCS-36600042.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator II consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XI quae appellantur I Gallorum et Pannoniorum et I Gallorum Atectorigiana et I Vespasiana Dardanorum et I Flavia Gaetulorum et II Hispanorum Arvacorum et I Bracaraugustanorum civium Romanorum et II Mattiacorum et I <F=E>lavia Numidarum et I Claudia Sugambrorum veterana et II Chalch(idenorum) sagittariorum et I Cilicum sagittaria et I Thracum Syriaca et I Germanorum et II Bracaraugustanorum et I Lusitanorum Cyrenaica et II Flavia Brittonum et sunt in Moesia inferiore sub Claudio Saturnino quinis et vicenis item classicis senis et vic<e=I>nis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum quas postea duxissent dum<t=I>axat singulis ante diem VII Idus Apriles Lucio Lamia Silvano Lucio Poblicola Prisco consulibus alae I Gallorum et nniorum cui praeest AIN Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator II consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XI quae appellantur I Gallorum et Pannoniorum et I <G=C>allorum et I Vespasiana et I Flavia et II Hispanorum et I Bracaraugustanorum et II Mattiacorum eat II <F=B>lavia et Cl(audia) et I Claudia et II Chalcidenorum et I Cilicum et I Thracum et I Germanorum et II Bracaraugustanorum et I Lusitanorum et II Flavia et sunt in Moe<s=T>ia inferiore XXV item classicis XXVI pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum quas postea d<u=O>xissent dumtaxat singulis \n
EDCS-36600042.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, to the cavalry and infantry who served in the five alae and eleven cohorts named I Gallorum et Pannoniorum, and I Gallorum Atectorigiana, and I Vespasiana Dardanorum, and I Flavia Gaetulorum, and II Hispanorum Arvacorum, and I Bracaraugustanorum civium Romanorum, and II Mattiacorum, and I Flavia Numidarum, and I Claudia Sugambrorum veterana, and II Chalch(idenorum) sagittariorum, and I Cilicum sagittaria, and I Thracum Syriaca, and I Germanorum, and II Bracaraugustanorum, and I Lusitanorum Cyrenaica, and II Flavia Brittonum, and who are in Lower Moesia under Claudius Saturninus, who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, and also regular soldiers who have completed twenty-six or more years of service, and discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed below, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would later marry, provided each man only one wife, on the seventh day before the Ides of April, with Lucius Lamia Silvanus and Lucius Poblicola Priscus as consuls. From [---] of ala I Gallorum et Pannoniorum, commanded by [---] Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, to the cavalry and infantry who served in the five alae and eleven cohorts named I Gallorum et Pannoniorum, and I Gallorum, and I Vespasiana, and I Flavia, and II Hispanorum, and I Bracaraugustanorum, and II Mattiacorum, and II Flavia, and Claudia, and I Claudia, and II Chalcidenorum, and I Cilicum, and I Thracum, and I Germanorum, and II Bracaraugustanorum, and I Lusitanorum, and II Flavia, and who are in Lower Moesia, twenty-five years of service, also regular soldiers twenty-six years of service, or more, discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are subscribed below, granted Roman citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would later marry, provided each man only one wife.
EDCS-36600042.summary: An imperial grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights issued by Emperor Antoninus Pius on April 5, 146 AD, to auxiliary soldiers and veterans serving in Lower Moesia, recorded on a bronze tablet.

EDCS-36900193.translation: With good fortune! During the priesthood of Diogenes, son of Athenades, twice. Priests of Dionysus Karpophos: Herophantos, son of Aischrion; Herakon, son of Aristomachos; Dionysios, son of Athenades; Aristomenes; Artemas.
EDCS-36900193.summary: A dedication to Dionysus Karpophos listing priests and officials, likely from a sanctuary in Greece, dated to the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900289.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris veterani et cives Romani et Bessi consistentes vico Quintionis curam agentibus magistris Iulio Floro et Derzeno Biti et quaestore Frontone Busitsinis Idibus Iunis Imperatore Commodo et Quintillo consulibus\n
EDCS-36900289.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety of the Emperor, veterans and Roman citizens and Bessi residing in the quarter of Quintio, with Julius Florus and Derzenus Biti as magistrates and Fronto Busitsinis as quaestor, in the Ides of June, with Commodus and Quintillus as consuls.
EDCS-36900289.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by veterans, Roman citizens, and Bessi for the Emperor's safety during the consulship of Commodus and Quintillus.

EDCS-36900292.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae cives Romani et Lai consistentes vico Secundini posuerunt pro salute Imperatoris Cai Iuli Marci Philippi Pii Augusti et Iuli Philippi nobilissimi Caesaris cura agentibus magistratis sic Claudium sic Antoninum sic et Cocceium sic Iustum sic Praesenti et Albino consulibus\n
EDCS-36900292.translation: Roman citizens and Lai residing in the quarter of Secundinus erected this, for the safety of the Emperor Caius Julius Marcus Philippus Pius Augustus and of Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, with Claudius, Antoninus, Cocceius Justus, and Praesens and Albinus as consuls.
EDCS-36900292.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina by Roman citizens and Lai for the safety of Emperors Philip the Arab and his son Philip the Younger, dating to their consulship.

EDCS-37000020.translation: Attas set up this stele for himself and his wife Mama, daughter of Kreskos, who lived 60 years. Farewell.
EDCS-37000020.summary: This Greek funerary inscription records a man named Attas dedicating a stele for himself and his wife Mama, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-38100356.translation: To Quintus Julius Felix, decurion of the colony.
EDCS-38100356.summary: This is a dedication to Quintus Julius Felix, a decurion in a Roman colony.

EDCS-38100357.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Titi filio Papiria Valentino equiti Romano flamini et II virali coloniae praefecto saltus patrono collegii fabrorum coloniarum Oescensium et Apulensium patrono coloniae Ulpiae Oescensium bono civi et amatori rei publicae ob merita eius in re publica conlata splendissimus ordo ex suo\n
EDCS-38100357.translation: To Titus Flavius Valentino, son of Titus, of the Papiria tribe, Roman knight, flamen, former chief magistrate (II viri) of the colony, prefect of the estate, patron of the guilds of smiths of the Oescenses and Apulenses colonies, patron of the Ulpia Oescensium colony, a good citizen and lover of the state, the most distinguished order, out of its own funds.
EDCS-38100357.summary: This inscription honors Titus Flavius Valentino, a prominent local official and patron, recognizing his contributions to the state and various colonies.

EDCS-38100359.summary: This is a simple name inscription, likely identifying a person named Gaius Antonius Magnus.

EDCS-39400114.translation: For good fortune. The association of ship-owners in Tomis set up this statue from their own funds for the son of the Emperor, Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar. Erected by Titus Titius the Younger.
EDCS-39400114.summary: This Greek inscription records the erection of a statue of Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar by ship-owners in Tomis, funded by Titus Titius the Younger.

EDCS-39400142.translation: For good fortune. The council and people of the most glorious metropolis and first of the Eponymous Pontus, Tomis, [set up this statue] for Iulia Mamaea Augusta, mother of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, and of his most noble field armies.
EDCS-39400142.summary: This Greek inscription records the council and people of Tomis honoring Julia Mamaea, mother of Emperor Alexander Severus, and his armies.

EDCS-43700579.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Gall<i>enio Pio Felici invicto Augusto pontefico sic maximo tribunicia potestate consuli patri patriae proconsuli dedicante Gaio Iulio Victore legato Auggustorum pro praetore per Aurelio Dometiano sic |(centurionem) legionis praepositum cohortis II reducum devotus numini ipsius de quaestura cohortis posuit pro praetore Posita VI Idus Octobres IOAV consulibus\n
EDCS-43700579.translation: For Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, dedicated by Gaius Julius Victor, legate of the Augusti, propraetor, through Aurelius Domenicus, centurion of a legion, commander of the cohort of the Reducum, devoted to his divinity, erected from the quaestorship of the cohort, [in the time of] the proconsul. Erected on the 6th day before the Ides of October, with [consular names lost] as consuls.
EDCS-43700579.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a bridge to Emperor Gallienus by a legate, with involvement of a centurion, during the proconsulship and consulship of unspecified individuals.

EDCS-44200301.text_edition: \n centuria Valeri Cri<s>pi legionis V Macedonicae Publius Vibius centuria Valeri Crispi Publi Sabini Marri centuria Valeri <Cri>spi\n
EDCS-44200301.translation: Centuria of Valerius Crispus, of the V Macedonica legion. Publius Vibius [in the] centuria of Valerius Crispus. Marrius [in the] centuria of Valerius Crispus.
EDCS-44200301.summary: This inscription lists individuals associated with specific centuriae (units of 100 men) within the V Macedonica legion.

EDCS-44200302.summary: This short inscription records a votive offering made by Marius Marcus.

EDCS-44500172.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to record a name, Maxima, and some illegible text, possibly part of a funerary dedication.

EDCS-44500176.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Candidiani Venustae vixit nnos XVIII Cocceius Cozi maritus bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-44500176.translation: To the departed spirits. Candidianus Venustus lived 18 years. Cocceius Cozius, her husband, placed this for a well-deserving woman.
EDCS-44500176.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Candidianus Venustus at the age of 18 and was dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-44500177.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Felix vixit annis LX Valeria Saturnina coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-44500177.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Valerius Felix lived 60 years. Valeria Saturnina placed this for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-44500177.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Caius Valerius Felix at the age of 60 and was dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-44500178.text_edition: \n decurio Montanensium vixit annos X Valerius Valerianus vixit annos XV ris d \n
EDCS-44500178.translation: Town councilor of the Montanenses, lived 10 years. Valerius Valerianus lived 15 years.
EDCS-44500178.summary: This inscription records the ages of two individuals, one of whom was a town councilor of the Montanenses.

EDCS-44500179.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Seneca F vixit annos LIIII et menses VI dies VIII Victor vixit menses VII Melitta vixit annos II dies XI Lupus vixit nnos V menses V Faustina vixit menses V dies VII Aelia Gemella conpari bene merito posuit\n
EDCS-44500179.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Seneca F lived 54 years and 6 months and 8 days. Victor lived 7 months. Melitta lived 2 years and 11 days. Lupus lived 5 years and 5 months. Faustina lived 5 months and 7 days. Aelia Gemella placed this for her deserving partner.
EDCS-44500179.summary: This elaborate funerary inscription lists the short lives of several individuals, including Aurelius Seneca F, Victor, Melitta, Lupus, and Faustina, and was dedicated by Aelia Gemella.

EDCS-45900107.text_edition: \n Deo Sancto Heroni Ulpius Maximianus speculator ex votu sic posuit\n
EDCS-45900107.translation: To the Holy God Heron. Ulpius Maximianus, a scout, placed this according to his vow.
EDCS-45900107.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Ulpius Maximianus, a scout, to the god Heron.

EDCS-45900108.text_edition: \n Heroni Sancto Publius Aelius Felix veteranus\n
EDCS-45900108.translation: To the Holy Heron. Publius Aelius Felix, a veteran.
EDCS-45900108.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Heron by Publius Aelius Felix, a veteran.

EDCS-45900109.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Valens posuit votum dominum sic PVVM\n
EDCS-45900109.translation: Titus Flavius Valens set up this vow to his lord.
EDCS-45900109.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Titus Flavius Valens to his lord.

EDCS-45900113.translation: Ianus, a staff officer of the consular governor, set up a vow.
EDCS-45900113.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Ianus, a beneficiarius consularis.

EDCS-45900117.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Aurelius Svius for himself or another, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-45900187.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeli Ingenui beneficiarii consularis legionis XI Claudiae vixit annos XL Ulpia Matrona coniugi bene merenti fecit vale viator\n
EDCS-45900187.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Ingenuus, a beneficiarius of the consular governor of the XI Claudia legion, lived for 40 years. Ulpia Matrona erected this for her well-deserving husband. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-45900187.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Ulpia Matrona for her husband, Aelius Ingenuus, a soldier in the XI Claudia legion, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-46300038.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Philippus cornicularius praefecti legionis I Italicae Severianae voto posuit\n
EDCS-46300038.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Philippus, a cornicularius (an officer's attendant) in the I Italica Severiana legion, erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-46300038.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Aurelius Philippus, an officer's attendant in the I Italica Severiana legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-46400822.text_edition: \n Dianae xanctae sic Cassius Surus miles legionis I Italicae Antoninianae SAVOM\n
EDCS-46400822.translation: To holy Diana. Cassius Surus, a soldier of the I Italica Antoniniana legion, [erected this].
EDCS-46400822.summary: This is a votive inscription to Diana by Cassius Surus, a soldier in the I Italica Antoniniana legion, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-46700099.text_edition: \n Iovius Maximinus Invictus Augustus XX H CY\n
EDCS-46700099.summary: This inscription appears to record a reign or period of rule associated with Emperor Maximinus Thrax, possibly a milestone or boundary marker.

EDCS-46700103.translation: To Markianos, son of Markos, father of the fatherland, and to Markiana. These honours have been erected by Soterides, a citizen, in the 11th year of his command, to the gods.
EDCS-46700103.summary: This is a dedication from Soterides, a citizen, to the gods Markianos and Markiana, likely from the Greek East during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48600013.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus Brittannicus fecit cohorti II Flaviae Brittonum sub L(ucio)? Septimio? Flacco legato Augusti pro praetore curante? Tito Flavio Celsiano Antiochiano praefecto\n
EDCS-48600013.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus Britannicus erected [this] for the II Flavia Brittonum cohort, under Lucius Septimius Flaccus, imperial legate with praetorian power, with Titus Flavius Celsianus Antiochianus, prefect, in charge.
EDCS-48600013.summary: This is an official dedication by Emperor Commodus for the II Flavia Brittonum cohort, likely dating to his reign (177-192 CE), specifying the military and administrative officials involved.

EDCS-51500002.text_edition: \n []ius [] [] Aureli? [] [] Antonin? [] []E[]O[] []TVLL[] EGA[] []M[] Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto et Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari\n
EDCS-51500002.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and to Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-51500002.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Philip I and his son Philip II, likely a fragment of a larger monument from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-51500042.text_edition: \n Ius Vrius Glyconi C I A posuit\n
EDCS-51500042.translation: Ius Vrius Glyco, a citizen of Alexandria, erected this.
EDCS-51500042.summary: This is a dedication by Ius Vrius Glyco, a citizen of Alexandria, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51500044.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno ubi ferrum nascitur Publius Aelius Benivolus decurio alae Commagenorum iussu dei ipsius\n
EDCS-51500044.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, where iron is born. Publius Aelius Benivolus, decurion of the ala Commagenorum, by the command of the god himself.
EDCS-51500044.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Dolichenus by Publius Aelius Benivolus, a decurion of the ala Commagenorum, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE, and possibly found near a mining site.

EDCS-51500050.summary: This inscription marks the territory or possession of the century of Aponius Valerius.

EDCS-52700528.text_edition: \n Sancto deo Asallacano Marcus Iulius Valens eques legionis I Italicae posuit votum libens merito\n
EDCS-52700528.translation: To the holy god Asallacanus, Marcus Iulius Valens, an equestrian of the I Italica legion, set up this vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-52700528.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Marcus Iulius Valens to the god Asallacanus.

EDCS-55200059.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iado Mucapori filia annnorum L posuit titulum Puris Cerzeni carus caru coniugi \n
EDCS-55200059.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iado, daughter of Mucapor, aged 50 years, set up this monument for Puris Cerzen, her dear, dear husband.
EDCS-55200059.summary: This funerary inscription records the monument set up by Iado for her husband Puris Cerzen.

EDCS-55200060.text_edition: \n VL mulo et Claudiae Eptaepyrae matri vivae fec---\n
EDCS-55200060.translation: Mulo and Claudia Eptaepyra, his mother, made this while alive...
EDCS-55200060.summary: This incomplete inscription records a dedication made by Mulo and his mother Claudia Eptaepyra.

EDCS-55701577.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Siste viator iter animumque intende sepulchro et lege quam dure sit mihi vita data ipso immarcebam florenti sic caro marito in XIIII annum mors mihi saeva fuit nam disceptarunt fata ne pia esse patri nec matri possem te<n=M>erae piae faeminae sic castae pro pietas! inter primas satiavi fata superba nomine V restituebar qui nunc quam scis raptam illa sed ab impia fata disceptata die ut nondum coniuncta marito crudelis thalamos post mortem invita reliqui teque rogo comis doleas tibi pulchrius illud quod mea virginitas mortali somno abolevit isque tuas cineres aurea terra teget ast hoc tantum vos ego nunc moneo genitores quid fletis nam fata potest quis rumpere nemo \n
EDCS-55701577.translation: Spirits of the Departed. Stop, traveler, and consider my soul and my tomb, and read how hard life was given to me. While I was in my full bloom, death was cruel to me. The Fates decreed that I could not be dutiful to my father or mother. My devoted daughter, my chaste wife! For her devotion! I first satisfied the proud Fates. My name was V... restored, who now, as you know, was snatched away, but by cruel Fates. On that day, I left your marriage chambers unwillingly, not yet joined to my husband. And I beg you, my dear ones, grieve for yourself, for that which my virginity abolished in mortal sleep. And the golden earth will cover your ashes. But this only I warn you now, my parents: why do you weep? For no one can break the Fates.
EDCS-55701577.summary: This epitaph, written from the perspective of the deceased, laments her premature death and the cruelty of fate, and warns her parents against excessive grief.

EDCS-55701589.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus CCaesaribus Aurelio Valerio Diocletiano et Marco Aurelio Valerio Maximiano Innvictis Auggustis et Flavio Valerio Constantio et Galerio Valerio Maximiano nobbilissimis CCaesaribus Cai Flavi Caesaris\n
EDCS-55701589.translation: To the Emperors, the two Caesars Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus, the Invincible Augusti, and to the noblest Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantinus and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, by Caius Flavius Caesar...
EDCS-55701589.summary: This inscription lists the reigning emperors and Caesars, likely part of a larger dedication.

EDCS-58800102.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Marcellinus posuit sibi et Claudiae Bersillae bene merenti coniugi et natis \n
EDCS-58800102.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Ulpius Marcellinus set this up for himself, and for Claudia Bersilla, his well-deserving wife, and for his children.
EDCS-58800102.summary: This funerary inscription records the tomb set up by Marcus Ulpius Marcellinus for himself, his wife, and his children.

EDCS-58800103.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Sola vivo suo sic sibi posuit et Aeliae Marciae coniugi pientissimae \n
EDCS-58800103.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Sola set this up for himself while alive, and for Aelia Marcia, his most devoted wife.
EDCS-58800103.summary: This funerary inscription records the tomb set up by Aelius Sola for himself and his wife during his lifetime.

EDCS-58800106.text_edition: \n bene merenti et filio Sisoni vixit annos X PEEN \n
EDCS-58800106.translation: To the well-deserving and his son Siso, he lived 10 years.
EDCS-58800106.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a person named Siso, recording their age at death and their merit.

EDCS-59400153.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-59400153.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Emperor Gordian III, likely part of an official monument.

EDCS-59600029.text_edition: \n pro salute Pii Augusti Macedo veteranus ex decurione alae Atectorianae Antoninianae veterani donum dederunt\n
EDCS-59600029.translation: For the health of the Pious Emperor, Macedo, a veteran from the decurions of the Atectorian Antoninian Cavalry, and other veterans gave this gift.
EDCS-59600029.summary: A gift was presented by Macedo, a veteran, and other veterans for the health of the Emperor, likely during the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-59600030.text_edition: \n semper Augusto veterani et cives Romani consistentes Abritto lustro Gai Iuli Aenoni veterani et Marci Ulpi Irenaei maggistrorum et Tiberio Claudio Claudiano quaestore\n
EDCS-59600030.translation: For the perpetual Emperor, veterans and Roman citizens residing in Abritto, under the magistracy of Gaius Julius Aenonius the veteran and Marcus Ulpius Irenaeus, and the quaestor Tiberius Claudius Claudianus.
EDCS-59600030.summary: This inscription records a dedication by veterans and citizens of Abritto, mentioning local officials, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-59600031.translation: Of the Fortunate Emperor, under the consulship of Gratus and Seleucus.
EDCS-59600031.summary: This fragment records the consulship of Gratus and Seleucus, likely dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-59600037.text_edition: \n Ddomini nnostri Constantius Au<g=C>(ustus) et Constasntinus \n
EDCS-59600037.translation: Our Lords Constantius Augustus and Constans.
EDCS-59600037.summary: This fragment names the emperors Constantius and Constans, dating it to the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-59600039.translation: Had this made for a deserving mother.
EDCS-59600039.summary: This fragmentary inscription indicates that a monument was erected by an individual for their mother, likely a funerary or honorific dedication.

EDCS-59600052.text_edition: \n Matri Deum dominus noster Licinius Invictus Augustus quod ex donariis in templo eius repertum est simulacrum argentum Numini eius in libris septem et unciis octo fieri iussit et consecrari per Aurelium Speratianum virum perfectissimum praesidem provinciae Scythiae\n
EDCS-59600052.translation: Our Lord Licinius Invictus Augustus ordered that the silver statue, found in his temple, weighing seven pounds and eight ounces, be made and consecrated to his divinity by Aurelius Speratianus, the perfectissmus, governor of the province of Scythia.
EDCS-59600052.summary: This inscription records Emperor Licinius's order to consecrate a silver statue to the Mother of the Gods, attributed to the governor of Scythia, dating to the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-59800116.text_edition: \n Invicto Herculi Imp(eratore) Severo Victorino consulibus Publius Aelius Epitynchanus sacerdos aram sub cura sua\n
EDCS-59800116.translation: To the unconquered Hercules. Under the consulship of Emperor Severus Victorinus, Publius Aelius Epitynchanus, the priest, consecrated this altar under his care.
EDCS-59800116.summary: This inscription records a dedication of an altar to Hercules by the priest Publius Aelius Epitynchanus during the consulship of Severus Victorinus, likely in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-61200005.translation: Sosippos, son of Kallikrates of Sidon, as a thank-offering to the Syrian Goddess.
EDCS-61200005.summary: This Greek inscription is a votive offering by Sosippos of Sidon to the Syrian Goddess, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-62600945.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus Germanicus Maximus, Gothicus Maximus, Parthicus Maximus, Carpicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, vested with tribunician power, father of the country.
EDCS-62600945.summary: This fragment is a dedication for the health of Emperor Aurelian, listing his extensive titles and achievements, dating to his reign (270-275 AD).

EDCS-62800027.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Libero Patri Conservatori Auggustorum et Caessarum nnostrorum et legionis I Italicae victricis Aurelius Porfyrius primipilarius ex provincia Foenice votum libens solvit Dedicata Imperatore domino nostro Gordiano Augusto II et Pompeiano consulibus accepta pariatoria Idibus Augustis\n
EDCS-62800027.translation: To the holy god Liber Pater, Conservator of our Augusti and Caesars, and of the victorious First Italian Legion, Aurelius Porfyrius, primipilarius, from the province of Phoenicia, willingly fulfilled his vow. Dedicated when our lord Gordian Augustus II and Pompeianus were consuls, received on the Ides of August.
EDCS-62800027.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aurelius Porfyrius, a primipilarius, to the god Liber Pater and the First Italian Legion, during the consulship of Gordian II and Pompeianus, dating to 239 AD.

EDCS-63202258.translation: To Diana the Holy, Valerius Victor.
EDCS-63202258.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Diana by Valerius Victor, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-63202263.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, the people of Odessa, 5 miles.
EDCS-63202263.summary: This inscription records the construction of 5 miles of road by the civitas Odessitanorum in honor of Emperor Gordian, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-64100130.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Aurelius Priscus vir perfectissimus dux votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-64100130.translation: Aurelius Priscus, a man of highest rank and dux, gladly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Preserver.
EDCS-64100130.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aurelius Priscus, a high-ranking military official, to Jupiter, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100139.translation: Dizzo and Euctasia, with love, thus.
EDCS-64100139.summary: This is a short dedication or funerary inscription, possibly with a personal sentiment, mentioning Dizzo and Euctasia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100140.text_edition: \n Numini Placidae Aurelius Valerius S FN \n
EDCS-64100140.translation: To the Kindly Divine Power, Aurelius Valerius, son of S FN, thus.
EDCS-64100140.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a divine power named 'Numen Placida' by Aurelius Valerius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100142.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus tio Erno et Crescenti \n
EDCS-64100142.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for tio Erno and Crescentius.
EDCS-64100142.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the memory of tio Erno and Crescentius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100143.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that mentions 'Aurel legion', possibly referring to a Roman legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100144.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or a memorial, with incomplete names or words, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100189.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Eutuches sic ex <vo=AV>to pro se et pro patronum sic suum sic Iulium! Maximum sic posuit et donavit vicos sic Gavidinam! Arnumtum sic supperiorem! \n
EDCS-64100189.translation: Iulius Eutuches placed and dedicated this, according to his vow, for himself and for his patron, Iulius Maximus, to Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the villages of Gavidina, Arnuntum, and the upper one.
