EDCS-64100189.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Iulius Eutuches to Jupiter Best and Greatest, for specific villages and in honor of his patron Iulius Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100190.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Diozeno Rigozi subtesserarius burgariorum se vivo sibi et Tiruni filio suo nnorum XXVII bene merenti posuit hic situs est\n
EDCS-64100190.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Diozenus Rigozi, a subordinate official of the burgarii, placed this for himself while alive and for his son Tirunus, aged 27, well-deserving; he is buried here.
EDCS-64100190.summary: This is a self-funded funerary inscription set up by Diozenus Rigozi for himself and his son Tirunus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65000243.translation: To the goddess Seicesi, Eutyches, as deserved.
EDCS-65000243.summary: A dedication to the goddess Seicesi by Eutyches, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-66300053.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpia Iulia vixit annos XL Publius Aelius Mithres arkarius classis coniugi bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-66300053.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpia Iulia lived 40 years. Publius Aelius Mithres, arkarius of the fleet, had this made for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-66300053.summary: A funerary monument for Ulpia Iulia, erected by her husband Publius Aelius Mithres, a naval official, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67300111.translation: Ailios Klaudios, hipparchos of the cavalry, to his wife who was dearest to him.
EDCS-67300111.summary: An epitaph for a wife, likely from the Greek East during the Roman Imperial period, set up by her husband Ailios Klaudios, a cavalry officer.

EDCS-67300135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoniae filiae Caius Antonius veteranus pater pientissimus et Coelia Firmina mater -- vixit annnos \n
EDCS-67300135.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Antonia, his daughter, lived X years. Caius Antonius veteranus, her most devoted father, and Coelia Firmina, mother...
EDCS-67300135.summary: A funerary inscription for Antonia, erected by her devoted father, a veteran Caius Antonius, and her mother Coelia Firmina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67300136.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Eternal, Ianuarius.
EDCS-67300136.summary: A dedication to Jupiter by Ianuarius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67400497.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate imperator consul censor perpetuus pater patriae in memoriam fortissimorum virorum qui pro re publica morte occubuerunt Cornelius Fuscus colonia Pompeiis domicilio Neapoli Italiae praefectus praetorio li A anus Hadria s Hilarus Segusio vius Reburrus Clunia Norius Priscus Cemenelo Rosicius Secundus Pollentia ius Priscus Hore Masculus Roma Lucius Valerius Sacer Vienna Lucius Gavillius Primus Agrippina Lucius Valerius Lunaris Iuvavo Octavius Secundus Foro Iulii Sextus ius Clemens Cemenelo Lucius Iulius Lollius Agrippina Titus Flavius Faustus Nicia sic Caius Valerius Secundus Celeia Caius Billius catus Dertona Lucius Flavius Cls V Caius Iulius Grac Caius Cadaricius Allius Lucius Cassius Germanusnd Caius Vitellius Seranuses Marcus Iulius Annius Agrip(pina) Lucius Valerius Primus Blaesius Varro Aequo Atrectus Roma Capito Heraclea Lucius Macrinius Valerius rus Agrippina Marcus Lollius Secundus Agrippina Iulius Valens Agrippina Aulus Albanius Macedo Agrippina Publius Pomponius Mansuetus Alba Voconius Tat Pollentia Valerius Saturninus Agrippina Caius Stratonius Caius Valerius ACA Iulius Germanus Agrippina Torius io Iuvavo nius I SV nd Iuva(vo) Valens Bo Clemens FP eques Marcus signifer Caius Iu signifer Titus Iu signifer Marcus M signifer Lucius O signifer Titus imaginifer Quintus D imaginifer Marcus A imaginifer Marcus M actarius Titus optio Publius M beneficiarius acil Caius D beneficiarius acil Caius V har Marcus C tesserarius c filius Bellovacus filius Raetus filius Tunger l Noricus Cannanef(as) Lexovius Agrippinensis en Cas Agrippinensis Lusitanus Nervius t Tunger Tunger filius Agrippinensis filius Agrippinensis Batavus filius Afer Britto Lusitanus r filius Lucensius us Agrippinensis c Batavus Barbarus L Carantius Tic Crisius Ver Vasco Ani Titus Flavius Ca Missicius Musae Veldes Texu Minicius MinO Donico Viro Crescens Senn cohors II Batavorum Naso Crescentis Saurnus Co Martialis Mar Viator Aquilae Bithi Seuti Amandi Dasi tubicen r cornicen r cornicen r cornicen r bucinator i bucinator s M s Ul us His us Max us Cel us Va IO tia CII O N ME TI O LII I II O cer rim A I filius D filius L ERV TO \n
EDCS-67400497.translation: Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, imperator, consul, perpetual censor, father of the country, in memory of the bravest men who died for the state: Cornelius Fuscus, from the colony of Pompeii, resident of Neapolis in Italy, praetorian prefect; [A]anus Hadria[nus] Hilarus; Segus[tius] vius Reburrus; [C]lunia Norius Priscus; Cemenelo Rosicius Secundus; Pollentia [P?]ius Priscus; Hore Masculus of Rome; Lucius Valerius Sacer of Vienna; Lucius Gavillius Primus of Agrippina; Lucius Valerius Lunaris of Iuvavum; Octavius Secundus of Foro Iulii; Sextus [P?]ius Clemens of Cemenelum; Lucius Iulius Lollius of Agrippina; Titus Flavius Faustus of Nicia; Caius Valerius Secundus of Celeia; Caius Billius catus of Dertona; Lucius Flavius Cl[au]d[i]us; Caius Iulius Grac[chus]; Caius Cadaricius Allius; Lucius Cassius Germanus; Caius Vitellius Seranus; Marcus Iulius Annius of Agrip(pina); Lucius Valerius Primus of Blaesius Varro; Aequo Atrectus of Rome; Capito of Heraclea; Lucius Macrinius Valerius [Macer] of Agrippina; Marcus Lollius Secundus of Agrippina; Iulius Valens of Agrippina; Aulus Albanius Macedo of Agrippina; Publius Pomponius Mansuetus of Alba; Voconius Tat[ius] of Pollentia; Valerius Saturninus of Agrippina; Caius Stratonius; Caius Valerius A[...]; Iulius Germanus of Agrippina; Torius [A]io of Iuvavum; nius I SV [?] and Iuva(vo) Valens; Bo Clemens FP, equestrian; Marcus, standard-bearer; Caius Iu[l]ius, standard-bearer; Titus Iu[l]ius, standard-bearer; Marcus M[arcus?], standard-bearer; Lucius O[ctavius?], standard-bearer; Titus imaginifer; Quintus D[...], imaginifer; Marcus A[...], imaginifer; Marcus M[acrinus?], actarius; Titus, optio; Publius M[acrinus?], beneficiarius; acil Caius D[...], beneficiarius; acil Caius V[al...] har Marcus C[...], tesserarius; c filius Bellovacus; filius Raetus; filius Tunger; Noricus; Cannanef(as); Lexovius; Agrippinensis; en Cas Agrippinensis; Lusitanus; Nervius; t Tunger; Tunger filius; Agrippinensis; filius Agrippinensis; Batavus; filius Afer; Britto Lusitanus; r filius Lucensius; us Agrippinensis; c Batavus; Barbarus; L Carantius; Tic Crisius Ver; Vasco Ani; Titus Flavius Ca[ius]; Missicius Musae; Veldes Texu; Minicius Min[icius?]; Donico Viro; Crescens Senn; cohors II Batavorum; Naso Crescentis; Saurnus; Co Martialis; Mar Viator Aquilae; Bithi Seuti; Amandi Dasi, tubicen; r cornicen; r cornicen; r cornicen; r bucinator; i bucinator; s M... us His... us Max... us Cel... us Va... IO CII O N ME TI O LII I II O cer rim A... I filius D... filius L ERV TO.
EDCS-67400497.summary: A large inscription from the Roman Imperial period, likely erected by Emperor Domitian, commemorating fallen soldiers and naming various military personnel, units, and ethnic groups, possibly associated with the Fifth Legion Macedonica.

EDCS-67400499.text_edition: \n legionis V Macedonicae AL A LL legionis et Orfio Sabino evocato legionis V Macedonicae votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-67400499.translation: From the Fifth Macedonian Legion, A[...] L[...]. Sabinus Evocatus and the Fifth Macedonian Legion have gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-67400499.summary: A votive dedication by members of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67400508.text_edition: \n Flavius Germanus decurio municipii Tropaei et Flavius Petronius sacerdos posuerunt\n
EDCS-67400508.translation: Flavius Germanus, decurion of the municipium of Tropaeum, and Flavius Petronius, priest, placed [this].
EDCS-67400508.summary: A dedication placed by Flavius Germanus, a military decurion, and Flavius Petronius, a priest, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67400510.text_edition: \n Antonius Marinus sacerdos Iovis Optimi Maximi Dolicheni ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-67400510.translation: Antonius Marinus, priest of Jupiter Best, Greatest, and Dolichenus, placed [this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-67400510.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by the priest Antonius Marinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67400515.text_edition: \n Aurelius Flavius Aquila sacerdos qui vixit annos\n
EDCS-67400515.translation: Aurelius Flavius Aquila, priest, who lived...
EDCS-67400515.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription for Aurelius Flavius Aquila, a priest, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67400523.text_edition: \n A CL Claudio Mucasio Claudia Rufiani filia V uxor\n
EDCS-67400523.translation: Claudia Rufiani, daughter of Aulus Claudius Mucasius, and his wife.
EDCS-67400523.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudia Rufiani, recording her parentage and marital status.

EDCS-67400526.text_edition: \n Ulpia Paulina ex boto sic posuit\n
EDCS-67400526.translation: Ulpia Paulina erected this, having made a vow.
EDCS-67400526.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by Ulpia Paulina.

EDCS-67400688.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus.
EDCS-67400688.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, likely part of a larger monument.

EDCS-67400715.text_edition: \n nnis L mensibus VII dies sic XXV Valeria Aurelia coniux una cum filibus sic suis pientissimo pa<t>ri bona memoria posuerunt\n
EDCS-67400715.translation: Valeria Aurelia, with her devoted children, set this up for her most devoted father, [in his X] years, VII months, and XXV days, with good memory.
EDCS-67400715.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Valeria Aurelia and her children for her father, detailing the length of his life in years, months, and days.

EDCS-67400716.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpi Victoris equitis alae II Aravacorum factus explorator stationis Saltensis vixit annis XXX et Ulpia Antonia quae vixit annis XX Ulpia Gaia generi et filiae bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-67400716.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ulpius Victor, an equestrian of the II Aravacorum ala, who became an explorator of the Saltensis station and lived 30 years, and Ulpia Antonia, who lived 20 years. Ulpia Gaia set this up for her deserving son-in-law and daughter.
EDCS-67400716.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ulpius Victor and Ulpia Antonia, set up by Ulpia Gaia, recording their military service and ages at death.

EDCS-67500002.text_edition: \n Apollini et Dianae Appius Claudius Martialis Iunius Silvinus legatus Augustorum legionis I Italicae cum Sextia Titi filia Torquata coniuge et liberis Montis praesidibus deis ninguosi sic natis de Iove in insula vaganti voto coniugis atque liberorum aram dedicat Appius legatus \n
EDCS-67500002.translation: Appius Claudius Martialis Iunius Silvinus, legate of the Emperors of the I Italica legion, together with his wife Sextia Torquata, daughter of Titus, and their children, dedicates this altar to Apollo and Diana, guardian deities of the snowy mountains, born of Jupiter, wandering on the island, fulfilling a vow made by his wife and children, Appius the legate.
EDCS-67500002.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to Apollo and Diana by Appius Claudius Martialis Iunius Silvinus, his family, and likely on behalf of the imperial cult, at a mountainous location.

EDCS-67500003.text_edition: \n Deanae et Apollini sacrum pro salute <P=T>(ubli) Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii et Marco Aureli Veri Caesaris et adventu Luci Iuli Severi consularis legati Augusti pro <praetore> Gaius Iulius Macedo beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-67500003.translation: Sacred to Diana and Apollo. Gaius Iulius Macedo, a beneficiarius of the consular legate of the province, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the safety of Publius Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, and Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar, and on the arrival of Gaius Iulius Severus, consular legate of the Emperor.
EDCS-67500003.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Iulius Macedo, a subordinate officer, fulfilling a vow for the well-being of the emperors and honoring a consular legate.

EDCS-67500005.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or ownership marker, for Aurelius Hegenianus.

EDCS-68900050.text_edition: \n Feliciter Dianae Reginae Titus Flavius Iulius |(centurio) legionis I Italicae praepositus numeri civium Romanorum cum suis ex voto solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-68900050.translation: Happily to Diana Regina. Titus Flavius Iulius, a centurion of the I Italica legion, prefect of a unit of Roman citizens, fulfilled his vow with his people, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-68900050.summary: This is a votive inscription by Titus Flavius Iulius, a military officer, dedicated to Diana Regina.

EDCS-68900083.translation: Gaius Iulius Saturninus, centurion of the I Italica Sev[erian] legion, with his people, gladly set up [his] vow.
EDCS-68900083.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Iulius Saturninus, a centurion of the I Italica legion, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-69200453.translation: For the safety of the Augusti for many years! To good fortune. This Dionysus, also giver of fruits, I, Flavius [---]anus, primipilarius of the province of Hellespontus, [---] of the first Italian legion, have set up in memory. To good fortune.
EDCS-69200453.summary: This Greek inscription, with Latin elements, is a dedication by a primipilarius of the Hellespontus province to Dionysus, likely commemorating his service with the Legio I Italica, and dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200454.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tetteiae Cai filiae Maximillae \n
EDCS-69200454.translation: To the departed spirits of Tetteia Maximilla, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-69200454.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Tetteia Maximilla, set up by her family and dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200455.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Ponticus veteranus\n
EDCS-69200455.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Julius Ponticus, a veteran.
EDCS-69200455.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a veteran named Julius Ponticus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200460.text_edition: \n ex viso Virius <L=I>inus veteranus Rustico et Aqui<l=I>ino consulibus \n
EDCS-69200460.translation: In accordance with a vision, Virius Vinus, a veteran, in the consulship of Rusticus and Aquilinus.
EDCS-69200460.summary: This inscription records a dedication made 'in accordance with a vision' by a veteran named Virius Vinus, dated by the consuls Rusticus and Aquilinus to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200461.text_edition: \n Deo Heroni Publius Licinius Romanus decurio alae votum posuit\n
EDCS-69200461.translation: To the god Heron, Publius Licinius Romanus, a cavalry decurion, dedicated his vow.
EDCS-69200461.summary: This inscription records a vow dedicated to the god Heron by Publius Licinius Romanus, a decurion of an auxiliary cavalry unit, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70200158.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Giver of Abundant Fruits, Gaius Antonius Celer and Viccia Satunina fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-70200158.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter as a fruit-giver, with a vow fulfilled by Gaius Antonius Celer and Viccia Satunina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300251.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Numini Augusti Titus Claudius Tiberinus miles agrimensor legionis XI Claudiae posuit\n
EDCS-71300251.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and to the divine power of Augustus, Titus Claudius Tiberinus, a soldier and surveyor of the eleventh Claudian legion, set this up.
EDCS-71300251.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter and the imperial cult by Titus Claudius Tiberinus, a soldier and surveyor of Legio XI Claudia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300252.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Publius Lae Comicus votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-71300252.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and to Juno Regina, Publius Lae Comicus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-71300252.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Juno Regina by Publius Lae Comicus, who willingly fulfilled a vow, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300261.text_edition: \n Flavius Cupitus veteranus de numero Orsariensium sic sagittariorum vixit nnos LX nubsit sic suam conparenm sic Firminam habuit filius sic Gutium et Vetranionem co<n=S>copivit sic una dati vitas \n
EDCS-71300261.translation: Flavius Cupitus, a veteran from the company of the Orsarienses, indeed archers, lived 60 years, married Firmina, had a son Gutius and Vetranio, together they shared their lives.
EDCS-71300261.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Flavius Cupitus, a veteran archer, detailing his age, wife, and children, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72500212.summary: This is a partial inscription identifying a unit of the Roman army, the 1st Cohort of the Legio V Macedonica, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72500213.summary: This inscription records the name of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72500214.summary: This inscription identifies the Macedonian Legion by its tribal affiliation (Varinia), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72500215.summary: This inscription identifies the Macedonian Legion by its tribal affiliation (Varinia), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72500217.summary: This inscription refers to a Prefect associated with the tribe Varina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72500218.summary: This inscription records the title of a Prefect of the riverbank, associated with the tribe Varina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72500219.summary: This inscription records the title of an officer in charge of a riverbank, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72700151.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus. For the well-being of Aelius Valerius, the IIvir of the colony of Ratiaria. Paulus, his slave, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-72700151.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mercury Augustus by a slave on behalf of his master, Aelius Valerius, a high-ranking official in the colony of Ratiaria, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72800155.text_edition: \n PRODomitius Antigonus vir clarissimus legatus Augusti pro praetore ex quaestura cohortis I Lusitanorum M[aximinia]nae consecravit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-72800155.translation: Domitius Antigonus, most distinguished man, governor of the province with the rank of legate of Augustus, consecrated and dedicated this from his quaestorship of the First Cohort of Lusitanians, the Maximiniana.
EDCS-72800155.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Domitius Antigonus, a high-ranking imperial official, likely from the 3rd century AD, associated with a military cohort.

EDCS-72900277.summary: This inscription records a vow made for an individual named Veronius (?), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-73300090.translation: For Porobonus. Domitius Domitianus set this up for himself and his family in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-73300090.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by Domitius Domitianus for himself and his family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-73300096.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, Persicus Maximus, Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power, Father of the Country, Proconsul, and to Marcus Julius Philippus, his most noble son, Caesar, under the supervision of Prastina Messalina, legate of Augustus, propraetor, 1 mile.
EDCS-73300096.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, dating to his reign (AD 244-249).

EDCS-73900222.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Rufinus Marcus |(centurio) legionis I Italicae regionarius \n
EDCS-73900222.translation: To Liber Pater, from Rufinus Marcus, centurion of the I Italica Legion, a regional officer.
EDCS-73900222.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Liber Pater by a centurion of the I Italica Legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-73900224.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matri Augusti et castrorum Marcus Aurelius Ulpia Teres Pautalia primus pilus legionis I Italicae Antoninianae Dedicatum Kalendis Maiis Caio Iulio Aspro II et Iulio Aspro consulibus\n
EDCS-73900224.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety of our lord Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and Julia Augusta, mother of the emperor and of the camps, from Marcus Aurelius Ulpia Teres, primus pilus of the I Italica Antoninianae Legion. Dedicated on the Kalends of May, when Gaius Julius Asper II and Julius Asper were consuls.
EDCS-73900224.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius and Empress Julia Domna, made by a primus pilus of the I Italica Legion, and dated by consuls to AD 217.

EDCS-73900225.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Pacuvius Marci filius Formiis Iustus VEn praefectus \n
EDCS-73900225.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, from Marcus Pacuvius Iustus, son of Marcus, from Formiae, prefect.
EDCS-73900225.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Pacuvius Iustus, a prefect, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-73900226.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Quintus Volumnius Firmus vexillarius legionis I Italicae Iuli M tribuni la<t=I>iclavii \n
EDCS-73900226.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, from Quintus Volumnius Firmus, standard-bearer of the I Italica Legion, under the tribuneship of Iulius M., a man of senatorial rank.
EDCS-73900226.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Quintus Volumnius Firmus, a vexillarius of the I Italica Legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-73900227.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Frontina vixit annos -XII Flavius Fronto una cum Flavia Pinna filia bene merenti posuit V Idus Maias Orfito et Rufo consulibus\n
EDCS-73900227.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Frontina lived 12 years. Flavius Fronto, together with his daughter Flavia Pinna, set this up for her, well-deserving. The fifth day before the Ides of May, when Orfitus and Rufus were consuls.
EDCS-73900227.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Flavia Frontina, set up by her father Flavius Fronto and sister Flavia Pinna, and dated by consuls to AD 165.

EDCS-74100160.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan Parhticus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, Father of the Country, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time, Marcus Antius Rufinus set this boundary between the Moesians and the Thracians.
EDCS-74100160.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary between Moesia and Thrace by Marcus Antius Rufinus, under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to AD 136-138.

EDCS-74100162.translation: Sacred to the god Silvanus, from Aelius Anton[...], quaestor(?).
EDCS-74100162.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus by Aelius Anton[...], who may have held the office of quaestor, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-74100171.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and all the gods and goddesses, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, from the detachment of the I Italica Legion, with Luctatius Maximus, tribune.
EDCS-74100171.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter and other gods for the safety of Emperor Hadrian by a detachment of the I Italica Legion under a tribune, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (AD 117-138).

EDCS-74700044.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to Dolichenus. Atidius willingly paid his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-74700044.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Jupiter Dolichenus, made by Atidius, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-74700047.translation: To Claudius Fuscus, who lived 6 years. Claudius Metrodorus and Sura, parents, [dedicated this] to their son.
EDCS-74700047.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy named Claudius Fuscus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74700050.translation: Julius Epiphanes lived 102 years. Oppia Julia, daughter, and Julianus, grandson, and Flavius Onesimus, his well-deserving father, erected this.
EDCS-74700050.summary: A funerary epitaph for Julius Epiphanes, erected by his daughter, grandson, and another individual, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300271.translation: Marcianus set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-75300271.summary: A votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by a person named Marcianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76100064.translation: To Hercules the Unconquered, Quintus Statilius Valentianus and Quintus Statilius Faedianis, brothers and possibly councilors of the colony, erected this.
EDCS-76100064.summary: A dedication to Hercules Invictus by two brothers, identified as Statilii, possibly local officials, from a Roman colony, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76100069.translation: To Diana and Apollo for the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, Aelius Elianus, son of Titus, from Traianensis, [dedicated this].
EDCS-76100069.summary: A dedication to Diana and Apollo for the health of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus, made by Aelius Elianus, possibly from Traianensis, dating to their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-76100070.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. For the safety of the Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, father of the fatherland, [---] Vindex, a courier, ordered [me] to place [this] from his own [funds]. Vindex made [this].
EDCS-76100070.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius, commissioned by a courier named Vindex, likely dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-76100073.translation: To the Conservator and various cult deities, Gaius Domitius and Xenianus [dedicated this].
EDCS-76100073.summary: A dedication to a protective deity known as Conservator and other deities by Gaius Domitius and Xenianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76700055.translation: [---] the great-grandsons of the deified Trajan, Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, [built this] from the LX milestone through the II Flavian Cohort of Britons, under Servilius Fabianus, imperial legate with proconsular power.
EDCS-76700055.summary: A milestone inscription recording construction by descendants of prominent emperors (Trajan and Nerva) and military units, under the command of a legate, Servilius Fabianus, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-76700106.translation: The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul for the first time, designated consul for the second time, [built this] from Oesco, 40 miles.
EDCS-76700106.summary: An imperial milestone indicating a road built or maintained under Emperor Hadrian, marking a distance of 40 miles from Oesco, dating to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-76900145.summary: A simple funerary epitaph naming Marcus and his father Secundus, likely from a Greek-speaking region of the Roman Empire.

EDCS-76900156.translation: Hermogenes, in memory of his father Kestios, who lived honorably for 60 years, and of his mother Moschia.
EDCS-76900156.summary: A funerary epigram from an unknown location, likely Roman imperial period, commemorating the deceased parents of Hermogenes.

EDCS-77000083.summary: A short inscription likely on a statue or artwork, indicating the maker, Euktemon, possibly from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-77000203.translation: [...] Longos for remembrance, for whom he set up this finely written stele. [...] Lenaeos Theophilos, twice [...]. [...] Diogenes son of Askos, Lenaeos Tanes son of Dionysios. [...] Diogenes son of Alexandros, Alitarhos. [...] Lis son of Kalleas, Alitarhos, Dionysios son of Timotheos, Alitarhos. [...] Pontikos son of Aistiaios, Alitarhos, Kasymedes son of Demosthenes, Edile. [...] Edile, Chairemon son of Dioskourides, Alitarhos.
EDCS-77000203.summary: A funerary stele from an unknown location, likely Greek East, listing names and titles such as Lenaeos and Alitarhos, dating probably to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-77500096.translation: To Asclepius and Hygeia. Marteinos, a *fiscalis benefactor*.
EDCS-77500096.summary: A dedication to the gods Asclepius and Hygeia by Marteinos, a fiscal official, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-77500104.translation: For good fortune, for the lord Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius [[Commodus]] Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus Britannicus, Archpriest, holder of tribunician power for the 9th time, Imperator for the 7th time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, for good fortune and victory and eternal endurance, and for his entire imperial household, the sacred Senate, and the Roman people; and for the most illustrious governor Iulius Castus, legate of Augustus, proconsul, and for the most excellent procurator of Augustus, Claudius Kensos, the city of Nikopolis on the Istros constructed the hot baths from its own revenues, while Iulius Castus was governor of the province and dedicated it.
EDCS-77500104.summary: A monumental inscription from Nikopolis on the Istros (modern Bulgaria), dated to the reign of Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus (AD 177-180), recording the construction of a bath complex by the city under the governor Iulius Castus.

EDCS-77500105.translation: For good fortune. The council and the people of the most illustrious city of Nikopolis on the Istros (dedicated this to) the greatest and most divine Imperator Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, during the consulate of the province (under) Tullius [Menophilos], legate of Augustus, proconsul, in joint rule with Iulius Iulianus, high priest and first magistrate, with supervision by Publius Aelius Meniannus, high priest, acting with goodwill.
EDCS-77500105.summary: A dedication from Nikopolis on the Istros (modern Bulgaria) to Emperor Gordian III, dated to the period of his reign (AD 238-244) based on the consuls and governor mentioned.

EDCS-77500107.translation: For good fortune. The city erected (this) to Zeus Keraunios in gratitude, on the 16th day before the Kalends of August, when Maximus and Paternus were consuls.
EDCS-77500107.summary: A public dedication to Zeus Keraunios by a city, dated by the consulship of Maximus and Paternus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-77700191.translation: To the Holy God Silvanus. For the safety of the Emperor Marcus Antoninus Gordianus and his son Ulpius Valerianus, Ulpius Claudianus and his son donated an altar to the college of Silvanus, through the priest Ulpius Ulpianus.
EDCS-77700191.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by Ulpius Claudianus and his son for the safety of Emperor Gordian III and his son, from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-78300245.translation: Ulp[ius] Theodorus, *beneficiarius consularis*, placed (this) for his own safety and that of his family and of the territory of the Augustani, according to his vow.
EDCS-78300245.summary: A votive inscription by Ulpius Theodorus, a *beneficiarius consularis*, for his own safety and that of his family and the territory, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-78300247.summary: A very brief inscription, likely a name on a tombstone or ownership mark, identifying Gaius Atisius Sabinus.

EDCS-78300248.translation: To Victory, Longinus the tribune, according to his vow, erected [this] with his whole family.
EDCS-78300248.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Victory by a tribune named Longinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300249.translation: To the divine will of Augustus and the public genius of the tax-collectors, Titus Julius Eutyches, contractor of the Illyrian tolls and of the Thracian coast, by his overseers Thallio and Agathopus, [erected this].
EDCS-78300249.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Titus Julius Eutyches, a tax contractor for Illyricum and Thrace, honoring the divine will of Augustus and the public genius of his department, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300715.translation: With good fortune, Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 20th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, by the council and the people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube.
EDCS-78300715.summary: This is a formal dedication honoring Emperor Hadrian, listing his titles and achievements, set up by the city of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube during his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-78300716.translation: With good fortune, the council and people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube raised [this] to Gaius Rubrius Vinicianus Porcius Marcellus, legate of Augustus, proconsul, who governed the most distinguished province of Thrace, as patron and benefactor.
EDCS-78300716.summary: This is a dedication by the city of Ulpia Nicopolis honoring Gaius Rubrius Vinicianus Porcius Marcellus, a governor of Thrace and patron of the city, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300718.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, Germanicus, Dacicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 24th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, by the council and people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube, under the governorship of Gargilius Anticitus, legate of Augustus, proconsul.
EDCS-78300718.summary: This is a formal dedication honoring Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his titles and achievements, set up by the city of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube during his reign (138-161 AD) and the governorship of Gargilius Anticitus.

EDCS-78300719.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the deified Commodus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, descendant of the deified Hadrian, descendant of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country; and Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, grandson of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, descendant of the deified Antoninus Pius, descendant of the deified Hadrian and the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holder of tribunician power, consul; to the governors and the council and the people of Nicopolis on the Istros, greetings. We have seen your most evident zeal through the decree. For as you are well-disposed and pious men, and strive for the best to exist among us, so you have declared to those present, sharing in and bringing a public festival for the good tidings of our successes, and for the universal peace existing for all men, through the defeat of the always insolent barbarians against the empire, and through our just alliance, having Caesar as our own and true protector. Therefore, we read the decree with the honor due to you, and accepted the completion of the funds, seventy myriad, as from well-disposed men. The decree was sent by Ovinius Tertullus, our most excellent friend and legate. May it be fortunate.
EDCS-78300719.summary: This is a lengthy official communication from Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla to the city of Nicopolis on the Istros, acknowledging their loyalty and financial contribution, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-78300720.translation: With good fortune, Emperor Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, descendant of the deified Hadrian and the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holder of tribunician power, proconsul, under the governorship of the province by Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, legate of the Augusti, proconsul, the most sacred council and the most excellent people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Istros raised [this].
EDCS-78300720.summary: This is a dedication by the city of Ulpia Nicopolis honoring Emperor Caracalla, listing his imperial titles and mentioning the governor Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-78300721.translation: With good fortune, the council and people of the most illustrious city of Nicopolis on the Istros raised [this] to the greatest and most divine Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Severus. Fortunately.
EDCS-78300721.summary: This is a dedication by the city of Nicopolis on the Istros honoring Emperor Severus Alexander, likely dating to his reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-78300722.translation: With good fortune, the most illustrious city of Nicopolis on the river Istros raised [this] to the greatest and most god-beloved Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Eutus Augustus, son of the greatest and most divine Emperor Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Augustus Pius Felix, fortunately, under the consulship of Flavius Lucillianus, legate of the Augusti, proconsul, under the supervision of [a certain official].
EDCS-78300722.summary: This is a dedication by the city of Nicopolis on the Istros honoring Emperor Maximinus Thrax, likely dating to his reign (235-238 AD) and mentioning the governor Flavius Lucillianus.

EDCS-78300723.translation: With good fortune, the most illustrious city of Nicopolis on the Istros raised [this] to the greatest and most divine Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, consul for the 1st time, father of the country, under the governorship of the province by Tullius Menophilus, legate of Augustus, proconsul, under the supervision of Antonius Felix, archiereus, a man of distinction. Fortunately.
EDCS-78300723.summary: This is a dedication by the city of Nicopolis on the Istros honoring Emperor Gordian III, listing his titles and mentioning the governor Tullius Menophilus and the supervising priest Antonius Felix, likely dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-78300725.translation: To Olympian Zeus, Hera, and Athena, for the victory and endurance of the Emperor Caesar Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Aelius Conon Ulpius Sempronius Sabinus, a sandal-maker from Nicopolis, dedicated this vow.
EDCS-78300725.summary: This inscription is a dedicatory monument to three gods for the emperor's victory and endurance, dedicated by a shoemaker from Nicopolis, likely during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-78300784.translation: To good fortune. In the consulship of Maecius Laetus (for the second time) and Sylla Cerialis, nine days before the Kalends of February, during the priesthood of the Great God Derzelas, on the seventh day of the month Boedromion, these are the ephebes and ephors of the collegium: Aurelius Dionysodorus and Agathanor, sons of Iulianus. [List of names follows].
EDCS-78300784.summary: This inscription records the names of ephebes and their leaders (ephors) in a civic or religious association, dated by the consuls and a month in the Greek calendar, likely in a Roman province in the East during the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-78300785.translation: To the Emperor Titus Caesar Augustus, son of the Emperor Vespasian, the high priest, holding tribunician power, to whom was given the name of Father of the Fatherland. Heracleon, son of Dionysius, the high priest, erected this first statue at his own expense, providing an endowment for the citizens, Romans, and foreigners.
EDCS-78300785.summary: This bilingual inscription records the dedication of a statue of Emperor Titus Caesar Augustus by the high priest Heracleon, son of Dionysius, at his own expense, with an endowment for the populace and visitors.

EDCS-78400086.translation: To the unconquered one, deservedly, [he] placed [this vow] for his own health. Sise Mucasi.
EDCS-78400086.summary: This is a dedication to an unconquered deity for the health of the dedicator, Sise Mucasi, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78400191.translation: To good fortune. The council and the people raised (a statue) to the divine Crispeina Augusta.
EDCS-78400191.summary: This is a dedication by a city council and people to the deified Empress Crispeina, likely erected in a public space.

EDCS-79300081.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus established the boundary between the Thracians and the Moesians.
EDCS-79300081.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary between the Thracians and Moesians by Marcus Antius Rufinus, acting under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, dating to Hadrian's 20th year of tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-79300082.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus established the boundary between the Thracians and the Moesians.
EDCS-79300082.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary between the Thracians and Moesians by Marcus Antius Rufinus, acting under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, dating to Hadrian's 20th year of tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-79300083.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus established the boundary between the Thracians and the Moesians.
EDCS-79300083.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary between the Thracians and Moesians by Marcus Antius Rufinus, acting under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, dating to Hadrian's 20th year of tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-79300084.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus established the boundary between the Thracians and the Moesians. In number: IIII.
EDCS-79300084.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary between the Thracians and Moesians by Marcus Antius Rufinus, acting under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, dating to Hadrian's 20th year of tribunician power and 3rd consulship, and notes the boundary markers as four.

EDCS-81000041.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Marcus Cocceius Zeuxius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-81000041.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Dolichenus, dedicated by Marcus Cocceius Zeuxius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81200279.translation: The council and the people [honored] Theognetus, the benefactor. By good fortune. During the priesthood of Zeus for the fifth time, and of Dionysus Karpophoros, the priests Hekataios son of Euxenidus, Hestiaios son of Diōn, and Kleitophon son of Dionysius.
EDCS-81200279.summary: This is a fragmentary honorary inscription from a city council and people to a benefactor, dated by priesthoods.

EDCS-81300048.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aufelianus [illegible text].
EDCS-81300048.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning Emperor Lucius Domitius Aufelianus, likely dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08300504.translation: For the safety of our Emperors Septimius Severus Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti.
EDCS-08300504.summary: This inscription is a dedication for the safety of the emperors Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08500593.summary: This is a simple personal name inscription, possibly a tombstone or ownership mark, with no further context provided.

EDCS-09200007.summary: This inscription records that Marcus Arretio was the maker of an unnamed object or structure.

EDCS-09300024.translation: This memorial for Victor and Alexandria.
EDCS-09300024.summary: This inscription designates a memorial dedicated to Victor and Alexandria.

EDCS-09300044.translation: To our Lords Flavius Constantinus Maximus Victor Semper Augustus, and Flavius Julius Crispus, and Flavius Claudius Constantinus, and Flavius Julius Constantius.
EDCS-09300044.summary: This is a dedication to several emperors including Constantine the Great and his sons, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-09300679.translation: Sperentes' son to Marturio the priest.
EDCS-09300679.summary: This inscription records a dedication from Sperentes' son to a priest named Marturio.

EDCS-09300680.translation: Austatis dedicated (this) living in Christ for the sacred realm.
EDCS-09300680.summary: This inscription is a religious dedication by Austatis, likely Christian, for sacred purposes in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-09401564.summary: This inscription identifies Gaius Antonius by his official position as magistrate.

EDCS-09700339.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus and Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta. Herennius Apollinaris, a beneficiary of a consular, the bedroom with its hunting net, a gift from his own resources to those below him: to Publius Valerius Chares, Theodore, Heron, Asclepiades, Hygiainos, Heron the Italian, Minicius Athenaeus, and Tryphon, Aelius Hephaistiodorus and Archius, Fronto son of Callistratus, Dionysius son of Callistratus, Aemilius son of Aemilianus, Heron son of Noumenius, Julius Flavius Heron son of Heron, Hygiainos son of Heron, Dionysius (twice), Asclepiades son of Theodorus, Theodorus son of Asclepiades, Heliodorus son of Theodorus, Eusebes son of Eusebes, Heliodorus son of Pharnaces, Dionysodorus son of Italianus, Asclepiades son of Hieronymus, Alexander son of Maximus, Ariston son of Euprepes, Pompeianus son of Heraclides, Aelius Julianus son of Athenaeus, Bocchus, Flavius Hephaistion, Pancharis son of Dionysius, Heracleon son of Cornelius, Pannoni, scribes; Socrates son of Calandius, an advocate; Carpus son of Syntrophus, a most excellent friend; Rigillianos, Callineicos, Valentus, Serapion, Zosimus, Asclepiades, Julius Eutychianus, Rhonoicus son of Diodorus, Heracleides, the official.
EDCS-09700339.summary: This extensive inscription records a dedication for the safety of Emperor Gordian III and his empress, listing numerous officials and subordinates, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701193.translation: To our Lords Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, victorious and triumphant emperors forever.
EDCS-09701193.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, celebrating their victories and rule, likely from the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-11300007.translation: In this tomb is laid Terentius, son of Gaiona, twenty-five years old, serving among the junior archers.
EDCS-11300007.summary: A tombstone for Terentius, a junior archer who died at age 25.

EDCS-11300009.translation: This memorial for Victor and Alexandria.
EDCS-11300009.summary: A memorial inscription dedicated to Victor and Alexandria.

EDCS-11300052.translation: Here the oration of Bishop Stephen was made: Light, Life.
EDCS-11300052.summary: An inscription recording the oration of Bishop Stephen, possibly at a church council or synod.

EDCS-11300057.translation: C., a scout from the detachment of 12 cataphractarii, which is with Trimamius, lived 33 years, served 13 years.
EDCS-11300057.summary: A military inscription for C., a scout in the 12th cataphractarii unit, detailing his age and service length.

EDCS-11300070.summary: A short dedicatory inscription to 'Vivamus', addressed as a lord brother.

EDCS-11300089.summary: An inscription possibly indicating a dedication or benefaction by Emperor Anastasius to Altina.

EDCS-11300091.translation: The squadron of juniors at Noviodunum, Emperor Anastasius, to Altina.
EDCS-11300091.summary: An inscription related to the squadron of juniors at Noviodunum, possibly honoring Emperor Anastasius and Altina.

EDCS-11300092.summary: A very short inscription likely related to Emperor Anastasius, perhaps a dedication or a reference to his reign.

EDCS-11300093.summary: A very short inscription likely related to Emperor Anastasius, perhaps a dedication or a reference to his reign.

EDCS-11300108.translation: To our lords Augusti, Flavius Constantine, greatest triumphant forever, and Flavius Constantine and Flavius Constantius, and Constantius, and Flavius.
EDCS-11300108.summary: A dedication to multiple Roman emperors, including Constantine the Great and his sons, likely from the 4th century.

EDCS-11300800.summary: This inscription contains only the names of three individuals, likely a funerary or commemorative marker.

EDCS-11300824.translation: Image of the Holy Sun God. Consecrated on the 14th day before the Kalends of December. It must be propitiated each year with incense, candles, and libations, on the same day, by the prefects and detachments serving in the Salsovia Camp, by sacred order of our lords Licinius Augustus and Licinius Caesar. Valerius Romulus, a most accomplished man, the governor, described this following the order. To the Divine Spirits. For Titus Flavius Faustinus, who lived 70 years. For Valerius Victor, who lived 75 years. Flavius Festus, centurion of the I Italica Legion, placed [this] for his father.
EDCS-11300824.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Titus Flavius Faustinus and Valerius Victor by Flavius Festus, a centurion, which also includes a complex religious dedication concerning the worship of Sol Invictus ordered by Licinius Augustus and Licinius Caesar and executed by Valerius Romulus, governor of the camps at Salsovia, likely dating to the Tetrarchy (early 4th century CE).

EDCS-11301227.translation: For Liber Pater Conservator of our Lords Augusti. Flavius Zosimus, primus pilus, from the province of Asia, of the citizenship of the Ephesians, erected [this] in fulfillment of a vow after the consulship of the soldiers.
EDCS-11301227.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Flavius Zosimus, a primus pilus from Ephesus, dedicated to Liber Pater, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301291.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly containing a personal name and a gentile name, with illegible text.

EDCS-11301312.translation: To the Great Mother Goddess. Caia Valeria, wife of Caius Valerius Longinus, a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion, willingly paid her vow.
EDCS-11301312.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caia Valeria, wife of a veteran of the V Macedonica Legion, dedicated to the Great Mother Goddess, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301451.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. My name is Basileus. I lie here with my mother, having perished by a harsh fate, struck down by a heavy illness. The man from Kyzikos buried me, and the glorious Secunda also, having died prematurely, plucked the flowers of youth by a harsh fate and a heavy illness. Under this mountain, Basileus is buried. Having met his end quickly, the youth's fate fulfilled. His mother lies with me, she too buried. To me from Kyzikos, honor was done, and also to Secunda.
EDCS-11301451.summary: This is a bilingual funerary epitaph in Greek and Latin for a young man named Basileus, who died young from illness, buried by a man from Kyzikos and his mother Secunda, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301494.translation: True god EROS ISIVS TRE OVIIC DOS SEPEL
EDCS-11301494.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription, possibly related to a deity named EROS or a funerary context, with a significant portion of the text missing.

EDCS-11700826.translation: From the gifts of God and Saints Cosmas and Damian, Stefanus the deacon ordered [it] to be built.
EDCS-11700826.summary: This inscription records the construction of a building or monument by Stefanus the deacon, dedicated to God and Saints Cosmas and Damian, likely from the early Christian period.

EDCS-11800269.translation: Gaius Herennius Charito paid his vow, having prayed.
EDCS-11800269.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Herennius Charito, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11800332.translation: Aelius Victor, beneficiarius of the consular governor of the I Italica Legion, lived 38 years, served 18 years. Aelius Severianus, decurion of the municipality of Durostorum, placed [this] for his dearest brother. Aelius Victor, beneficiarius of the consular governor of the first Italica Legion, lived 38 years, served 18 years. Aelius Severianus, council member of the Durostorians, dedicated [this] to his dearest brother.
EDCS-11800332.summary: This bilingual funerary inscription in Latin and Greek records the life and death of Aelius Victor, a beneficiarius of the I Italica Legion, and his brother Aelius Severianus, a decurion from Durostorum, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302623.translation: To Timotheus, son of Nonius, from the Nemia region, for kindness. Cornelia Fortunata, and also Doutouros, greetings. Cornelia Fortunata, wife of Stabyllion, read and praise her, stranger, for her distinguished and chaste devotion to her husband, and for leaving behind shared tears. Then, pass by and obtain blessings. Cornelia Fortunata, wife of Marcus Cornelius Stabilio, lived 30 years, is buried here. Timotheus, son of Noni, at his own expense, for the sake of memory.
EDCS-13302623.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Fortunata, commending her character and relationship with her husband, erected by her wife and an associate named Doutouros, and a memorial at her expense by Timotheus, son of Nonius. It is dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16000357.translation: To Marcus Cocceius Ter, cavalry prefect.
EDCS-16000357.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Cocceius Ter, who held the position of cavalry prefect during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200564.translation: To the Chthonic Gods, to Hosia, daughter of Flavius Abascantus, who lived 25 years. Flavius Abascantus and Primigenia, her parents, dedicated [this] to their dutiful daughter. To the Spirits of the Dead. Hosia, daughter of Flavius Abascantus, lived 25 years. Flavius Abascantus and Primigenia, her parents, had this made for their deserving daughter.
EDCS-16200564.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hosia, daughter of Flavius Abascantus and Primigenia, who lived 25 years, erected by her parents. It is dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200797.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, father of the country. The people of Histria [dedicated this], Titus Pomponius Proculus Vitrasius Pollio, imperial legate with proconsular authority, saw to its construction. To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, high priest, father of the country. The people of Histria, through Titus Pomponius Proculus Vitrasius Pollio, legate of Augustus and vice-regent, saw to its construction.
EDCS-16200797.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius by the city of Histria, with the inscription also mentioning the imperial legate Titus Pomponius Proculus Vitrasius Pollio, likely dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-26600719.translation: Appollonius and Helpis, freedmen, erected [this] for their deserving patron.
EDCS-26600719.summary: This is a dedication by two freedmen, Apollonius and Helpis, to their patron, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100034.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Valerius Alexander made this tomb for himself while alive, and is buried here. Gaius Valerius Alexander made the monument for himself while alive.
EDCS-27100034.summary: This is a self-funded tomb inscription for Gaius Valerius Alexander, indicating he prepared his burial place during his lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100035.translation: While Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius was chief priest and father of the country, the city of Odessus brought water by a new duct, under the supervision of Titus Vitrasius Pollio, imperial legate with proconsular authority. For good fortune! To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, high priest, father of the country. The city of Odessus brought water by a new conduit, through the supervision of Titus Vitrasius Pollio, legate of Augustus and vice-regent.
EDCS-27100035.summary: This inscription records the construction of a new water conduit to the city of Odessus during the reign of Emperor Antoninus Pius, facilitated by the legate Titus Vitrasius Pollio, dating to the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-27100050.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the country. Publius Calpurnius Macro Caulius Rufus, imperial legate with proconsular authority, [erected this].
EDCS-27100050.summary: This is an inscription dedicating something to Emperor Hadrian, noting his numerous titles and honours, and mentioning the legate Publius Calpurnius Macro Caulius Rufus, dating to Hadrian's reign (likely around 132 AD based on the tribunician power).

EDCS-27100051.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the health and victory and concord of the Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus. Cornelius Firmus, centurion of the I Italica legion, erected this altar.
EDCS-27100051.summary: This is a votive altar erected by Cornelius Firmus, a centurion of the I Italica legion, to Jupiter for the well-being and success of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus, dating to their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-27800434.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time. The state of Tomis, Gaius Ummidius Quadratus Sallustius Sertorius, imperial legate with proconsular authority, dedicated [this]. To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, high priest, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time. The council and people of Tomis dedicated [this], Gaius Ummidius Quadratus Sallustius Sertorius, legate of Augustus and vice-regent.
EDCS-27800434.summary: This inscription dedicates something to Emperor Hadrian by the state of Tomis, mentioning his titles and the legate Gaius Ummidius Quadratus Sallustius Sertorius, dating to Hadrian's reign (likely around 120-121 AD based on his consulship and tribunician power).

EDCS-27800439.translation: Gaius Antonius Fronto, veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, serving as a *beneficiarius consularis*, adorned this sacred grove and tomb for himself and his family while alive. Greetings! Gaius Antonius Fronto, veteran of the 13th Twin legion, decorated the grove and tomb for himself and his own people while alive. Farewell, traveler!
EDCS-27800439.summary: This inscription records a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion dedicating a grove and tomb for himself and his family.

EDCS-27800442.translation: To the departed spirits. Valerius Felix, *princeps officii* of the governor, lived 45 years. Aurelia Aemilia placed this for her deserving husband. Farewell, traveler! To the departed spirits, Valerius Felix, commander of the governor's office, lived 45 years. Aurelia Aemilia erected this column for her ever-memorable husband, a virgin. Farewell, passerby!
EDCS-27800442.summary: This tombstone records the life of Valerius Felix, a Roman official, and was dedicated by his wife Aurelia Aemilia.

EDCS-27800457.translation: To the departed spirits. Servius Sulpicius Priscinus lived 30 years and Servius Sulpicius Festus lived 27 years; two brothers are buried here. Their most dutiful parents had this made. To the chthonic gods, Servius Sulpicius Priscinus lived 30 years and Servius Sulpicius Festus lived 27 years; two brothers are buried here. Their most dutiful parents had this made.
EDCS-27800457.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of two brothers, Servius Sulpicius Priscinus and Servius Sulpicius Festus, and states that their parents commissioned the monument.

EDCS-27800467.translation: Of the holy EI. This memorial of Nazarinianus, son of Aburius, whom he had in marriage, Diogenia [lived with him for] three years and seven months and completed [this] in peace.
EDCS-27800467.summary: This epitaph records the death of Nazarinianus and mentions his wife Diogenia and the duration of their marriage.

EDCS-27800492.translation: Veturius, physician of the I Italica legion, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27800492.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Veturius, a physician of the I Italica legion.

EDCS-27800497.translation: To the genius of the century of Flavius Ianuarius. Flavius Avitianus, standard-bearer of the unit of Syrian archers, willingly placed this according to his vow.
EDCS-27800497.summary: This dedication was made by Flavius Avitianus, a standard-bearer of Syrian archers, to the genius of his centurion, Flavius Ianuarius.

EDCS-27800917.translation: MII OASM monument ias here located with the inscription I Manta Purulae Di MARITAO
EDCS-27800917.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording a burial monument for Manta Purulae, with significant portions missing or illegible.

EDCS-27800986.translation: Marrenus Anencletus lived 72 years. Terentia Victoria, his wife, and Marrena Victoria, his daughter.
EDCS-27800986.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Marrenus Anencletus and mentions his wife and daughter.

EDCS-28400716.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly recording the name Gaius Flavius Iulius Victi, perhaps related to an altar (ara).

EDCS-28500136.translation: To Heron the Invincible. Titus [dedicated this].
EDCS-28500136.summary: This dedication by Titus is made to a figure named Heron the Invincible.

EDCS-28500137.translation: Quinctianus, prefect of detachments, set this up in fulfillment of a vow to Paternos.
EDCS-28500137.summary: This inscription records a military official dedicating a monument to the deity Paternos.

EDCS-28500138.translation: Titus Iunius Hermes, second-ranking magistrate, to Jupiter, having vowed to repay a debt.
EDCS-28500138.summary: This inscription records a local magistrate dedicating a votive offering to Jupiter.

EDCS-28500139.translation: Aurelius Victor, a veteran of the Praetorian Guard, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28500139.summary: This inscription records a former Praetorian Guard veteran dedicating a monument.

EDCS-28500140.translation: From the interest of Riana's money, they established a temple and a treasury for the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus, the Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, conqueror of Arabia, the Adiabeni, the Parthians, and the Germans, to his divine power and majesty, the devoted Cleventini.
EDCS-28500140.summary: This inscription records a community establishing a temple and treasury from interest money for Emperor Caracalla.

EDCS-28500154.translation: To our lord Iulianus, the victorious and triumphant, always Augustus.
EDCS-28500154.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Julian, likely from the 4th century AD.

EDCS-28500158.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, from Tomis.
EDCS-28500158.summary: This inscription, found in Tomis, records titles of Emperor Hadrian, dating it to his reign.

EDCS-29000005.translation: To Victoria, chiliarch of the 13th Legion Gemina.
EDCS-29000005.summary: This inscription, written in Greek, records a dedication to Victoria by a commander of a Roman legion.

EDCS-29000006.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paternos. Iulius Menophilus set this up.
EDCS-29000006.summary: This inscription records an individual dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paternos.

EDCS-29000007.translation: For the safety of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar.
EDCS-29000007.summary: This inscription records a prayer for the well-being of the Severan emperors, including Caracalla and Geta.

EDCS-29000008.translation: Caius Nolanius Amandus, formerly a centurion, honorably discharged.
EDCS-29000008.summary: This inscription records a former centurion who received an honorable discharge.

EDCS-29100010.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Julia Saturnina, a most devoted mother, who lived 50 years. Lucius Julius Saturninus and Lucius Julius Attalus the younger, sons and heirs, set this up for her, her deserving benefactor.
EDCS-29100010.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by sons for their mother, Julia Saturnina.

EDCS-29100034.translation: To our Lords Emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, the Pious Augusts. A Callatide, 6 miles.
EDCS-29100034.summary: This milestone inscription commemorates a road of 6 miles under the reign of Emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian.

EDCS-29100183.translation: Our measure, a good wine-measure; our measure, a measure of wine; a measure of grain. The market superintendent of the market of Piretus placed this from his own funds.
EDCS-29100183.summary: This is an inscription recording the dedication of measures by the market superintendent of Piretus.

EDCS-29601654.translation: To Good Fortune. To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Ruler, to Juno Regina, Minerva, Victoria, Vulcan, Mercury, and the Divine Fates. Aurelius Pudens, consular adjutant, willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-29601654.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Aurelius Pudens to several deities.

EDCS-29900508.translation: In the month of June, on the Kalends, on a Monday, this gate was made during the 14th indiction, under the vicarius Daniel and Maior Probinius. SERSONSEBD.
EDCS-29900508.summary: This inscription records the construction of a gate during the reign of Daniel and Probinius in the 14th indiction.

EDCS-29900513.translation: Rejoice, reader. Here lies happy Augusta, 3 years and 102 days old, daughter of Monius, from the number of the senior Constantinians.
EDCS-29900513.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Augusta, a young girl from a prominent family.

EDCS-29900519.translation: To our Lords Theodosius and Arcadius, the Augusts.
EDCS-29900519.summary: This is an inscription acknowledging the reigning emperors Theodosius and Arcadius.

EDCS-29900534.translation: This is the tomb of Bizantia, daughter of Dominius Alziola, a numerary, buried under Dominius Iustinus in the month of October, in the sixth indiction.
EDCS-29900534.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Bizantia under the supervision of Iustinus.

EDCS-30000419.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, the Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered. 2 miles.
EDCS-30000419.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance of 2 miles under the reign of Emperor Tacitus.

EDCS-30100802.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of the Emperor. The villagers of Trullae, through the magistrates Publius Aelius Attalus and Titus Aelius Secundus. Agathocles made it.
EDCS-30100802.summary: This is a votive inscription by the villagers of Trullae to Jupiter for the emperor's safety, made by Agathocles.

EDCS-30200686.translation: To the divine spirits and the memory of Antistia Firmina, his most rare wife, who lived with me for 23 years. Malius Secundus, a beneficiarius of the consular governor, his husband, had this set up, [stating that] if anyone wishes to place another [person] in this heroön where the aforementioned lies, he shall give 2500 denarii to the treasury and 2500 denarii to the city of Odessos.
EDCS-30200686.summary: A funerary monument set up by Malius Secundus for his wife Antistia Firmina, with a penalty clause for unauthorized burials, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31300286.translation: Sacred to the Hero. Tiberius Claudius Mucasius gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow. Tiberius Claudius Mucasius consecrated this, having made a vow.
EDCS-31300286.summary: A dedication to a Hero by Tiberius Claudius Mucasius, likely a private votive offering from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400057.translation: To Diana. Decumus Mussidius Proculus, a centurion of the V Macedonica legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31400057.summary: A dedication to Diana by Decumus Mussidius Proculus, a centurion of the V Macedonica legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400062.summary: A brief inscription recording a sum of money left by a veteran named Firmus, possibly for a funerary monument or endowment, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400068.summary: A very brief inscription possibly identifying a veteran, with partially illegible text, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400069.summary: An incomplete inscription likely naming a person, Lucius Mes, possibly a soldier or veteran, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400078.translation: To the divine spirits of the young man, so Pirom [---].
EDCS-31400078.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to a young man, with the name partially preserved, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500051.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Iulius Glyco [dedicates this].
EDCS-32500051.summary: A dedication to the syncretic deity Jupiter Dolichenus by Iulius Glyco, likely a private offering from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500061.summary: A simple name inscription, possibly marking ownership or a dedication, belonging to Gaius Antonius Magnus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33400613.translation: Leo the soldier set up this gift.
EDCS-33400613.summary: A dedication by a soldier named Leo, likely a private offering, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33400640.translation: I, Skirtos, son of Dakesis, freedman, having departed life before my time, lie here in Tomis, possessing my tomb. I pray that you are well.
EDCS-33400640.summary: A funerary epigram from Tomis, in which a freedman named Skirtos laments his premature death and wishes well to the living.

EDCS-33400641.translation: To Naetas, son of Dekebalus, for his own wife.
EDCS-33400641.summary: An incomplete dedication, likely for a tomb, from Naetas to his wife.

EDCS-35900019.translation: Laudica lived 80 years. Caius Iulius Octavenius, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, placed this monument and arranged for the tomb to be made for his father Dionysius.
EDCS-35900019.summary: A funerary monument erected by a soldier for his father, specifying the father's name, the son's military service, and the deceased's name and age.

EDCS-35900020.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul.
EDCS-35900020.summary: A full imperial titulature honoring Emperor Decius, likely part of a larger inscription detailing a dedication or honor.

EDCS-35900078.translation: of the soldier Frigeridus the commander
EDCS-35900078.summary: A fragment possibly naming a soldier and a commander, likely part of a larger inscription.

EDCS-36900018.translation: With good fortune. It was resolved by the council and the people, under the supervision of Dorous, son of Harmodius, in the first month of Siton. It was proposed because Diogenes, son of Diogenes, who previously had a need of the people, lent five hundred gold coins interest-free, so that he might provision the people. And in the priesthood of Apollonius, son of Iatros, and Agathinus, son of Simylos, and S. E. Ontos, his great-grandson, he gives fifteen hundred gold coins interest-free, while he is still a child, so that there may be sustenance for the people. For these reasons, praise Diogenes, son of Diogenes, and proclaim him a benefactor of the people, and decree that he be crowned with a golden crown in all contests for his virtue and goodwill towards the people. Also, that a bronze statue of him be erected in the agora wherever he wishes. His father, Diogenes, son of Glaukias, having dedicated the Museum and left three hundred gold coins for the sacrifice to the Muses and a gathering for the people, he is to be priest of the Muses and to offer sacrifices to the Muses, and to be invited to all public sacrifices and to wear a garland, sharing equally in all respects with the other priests. The priesthood is to pass to his descendants, always to the eldest, so that they may become more ambitious, observing that the people honor all who benefit it. This decree is to be inscribed on three stone stelai and erected.
EDCS-36900018.summary: A decree honoring Diogenes for his financial contributions to provision the city and his family's religious endowments, granting him honors including a golden crown, a bronze statue, and a hereditary priesthood of the Muses.

EDCS-36900025.translation: It was resolved by the council and the people, under the supervision of Theokritos, son of Pythoteles, and Archelocos. It was proposed because Diodorus, son of Thrasykles, Prokritus, son of Pherekles, and Klearchos, son of Aristomachus, having been appointed envoys for the hostages to Zalmodegikos, traveled through enemy territory, enduring all danger and showing every zeal, and brought back the hostages, more than sixty in number, and persuaded him to pay the revenues to the people. It has been resolved by the council and the people to proclaim them and their descendants as benefactors of the people, and that they and their descendants be crowned with a golden crown in all theaters for their valor and goodwill towards the people, so that others may also be ambitious for the city, knowing that the people honor good men. The leaders are to inscribe this decree on two stelai and erect one in the agora before the stoa and the other before the altar of Zeus Polieus.
EDCS-36900025.summary: A decree honoring Diodorus, Prokritus, and Klearchos for successfully recovering hostages and revenues, proclaiming them and their descendants as benefactors and awarding them golden crowns.

EDCS-36900026.translation: It was resolved by the council and the people, under the supervision of Dionysios, son of Hieron. The archons spoke. Because Hephaistion, son of Matrios of Kallatis, a good and honorable man who has comported himself well in the city's affairs, having a paternal debt of three hundred gold coins owed to the city according to a contract from many years ago, with considerable interest accrued, has waived the interest, four hundred gold coins, and agreed to receive the principal owed according to the contract, three hundred gold coins, interest-free over two years. Hephaistion, son of Matrios of Kallatis, is to be praised for this.
EDCS-36900026.summary: A decree acknowledging and praising Hephaistion for waiving accrued interest on a significant debt owed to the city, allowing him to repay the principal interest-free over two years.

EDCS-36900029.translation: It was resolved by the people, under the supervision of Glaukias, son of Polyphēmos. The archons spoke. Because Dioskourides, son of Strouthion, a good man towards the city and its citizens, has shown himself zealous for the people, serving as an envoy on many Hellenic and barbarian embassies for peace, without regard for danger, and has always shown himself ambitious and just in holding offices and responsibilities. Praise Dioskourides, son of Strouthion, for these actions and proclaim him a benefactor of the people. He is to be crowned with a golden crown in all contests for his virtue and goodwill towards the people. A bronze statue of him is to be erected in the agora. This decree and the statue are to be inscribed.
EDCS-36900029.summary: A decree honoring Dioskourides for his service as an envoy, his bravery, and his just conduct in public office, proclaiming him a benefactor and awarding him a golden crown and a bronze statue.

EDCS-36900032.translation: It was resolved by the council and the people, under the supervision of Dionysios, son of Bianor. Apollonios, son of Kleombrotos, spoke. Because Agathokles, son of Antiphilos, whose father was a benefactor, is and has always been a good and honorable man towards the city and its citizens, showing zeal in all the city's affairs, in magistracies, responsibilities, and councils, always speaking and acting for the best interests of the people. During a time of unrest for the city and with many Thracian raiders, while the harvests were vulnerable and the citizens in distress, he was elected toxarch (leader of archers) and, taking mercenary soldiers, he protected the territory and the harvests, allowing the citizens to gather them without harm. When the Thracians under Zoltes, with a large army, came into Scythia and the Hellenic cities subject to King Rhemaxos, he was elected envoy and traveled through enemy territory, enduring no danger, and persuaded the barbarians not only to spare our city but also to return the livestock previously stolen from the people by pirates. They, under Zoltes, having been ordered to search and recover everything, hurried with these matters and persuaded the city to give five talents for a treaty.
