After that, when they invaded the territory and besieged Bizone, plundering the territory while the harvests were vulnerable to us, he was elected envoy and, traveling to the citizens' army, was given a command to buy back the territory and the harvests. He persuaded Zoltes and the Thracians not to invade the territory or approach the city for six hundred gold coins, which resulted in the citizens possessing all the produce from the land. Again, he was elected envoy to Thrace and to their leader, Zoltes. He renewed the existing agreements and treaties with them. Sensing a gathering of many pirates, he informed Zoltes and revealed it to the citizens, which resulted in their plot being thwarted. When the Thracians violated their oaths and agreements and made continuous raids, he was elected by the people as general of the territory with supreme authority. Taking volunteer soldiers from the citizens and barbarians who had taken refuge in the city, he safely preserved the territory, livestock, and harvests until the crossing of King Rhemaxos. After the king crossed to the other side, leaving no garrison out of fear, and sent envoys demanding tribute from the territory, which was at war, he was elected envoy and traveled by sea, persuading King Rhemaxos to provide one hundred cavalrymen for defense. When a larger Thracian raid occurred against the outposts and they retreated to the other side out of fear, and the territory was left unguarded, he was elected envoy to the king's son, Phradmon, and persuaded him to provide six hundred cavalrymen as a guard. These, outnumbering the enemy armies, defeated their commander Zoltes and many Thracians. [Translation of remaining part is incomplete due to text loss]
EDCS-36900032.summary: A lengthy decree honoring Agathokles for his exceptional military and diplomatic service in defending the city and its territory against Thracian incursions and pirate raids, detailing his actions as a toxarch, envoy, and general, and granting him significant honors and powers.

EDCS-36900071.translation: To good fortune. In the priesthood of Aristagoras, son of Apatourios, for the fourth time, on the tenth day of the month Artemisius, the moon waxing, with Athenades, son of Apollodoros, serving as city superintendent, Xenochares son of Hekataios said: Since Aristagoras, son of Apatourios, born of a good father and benefactors among his ancestors and a priest of all the gods, and himself desiring to follow in their footsteps, came down to his fatherland after the disturbance that had befallen the city, while the city was unwalled and the citizens were again in danger with their wives and children, he was appointed by the citizens as builder of the walls and most manfully and sincerely took charge of the works, not shirking bodily labors nor anything pertaining to construction. And when the fatherland had been fortified, and some of the citizens were returning to the city from the barbarian incursions, he met the barbarian rulers of the land skillfully, and by offering ransom to some of the citizens, he showed himself to be amenable to all in the defense of those being saved, and, engaging in many transactions with citizens and foreigners alike, he unselfishly supported everyone. Advancing in age and growing in piety, as was fitting for him, he first honored the gods by taking up the wreath of Zeus Polieus and performing the sacrifice pleasingly, and was praised by all the citizens. Then, having become an appointed priest and taken up the wreath of Apollo, the city's namesake, with universal festivals and sacred processions and contributions of the tribes, he honored the gods and the city, wishing to show that those who conduct themselves most piously and excellently in civic life also receive favor from the gods and from those who have been benefited. Three years later, when the barbarians ruling the land were causing further trouble, and the citizens were seeking a priest of Apollo Iatros, and private lives were disrupted, he offered himself and came before the assembly and took up the same wreath of the god, doubling for himself both the favor from the gods and the favor from those benefited. And in the third instance, during the same circumstances, with the city and land under occupation, wishing to render full favors to the gods and also to spend generously from his own resources for the citizens, he performed the priesthood. And a year later, with no one else offering himself, he took up the same wreath and performed the priesthood, sparing nothing in regard to his duties to the gods or to the citizens, as a result of which the city remained stable and the citizens were saved. And having been appointed agoranomos for a year, he served as market supervisor in a manner befitting a good and honorable man, overseeing the prices of grain, wine, and other commodities for the greatest benefit of the citizens, and having received praise for these actions, he established a market building at his own expense. The people, having accepted his excellence, appointed him market supervisor for two more years, during which he was equally successful in the aforementioned matters. He also served as an envoy on many occasions, representing the city to the rulers of the land and the barbarians of the river, and achieved what was beneficial for the citizens. It has been decreed by the council and the people to praise Aristagoras, son of Apatourios, the benefactor from his ancestors, for these deeds, and to inscribe this decree and dedicate it in the most prominent place. It has been decreed.
EDCS-36900071.summary: This is a decree from an unidentified city, likely in Ionia, praising Aristagoras for his service in rebuilding the city walls, his dedication as a priest of Zeus and Apollo on multiple occasions, his role as agoranomos, and his diplomatic missions, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900074.translation: To good fortune. It has been decreed by the council and the people. Ulpius Demetrius said, with Diogenes son of Theodorus endorsing: Since Aba, daughter of Hekataios son of Euxenides, and wife of Herakion son of Aristomachos, coming from distinguished parents and most prominent ancestors who never shirked any honor or distinguished public service but rather performed with unparalleled magnificence and nobility and piety, and received every exceptional honor, she considered the glory derived from family alone insufficient unless she also acquired renown from her own good deeds towards the people. Therefore, she voluntarily undertook the priesthood of the Mother of the Gods and not only performed all duties befitting noble women, priestesses, and those of ancient times with magnificence, nobility, and piety, but also strove to emulate the great archpriesthoods of men and other public liturgies. First, at the very beginning of the year, she performed the initial processions and sacrifices and prayers to the gods with magnificent festivity and feasting. For she made a distribution of two denarii per person to all the council members, senators, Taurostatai, physicians, and teachers, and to those invited individually by name, a distribution that no one else had ever made before. To those in the tribes, distributed by fifties, and also to the hymn singers, carpenters, hieroplateitai, and Herakleiastai, she provided wine for drinking, as much as those who are honored with great names provide. After this, on every new moon and at all sacrifices and festivals and ancestral prayers each month, she served with utmost generosity and sacred dignity. For whatever expenses were customary for the priesthood, she readily accepted, and whatever she devised for the sake of piety, she performed with enthusiasm, disregarding expenses but not neglecting glory. Even when some visitors of influence were present, and the populace asked for them, and others were more reluctant, she gave to everyone on the day she wished. In general, throughout the year, she has been most generous and worthy of honor. It has been decreed to praise Aba, daughter of Hekataios, and to proclaim and crown her at all festivals, and that she should share in all other honors, including painted portraits. The decree is to be inscribed.
EDCS-36900074.summary: This is a decree from the city of Istrus (modern Bulgaria) honoring Aba, daughter of Hekataios, for her extraordinary generosity and piety, particularly during her priesthood of the Mother of the Gods, including lavish distributions of food and wine and other public services, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900075.translation: Providing now again... so that the tribes hold their assembly on a day named after him, from the revenues generated from the grants made by him. To good fortune. It has been decreed by the tribes to praise Meniskos, a good and honorable man who has provided benefactions to the citizens throughout his life, and to hold a day in his honor each year on the twelfth of the month Anthesterion, on which, after sacrificing to the gods, they hold their assembly from the distributed revenues and crown him with a golden wreath for his virtue and goodwill towards the tribes. The bishops chosen each year are to oversee the assembly and the proclamation of the crown, and to send him and his descendants the customary honors at these assemblies, as they do for priests. Whoever proposes to abolish the assembly or the bishops fail to perform the sacred rites or crown the benefactor shall pay five gold coins to the tribes and be declared a temple robber. This decree is to be inscribed on a white marble slab and dedicated in the Samothrakion.
EDCS-36900075.summary: This is a decree from the tribes of an unidentified city, likely Greek, praising Meniskos for his lifelong benefactions to the citizens and establishing an annual festival in his honor, including a golden wreath, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900081.translation: It has been decreed by the council and the people, on the opinion of the synedroi: Since the Messembrians of the land across the river, having declared war upon us without provocation and committed many grave impieties against the sacred precinct of Apollo and brought the city into extreme peril, the Istrianoi, being related, friends, and well-wishers of the people, sent long ships and soldiers to aid us, appointing Hegesagoras, son of Monimos, a good and honorable man, as commander-in-chief. Upon his arrival, he, along with us and our allies, protected the city, the land, and the harbors. Furthermore, he besieged and razed to the ground the fortress at Anchialos, which had been alienated and was held by the enemy, a situation that caused great damage to the city and its revenues. And when the enemy attacked our fleet off Anchialos, he risked himself against superior numbers and defeated the enemy, capturing a ship with its crew. Similarly, in the landings, he exposed himself most daringly in the engagements and in all other matters fought bravely, always taking the initiative and ensuring his soldiers were enthusiastic and useful in the war. Therefore, so that the people may show gratitude and honor good men, it has been decreed by the council and the people to praise the people of Istros, who are friends, relatives, and allies, and for sending the commander Hegesagoras, son of Monimos, and to restore the honors previously decreed for the Istrianoi. Also, Hegesagoras, son of Monimos, is to be crowned with a golden wreath during the Dionysia, and a bronze statue in armor is to be erected on the promontory and dedicated in the sacred precinct of Apollo Iatros. This decree is to be inscribed on the base, and the honors are to be announced at Istros during the contests held there, with the announcement always being made. It has been decreed.
EDCS-36900081.summary: This is a decree from the city of Istrus (modern Bulgaria) praising the Istrianoi for their aid in a war against the Messembrians and honoring their commander, Hegesagoras, son of Monimos, for his bravery and strategic successes, including the capture of a fortress and naval victories, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900082.translation: On the tenth day, the synedroi said: Since Eupolis, son of Philomelos, was sent as envoy to Byzantium and, having received instructions to bring an architect, hired Epikrates. Upon his arrival, he accomplished many beneficial tasks beyond the projected costs of the works, adapting to the city's circumstances and receiving the wages the people deemed appropriate. When the Olatic war was announced, he prepared expertly and zealously for the needs of the time, which included being appointed as supervisor of the walls by the people for two years. He was summoned by the synedroi and supervised them advantageously. He also lived in the city for many years without reproach and continues to be well-disposed towards the people. It has been decreed by the council and the people to praise Epikrates, son of Nikoboulos of Byzantium, for his virtue and goodwill towards the city, and to crown him with a golden wreath during the Thargelia in the theater. He is to be granted a pension and salary, and he and his descendants are to be granted proxeny, citizenship, the right to enter and leave freely, both in war and in peace, inviolability, and the right to enter the council and the assembly after the sacred rites. This decree is to be inscribed on a stone tablet by the magistrates and set up in the sacred precinct of Apollo. The expense for the tablet is to be paid by the oikonomoi from funds they manage. He is also to be invited to a communal meal in the sacred precinct of Apollo. It has been decreed.
EDCS-36900082.summary: This is a decree from an unidentified Greek city, likely in the Roman period, praising Epikrates, an architect from Byzantium, for his service in constructing and supervising city walls, his contributions during wartime, and his continued goodwill towards the city, granting him honors and privileges.

EDCS-36900084.translation: Boundary demarcation of Laevius Maximus, consular legate. The fines of the Histrians are established to be these: from Peuce Lake, from the dominion of the Argamensians, then along the highest ridge to the confluence of the Picusculus and Gabranus streams, then from the lower Gabranus to the source of the same, then along the Sanpaeus stream, then to the Turgiculus stream, from the Calabaeus stream, approximately 516 Roman miles. Letter from Sabinus. Flavius Sabinus to the archons, council, and people of Istria, greetings. The matter concerning Peuce is rightly yours, and it shall be the concern of the prefect Aruntius Flammas to keep it intact. For I have written to him thus, and I will speak to Aelianus, my successor, and I will fully support you. Another letter from the same Sabinus. Flavius Sabinus, legate, to the archons, council, and people of Istria, greetings. Although the boundary along the bank of the Istros extends to the sea, and the city is distant from the mouths of the river by so great a space, nevertheless, since your ambassadors assured me, and the prefect Asiaticus said that this is practically the only source of income for the city from salted fish, I decided that according to your custom, the same freedom should remain for you to fish at the mouth of Peuce and to bring your torches for the needs of each person without tax. For regarding the needs of the forest, you have undisputed boundaries and full use of them, not accountable to the treasury. Letter from Pomponius Peius. Pomponius Peius to the archons, council, and people of Istria, greetings. From what Flavius Sabinus and Aelianus, most distinguished men and most honored by me, have written to you, it is my duty to support you, recognizing that the weakness of your city receives consideration. Therefore, above all, since the most divine Caesar, our true savior, is concerned that the rights of the cities not only be preserved but also increased, I have decided that the revenue from the fish caught at the mouth of Peuce shall be yours, by which right your ancestors and fathers, by the grace of the Augusti, have continuously held these taxes. Letter from Plautius Aelianus. Plautius Aelianus to the archons of Istria, greetings. The ambassadors Kallistratos son of Demetrius and Meidias son of Artemidorus have delivered your decree to me. You request, through the decree, that the embassy offering thanks to our most honored Sabinus be forwarded, which I would gladly do for Sabinus alone. You also request that your rights concerning Peuce remain inviolate. I am so far from violating any of your long-held rights that I would gladly assist in any way possible to adorn your ancient and Hellenic city, which is pious towards the Augustus and also pious towards us. Letter from Tullius Geminus. Tullius Geminus, legate and legatus Augusti pro praetore of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, to the archons, council, and people of Istria, greetings. Your ambassadors Demetrius Eschrion, Ota, Meidias Dionysodorus, Hegesagoras Aristagoras, Metrodorus, having met me in Tomi, presented your decree and, showing your goodwill towards our Augustus, rejoiced at our health and presence, diligently attending to the matters you entrusted to them. Recognizing the disposition of your city towards us, which they revealed, I will endeavor to be the cause of some good for you. Regarding Peuce and its mouths, having been instructed by your ambassadors, I have decided that the ancestral boundaries of your ancestors should be preserved for you. Exemplary decree of Mani Laberius Maximus, consular legate, under Emperor Caesar Trajan Augustus Germanicus, with Julius Frontinus as consuls, on the eighth day before the Kalends of November, recorded and reviewed from the commentaries of Mani Laberius Maximus, consular legate, with the permission of Fabius Pompeianus, which had already been written by Charagonius Philopalaestrus, contractor of the public revenues of the Thracian bank, who requested that the customs of Halmyris and Peuce be granted to him, according to the form he received. He will have the right to collect customs from the borders of the Dimensians up to the sea. The boundary of the Histrians is set here.
EDCS-36900084.summary: This inscription consists of a series of letters and a decree concerning the territorial boundaries and fishing rights of the city of Istros (modern Bulgaria), particularly regarding the Peuce River mouth and Lake Peuce, confirming their ancient rights and privileges in relation to Roman officials and local administration, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900086.translation: The council and the people [honor] Calliades, son of Calliades, the benefactor.
EDCS-36900086.summary: This is a dedication from an unidentified city, likely Greek, honoring Calliades, son of Calliades, as a benefactor, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900087.translation: The council and the people [honor] Apatourios, son of Theopompos, the benefactor.
EDCS-36900087.summary: This is a dedication from an unidentified city, likely Greek, honoring Apatourios, son of Theopompos, as a benefactor, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900093.translation: To good fortune. The council and the people [honor] Gaius Iulius Pollio, the high priest, for the sake of honor.
EDCS-36900093.summary: This is a dedication from an unidentified city, likely Greek, honoring Gaius Iulius Pollio as high priest, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900095.translation: To good fortune. Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar Augustus, son of the most divine Emperor Augustus, Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax, [honored by] the council and people of Istria.
EDCS-36900095.summary: This is a dedication from the city of Istros (modern Bulgaria) honoring the Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900101.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of Istra have [honored] Julia Domna, the Augusta.
EDCS-36900101.summary: This is a dedication by the people of Istra to Julia Domna, likely erected during the reign of her husband, Septimius Severus.

EDCS-36900103.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of Istra [have honored] the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus.
EDCS-36900103.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), proclaiming his parentage and titles, and was erected by the people of Istra.

EDCS-36900104.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of the most brilliant city of Istra [have honored] Julia Domna, Augusta, Fortune of the inhabited world, mother of the most divine and unconquered Emperor Antoninus.
EDCS-36900104.summary: This is a dedication by the people of Istra to Julia Domna, venerated as the 'Mother of the Camp' and mother of Emperor Caracalla.

EDCS-36900105.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of the most brilliant city of Istra [have honored] the lord of land and sea, the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-36900105.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Caracalla with a full titulature, including his numerous victories and honors, and was set up by the people of Istra.

EDCS-36900106.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of the most brilliant city of Istra [have honored] Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus Augustus Caesar, son of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Eutychus Pius Augustus.
EDCS-36900106.summary: This dedication by the people of Istra honors Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, son and co-emperor of Macrinus.

EDCS-36900107.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of the most brilliant city of Istra [have honored] the Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Eutychus Pius, most divine and unconquered Augustus, father of the fatherland, chief priest, proconsul, with tribunician power.
EDCS-36900107.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Macrinus, listing his imperial titles and honors, and was dedicated by the people of Istra.

EDCS-36900108.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of the most brilliant city of Istra [have honored] Julia Maesa, Augusta, Fortune of the inhabited world, grandmother of the most divine and unconquered Emperor Antoninus.
EDCS-36900108.summary: This is a dedication by the people of Istra to Julia Maesa, grandmother of Emperor Elagabalus, who held significant influence.

EDCS-36900109.translation: To good fortune. The assembly and people of Istra [have honored] the lord of land and sea and all the race of men, the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Eutychus Augustus, the unconquered.
EDCS-36900109.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Maximinus Thrax, proclaiming him lord of the world and the unconquered, and was erected by the people of Istra.

EDCS-36900114.translation: To good fortune, for the fortune of our lord Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Eutychus, and of Julia Augusta, mother of the camps, and of the most excellent prefects of our lord Emperor, Valerius Comazon and Julius Flavius, and of the sacred Senate, and of the sacred armies, and for the governor of the province, the consular Titus Flavius Novius Rufus, and for the council and people of the most brilliant city of Istra, the cohort of Dionysiacs, elders, those around Father Achilleas Achillas, and the priest Aurelius Victor Castus, and the hierophant Flavius Iucundus, and Flavius Severus, and Aristides.
EDCS-36900114.summary: This is a complex dedication by a military unit and various officials of Istra, honoring Emperor Caracalla, Julia Domna, prefects, the Senate, armies, the provincial governor, and the city itself.

EDCS-36900115.translation: And of the most excellent consular Julius Gaitulicus, the elder hymn-singers around the great god Dionysus, the patrons Flavius Iucundus, Flavius Diogenes, Flavius Longinianus, and Aurelius Dionysius (2nd), of Hestia, having won a competition under the agonothete Aurelius Gregorius son of Artemidorus, and father Achilleas Achillas, and the choregus and statosinger Aurelius Heil of Heil, the music director Demetrius son of Domitian, the co-competitors: Aurelius Aelianus son of Heil, Aurelius Alexander son of Heil, Aurelius Saturneilus son of Heil, Athenades son of Damatrius, Callinicus son of Heraclianus, Neikeratos son of Hermippus, Demetris son of Damatrius (6), Rufus son of Marcianus, Dionysius P, Aurelius Artemidorus son of Aristocrates, priest, Aurelius Aristocrates son of Aristocrates, priest, Publius Aelius son of Hermerinus, Aurelius Ponticus F.
EDCS-36900115.summary: This inscription details the victors and participants in a Dionysiac festival competition in Istra, mentioning various officials and performers, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-36900117.translation: Belonging to Telon, belonging to Telon.
EDCS-36900117.summary: This is a short ownership or dedication inscription, likely from a funerary context, stating 'Belonging to Telon'.

EDCS-36900124.translation: Thalonis, daughter of Artemidorus, wife of Arsiphron, a vow to Demeter, under the priest Hieron, son of Anti-anax.
EDCS-36900124.summary: This is a religious dedication inscription to Demeter by Thalonis, daughter of Artemidorus and wife of Arsiphron, during the priesthood of Hieron.

EDCS-36900127.translation: Kallikrates and his soldiers, having sailed to give aid to the Apolloniatae, to the Dioscuri, saviors.
EDCS-36900127.summary: This is a dedication or commemoration from Kallikrates and his soldiers, likely military, thanking the Dioscuri for aid to Apolloniatae.

EDCS-36900128.translation: Athenagoras, son of Theodotos, having served Aphrodite.
EDCS-36900128.summary: This is a religious dedication by Athenagoras, son of Theodotos, offering service to Aphrodite.

EDCS-36900129.translation: Gykeia, daughter of Dionysius, wife of Theophilos, to the Moirai.
EDCS-36900129.summary: This is a dedication to the Moirai by Gykeia, daughter of Dionysius and wife of Theophilos.

EDCS-36900137.summary: This is a simple dedication inscription to the deities Pankrates and Nike.

EDCS-36900140.translation: Moiro, daughter of Dionysius son of Meniskos, a vow to Demeter.
EDCS-36900140.summary: This is a dedication inscription to Demeter by Moiro, daughter of Dionysius and granddaughter of Meniskos.

EDCS-36900141.translation: Alexandros, son of Hegesagoras, to the Mother of the Gods, according to a vow.
EDCS-36900141.summary: This is a religious dedication by Alexandros, son of Hegesagoras, to the Mother of the Gods in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-36900142.translation: [...] of Philinus, to the Mother of the Gods.
EDCS-36900142.summary: This is a fragmentary religious dedication to the Mother of the Gods, mentioning Philinus.

EDCS-36900143.translation: Helios, a thank-offering to the Mother of the Gods.
EDCS-36900143.summary: This is a votive inscription by Helios, offering a thank-offering to the Mother of the Gods.

EDCS-36900145.translation: With good fortune, Hero Bianor son of Cleogenes dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-36900145.summary: This is a dedication by Hero Bianor son of Cleogenes to an unspecified deity, likely made in fulfillment of a vow, indicated by the phrase 'κατ' εὐχήν'.

EDCS-36900147.translation: I, Tatarion the coppersmith, dedicated this gift to Athena.
EDCS-36900147.summary: A dedication by Tatarion, a coppersmith, to the goddess Athena, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-36900148.translation: To Asclepius of Pergamum, Sabinianus [dedicated this].
EDCS-36900148.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Asclepius of Pergamum by Sabinianus.

EDCS-36900150.translation: With good fortune, to the unconquered Sun and Mithras, the following contributed to the construction of the sacred cave and served in piety: Meniskos Noumenios, father of the pious Numisius; Marcus Ulpius Artemidorus, pontarch; Hippolochus son of Pythion; Karpos son of Apollodorus; Kallistratus son of Apollodorus; Aelius Dionysius son of Democrates; Iulius Bassus, staff officer of the consular governor; Aurelius Aemilianus; Aelius Firmus; Dionysius son of Dionysodorus.
EDCS-36900150.summary: This inscription records contributions by multiple individuals to the construction of a sacred cave dedicated to Sol Invictus and Mithras, during the priesthood of Iulius Severus, a former consular governor, and with specific officials listed.

EDCS-36900153.translation: With good fortune, on the 4th day of Artemisius, Aurelius Asiarches son of Charmides of the Roman tribe, having served as priest, dedicated this to Dionysus.
EDCS-36900153.summary: This is a dedication to Dionysus by Aurelius Asiarches, a priest and member of the Roman tribe, during a specific month and day.

EDCS-36900155.translation: The children of Hippolochus, son of Hegesagoras: Xenocles, Theoxenus, dedicated this to Apollo the Healer, during the priesthood of Hegesagoras, son of Theodotus.
EDCS-36900155.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Apollo the Healer by Xenocles and Theoxenus, sons of Hippolochus, during the priesthood of Hegesagoras son of Theodotus.

EDCS-36900156.translation: Pisistratus son of Mnesistratus of Thasos, to the Great God, during the priesthood of Xenochares son of Apollonius.
EDCS-36900156.summary: A dedication by Pisistratus of Thasos to the 'Great God' during the priesthood of Xenochares son of Apollonius.

EDCS-36900163.translation: With good fortune, for the victory and salvation of the Augusti, the council and the people of Istria, Zipas son of Diokles, made this bench for the elder hymn-singers, sacred victors, who are about Dionysus, from his own funds, in place of being a choragus, with Kokkeius Domitianus as archon and Aelianus Helios as lifelong secretary, through the zeal of his friend Marcus Dynytas son of Artemidorus, with Diogenes son of Zoilus supervising.
EDCS-36900163.summary: This inscription records Zipas son of Diokles, a citizen of Istria, funding the construction of a bench for sacred victors associated with Dionysus, to ensure the victory and salvation of the emperors, with various officials named.

EDCS-36900164.translation: Theoxenus son of Hippolochus dedicated this to Apollo the Healer, during the priesthood of Hippolochus son of Theodotus.
EDCS-36900164.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Apollo the Healer by Theoxenus son of Hippolochus, during the priesthood of Hippolochus son of Theodotus.

EDCS-36900168.translation: Apollonius son of Metrobius, to Aphrodite Pontia the helper.
EDCS-36900168.summary: A dedication by Apollonius son of Metrobius to the goddess Aphrodite Pontia, invoking her as a helper.

EDCS-36900169.translation: Herophon, son of Sostratus, of Thasos.
EDCS-36900169.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Herophon of Thasos.

EDCS-36900170.translation: So-and-so, who served as agoranomos (market supervisor), to Hermes Agoraios (of the market).
EDCS-36900170.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of an altar or statue to Hermes by a former market supervisor.

EDCS-36900171.translation: Philotheos, son of Menippos, who served as agoranomos (market supervisor), to Hermes Agoraios (of the market).
EDCS-36900171.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of an altar or statue to Hermes by a former market supervisor named Philotheos.

EDCS-36900173.translation: With good fortune, the council and the people rewarded the high priest and pontarch, who, having distinguished himself in many ways for the fatherland, and having also taken care of the construction of the harbor, and having served as accountant with all faithfulness, for the public restoration of the statue.
EDCS-36900173.summary: A decree by the council and people honoring a high priest and pontarch for his service to the city, including harbor construction and financial management.

EDCS-36900178.translation: Areia Phlainei, a woman, repaired the statue.
EDCS-36900178.summary: An inscription stating that a woman named Areia Phlainei repaired a statue.

EDCS-36900181.translation: Demetrios, son of Kratesippos; Menodoros, son of Bianor; Eugenides, son of Karpos.
EDCS-36900181.summary: A list of names, possibly part of a dedication or a record of individuals associated with a particular place or event.

EDCS-36900186.translation: With good fortune, these are the benefactors of the Boreis tribe after the second founding of the city: Moschos, son of Horas, second year; Aninios, son of Monimos; Moschos, son of Monimos; Polyainetos, son of Anthropos; Ros, second year, son of Monimos; son of Patas; Moschos, son of Aristokrates; Moschos, second year, second year; Moschos, son of Apollonios; Diogenes, son of Diogenes; son of Theodotos, second year; Diogenes, son of Noumenios; Diogenes, son of Struthion, second year; youngest, second year, son of Struthion, son of Demetrios; son of Moschos, son of Philistides; Apollodoros, son of Dionysios; son of Diokles; of Noumenios, second year; Theodoros, Theodoros, Theokles, Theokles, Diogenes, wife of Diogenes, second year; son of Chairemon, second year, wife; of Kleombrotos, second year, youngest; son of Archelaos; mos; of Dateizis; las; Aristagoras; machos, youngest; Apollonios, son of Athenodoros; Aristoneikos, son of Athenodoros; Sarapion, son of Athenodoros; Chrysippos, son of Lysimachos; Moschos, youngest; Diogenes, son of Papias; Dionysios, son of Kleitophon; Apollodoros, son of Kleitophon; Achillodoros, son of Kleitophon; Demetrios, second year; Ana, wife of Demetrios, second year; Noumenios, son of Diokles; Polyxenos, son of Neikheratos; Xenochares, son of Neikheratos; Artemidoros, son of Apollodoros; Artemidoros, son of Pythodoros; Aristagoras, son of Pythodoros; Archelaos, son of Pythodoros; Aristolaos, son of Theophilos; Eukles, son of Theophilos; Theodoros, son of Artemidoros; Apollonides, son of Lysimachos; Zoilus, son of Diogenes; Democharis, son of Artemidoros; Dionysios, son of Artemidoros; Demetrios, son of Artemidoros; Apellas, son of Lysimachos; Hediste, wife of Papias, son of Diogenes; Philistides, son of Moschos; Dionysios, youngest second year; Polemon, son of Ochos; Dioskourides, son of Theokles; Aristagoras, son of Neikheratos; Philippos, son of Theodoros; Dionysios, son of Theokles; Diogenes, son of Zoilus; Theognetos, son of Papias; Moschos, son of Zoilus; Dioskourides, son of Papias; Democharis, son of Poseidonios; Sarapion, son of Ochos; Aristokrates, son of Klytos; Anthropos, son of Moschos; Apollonios, son of Polyainetos; Aristokrates, son of Polyainetos; Aristoneikos, son of Ochos; Aristoemenes, son of Minios; Demetrios, second year, son of Demetrios; Demetrios, son of Apatourios; Dotos, son of Dateizis; Aptelas, son of Meniskos; Attas, son of Polyainetos; Diogenes, son of Theophilos.
EDCS-36900186.summary: This is a list of benefactors of the Boreis tribe from after the second founding of the city, inscribed on a monument or a list of donors.

EDCS-36900188.translation: With good fortune, for the health and salvation of the Emperor Trajan Hadrian and his son Titus Aelius Caesar Antoninus, and for the eternal endurance and victory of their entire house, the generous people, after the second founding of the city, including Kallistratos, son of Demetrios, synagogue president; Artemidoros, son of Eugenides, synagogue president; Herodoros, son of Dionysios, synagogue president; Karpos, son of Karpos; Megistion, son of Noumenios; Artamas, son of Aristagoras; Kousias, son of Dionysodoros; Aristolaos, son of Theophilos; Polyphēmos, son of Hestiaios; Dionysios, son of Kratesippos; Philemon, son of Philemon; Chaireas, son of Aristagoras; Apatourios, son of Demetrios; Chaireas, son of Apullos; Hegemon, son of Dionysios; Dionysios, son of Herodoru; Meniskos, son of Demetrios; Eugenides, son of Pankratos; Dionysios, son of Minios; Eugenides, son of Eugenides; Gregoras, son of Karpos; Apollonios, son of Apollonios; Meidias, son of Phanokles; Flavius Meniskos, son of Eugleon; Sestos; Aristokrates, son of Herodoru; Artemidoros, son of Herodoru; Dionysios, son of Dionysios; Flavius Eugenides, son of Hippolochos; Eugenides; Marcus Ulpius Demetrios, son of Artemas; Noumenios, son of Polemon; Karpos, son of Artemidoros; Aelius Artemidoros, son of Dioskourides; Kratesippos, son of Theodoros; Hipposthenes, son of Herodoru; Dionysodoros, son of Kousios; Aristomachos, son of Herakon; Meidias, son of Theokles; Hestiaios, son of Dion; Kleogenes, son of Noumenios; Polemon, son of Noumenios; Sideros, son of Siderou; Theodoros, son of Kouragenos; Dionysios, son of Dionysios; Herakleon, son of Tertius; Aischrion, son of Aristoneikos; Hegesagoras, son of Apollodoros; Democharis, son of Aristagoras; Eugenides, son of Polyainetos; Hestiaios, son of Dionysios; Antigenes, son of Philippos; Quintus Hostius; Valerius Sarapion, son of Sarapion; Hippolochos, son of Chaireas; Artemas, son of Aristagoras; Flavius Gallus; Flavius Quartus; Ninos, son of Neikiu; Dionysodoros, son of Dionysios; Meniskos, son of Mirodoros; Phanokles, son of Meidios; Monimos, son of Meniskos; Zoilus, son of Neikandros; Marcus Ulpius Eugenides, son of Artemidoros; Marcus Ulpius Demetrios, son of Artemidoros; Aelius Artemidoros, son of Phaidrios; Hipposthenes, son of Sarapion; Marcus Ulpius Artemidoros, son of Eugenides; Diogenes, son of Karpos; Diogenes, son of Dionysios; Pistos, son of Dionysios; Dionysios, son of Dionysodoros; Philodemos, son of Sarapion; Artemidoros, son of Hegemon; Aristoemenes, son of Hestiaios; Antipatros, son of Antipatroos; Sextus Pedius Asiaticus; Ariston, son of Philodemos; Eugenides, son of Eugenides; Theognetos, son of Dionysios; Chrestos, son of Protas; Demophilos, son of Athenades; Aristolaos, son of Theophilos; Aristoneikos, son of Theotimos; Polemon, son of Noumenios, the second; Poseidonios, son of Apollonios; Barbarios, son of Tithas; Dionysodoros, son of Dionysios; Phaidrias, son of Meniskos; Rufus, son of Androneikos; Neikias, son of Ninnos; Monimos, son of Dionysios; Artemas, son of Philon; Aelius Demokrates; Marcus Julius Moschos, second year; Eugenides, son of Noumenios; Dionysios, son of Philon; Apollodoros, son of Herophantos; Aischrion, son of Athenades; Satyrios, son of Aischrion, it was decided by the senators around Artemidoros, son of Herodoru, since our synagogue president Artemidoros, son of Herodoru, has often and promptly benefited the senate and has maintained our trust in writings, and now for himself and his son Karpos, he contributes one thousand denarii for a rose festival, that the festival be held annually from the interest and that they be crowned at the rose festival, with the herald proclaiming: The senators crown Artemidoros, son of Herodoru, the synagogue president and benefactor and generous man, and Karpos, son of Artemidoros, the generous and benefactor, for their goodwill; and that our synagogue president Artemidoros, son of Herodoru, be crowned after the gods and the Emperors, and that this decree be inscribed on a tenia. Gaius Julius Castus Dadas, son of Minios; Artemidoros, son of Theodoros; Aelius Mirodoros; Poseidonios, son of Dioskourides; Archelaos, son of Xenochares; Aelius Demosthenes; Aelius Minios, son of Protas; Karpos, son of Apollodoros; Aelius Neikeratos; Artemidoros, son of Phaidros; Eugenides, son of Dionysios; Bianor, son of Hekatai; Dionysios, son of Philon; Hoplex, son of Polyainetos; Dionysios, son of Monimos; Herophantos, son of Minios; Herapides, son of Monimos; Herodoros, son of Karpos; Kallistratos, son of Karpos; Marcus Ulpius Praises; Gaius Julius Kytos; Publius Aelius Gaukos; Theadon, son of Meniskos; Hieronymos, son of Dionysodoros; Marcus Kokkeios Polemon; Theodoros, son of Karpos; Diogenes, son of Chaireon; Marcus Ulpius Ianouarios; Dionysios, son of Aischrion; Archelaos, son of Dionysios; Basillos, son of Philippos; Menekharumos, son of Sosius; Artemidoros, son of Eugenides; Artemidoros, son of Karpos; Valerius Montanus; Aelius Flavius; Marcus Ulpius Kallistratos; Artemidoros, son of Noumenios; Artemidoros, son of Gregoras; Theokles, son of Aristagoras; Aelius Marcian; Theopompos, son of Dioskourides; Castus, son of Alexandros; Evodos, son of Theopompos; Diogenes, son of Aristoneikos; Aristolaos, son of Satyrios; Philon, son of Artemas; Titus Flavius Salloustios; Demetrios, son of Antigenes; Chairemon, son of Dionysodoros; Kleitophon, son of Hermodoros; Dionysios, son of Philodemos, youngest; Markion, son of Marcus; Mirodoros, son of Eugenides.
EDCS-36900188.summary: This is a decree from a synagogue and its associated senate, honoring the synagogue president Artemidoros and his son Karpos for their benefactions and financial contributions, establishing an annual festival and decreeing that their names be inscribed.

EDCS-36900191.translation: Names of the winners of the sacred contest: Monimos, son of Apellas; Glykon, son of Glykon; Aelius Sos; Aelius Niger; Noumenis, son of Theodoros; Koros, son of Pharnakes; Rufus, son of Phoebos; Aischrion, son of Korinthos; Glykon, son of Pistos; Mennylos, son of Chrysion; Theophilos, son of Alexandros; Gaukos, son of Proklos; Glykon, son of Glykon; Helis, son of Noumenios; Makreinos, son of Julianos; the Alexandrou family; Theodoros, son of Neikheratos; Ingenous, son of Sinnos; Koïntos, son of Koïntos; Akylenus, son of Loukipher; Donyttas, son of Pistos; Aelius Teimothes; Paphias, son of Anthas; Aelius Hermogenes; Herakleides, son of Helios; Kallias, son of Doryphoros; Polyteimos, son of Artemidoros; Teimokles, son of Anthas; Korbulon, son of Marcus; Heliodoros, second year; Julius Maskmos; Setonius Restitutus; Polystratos, son of Diodotos; Aelius Eutychos, second year; Domethis, son of Loukius; Aelius Rufus; Fulvius Telesphorus; Philippos, second year; Chrysion, son of Nestor; Messius Cornelius Papas; Pistos, son of Hermodoros; Tertes, son of Dionysios; Alexandros.
EDCS-36900191.summary: This inscription lists the names of victors in a sacred athletic or competitive event, likely associated with a local festival or games.

EDCS-36900192.translation: Valentinus, son of Kastos; Chaireas, son of Xenochares; Ponticus, son of Hermodoros; Kastos, son of Pompeius; Arabianus, son of Arabianus; Kastos, son of Tertius; Makreinos, son of Gauos; Kiserneos, son of Romanos; Parthenopaios, son of Pollion; Serapion, son of Apronianos; Chrysianus, son of Chrysos; Alexandros, son of Loukius; Sulpicianus, son of Kastos; Lucius, son of Herakleides; Neilôn, son of Xenochares; Gauos, son of Gauos; Kastos, son of Nymphidianus; MOI; Lysimachos, son of Theodoros; Kalandion, son of Glykon; Aemilius, son of Aelianus; Valerius, son of Helios; Andron, son of Longineius; Aelius Martinos; Paulus, son of Paulus; Antiochus, son of Asklepiodoros, dedicated the tenia (a fillet or band) while Lucius Bruttius was scribe; Aelius Eleutherion.
EDCS-36900192.summary: This inscription lists names of individuals and mentions the dedication of a tenia (a ceremonial band) by Antiochus, son of Asklepiodoros, during the time of a scribe named Lucius Bruttius.

EDCS-36900195.translation: Aeschrion son of Telestes, Moirodorus son of Hegesagoras, Athenades son of Aeschrion, Demochares son of Gregoras, Chareas, Zouri, Neilôn son of Synkdemus, Neikandros son of Neikippos, Apollonius son of Artemidorus, Soussoulôn, Patei, Pankrates, Zeiskeagi, Monimos son of Pistus, Polyaenetus son of Gregoras.
EDCS-36900195.summary: This inscription is a list of names, likely commemorating individuals or a group involved in some communal activity, possibly an association or a festival.

EDCS-36900196.translation: Kleiton, Glykon son of Glykon, Noumenios son of Theodorus, Kallistratos son of Karpos, Glykon son of Pistus, Mennylos son of Chrysiôn, Demosthenes, Marcus Aelius Niger, Domitius son of Lucius, Iulianus, Monimos, Kalleas the bodyguard, Quintus son of Romanus, Makreinos son of Gauos, Elis daughter of Noumenios, Marcus Sai son of Ulpius, Polyteimos son of Papas, Papias son of Anthas, Teimokles son of Anthas, Messius son of Cornelius, Epaphroditos, Theodoros son of Neikheratus, Polystratus son of Diodotus, Setonius Restitutus son of Philippus, Chrysiôn son of Ulpius, Ianuaris, Heliodorus son of Corbulon, Philippos son of Hieronymus, Aelius Hermes, Togênês son of Mirodoros, Quintus son of Quintus, Pêsitheos, Demokrates son of Theagenes, Heraklas, Gaius Valerius Valês, Fulvius Decmus, Euarestos son of Ulpius, Artemidorus son of Chrysiôn, Nestor. (II)INS
EDCS-36900196.summary: This inscription is a long list of personal names, possibly a roster of members of an association, a legal record, or a dedication.

EDCS-36900199.translation: Good fortune! Under the priesthood of Iulius Pollion, priest of Dionysus Karpophoros, priests Beltidios Sanktos, Poseidonios son of Theopompos, Apollonides son of Menecharms, Zoilos son of Lysimachos.
EDCS-36900199.summary: This inscription records the names of priests serving under Iulius Pollion, priest of Dionysus Karpophoros, likely associated with a religious cult.

EDCS-36900202.translation: SAMO, victors under the agonothete Iou, with the proestotes Marcus Ulpius Artemidorus, first pontarch, and son of the city Marcus Ulpius Kallistratus, Marcus Ulpius Sabinianus, Marcus Ulpius Praesentus, Marcus Ulpius Germanicus, Titus Cominius Euxenidus the younger pontarch, Marcus Ulpius Demetrius, Diogenes son of Karpos, Artemidorus son of Karpos, Kalpurnius son of Karpos, Herodorus son of Karpos, Kallistratus son of Karpos, Aelius Artemidorus son of Dioskourides, Publius Aelius Phaidrios son of Karpos, Apollodorus son of Dionysius, Mirodoros son of Chairemon, Papius son of Pollion, Memnon son of Theokles, (IA) during the high priesthood of Publius Aelius Hekataios, and the mesochoreia of Marcus Aelius Valerianus, the sacred games, names of those who sacrificed and competed together and showed generosity: Marcus Ulpius Valerianus Pistos son of Glykon, Gaius Valerius Proclus son of Germanus, Romanus son of Menekrates, (LI) (LI) Kallikles.
EDCS-36900202.summary: This inscription lists victors and officials involved in a sacred games festival, including agonothetes, pontarchs, and priests, possibly from Samos, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-36900206.translation: Herakon son of Demetrius, Herakleides (LI), Maximos son of Monimos, Diogenes son of Apphos, Barbaros son of Teimylos, Ulpius Ingênês, Athenades son of Monimos, Kerkas son of Eunostos, Flavius Hadrianus, Kerkion son of Korinthos, Dionysius son of Aeschrion, Marcianus son of Magnus, Artemidorus son of Antigenes, Ulpius Marcus the Younger, Dionytas son of Pistus, Artemidorus son of Phaidros, wrote.
EDCS-36900206.summary: This is a list of names, likely of a group or association, with one individual identified as 'wrote', possibly indicating a scribe or a signatory.

EDCS-36900207.translation: Artemidorus son of Kallistratus, Bianor the second, Agathocles son of Herodoros, Meniskos son of Kousios, Noumenios son of Kallistratus, Hephaistodoros son of Theodorus, Aeschrion the second, Symmachos son of Artemidorus, Herennius son of Kleitophon, Pontikos son of Demetrius, Meniskos son of Athenades, Artemidorus son of Kourageus, Euxenides son of Monimos, (N XI TON IA GISOUS), Demochares son of Dekrimos, Bassos son of Ninnos, Anaximenes son of Kleitophon, Aristokrates son of Artemidorus, Asklepiades son of Kleinios, Athenades the second son of Lysimachos.
EDCS-36900207.summary: This inscription contains a list of names, possibly representing members of a group or participants in an event, with some individuals noted as 'the second'.

EDCS-36900210.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary list of names beginning with 'Aurelius', possibly indicating Roman citizens or officials.

EDCS-36900213.translation: IOS ANOS, Hilarios son of Lucius, Titus Flavius Ulpius son of Andron, Iulius Gemellinus, Aurelius Olympios son of Asklepiades, Claudius Sextus, Aurelius Amandos son of Artemidorus, Claudius Demetris son of Sextus, Neikias son of Valerius, also called Marcus.
EDCS-36900213.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, including Roman citizens identified by multiple names and patronymics, possibly a dedication or a list of benefactors.

EDCS-36900220.translation: Lo Titus Posy, Achilleus son of He..., Alexander son of Archid..., Delphikos M N..., Natalis son of Pomponius, Demetris son of Amo..., Poseidonios Turbon D..., Andron L A..., Agathon A T..., Titus Flavius Thalassius, Aristomenes son of Papas, L., Demetris.
EDCS-36900220.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmentary list of names, possibly related to a public record, a dedication, or a group of individuals.

EDCS-36900222.translation: T LA and B, the foundation, the THEO, patrons Euxinios LI, Artemidorus E E, Euxenides Eu, Artemidorus, Hippolochus, Ulpius Atti, Hegesagoras Amo, Dionysius E.
EDCS-36900222.summary: This inscription records the 'foundation' and names patrons and possibly builders or benefactors, including several individuals with multiple names and patronymics.

EDCS-36900224.translation: With good fortune. Alexandros the priest, Apollonios the benefactor, Alexandros Kouthiouras the benefactor, Maximina, Sisi, Theophilos.
EDCS-36900224.summary: This inscription lists names of individuals, possibly officials or benefactors, associated with a community or religious office.

EDCS-36900226.translation: Sweet Hediste, daughter of Evagoras, what mortal’s heart could be so unfeeling as not to shed tears for you, maiden, who always approached with beauty and grace, losing your youth before marriage.
EDCS-36900226.summary: This is a funerary epigram lamenting the premature death of a young woman named Hediste, the daughter of Evagoras.

EDCS-36900227.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication or identification of Hegesander, son of Alexander.

EDCS-36900229.translation: For Diophantus, son of Sokles of Drytes, Niketes honors him with this column and tomb for his excellence.
EDCS-36900229.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Niketes to honor Diophantus for his virtue.

EDCS-36900231.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication or identification of Hippolochos, son of Hegesagoras.

EDCS-36900232.summary: This inscription identifies Artis as the wife of Hegesagoras.

EDCS-36900233.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication or identification of Heronymos, son of Diagoras.

EDCS-36900234.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication or identification of Artemidoros, son of Theokles.

EDCS-36900235.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication or identification of Auxeisileos, son of Hephaistokles.

EDCS-36900237.summary: This inscription identifies Saine as the wife of Tiberius.

EDCS-36900240.summary: This is a gravestone or funerary marker for Apollas, daughter of Helpius.

EDCS-36900243.summary: This is a gravestone or funerary marker for Aristōn, son of Pausanias.

EDCS-36900244.summary: This is a gravestone or funerary marker for Metrodoēros, son of Archelochos.

EDCS-36900245.summary: This is a gravestone or funerary marker for Dēmō, wife of Diopithēs.

EDCS-36900250.summary: This is a gravestone or funerary marker for Hestiaios, son of Mirodoēros.

EDCS-36900253.summary: This is a gravestone or funerary marker for Nausikles, son of Leon.

EDCS-36900258.translation: To Noumēnios, son of Hestiaios, greetings. We hoped you would increase the glory of our fatherland, being destined for our fatherland and already pursuing esteemed studies, when Kyzikos granted you the land you longed for. Mēidia, lamenting your too-early death with your companions, cries out in bitter grief; your parents weep with pain, pouring their only care for their elderly support into ashes. They did not live to light the torches at your wedding chambers. Alas, the hope of children, a fleeting breeze! You have appeared envied by citizens and foreigners alike, and your virtue lives on even among the dead.
EDCS-36900258.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Noumēnios, son of Hestiaios, lamenting his early death and celebrating his virtue.

EDCS-36900262.translation: To Hieron, son of Meniskos, gymnasiarch, greetings. Greetings also to you, passerby.
EDCS-36900262.summary: This is an inscription dedicating a monument or honor to Hieron, son of Meniskos, who held the office of gymnasiarch.

EDCS-36900263.translation: N. son of Aristokrates, greetings. Aristokrates, greetings.
EDCS-36900263.summary: This is a greeting inscription likely for two individuals named N. and Aristokrates, perhaps father and son or related.

EDCS-36900265.translation: Aristokrates son of N. Greetings. Aristokrates son of N. Greetings.
EDCS-36900265.summary: This is a repetitive greeting inscription, possibly for a father and son named Aristokrates, or for one person multiple times.

EDCS-36900266.translation: You see the symbols of art, and the name was given by the beautiful Corinth, daughter of Sextus. Having lived for seventy years, I left my heart on the lips of my beloved wife.
EDCS-36900266.summary: This funerary epigram, written in Greek, commemorates a woman named Corinth and her husband's enduring love, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900268.summary: This is a simple greeting inscribed on a monument, likely a tombstone, for Polemōn, son of Noumenios, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900269.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Alessandra dedicated this tomb to her father, Alexandros, son of Herakleon, and a senator, be well.
EDCS-36900269.summary: This Greek funerary inscription records the dedication of a tomb by Alessandra to her father, Alexandros, who was a senator, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900271.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Apollinaris Dolichenaris lies here, having lived 35 years. Aurelius Marcus, her brother, erected this stele in remembrance. Greetings, passerby.
EDCS-36900271.summary: This bilingual (Greek and Latin) funerary inscription records Apollinaris Dolichenaris, who lived 35 years, and commemorates her brother Aurelius Marcus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900272.translation: Apollonios, son of Dateis, greetings. Greetings also, passerby.
EDCS-36900272.summary: This is a simple Greek greeting inscribed for Apollonios, son of Dateis, likely on a tombstone, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900273.translation: Apellas, son of Apellas, greetings. Greetings also, passerby. Greetings.
EDCS-36900273.summary: This is a simple Greek greeting inscribed for Apellas, son of Apellas, likely on a tombstone, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900274.translation: Not only does the whole of Eistros praise Meikos's hospitable character, but I also praise it. While alive, you had a pleasant life; now you have a carefree one, no longer worrying about anything.
EDCS-36900274.summary: This Greek epigram praises the hospitable character of Meikos and offers him a posthumous wish for a carefree afterlife, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900275.summary: This fragmentary Greek funerary inscription appears to be a posthumous greeting to the deceased and a passerby, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900276.translation: Passerby, halt your swift passage. I wish to tell you that Petros, who is over the tombs, [text illegible] lies in this dust. Demetrios [text illegible] Demetrios has been completed.
EDCS-36900276.summary: This Greek funerary inscription, though fragmentary, seems to identify the deceased as Demetrios and provides the name of the overseer of the tombs, Petros, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900277.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Here I lie, renowned Apphia, daughter of Philippos, having ceased from the light of life at twenty-eight years. I left behind on earth my only son, born of me, a five-year-old boy. With my dear husband, Aischrion, son of Aristagoras, a councilor, who, for the sake of memory and the love I had for him, while alive and of sound mind, erected this epitaph. Rejoice, all you who pass through life without troubles.
EDCS-36900277.summary: This Greek funerary inscription commemorates Apphia, who died at 28, leaving a young son, and records her husband Aischrion, a councilor, erecting the monument in her memory, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36900278.translation: Julia Glykena, daughter of Julia Calliote and heir in part of Julius Saturninus, my patron, centurion, erected this statue to Julius Saturninus, centurion of the 14th Gemina Legion, together with my husband, as my mother legally handed me over, to Aurelius Christos Severus.
EDCS-36900278.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue by a woman to her patron, a centurion of the 14th Legion.

EDCS-36900279.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Koutiles to his wife.

EDCS-36900280.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Chrysippus of Sidere, alive, made [this] for himself, for his wife Pomē daughter of Ariston, and for his children: Ariston, who lived 4 years, Zouthis, who lived 1 year, Sideros, who lived 10 years, Artemidoros, who lived 18 years, in memory. Rejoice, and rejoice, passerby.
EDCS-36900280.summary: This funerary monument was constructed by Chrysippus for himself, his wife, and their children, listing their ages and expressing a wish for joy.

EDCS-36900284.translation: Diogenes of Zoilos dedicated this hero shrine to his beloved son, Zoilus, in his name, for the gods. The tomb holds the ashes. Zoilus, aged six. Rejoice, and rejoice, passerby.
EDCS-36900284.summary: This inscription is a dedication of a hero shrine by Diogenes to his young son, Zoilus, indicating the tomb holds his ashes.

EDCS-36900285.translation: Diomedes of Heracleia, son of Diomedes, aged 50. Rejoice.
EDCS-36900285.summary: A simple funerary epitaph for Diomedes of Heracleia, recording his name, patronymic, and age.

EDCS-36900290.translation: Good luck. The tribe of Aigikorians erected this altar to the Nymphs from their own funds during the magistracy of Skapoulos, son of Nikolaos.
EDCS-36900290.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar to the Nymphs by the tribe of Aigikorians during a specific magistracy.

EDCS-36900294.translation: Asklepiades, son of Minophilos of Nikomedeia, also called Azanites, merchant, to Minophilos Bassos, his brother, merchant, and to Asklepiades, son of Minophilos, his father. He lived 60 years. This altar, in memory. Rejoice, passerby.
EDCS-36900294.summary: This inscription dedicates an altar in memory of a father and brother, both merchants, by Asklepiades, who also lived 60 years.

EDCS-36900298.translation: Dionysios and Herodoros, sons of Satyrion, and Artemidoros, son of Dionysios, constructed the work of the abitory from their own funds for the village, above the magistrate.
EDCS-36900298.summary: This inscription records the construction of a building ('abitory') by three individuals for the village, funded by themselves.

EDCS-36900299.translation: Simadas, son of Dionysios. Rejoice, and rejoice, passerby.
EDCS-36900299.summary: A brief epitaph for Simadas, son of Dionysios.

EDCS-36900302.summary: This inscription appears to be a brief dedication or label related to a woman named Kasianá associated with a cave.

EDCS-36900316.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication naming Eukrates and his father Epikrates.

EDCS-37000008.translation: Attas set up this stele for his own son, Ioustos, a hero who lived for 20 years, in memory. Greetings, passerby.
EDCS-37000008.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Attas for his son Ioustos, who died at age 20.

EDCS-37000019.translation: Aurelius Theodorus, son of Theodorus, for Flavia Pannonia, the most excellent, his benefactor, in honor, by decree of the council and people.
EDCS-37000019.summary: This inscription honors Flavia Pannonia as a benefactor, decreed by the council and people, likely erected by Aurelius Theodorus.

EDCS-37000025.translation: To Euphrosynos, my brother, a hero who lived for 25 years, in memory. Greetings. Marcus Ulpius Fortunatus, aged 43, a hero. Greetings.
EDCS-37000025.summary: This funerary dedication is by Marcus Ulpius Fortunatus for his brother Euphrosynos, who died at age 25.

EDCS-37000028.translation: To the Gods Below. Alesandra placed this sarcophagus for Alexandros, son of Herakleon, her father and senator. Greetings.
EDCS-37000028.summary: This is a burial inscription where Alesandra dedicated a sarcophagus to her father, Alexandros, who was a senator.

EDCS-37000029.translation: Teimos, companion of Teius, honored descendant of the family, I set up this stele. Having been born of the thoughtful Teimotheos, remember our renowned fatherland and cherished companion, and the sweet friendship which death has divided. Farewell, you who pass by, as you are sent forth from the mouths of the departed Meropes with grace.
EDCS-37000029.summary: This is a funerary stele erected by Teimos for his father Teimotheos (or a father named Teios), commemorating family, friendship, and homeland, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37000034.translation: Of Philimon, of Philimon, of Philimon, of Philimon.
EDCS-37000034.summary: This inscription is a repetitive naming of Philimon, possibly a dedication or a marker of ownership.

EDCS-37000038.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Theodorus Rufus, likely a name on a monument or object.

EDCS-38100360.translation: Flavii Severinus and Eubolium, primipilarii from the province of Hellespont, pasto militum, in the 13th indiction, erected a statue in the likeness of Dionysus, in the consulship of Theodosius and who was announced.
EDCS-38100360.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue of Dionysus by two primipilarii, Flavii Severinus and Eubolium, during the 13th indiction and the consulship of Theodosius.

EDCS-38100361.translation: Flavii Agapitus and Diogenis, primipilarii from the province of Insulanea, pasto militum, erected a statue in the likeness of Dionysus, in the 14th indiction, in the consulship of Antiochus and Bassus, most distinguished men.
EDCS-38100361.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue of Dionysus by two primipilarii, Flavii Agapitus and Diogenis, during the 14th indiction and the consulship of Antiochus and Bassus.

EDCS-38100362.translation: Flavius Afonius and Diodorus, former primipilares from the province of Hellas, erected this statue of a soldier, in the consulship of Valerius and Aetius, most excellent men, in the fifteenth indiction.
EDCS-38100362.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue of a soldier by two former centurions in the consulship of Valerius and Aetius.

EDCS-38700262.translation: It hangs in the arms of a helpless child, like me, a prisoner, tormented by sorrows in my limbs, neck, and nerves, and in my sight and brain, so that you may understand.
EDCS-38700262.summary: This is a very fragmented and difficult text, possibly a personal lament or a curse, describing physical suffering.

EDCS-39400070.translation: Let the elected priest of the initiated gods of Samothrace, Diagoras, for himself, with good fortune, the one who bought the priesthood, serve for life and on the seventh day of Apatoureon, provide the wood, split it, pour the wine for the initiates, and lead the procession with a ram. He will be crowned by the initiates for the sake of their goodwill towards him. On the day he serves, he will also offer the incense in all meetings with the preceding priest of the initiates and those prescribed by law. He shall have the crown in perpetuity. Timaios, son of Straton, bought the priesthood for seven gold [units] and sixty bronze [units], which he paid immediately.
EDCS-39400070.summary: This inscription details the duties and purchase of the priesthood of the initiated gods of Samothrace by Diagoras, and later records the sale of the priesthood to Timaios.

EDCS-39400071.translation: In the time of the priest Sarapion, son of Dioskourides, the magistrates spoke. Since, due to the circumstances of the times, the people are in great distress and difficulty and most of all are suffering concerning the city's defenses, with many leaving the city due to poverty and others unable to guard the homeland because of the plague and illnesses, and thus needing more and greater protection, it is deemed necessary to appoint some to oversee and guard the most important places of the city. It has been resolved by the council and the people to choose two leaders from all the citizens who will register forty chosen men to serve as guards at the gates, keep watch at night, and patrol the city until the people are in a better state and have escaped the surrounding dangers, and can give due thanks to the gods. The chosen leaders will have the authority to compel and fine each day ten silver [units], and to sell those who disobey in any way they can, remaining themselves free from punishment and accountability. They are to be given by the people four gold [units] for an offering and meeting. They are to be permitted by the magistrates to sell [goods] in the harbor. In the time of the priest Aristophanes, Apollous, son of Nikeartos, and Poseidonios, son of Geron, were elected. In the time of the priest Theophilos, son of Noumenios, the magistrates spoke. Since we, having been elected leaders by the people according to a decree in the time of the priest Sarapion, son of Dioskourides, Apollous, son of Nikeartos, and Poseidonios, son of Geron, registered forty chosen men, and with great diligence performed our duties, serving at the gates, keeping watch at night, and patrolling, we have preserved the city until the people were restored to better hopes. And the four gold [units] given to us by the city as an offering, having increased each year, we offer sacrifices for the salvation of the people to the Mother of the Gods and the Dioskouroi. It has been resolved by the council and the people to praise the leaders and the chosen men for these deeds, and to inscribe the decrees and their names on a white stone tablet and dedicate it in a place of their choice, so that others, knowing the gratitude of the people, may become more zealous in defending the homeland. Apollous, son of Nikeartos, Poseidonios, son of Geron, Heraios, son of Artemidoros, Noumenios, son of Noumenios, Dioskourides, son of Heronumos, Demetrios, son of Geron, Menis, son of Artemidoros, Antikrates, son of Eudoxos, Timokles, son of Aristomachos, Noumenios, son of Monimos, Dionysios, son of Diogenes, Dionysios, son of Demetrios, Menis, son of Apollodoros, Noumenios, son of Heiranichos, Demetrios, son of Hekataios, Glaukias, son of Straton, Dioskourides, son of Sokrates, Pausimachos, son of Polyphēmos, Apollodoros, son of Heraios, Meniskos, son of Skymnos, Noumenios, son of Noumenios, Chairemon, son of Eukrates, also called Theophilos, Hephaistion, son of Dionysios, Triakadas, son of Dokimos, Apollonios, son of Hygiainon, Hestaios, son of Aristeides, Hedylos, son of Theophanios.
EDCS-39400071.summary: This decree from an ancient Greek city records the appointment of leaders and guards to protect the city during a time of crisis, praises their service, and records their names.

EDCS-39400083.translation: Antigonos, son of Rufus, lover of the city; Chrysē, daughter of Dionysios, lover of the city; Krēskēs, son of Kathios, lover of the city; Hilaros, son of Apollonios, lover of the city; Valēs, son of Valentos, lover of the city; Loungeinos, son of Valentos, lover of the city; Leon, son of Epistratos, lover of the city; Chrysē, daughter of Chrysēs, lover of the city; Chrestos, son of Helios, lover of the city; Natalios, son of Epistratos, lover of the city; Epistratos, son of Epistratos, lover of the city; Dadas, son of Hestiaiou, lover of the city; Theadon, son of Kouthios, gymnasiarch; Alexandros, son of Herakleidas, gymnasiarch; Attas, son of Dioskourides, nomophylax; Serapion, son of Dadas, hierokeryx; Ailios, son of Asklepiades, ekdikos.
EDCS-39400083.summary: This is a list of citizens honored with the title 'lover of the city' (philotimes), along with various officials, likely from a gymnasium or civic board.

EDCS-39400084.translation: Dadas, ekdikos; [---]atoros, lover of the city; [---]onos, lover of the city; Annianos, son of Priskos, lover of the city; Ioulis Theodoulos, lover of the city; Loungeinos, son of Domitius Finix, lover of the city; [---]ou, lover of the city; Markos, lover of the city; [---]IMO.
EDCS-39400084.summary: This is a fragmented inscription listing individuals honored with the title 'lover of the city' and an official (ekdikos).

EDCS-39400087.translation: For the section of the wall, thirteen cubits, from their own funds. The undersigned astynomoi: Ulpius, son of Serapion, a hundred cubits; Iulianus, four cubits; [---]stou, two cubits; [---]enos(?), one cubit; Plōtios, two cubits; Bosporos, two cubits.
EDCS-39400087.summary: This inscription records the lengths of wall sections contributed by various individuals (astynomoi) at their own expense.

EDCS-39400088.translation: Aurelius Mun, Aurelius Theodoros Diogenes, Aurelius Aquilas, son of Athenades, cubits; Aurelius Basileus, Aurelius Mun, Aurelius Annis, Iulius; Aurelius Diogenes Romanos, Aurelius Romanos, Aurelius Zoilus.
EDCS-39400088.summary: This inscription lists the names of several individuals, likely members of a civic or religious group, with some indicating measurements in cubits.

EDCS-39400092.translation: Hermogenes, son of Marcus, lover of the city and gymnasiarch; Valerianus, son of Valerius, priest; Poseidonios, son of Papas, priest; Dionysios, son of Marcus, priest; Dadas, son of Dionysios, priest; Hestiaiou, priest; Demetris, son of Papas, priest; Poseidonnis, son of Valerius, priest; Dionyssis, son of Hestiaiou, priest. They erected this stele. MILOGSITO PO I of the ancestors L NPRO SILN STELE ISIPAGIOSTHL MBENŌK EPOIŌN five.
EDCS-39400092.summary: This inscription records the names of priests and a gymnasiarch who erected a stele, possibly commemorating ancestral lineage or a shared religious duty.

EDCS-39400097.translation: [---] of Phaedrus, Dionysios Tauros, lover of the city; [---]ulos, high priest; [---]by, keeper of books; Heraklas, son of Tomos; Heraklas (second), Hephaistodoros (second); Herakleides, son of Theodotos; Hephaistion, son of Heraklas; Thrasymedes (second), lover of the city; Thrasymedes, son of Chrysomaos, lover of the city; high priest Theodotos, son of Kalos; high priest Theodoros, son of Kyrenios; Theodotos, son of Magnus; Theodoros, son of Neikostratos; Theodoros, son of Epikrates; Theodotos, son of Pontianos; Theodoros, son of Hestiaiou; Theodoros, son of Rufus; Iulianus, son of Faustinianos; Iulianus, son of Pakkatianos; Iulianus, son of Thrasymedes; Iulianus, son of Nigreinos; Iulianus, son of Loungeinos; Iulianus, son of Loungeinos the younger; Iulianus, son of Flavius; Iulianus (second); Iulianus, son of Epictetus; Iulianus, son of Epictetus the younger; Iulianus, son of Crispus; Iulianus, son of Poseidonios; Iulianus, son of Iulianus; Loungeinos; Marcus (second), high priest; Munnios, son of Loungeinos; Modestus Ponticus; Markios, son of Dionysios; Markios, son of Dionysios Makreinos; Markios, son of Dionysios; Markianos; Myron; Myron; Markianos; Marcus Terentianos; Marcus Terentianos; Marcus Terentianos; Marcus; Marcus; Marcus.
EDCS-39400097.summary: This inscription is a long list of names, predominantly featuring individuals honored as 'lovers of the city' (philotimes) and holding priestly or official titles, likely from a civic or religious association.

EDCS-39400098.translation: Flavius Marcianus Messius Bassus Cassis Eunu, Eusche, Tertullus, with the 300 of Nipaia Prunaon, son of Menou, the keeper of the records. (restored)
EDCS-39400098.summary: This inscription, likely a funerary monument, lists several names and possibly offices or affiliations, with a lacuna indicating missing information.

EDCS-39400101.translation: The tribe of the Argadeis crowned Kerkion, son of Timomachos, who served as phylarch.
EDCS-39400101.summary: This is a decree honoring Kerkion, son of Timomachos, for his service as phylarch, issued by the tribe of Argadeis.

EDCS-39400102.translation: The archons, Poseidonios son of Noumenios and Kallimachos son of Kallimachos, honored Dionysios son of Dioskourides and Heroides.
EDCS-39400102.summary: This inscription records a decree by the archons Poseidonios and Kallimachos to honor Dionysios and Heroides, likely officials in the service of Demeter.

EDCS-39400108.translation: To good fortune! The tribe of the Argadeis honored Titus Flavius Poseidonios, son of Phaedrus, the pontarch and high priest of the Hexapolis, son of Pontus, and first agonothetes of the god Antinous, and son of the city, their patron.
EDCS-39400108.summary: This is a civic honorific inscription from the tribe of Argadeis in honor of Titus Flavius Poseidonios, who held multiple important religious and civic titles.

EDCS-39400112.translation: The council and the people of the metropolis of Tomis, in honor of Alexandros, for serving as agoranomos and for ruling with full honor.
EDCS-39400112.summary: This is a decree from the city council and people of Tomis honoring Alexandros for his distinguished service as agoranomos and ruler.

EDCS-39400113.translation: To good fortune! The council and the people of the metropolis of Tomis, in honor of Publius Aelius Antoninus Zoilus, the high priest and priest of Demeter.
EDCS-39400113.summary: This is an honorific decree by the council and people of Tomis for Publius Aelius Antoninus Zoilus, recognizing his priestly roles.

EDCS-39400115.translation: To good fortune! The council and people of the metropolis of Tomis, in honor of Publius Aelius Gaius, who served as archon with distinction, diligently served justice, and was honorably sent as an ambassador to Rome before the most divine Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus, at his own expense.
EDCS-39400115.summary: This honorific inscription from Tomis commends Publius Aelius Gaius for his distinguished service as archon, legal advocacy, and diplomatic mission to Rome.

EDCS-39400122.translation: To good fortune! The council and people honored Titus Cominius Claudianus Hermaphilus, the sophist and agonothetes of virtue, the pontarch of the Hexapolis, and high priest and priest of the two emperors.
EDCS-39400122.summary: This is an honorific decree by the council and people honoring Titus Cominius Claudianus Hermaphilus for his achievements as a sophist, agonothetes, pontarch, and imperial priest.

EDCS-39400123.translation: To good fortune! The council and people of the metropolis of Pontos, Tomis, honored Attalos, the brother of Titus Cominius Claudianus Hermaphilus, the pontarch, for being a lover of his brothers, for ruling his first term officially, for serving as agoranomos soundly, for presiding over the festival of the theatrical assembly with generosity, and for faithfully serving as treasurer of the people.
EDCS-39400123.summary: This is an honorific inscription by the people of Tomis honoring Attalos for his various civic and religious roles, including his relationship to the pontarch Titus Cominius Claudianus Hermaphilus.

EDCS-39400124.translation: To good fortune! The council and people of the metropolis of Tomis honored Africanus Quintus, who served with distinction in the army, served as agoranomos with brilliance, and surpassed those before him in honor. His wife, Sossia Africana, erected the statue.
EDCS-39400124.summary: This inscription records a decree by the council and people of Tomis to honor Africanus Quintus for his military service and distinguished tenure as agoranomos, with his statue erected by his wife.

EDCS-39400125.translation: With good fortune, the council and the people of the metropolis of Tomis honored Sossiana Africana, wife of Kyetos, priestess of the Mother of the Gods, daughter of Gaius Julius Africanus, who surpassed those before her and adorned the goddess with golden offerings.
EDCS-39400125.summary: A dedication by the council and people of Tomis to Sossiana Africana, a priestess of the Mother of the Gods, for her embellishments to the goddess.

EDCS-39400130.translation: The city honors its patron and benefactor for all virtue, with Bassus Menekrates serving as envoy.
EDCS-39400130.summary: A dedication by a city to its patron and benefactor, with mention of an envoy named Bassus Menekrates.

EDCS-39400135.translation: With good fortune, for the fortune, victory, and eternal endurance of the most divine Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Parthicus Medicus Britannicus, and Publius Septimius Geta, most distinguished Caesar, and of the Empress Julia Augusta and their entire house, and for the governor of the province, the consular Ovinius Tertullus, those who gave us this gift: Titus Flavius Sallustius, Titus Flavius Salvianus Pollion, son of Pollion, priest Achilles, son of Achillas, father Alexander, son of Helei, chief Dendrophoros; Alexander, son of Alexander, chief Dendrophoros; Demetris, son of Amphionides; Poseidonios, son of Poponius Turbon; Dionysius, son of Andron Lonigeinus; Titus Flavius, son of Ths Pollion; Demetris, son of Sios; Titus Flavius Eutyches; Dionysius, son of Dotios; Au, son of Helei; Hermodoros, son of Theodotos; Neikostratos, son of Marcus Pontikos; Hermodoros, son of Chrysao, of the stock of Gaucus; Skeipion, son of Pontikos, also called Pontikos; Poseidonios, son of Poponius; Aurelius, son of Alexander; Aurelius Hygeinos; Titus Flavius Eutyches, son of Nanas; Theadon's mother, dendrophoron; Publia Aelia Olympia, chief Rabdoukhisa; Basiliskos, son of Alexander; Flavius Symphoros; Aurelius Serapodorus, son of Poseidoneios; Publius Aelius Hermeros; Iulius Herakleides; Hilarion, son of Lucius; Aurelius Iulius Chrysogonos; Aurelius Iulius Histius; Flavius Histius; Aurelius Athenados, son of M.
EDCS-39400135.summary: A lengthy list of dedicants and officials honoring the Roman Emperors and their governor, likely for a public gift or benefaction during the imperial period.

EDCS-39400140.translation: With good fortune, the council and the people honored Marcus Kokkeios Chrysogonos, the mystagogue.
EDCS-39400140.summary: A dedication by the council and people of Tomis to Marcus Kokkeios Chrysogonos, a mystagogue, likely in recognition of his religious office.

EDCS-39400144.translation: With good fortune, according to the decisions of the most excellent council and the most brilliant people of the most brilliant metropolis of Tomis, for Aurelius Preiskios Isidoros, pontarch and first archon, who served purely and blamelessly, and who, as high priest, zealously and generously performed the 'through arms' and hunting festival for six consecutive days, and for his wife, the high priestess Ulpia Matrona, for all honor and virtue, she who is also a council member and one of the first citizens of the most brilliant Flavia Nea Polis and Antiphris.
EDCS-39400144.summary: A dedication by the council and people of Tomis to Aurelius Preiskios Isidoros, pontarch and archon, and his wife Ulpia Matrona, high priestess, for their public service and religious duties, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.
