EDCS-39400145.translation: With good fortune, according to the decisions of the most excellent council and the most brilliant people of the most brilliant metropolis and of the blessed Pontus of Tomis, for the pontarch Aurelius Preiskios Annianos, who served as archon of the Koinon of the Hellenes and of the metropolis with purity, and who zealously and generously performed the 'through arms' and hunting festival, and who is also a council member and one of the first citizens of Flavia Nea Polis, and for his wife, the high priestess Ioulia Aplaustē, for all honor.
EDCS-39400145.summary: A dedication by the council and people of Tomis to Aurelius Preiskios Annianos, pontarch and archon, and his wife Ioulia Aplaustē, high priestess, for their public service and religious duties.

EDCS-39400148.translation: With good fortune, the cohort of Romans honored Vitalius, the priest, with twenty statues for honor.
EDCS-39400148.summary: A dedication by a Roman cohort to Vitalius, a priest, for honor, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400149.translation: With good fortune, the council and people honor Aurelius [[name]] [[title]], the most distinguished patron of the metropolis of Tomis.
EDCS-39400149.summary: A fragmentary dedication by the council and people of Tomis to a distinguished patron named Aurelius.

EDCS-39400154.translation: With good fortune, for Publius Aelius Ammonios, the most excellent procurator of the Emperor, who faithfully administered the province, prefect of the troop of Espanoi, tribune of the first cohort of Germans, who led the soldiers in battle in Armenia, prefect of another first cohort of Flavia Getulorum, who led the soldiers of this province, prefect of the fleet Flavia Mysica Gordiana, Catellinus, freedman of the lord Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus, his librarian.
EDCS-39400154.summary: An inscription detailing the military and administrative career of Publius Aelius Ammonios, serving under Emperor Gordian III, likely dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-39400155.translation: With good fortune, for the fortune, victory, and eternal endurance of the lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus and of the Augusta Sabinia Tranquillina.
EDCS-39400155.summary: A dedication for the well-being and endurance of Emperor Gordian III and Empress Sabinia Tranquillina.

EDCS-39400157.translation: With good fortune, the council and the people to Tomeis of Lokra Praisteinianos, most distinguished and patron of the metropolis.
EDCS-39400157.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the council and people of Tomeis to their patron, Lokra Praisteinianos.

EDCS-39400158.translation: With good fortune, the council and people of Tomeis to the most god-loving Emperor Gaius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-39400158.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the council and people of Tomeis to Emperor Diocletian.

EDCS-39400159.translation: To Apollo Agieus, in accordance with an oracle, for the safety and victory of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Armenian, Parthian, Mede, and for the health, safety, and prosperity of the metropolis of Tomeis, with good fortune, to Apollo Agieus, in accordance with an oracle, during the consulship of Catonius Vindex, under the administration of Publius Flavius Theodorus, dispontarch.
EDCS-39400159.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Apollo Agieus by officials in Tomeis for the well-being of the emperor and the city, dating to the consulship of Catonius Vindex.

EDCS-39400162.translation: Pure, for the assembly, a statue burning with fire, a gift from their own craftsmanship, was granted to you; having received a secret crown among the Bacchae, showing an ancient rite, receive, O bull-horned one, this work of Hermogenes' hand, and save, O Pasous, the sacred assembly.
EDCS-39400162.summary: This is a dedication to a deity, likely related to Bacchic rites, with the statue being a gift and the inscription requesting protection for the assembly.

EDCS-39400163.translation: To Dionysus, with good fortune, under the leadership of Aculeinus son of Artemidorus, in fulfillment of a vow, successfully, for good.
EDCS-39400163.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Dionysus by Aculeinus, son of Artemidorus.

EDCS-39400165.translation: With good fortune, the protoplast, disphylarch, lover of honor, and overseer of the household, Apatourios son of Euelpistou son of Poseidonios the protoplast and benefactor of good things, erected this display of generosity, responsive gods, for the Hopleiton tribe, for the disphylarchy.
EDCS-39400165.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Apatourios, a prominent official, for the Hopleiton tribe, expressing generosity and appealing to the gods.

EDCS-39400166.translation: With good fortune, a thank-offering to the Hero was erected by those around the synagogue: Heroxenos son of Dioscurides, and the lover of honor Noumenios son of Lysimachus, and the lover of honor Attas son of Lucius, and the lover of honor Thallos son of Agathon, and the lover of honor Secundus son of Epicrates, and the lover of honor Chrestos son of Nicias, and the lover of honor Demonax son of Demonactou, and Secundus son of Demetrius, and the priestess Bouteis daughter of Heroxenos, and the scribe Philotas son of Euboulous, and the nomophylax Acornion son of Lysimachus, and the ekdikos Papas son of Heroxenos, and the hierokeryx Dion son of Aotaios. And these are the ones gathered, with the lovers of honor: Serapion son of Serapion, Posidonios son of Dae, Papas son of Antoniou, Phidias son of Dionysius, Damas son of Lysimachus, Timotheos son of Lysimachus, Demetrios son of Demonactus, Galenos son of Attas, Magniōn son of Aotaios, Helios son of Attas, Ponticus son of Alexandrou, Areiballos son of Gaius, Artemidorus son of Asclepiadou, Asclepiades.
EDCS-39400166.summary: This inscription records a thank-offering to a Hero by members of a synagogue, listing numerous individuals including officials and benefactors, likely dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-39400167.translation: Dioscurides son of Ariston dedicated this vow to the Hero Manimazos.
EDCS-39400167.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the Hero Manimazos by Dioscurides, son of Ariston.

EDCS-39400168.translation: With good fortune, Sedatios Apollonios, prefect of a troop of horsemen of the Gaetulian cohort in Arabia, dedicated this vow to the Hero Manibazos.
EDCS-39400168.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the Hero Manibazos by Sedatios Apollonios, a Roman military officer.

EDCS-39400169.translation: Marcus son of Graphikos, and Demosthenes, and Demetrios, sons of Marcus, [dedicated] this thank-offering to the Hero Katoikadios.
EDCS-39400169.summary: This inscription records a thank-offering to the Hero Katoikadios by Marcus and his sons, Demosthenes and Demetrios.

EDCS-39400170.translation: To good fortune. Philocles son of Chrestos, a benefactor of the shipowners' guild, built the hearth at his own expense.
EDCS-39400170.summary: This is a dedication for a hearth, built by Philocles, a member of the shipowners' guild, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400173.translation: Satyrus Numinius son of Dioscurides and Marcus son of Rufus, who also served as archon and priest, and Marcus son of Marcus the younger, also priest, dedicated this to Plouton, Demeter, and the goddess Kore, at their own expense.
EDCS-39400173.summary: This is a dedication to agricultural deities, made by multiple individuals including a priest and an archon, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400174.translation: Poseidonios dedicated this as a thank-offering to Poseidon Heliconius.
EDCS-39400174.summary: This is a votive offering to Poseidon Heliconius, dedicated by Poseidonios, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400175.translation: To Sarapis. By Polydoros of Sinope, according to a dream.
EDCS-39400175.summary: This is a dedication to Sarapis by Polydoros of Sinope, prompted by a dream, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400176.translation: To the great god Sarapis and the gods sharing his temple, and to the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and Caesar Marcus Aurelius Verus, Karpion son of Anoubion, of the guild of Alexandrians, dedicated the altar at his own expense. Year 23, Pharmouthi 1. In the priesthood of Cornutus, also called Sarapion, and Polymnos, also called Longinus, son of Ar[---].
EDCS-39400176.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to Sarapis and Roman emperors, made by Karpion of the Alexandrian guild, with a precise date in the Egyptian calendar corresponding to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-39400177.translation: To good fortune. Dionysios son of Hedylos built this at his own expense through the priest of Isis, also called Isidoros, and of Sarapis, Isis, Anubis, and all the gods.
EDCS-39400177.summary: This records the construction of a building or feature by Dionysios son of Hedylos, dedicated to a pantheon of Egyptian and Greco-Roman deities, through a priest.

EDCS-39400178.translation: To good fortune. Agrippas and Ascleus, benefactors.
EDCS-39400178.summary: This is a dedication by Agrippas and Ascleus, identified as benefactors, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400185.translation: To Antigone. By Avlosanis, wife of Seuthes.
EDCS-39400185.summary: This is a dedication to Antigone, made by Avlosanis, wife of Seuthes, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400186.translation: Two dear sons lie beneath a weeping mound, having received the ashes of their father; the godlike son whom I raised, Philon, son of Heracleitus. They lie here at my expense, not mine but Hades'. For the tomb hides the bones of these short-lived ones. My family grieves, and life holds nothing. Here I lie with my brother Heracleides, awaiting the pains. Having brought glory to men, we depart. Alas! Heracleides, may you have the glory of virtue, you who are distinguished! The family.
EDCS-39400186.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for two sons, Philon and Heracleides, sons of Heracleitus, highlighting their nobility and the grief of their father and brother, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-39400188.translation: To the chthonic heroes. Asclepiades erected this in memory of his own children, Flavia Aquilina, who lived 8 years, and Julius Augurinus, who lived 2 years.
EDCS-39400188.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Asclepiades for his two young children, Flavia Aquilina and Julius Augurinus, in the Roman period.

EDCS-39400189.translation: I, Akylas, twenty years old, lie buried beneath this tomb, not a stranger but a citizen, leaving my father in sorrow. Live, father, and rejoice, and you too, passerby, rejoice in the daughters that an envious god granted you in my place as nurses.
EDCS-39400189.summary: An epitaph for a young man named Akylas, who died at age 20, lamenting his absence and expressing love for his father and sisters.

EDCS-39400191.translation: I, Markianos, son of Markianos, of the Argadean tribe, having served as scribe, constructed this inscribed stone for myself while alive and in my right mind.
EDCS-39400191.summary: A funerary epigram by Markianos, a scribe from the Argadean tribe, who dedicated a stone to himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-39400192.translation: Gaios and Diogenes for Eia, our sweetest mother, who lived with our father Diogenes for 34 years irreproachably and piously, for the sake of remembrance.
EDCS-39400192.summary: A memorial inscription by Gaios and Diogenes for their mother Eia, honoring her piety and her 34-year marriage to their father Diogenes.

EDCS-39400193.translation: Roufeina erected this stone for Theokritos, son of Theokritos, a shipowner and also a king, who lived for 22 years and 9 months. Rejoice!
EDCS-39400193.summary: A funerary monument erected by Roufeina for Theokritos, a shipowner who lived for 22 years and 9 months.

EDCS-39400194.translation: Kallistos for Midas, aged 55. Marcia, wife of Markus, together with her daughter Kalliste, dedicated this out of affection for her husband and her father.
EDCS-39400194.summary: A dedication by Marcia and her daughter Kalliste for Kallistos, whose age is given as 55, honoring Midas.

EDCS-39400195.translation: My native city, the wide-streeted Neapolis, enrolled me, but my homeland, Tomis, crowned me even more. It saw me, more than other mortals, adorned with golden crowns and purple robes. For I served as hipparch twice and completed the trials of the Areioi twice, and three times completed my term of office and did not insult the city. Having accomplished all these things, I was struck by envy and censured, but by the hostile plans of the gods my soul has been extinguished. Athletes of Ares, who were overcome in my contests, carry this honor even in death.
EDCS-39400195.summary: An epitaph, likely for an athlete or official, from Neapolis and Tomis, recounting honors and achievements before an untimely death attributed to divine envy.

EDCS-39400196.translation: Agathandros, son of Ioulianos and Reigine, seven and ten years old, left the light of the sun. I die in beautiful Smyrna, hastening to win the Pythian games and bring home the crown of wrestling for my homeland. But instead of my crowns, my homeland has only my bones, hidden in this stone. Rejoice, passerby.
EDCS-39400196.summary: An epitaph for Agathandros, a young athlete from Smyrna who died at age 17, lamenting his unfulfilled ambitions for victory and his lost prizes.

EDCS-39400197.translation: For Kronion has made our lineage unique. Sotâs, son of Pollion, was unique, my mother was unique, and I, Herotis, was also born unique. I married at the age of 13 a man sweeter to me than honey, and I myself, leaving behind my unique child, die, and gave birth to a unique child to be carried into the halls of Hades. I die, but you, reader, rejoice and be well.
EDCS-39400197.summary: A self-written epitaph by Herotis, who describes herself and her family as unique, detailing her early marriage and death after childbirth.

EDCS-39400198.translation: Publius Cornelius Calpurnius for his dear brother Publius Cornelius Maximus, a beneficiary.
EDCS-39400198.summary: A dedication by Publius Cornelius Calpurnius for his brother Publius Cornelius Maximus, who served as a beneficiary.

EDCS-39400201.translation: Having lived for 9 years, having cohabited with me for 46 years, from whom children were born to me, alive and well and in my right mind, I constructed this monument. Rejoice, passerby.
EDCS-39400201.summary: A funerary monument erected by a husband who lived with his wife for 46 years, and who had 9 children with her, dedicating the monument while alive and well.

EDCS-39400205.translation: To Panthia, in memory of her husband Spiklos.
EDCS-39400205.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her deceased husband.

EDCS-39400206.translation: Epiktēsis, daughter of Herakleitos, freedwoman, aged 10. Greetings.
EDCS-39400206.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a young freedwoman, likely set up by her former master or patron.

EDCS-39400207.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment continuing a previous dedication, likely for a spouse.

EDCS-39400220.translation: Apphēs, wife of Valerius Archelaos, erected this stele in memory of her husband, having lived with him for 33 years. Greetings, passerby, you too.
EDCS-39400220.summary: This funerary stele was erected by a wife to commemorate her husband, with whom she shared 33 years of marriage.

EDCS-39400226.translation: Having lived 32 years, I erected this stele for my dutiful son. Be well.
EDCS-39400226.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a parent for their deceased son, who lived 32 years.

EDCS-39400229.translation: Chrestos, son of Holokales, philotimos, of the Aikorians tribe, having lived well for 63 years. Greetings, passerby.
EDCS-39400229.summary: This tombstone commemorates Chrestos, a man who lived 63 years and was a member of the Aikorians tribe.

EDCS-39400232.translation: Greetings, passerby. Alexander, son of Smaragdos, of the Oinopai tribe, philotimos, made this basin for himself and for his wife Kyrilla, daughter of Philokles. In.
EDCS-39400232.summary: This inscription records Alexander of the Oinopai tribe dedicating a basin for himself and his wife Kyrilla, identifying himself as philotimos.

EDCS-39400234.translation: Aurelius Sozomenos, son of Zotichos, from Byzantium, having lived for 40 years. Greetings, passerby.
EDCS-39400234.summary: This tombstone marks the grave of Aurelius Sozomenos, a man from Byzantium who lived 40 years.

EDCS-39400235.translation: Theisios Skōritos, having lived eighteen years. Greetings, passersby.
EDCS-39400235.summary: This inscription commemorates Theisios Skōritos, who died at the age of eighteen.

EDCS-39400237.translation: My name was Marcellus, and also my homeland was Africa; then after living for thirty years, I died. You see me here, and my companion Asklepiades set up this tomb for me.
EDCS-39400237.summary: This epitaph describes the deceased as Marcellus from Africa, who lived 30 years, and states that his companion Asklepiades erected the tomb.

EDCS-39400238.translation: Thracian, rejoice! Wife of Marcus, rejoice! It is of the ditch, of the garden of the house, on the east side, a fence; not in the fencing to those in charge of it to have it and
EDCS-39400238.summary: This fragmentary inscription, likely from a boundary marker or property division, discusses fencing and property lines, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400240.translation: To Iadon, for his son who lived 25 years, he erected this stele in memory. Rejoice!
EDCS-39400240.summary: This epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records the erection of a commemorative stele for a man named Iadon by his son.

EDCS-39400241.translation: For Aurelius Eutychianus, son of Diogenes, the equestrian, who served as magistrate and market overseer; the hero. Statilia Elpidia, his wife, and Aurelius Musicius, his son, in memory.
EDCS-39400241.summary: This epitaph honors Aurelius Eutychianus, an equestrian who held civic offices, and was set up by his wife and son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400242.translation: My name was Valerius Maior. His devout wife Valeria Theodora set me up. He lived 40 years. Rejoice, passerby!
EDCS-39400242.summary: This epitaph records the erection of a tombstone for Valerius Maior by his wife Valeria Theodora, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400243.translation: Here lies Eros, asleep in sleep. But among the dead there is no longing, no affection for the departed. But the dead man lies in the plain, a stone as it were, having scattered the flesh of tender forms. Therefore, for those who remain, he himself won glorious renown in life, bearing love for all time, from water and earth and spirit. But I, dead, lie here, having paid back all to all. What more? From where did it come? Into this it dissolved, a body fading.
EDCS-39400243.summary: This elaborate funerary epigram, likely from the Roman Imperial period, reflects on the nature of life, death, and remembrance, and serves as an epitaph for the deceased.

EDCS-39400244.translation: With good fortune. Aurelius Eutychis, freedman of Aurelius Dionysius, also called Haimonius, having made this monument, I dedicated this stele. Rejoice, passerby!
EDCS-39400244.summary: This epitaph records the dedication of a monument and stele by a freedman, Aurelius Eutychis, for himself or his patron, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400246.translation: Aurelius Demosthenes, son of Demosthenes, having lived diligently for 90 years, and his wife Aurelia Tu[...]
EDCS-39400246.summary: This fragmentary epitaph records the name and lifespan of Aurelius Demosthenes and the beginning of his wife's name, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400247.translation: Ponticus, son of Neikios, of Olbiopolis, for his own son Satyrus, in memory. 700, 700.
EDCS-39400247.summary: This epitaph records the dedication of a monument by Ponticus for his son Satyrus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400249.translation: Damostratos, son of Heras, from Neokomeia, made this for his mother, having lived a venerable life. Rejoice, passerby!
EDCS-39400249.summary: This epitaph records the erection of a tomb by Damostratos for his mother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400250.translation: Chryse, wife of Demosthenes, and having been the wife of Aphphos Alexander, lived 25 years. Rejoice!
EDCS-39400250.summary: This epitaph records the name, familial relationships, and lifespan of a woman named Chryse, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400255.translation: A small tomb concealed the great Agathos, boxer, champion, and skilled warrior, in the stadium.
EDCS-39400255.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Agathos, a boxer and warrior, likely from a stadium context, with an approximate date inferable from the style and language.

EDCS-39400256.translation: Castresius Julius Fronton, a merchant, made this for his most precious wife Euphrosyne, who lived 25 years. The merchant, while alive, made this for himself and his wife Euphrosyne, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-39400256.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Castresius Julius Fronton, a merchant, for his wife Euphrosyne, and also for himself, with both living for 25 years.

EDCS-39400257.translation: Ampliata Gennidos of Sidon made this altar from her own funds for herself and her husband Hermetis Sokrates, and for her son Hermaphilos. Hermetis lived 63 years and their son Hermaphilos lived 25 years. Ampliata herself lived 77 years. Homerichos Hermaphilos supervised the rebuilding of the foundation, altar, and stele by order of Ampliata. Farewell, passerby.
EDCS-39400257.summary: This is a funerary monument with an altar and stele dedicated by Ampliata for herself, her husband Hermetis, and her son Hermaphilos, with ages provided and a supervisor mentioned, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-39400258.translation: ... who was also called Basileus, a shipowner, who lived 30 years, I made this. Farewell.
EDCS-39400258.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a shipowner named Basileus, who lived 30 years, with the text implying he commissioned his own monument.

EDCS-39400259.translation: For Rufos Priskos, who served the god Dolochenos for five years, Julianos Alexandrou erected this co-priest in his own place for the sake of memory.
EDCS-39400259.summary: This is a commemorative monument dedicated by Julianos Alexandrou for his co-priest Rufos Priskos, who served the god Dolochenos for five years.

EDCS-39400261.translation: Abaskantos Sokratou, while alive and of sound mind, made this stele from his own funds for himself and for his wife Zosime, who lived happily with me. Farewell, passerby.
EDCS-39400261.summary: This is a funerary stele commissioned by Abaskantos Sokratou for himself and his wife Zosime, who lived with him.

EDCS-39400262.translation: Attios Onesimos, a grain merchant, made this monument from his own funds for himself and his family. If anyone else dares to place someone in it, they will pay a fine of fifteen thousand to the treasury and five thousand to the city.
EDCS-39400262.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Attios Onesimos, a grain merchant, for himself and his family, with a strict penalty for unauthorized burials.

EDCS-39400263.translation: With good fortune, the metropolis, by the governor, indicated me, the councilor. Aelius, having set this up, raises the glory of the fatherland.
EDCS-39400263.summary: This inscription, possibly a dedication or honorary monument, mentions a governor, a councilor (Aelius), and the metropolis, suggesting a civic or administrative context.

EDCS-39400266.translation: Menophilos, who lived 40 years, his wife Tithisatta, along with their children Honerathmios Kiattas and Sozeimios Menekleous, dedicated this stele for the sake of memory. Farewell, passerby.
EDCS-39400266.summary: This is a funerary stele dedicated by the wife and children of Menophilos, who lived 40 years, for his memory.

EDCS-39400267.translation: Euelpistus of Prusa, also known as Tomeites, erected this monument for his wife BLAS STES.
EDCS-39400267.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Euelpistus of Prusa (Tomeites) for his wife, whose name appears to be BLAS STES.

EDCS-39400271.translation: Irastamos, Adiagos, and Abragos erected a support for their own father, Atta Tyrannus, who had died in the 200th year.
EDCS-39400271.summary: This inscription records the erection of a support by three individuals for their father, Atta Tyrannus, who died in an unspecified 200th year.

EDCS-39400283.translation: Farewell, passerby. Hades snatched me, Longina, to death when I was still a child, not yet initiated, at the age of four, from my loving mother.
EDCS-39400283.summary: This funerary epitaph laments the death of a four-year-old girl named Longina, snatched by Hades before her initiation, and commiserates with her grieving mother.

EDCS-39400284.translation: To Vibius Severus, a specularius from Pontus, aged 26; his brothers Alexander and Hipparchus, in memory.
EDCS-39400284.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by two brothers, Alexander and Hipparchus, to their brother Vibius Severus, a specularius from Pontus, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-39400295.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a name and patronymic with a single letter, possibly part of a larger monument or a tombstone.

EDCS-39400297.translation: I, Attalus, a hunter, lie here. Passerby, I struck down many bulls in the stadia; but I came to the dead, having encountered a wild bull which killed me.
EDCS-39400297.summary: This is the epitaph of Attalus, a hunter who claims to have killed many bulls in athletic contests but was ultimately killed by a wild bull.

EDCS-39400299.translation: Sophon buried Amarantus, who died in battles of heroes, in memory; he erected this finely inscribed stele. Farewell, passerby.
EDCS-39400299.summary: This inscription records that Sophon buried Amarantus, who died in battle, and erected a stele in his memory.

EDCS-39400300.translation: I, Argoutos, a rhetorician, lie here, having won 6 contests. Orestes buried me in memory. Farewell, passerby.
EDCS-39400300.summary: This epitaph commemorates Argoutos, a rhetorician who won six contests, buried by Orestes.

EDCS-39400303.summary: This is a short epitaph for Iuliana, who lived 10 years, with a note about her adornments.

EDCS-39400310.translation: Papeias dedicated this stele to his wife, Isiacos daughter of Hecateus, aged 30. Farewell, farewell to you too, passerby.
EDCS-39400310.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Papeias to his wife, daughter of Isiacos and Hecateus, who died at age 30.

EDCS-39400311.translation: This pyre was constructed for Annius Superus, who lived 27 years, son of a standard-bearer of the 13th Gemina legion. The heir shall not possess it. If anyone dares to place anyone else in it, he shall pay a fine to the city.
EDCS-39400311.summary: This inscription records the construction of a funerary pyre for Annius Superus, son of a legionary standard-bearer, with a strict clause forbidding others from being buried there and imposing a penalty.

EDCS-39400312.translation: You see a stone image of the deceased Cyrillus Bissus Fabianus, child of one who mingled with the dead. This was his form, this his measure, but dead he has brought great grief to this small tomb. Live, passerby!
EDCS-39400312.summary: A funerary epigram for Cyrillus, son of Fabianus, lamenting his early death and describing his physical appearance.

EDCS-39400313.translation: Perinthos was my husband, who erected this altar and stele. If you wish to know, traveler, who I am: I was a child of thirteen years, a beloved son. Whether I married him and bore him three children, a son from my first, two daughters, a sample of my types, or if I bore a fourth. Alas, that I bore! For the first infant dies, and I too, shortly after, left the light of the sun. I, Cecilia Artemisia, lie here. Perinthos is my fatherland and my husband; my son is called Preiscos, my daughter Hieronis. At home, the infant Theodora is. For Perinthos, my husband, lives; and my daughters mourn me, and my dear father laments that I have fled here. My mother Flavia Theodora is also here, and my husband's father, Caecilius Preiscos, lies here. Of this lineage I was born, but I have just died. Farewell, and you too, farewell from my tomb. How much further do you travel?
EDCS-39400313.summary: A complex funerary epitaph of Cecilia Artemisia, detailing her family, her children, and the deaths of some of them, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400314.translation: My name was Hermogenes, and they called me Kyzicenos. I served my city with distinction, and I also completed the market magistracy. I was honored exceedingly for my friendship with all. I died after completing fifty years of life, just as it is fated for all to come. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-39400314.summary: An epitaph for Hermogenes of Kyzikos, who served as a magistrate and was known for his friendships, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400316.translation: Dionysodorus, the pontarch, raised children to leave successors in the city after him, from his lawful wife Iulia, a greatly renowned priestess, as was fitting in life. But misfortune struck: Ioulia, Dionysodorus, Leontianus, and Dionysia, and the daughter Agrippeina, who left her husband quickly, descended beneath the earth. Alas for fate's cruel offspring!
EDCS-39400316.summary: A lament for the untimely deaths of the children of Dionysodorus, a pontarch, and Iulia, a priestess, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400317.translation: I died, weighed down by a harsh illness. Hylas buried Hermogenes, my dear child, but Matrona also laid beside me her daughter, Pantheira, who had exchanged her respectable status.
EDCS-39400317.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Hylas, who buried his son Hermogenes and also his daughter Pantheira, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400318.translation: Farewell, traveler, and you too. You stand here, thinking, 'Who and from where is this?' Listen now, stranger. My homeland and my name were once Greece. From my mother Athenaïs and my father Hermionios, Epiphania was my name. I saw much of the earth and sailed all the sea, for I had ship-owners as father and husband. Those whom I chose, I left beneath this tomb, dead by their own hands. My life was once blessed. I was born among the Muses and partook of wisdom. And dear friends, leaving me behind, I provided much for women in my piety. And even the renowned, enduring much toil, clearly learned that the fortunes of mortals are not according to piety. Hermogenes of Ankyra and Tomites of the Oineis tribe, dedicating this in gratitude to his own wife, for the sake of remembrance.
EDCS-39400318.summary: An epitaph for Epiphania, detailing her life as a traveler and a pious woman, and a dedication by her husband Hermogenes of Ankyra to her memory, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400320.translation: We laid our two dearest children to rest in this earth, victims of pitiless fate, which has always caused us sorrow with the grief of short-lived children. Pontianus, aged 3, and Pontiana, aged 6. Chrysodorus and Matrona, for their ever-memorable children.
EDCS-39400320.summary: A funerary monument erected by Chrysodorus and Matrona for their two young children, Pontianus and Pontiana, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400321.translation: Flavius Catullus, while alive and well, erected this statue of my daughter Catulla.
EDCS-39400321.summary: A funerary monument with a statue erected by Flavius Catullus for his daughter Catulla, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400322.translation: Andrys made this intricately crafted tomb for his wife Cyrilla, remembering her chaste and renowned modesty, for the sake of the bridal bed. He did this pious deed alone for the sake of her marriage. For tombs here cause the memory of those before to flourish for those after. Time has learned to destroy all things, but this preserves the glory of the living and the virtue of the dead.
EDCS-39400322.summary: A funerary monument built by Andrys for his wife Cyrilla, praising her chastity and piety, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400323.translation: Gaius Pontius Licinianus made this monument for Gaius Pontius Phoibianus and Gaius Pontius Marcianus, his brothers, for the sake of remembrance.
EDCS-39400323.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Gaius Pontius Licinianus to his brothers Gaius Pontius Phoibianus and Gaius Pontius Marcianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400324.translation: A memorial: nothing is of this world, but with myrrh all is circled. Indeed, I was eager to raise a child and bring him to hopes, but a judgment arrived before me. For, as judgment is, my friends, I paid the debt of a saying before I came to the measure of my age and reached manhood. A child, a tiny six-year-old, I was called Neos. For whom Bascianus, my father, and his wife Ianbaria, exceedingly tearful, did not righteously raise a tomb, mourning the innocent birth of their child. Rejoice, passerby, and be healthy to return to others.
EDCS-39400324.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a six-year-old boy named Neos, set up by his parents Bascianus and Ianbaria.

EDCS-39400328.translation: X I S two-stadia X K of Seon in all directions, Terentianus and the of Sai he least neglected, two-stadia NXK two-stadia XIS.
EDCS-39400328.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records distances or measurements related to roads or boundaries, possibly mentioning a person named Terentianus.

EDCS-39400329.translation: The linen-press was made for the sacred council by Hermippos Attas.
EDCS-39400329.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a linen-press to the city council by Hermippos Attas.

EDCS-39400392.summary: A simple funerary epitaph naming Nikephorus, son of Artemidoros.

EDCS-39400393.translation: But cruel Moira took the numberless youth. She extinguished the hopes of his parents. His father set up this stele in memory of his luckless fate. With tearful eyes, he will greet it.
EDCS-39400393.summary: This funerary epitaph laments the early death of a young man, commemorating his lost hopes and setting up a stele.

EDCS-39400395.translation: My name is Matrona, I lie here, wise.
EDCS-39400395.summary: A funerary epitaph for Matrona, who describes herself as noble and wise.

EDCS-39400396.translation: Metrodoros of Prousa, son of Gaios, from Hypios, a merchant, having lived virtuously for 109 years. Farewell.
EDCS-39400396.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Metrodoros, a merchant from Prousa, who lived to be 109 years old.

EDCS-39400397.translation: Pios, son of Seppos, a wine merchant from Alexandria.
EDCS-39400397.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Pios, a wine merchant from Alexandria.

EDCS-39400399.translation: Alexandros, son of Theodotos, dedicated this while alive and of sound mind, as a remembrance for his own daughter.
EDCS-39400399.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Alexandros, son of Theodotos, for his daughter while he was still alive.

EDCS-39400400.translation: Opos, Eudos, chief official; Gykeis, son of Kokkios; Theophilos, second; Dionysius, son of Metrodoros; from Diogenes, son of Askos; Tanes, son of Dionysius; Diogenes, son of Alexandros; aliou, chief official; Theodotos, son of Helios, chief official; Metrodoros, second, chief official; lis, son of Kalleas; ali, Dionysius, son of Timotheos, ali; Pontikos, son of Aistiaios, ali; Kasymedes, son of Demosthenes, chief official; ou, chief official; Kyronas, son of Kasios; k, Marcus, son of Nestor, chief official; ou, chief official; Emilys, son of Karpounnis; Antiochus, second, chief official; stas, son of Noumenios, chief official; Metrodoros, second, chief official; chief official; Chairemon, son of Dioskourides, ali; chief official; Eutyches, chief official; Julianus, son of Alexandros; s, son of Alexandros, chief official; Kouthias, son of Kallikrates; ou, Dionysius, son of Kallikrates, chief official; Alexandros, son of Mene; n, of Posidonios; Andros; Audokos; EPNASA; Alexandros; as, Valeris.
EDCS-39400400.summary: This inscription is a list of officials, likely from a city or administrative body, possibly related to legal or financial duties, including individuals with titles such as 'chief official' (ἔκδικος).

EDCS-39400402.translation: That Leon has been granted citizenship, proxeny, benefaction, and isoteleia, with freedom of entry and exit in peace and war, inviolability, and exemption from sacrifices. The pro Boulai are to crown him with a golden wreath during the Hellenistic Dionysia in the theater for his valor, and to announce that he is noble and good and zealous and well-disposed towards the city. The archons are to ensure that his shield is dedicated in the sanctuary of Herakles. The king is to invite him to the prytaneion and send him gifts from the common table. The treasurer is to pay the expenses.
EDCS-39400402.summary: This is a decree honoring Leon, granting him numerous privileges and honors for his services to the city, likely issued by a Greek city to a Roman benefactor, possibly in the late Hellenistic or early Roman imperial period.

EDCS-39400405.translation: Under King Euboulidas, in the month Artemisius, on the fourteenth day, Leontiskos son of Athanaios spoke: 'Since Heronax and Bakchios, sons of Kalliphanes from Mytilene, have always been well-disposed and zealous towards the city, and have provided assistance to arriving citizens both publicly and privately, it has been resolved by the council and the people to praise Heronax and Bakchios, sons of Kalliphanes from Mytilene, for these services, and to grant them and their descendants proxeny and citizenship.'
EDCS-39400405.summary: This is a decree passed by a Greek city council and people, praising and granting citizenship and proxeny to Heronax and Bakchios of Mytilene for their services to the city and its visitors, dating to the reign of King Euboulidas.

EDCS-39400410.translation: Under King Simos, Dionysios son of Potamon spoke: 'Since Philon son of Hekataios from Apollonia, being well-disposed and zealous, has always provided assistance to the city and its citizens, both publicly and privately to arriving citizens, with good fortune, it has been resolved by the council and the people.'
EDCS-39400410.summary: This is a decree from a Greek city, commending Philon of Apollonia for his consistent goodwill and assistance to the city and its citizens, likely passed during the reign of King Simos.

EDCS-39400412.translation: Iphiadas spoke: 'Since Pasiadas son of Herodotus from Chersonesos, being well-disposed and zealous, has always provided assistance to the city, both to the people generally and to individual citizens who approach him, it has been resolved by the council and the people to praise Pasiadas for these services, and to grant him and his descendants proxeny, citizenship, isoteleia, and freedom of entry and exit in peace and war, inviolability, and exemption from sacrifices.'
EDCS-39400412.summary: This decree honors Pasiadas of Chersonesos with praise and grants him and his descendants extensive privileges, including citizenship and proxeny, for his services to the city and its people.

EDCS-39400431.translation: To feast the people with meat. It has been resolved by the people to praise him for these things, and in every assembly, meeting, public feast, Caesarian games, festival, and high-priestly assembly, he is to be crowned while alive, and forever recognized as the generous euergete, the public benefactor, and the archiereutic official. Isagoras son of Iatrokles, who was the greatest of the people, is to be inscribed on a stele of white stone and dedicated in the most prominent place in the city. He is permitted to enter and act within the city. This decree was ratified in the high-priestly assembly on the third day. He also took care of the sanctuary of Artemis the pure goddess, covering all expenses and services, and rebuilt the front wall with... building.
EDCS-39400431.summary: This is a decree honoring Isagoras, son of Iatrokles, for his extensive benefactions to the city, particularly concerning religious festivals and the sanctuary of Artemis, and granting him numerous honors and privileges, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-39400437.translation: With good fortune, under King Simos son of Asklapiadas, in the month Dionysios, the praesimnes of Pythion. It was decided by the cult members that a temple be constructed for the god. Those cult members wishing to participate in the construction are to announce what they are willing to contribute. For those contributing up to a gold stater, there will be a lifelong wreath of honor and an inscription on a stele. For those contributing less than a gold stater, up to thirty silver drachmas, there will be recognition and a wreath of gratitude annually for life. For those contributing less than that, there will be an inscription of their pledge on the stele. In order that the temple may be constructed as beautifully and quickly as possible, three men are to be chosen from all the cult members. These chosen men, after receiving funds from those who pledged, will manage the affairs and provide a written account of their management. When the work is completed, the chosen men will also receive a wreath at the triennial gatherings of the cult members. With good fortune, the following have pledged for the construction of the temple: Apollonymos son of Satyrus (a gold stater), Apollonios son of Apollonios, Philippos son of Apollonios, Dionysios son of Kalkadon (will build the temple), Meniskos son of Herakles (a gold stater), Damatrios son of Damatrios (a gold stater), Simos son of Promathion (a gold stater), Krathinos son of Mikos (a gold stater), Nautimos son of Pasiadas (a gold stater), Zopyros son of Protopolios (a gold stater), Harmagenes son of Damophon (a gold stater), Kritoboulos son of Pyrrhos (a gold stater), Asklapiodoros son of Apollodos (a gold stater), Nossion son of Hierokles (a gold stater), Zopyros son of Hestios (a gold stater). Damosethenes son of Dionysios (for the altar area, a hollow and hinges). Menis son of Hiksesios (thirty silver drachmas). Sosibios son of Protomachos (thirty silver drachmas). Hereas son of Damophon (thirty silver drachmas). Euphraios son of Satyrus (thirty silver drachmas). [---] (thirty silver drachmas). [---] (thirty silver drachmas). Apollodotos (thirty laborers). Promathion son of Promathion (fifteen laborers). Hagemon son of Pythion (a horse stall and fifteen laborers). Olympos son of Sotirichos (fifteen laborers). Dion son of Aristokles (fifteen laborers). Hephaistion son of Skytha (fifteen laborers). Dionysios (ten laborers). Apollonios son of Simos (ten laborers).
EDCS-39400437.summary: This is a decree from a religious association (thiasos) detailing plans and financial contributions for the construction of a new temple, including honors for donors and the appointed managers, dated to the reign of King Simos.

EDCS-39400438.translation: It was resolved by the cult members: Since Bikon son of Dioskouridas, being fond of glory and honor, has made contributions from his own funds and has been entrusted with the common funds, and has lent them profitably to the thiasos, he has repaid the money along with the interest, even though he lost the thiasos's funds due to the circumstances, and has been released from his legal obligation. Therefore, so that the cult members may also show worthy honors to those who are generous towards them, it has been resolved by the cult members.
EDCS-39400438.summary: This is a resolution by a religious association (thiasos) to honor Bikon son of Dioskouridas for his financial contributions, prudent management, and repayment of loans, despite personal losses.

EDCS-39400440.translation: Under King Agathos Daimon, in the month Malophorios, the importers around Herakles son of Androsthenes countered the registration of the importers under King Heraion son of Hiksesios [---].
EDCS-39400440.summary: This is a brief, partially preserved record concerning the registration of importers, possibly a dispute or administrative action, during the reign of King Agathos Daimon and King Heraion.

EDCS-39400441.translation: From Morphios: NISSE IERIAULI NDIE SETI thirty drachmas from the branches, of the sacred... of the shipowners and for the transaction of the importers Apollonian, he was in charge of the legate in the A... of Demalkos son of Morphios son of M. And in the court Apollon... of the merchants' goods due to the scarcity... of the penalties.
EDCS-39400441.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmented financial or legal record related to trade, importers, and potentially penalties, mentioning specific individuals and amounts of money.

EDCS-39400442.translation: It was resolved by the Thoinaitai of Demeter Chthonia: Noumenios son of Noumenios spoke: 'Since Ariston son of Ariston, being a benefactor of the people, has undertaken for the city and the Thoinaitai.'
EDCS-39400442.summary: This is a decree by the Thoinaitai of Demeter Chthonia, honoring Ariston son of Ariston for his benefactions to the city and the association.

EDCS-39400445.translation: Having constructed a long ship for the harbor at his own expense, he kept both the harbor and the shores safe from war and donated the ship to the city, always showing himself to be generous and eager to bring good things to the members of the association, so that the association might also appear to honor good men with worthy rewards, it has been decreed by the members of the association to crown him.
EDCS-39400445.summary: A decree by a Dionysiac association honoring a member for his generosity in maintaining the harbor and donating a ship, likely dating to the Hellenistic period.

EDCS-39400446.translation: Under King Cotys, son of Rhoimetalkes, in the month Dionysius, during the triennial festival, it was resolved by the members of the association: Diodorus, son of Damatras, spoke. Since Ariston, son of Ariston, is a benefactor and founder of the city and generous to our association, and he continues to increase the virtues of his father, and shows the same disposition towards the people at all times and in danger, saving and benefiting the citizens, and always promising to be the cause of some good for our association, showing himself well-disposed and increasing the honors of Dionysus, even though he is very young, he shows his ambition to benefit the citizens more than those who were quicker to be chosen to benefit him. Therefore, so that the members of the association also appear to maintain their goodwill towards him and to remember their genuine and prompt kindness towards them, they have granted to his father the greatest honors among the other benefactors. Now, since he himself has proclaimed the awarding of the crown, because he is honored by the people with the same honors as his father, it has been decreed by the members of the association to crown Ariston, son of Ariston, the benefactor of the people and generous to the association, at every meeting and on every day on which they gather. The crown is to be granted for eternal remembrance of his benefaction and virtue towards the people, and for his goodwill and generosity towards the members of the association. The members of the association shall inscribe this decree on a white limestone plinth and dedicate it in the most prominent place of the recess until the month Lykeios, during the foreign Dionysia.
EDCS-39400446.summary: A decree by a Dionysiac association honoring Ariston, son of Ariston, for his benefactions to the city and the association, mirroring the honors given to his father, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period under King Cotys.

EDCS-39400447.translation: It was resolved by the members of the association around Philon, son of Dioskourides, of the Bacchic association: the priest Philon spoke. Since Ariston, son of Ariston, is a benefactor, and has become founder of the city for a second time, and is generous to our association, he himself also appears to have the same disposition, following his father's ambition, saving the city in all circumstances from the events befalling it, and the citizens...
EDCS-39400447.summary: A fragment of a decree by a Bacchic association honoring Ariston, son of Ariston, for his benefactions to the city and the association, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400448.translation: For Phileinus, with good fortune. Under King Tiberius Caesar, on the twenty-eighth day of the month [---]. Since Phileinus, son of Phileinus, undertook for the members of the association to build the portico of the courtyard and to surround the courtyard [---] gave denarii, and completed the doors for the entrance, he shall have the inscription of the portico and a crown for eternal remembrance. It has been decreed by the members of the association to inscribe this decree on a white limestone plinth and dedicate it in the sanctuary of Dionysus. He himself shall undertake the remaining expenses. Having received all the doors from the members of the association, he shall install them in the portico.
EDCS-39400448.summary: A decree by a Dionysiac association granting honors, including an inscription and a crown, to Phileinus, son of Phileinus, for his construction work on a portico and courtyard, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period under Tiberius.

EDCS-39400463.translation: To Heracles the Defender, from the members of the feast, including the priest Heracleon, son of Pyrrhos, a thank-offering.
EDCS-39400463.summary: A thank-offering to the epithet Heracles Alexikakos (the Defender) from a group identified as 'members of the feast', likely a religious association.

EDCS-39400467.translation: With good fortune. To Heracles Pharangites, a thank-offering from the association of the Heracleopolitans, including the secretary Satyrus, son of Satyrus, Alexander, son of Heracleon, Philoumenos, son of Menius, Androneikos, son of Satyrus, Antonius, son of Makedon, Heracles, son of Dionysius, Alexander, son of Alexander, Marcus, son of Lucius Niger, Titus, Terentius, Philon, son of Fronton, Thrasymedes, son of Satyrus, Heracles, son of Chrysogonos, Helios, son of Helios, Flavius, son of Heracles, Gaius, son of Agenor, Helios, son of Cerealis, Julianus, son of Titus, Alexander, son of Titus, Quintus, son of Sinnius, Demetrius, son of Lucius, Cerealis, son of Helios, Artemidorus, son of Dionysius, Artemidorus.
EDCS-39400467.summary: A thank-offering inscription to the epithet Heracles Pharangites from a group of Heracleopolitans, listing many members and officials, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39400470.translation: With good fortune, for the city. The magistrates including Flavius Pharon, pontarch, king, and high priest.
EDCS-39400470.summary: An inscription invoking good fortune for the city, mentioning officials including a pontarch, king, and high priest, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period in a syncretic religious context.

EDCS-39400471.translation: To Athena Polias, the Majestic, the Defender.
EDCS-39400471.summary: A dedication to the epithet Athena Polias Megistos Epikydes, likely a local cultic epithet.

EDCS-39400472.translation: To Toko, son of Eraton, a vow.
EDCS-39400472.summary: A votive offering to a deity named Toko, son of Eraton.

EDCS-39400473.translation: For Cathara, daughter of Dionysius, who served as priestess to Artemis.
EDCS-39400473.summary: An inscription commemorating Cathara, daughter of Dionysius, who served as a priestess of Artemis.

EDCS-39400478.translation: Julius Carpus and Claudia Ploteina erected these altars as a thank-offering.
EDCS-39400478.summary: A private dedication of altars by Julius Carpus and Claudia Ploteina.

EDCS-39400485.translation: To the Strong God, with good fortune. Marcus Aurelius Moschion renewed [it].
EDCS-39400485.summary: A renewal inscription by Marcus Aurelius Moschion, dedicated to the Strong God.

EDCS-39400487.translation: With good fortune. I, Aurelia Chrysea, priestess of Dionysus, have completed the work for the goddess from my own resources.
EDCS-39400487.summary: A private dedication by a priestess, Aurelia Chrysea, to a goddess, possibly associated with Dionysus.

EDCS-39400490.translation: With good fortune, to all the gods, to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Armeniaicus, Parthicus, Medicus, Germanicus, Augustus, and his sons. While Valerius Brodoua, the legate and proconsul of the August, was governing the province, Titus Aelius Minius Athanaios, first pontarch, high priest, and archon of the Hexapolis, and his son Titus Aelius Minius Moschion, pontarch, rebuilt the seven towers and the walls around them from the foundation, and they supported and repaired them, and the gates and...
EDCS-39400490.summary: A major public works inscription recording the rebuilding and repair of fortifications by local officials under the reign of Marcus Aurelius, likely in the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-39400499.translation: The council and the people [honored] Basileidas, son of Basileidas, for his virtue and goodwill towards the city.
EDCS-39400499.summary: A civic honorific inscription from a Greek city, recognizing Basileidas for his virtue and loyalty.

EDCS-39400508.translation: With good fortune, the council and people [honored] Publius Flavius of the Kallatians.
EDCS-39400508.summary: A civic honorific inscription by the council and people of Kallatis, honoring Publius Flavius.

EDCS-39400516.translation: Mekius, Vicon, Aige Sillis, Pas, Ariston, from Ageidas, from Eucampidas, Elasippidas, Apollophanes, Apollocles Nisaeus, son of Matris, son of Epaischinas.
EDCS-39400516.summary: A list of names, possibly recording individuals associated with a tribal or local group, with patronymic and tribal affiliations.

EDCS-39400517.translation: Hierôn, son of Menalkes, ...ratos, son of Gorguthos, ...son, son of Kerkines, ...ateas, son of Eukles, Herakleidas, son of Hierôn, Kerkines, son of Eucheia, Satyros, son of Matrios, ...oitas, son of Histies.
EDCS-39400517.summary: A list of names, likely genealogies or membership in a group, with patronymics.

EDCS-39400522.translation: Rejoice, Nicia, daughter of the renowned Herakleida, nymph of great Nikasoi. In return for the chastity which your husband still cherishes, and because no other wife came to his bedchamber...
EDCS-39400522.summary: A tombstone inscription for Nicia, praising her chastity and fidelity to her husband.

EDCS-39400524.translation: Let no one pass by this tomb of the youth without tears, but rather, considering this fate, lament the wise Nikomedeas, son of Theophrons, because he reached the end of his life at the age of six.
EDCS-39400524.summary: A tombstone inscription lamenting the death of a six-year-old boy named Nikomedeas, son of Theophrons.

EDCS-39400525.translation: Asclepius, hail! You protected the pains of childbirth for her who hoped to have children, freeing her from the necessities of life. Fate allotted her six years; she lies here wretchedly, I suspect, from difficult childbirth, O deity.
EDCS-39400525.summary: This is a funerary epigram, likely a tombstone inscription, lamenting the death of a young girl who died at six years old, possibly during or shortly after childbirth.

EDCS-39400526.translation: One tomb covers two, hiding them in a single night. The Fates spun the thread of life from the same womb. Fate sent one to be childless, the other, still young, to leave his parents with unending grief. My name is Apollonius, son of [---]. The other is in memory of Menekrates, my grandfather. Both have been extinguished, and we are proclaimed with wreaths from our ancestors, because we protect our fatherland. Hail to you, Menekrates and Apollonius, sons of Apollonius!
EDCS-39400526.summary: This is a funerary epigram for two individuals named Apollonius and Menekrates, father and son, and grandfather. It is a tomb inscription from an unknown date, possibly Roman Imperial period, given the mention of ancestors and fatherland.

EDCS-39400529.translation: Dionysius, son of Poseidonius, behold me, a childless man, stranger, behold this rock. I lie on a cliff, because Dionysius died of a foreign disease. The remains from his ashes came to his parents. His mother at home always weeps anew, longing for her perished son.
EDCS-39400529.summary: This is a funerary epigram by Dionysius, son of Poseidonius, mourning his own death from a foreign disease and his parents' grief. It is a tomb inscription of unknown date.

EDCS-39400533.translation: Dikaiophron erected this stele for Amyntiane, in memory of his wife, he carved these in gratitude.
EDCS-39400533.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription from an unknown date, where Dikaiophron dedicates a stele to his wife Amyntiane.

EDCS-39400539.translation: Nothing is under mortals; all things revolve under the Fates. For I too hastened to raise a child and lead her to hopes, but judgment overtook my plan. Before I died, she was hurried to the marriage bed. She died sixteen years old and was called Euphronis. He, Imerios, her father, did not rightly erect a tomb in memory, along with his wife Mousike, who weeps most bitterly, mourning their child's innocent demise. Hail, passerby, and walk on healthy again!
EDCS-39400539.summary: This is a funerary epigram by Imerios, mourning the premature death of his sixteen-year-old daughter Euphronis, and his wife Mousike's grief. It is a tomb inscription from an unknown date.

EDCS-39400540.summary: This is a simple name inscription, likely from a tomb or dedication, from an unknown date.

EDCS-39400544.translation: Aba, wife of Timokles, of Doros.
EDCS-39400544.summary: This is a funerary epitaph naming Aba, wife of Timokles, from an unknown date and location.

EDCS-39400545.summary: This is a simple name inscription, possibly a tomb or dedication, from an unknown date.

EDCS-39400546.translation: Theon, son of Potamon, of Alexandria.
EDCS-39400546.summary: This is a name and origin inscription, likely funerary or a public record, from an unknown date.

EDCS-39400548.summary: This is a patronymic inscription, likely funerary or a dedication, from an unknown date.

EDCS-39400549.translation: Satyrís, daughter of Saturas, wife of Athanaion.
EDCS-39400549.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a woman named Satyrís, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-39400550.translation: Tusa, daughter of Antiphios, wife of Lykinos.
EDCS-39400550.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a woman named Tusa, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-39400551.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a woman named Chelona, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-39400552.translation: Saturos, son of Bacchus; Eisagoras, son of Olympios; Mênis, son of Saturos; Damatrios, son of Krethes; Phaïkon, son of Kleomedees; Apollodoros, son of Dionysios; Aisias, son of Epistratos.
EDCS-39400552.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly as a group epitaph or a record of citizens.

EDCS-39400553.summary: This inscription records the name Apollonios, son of Lampios, possibly a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-39400554.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Eupolemos, son of Herodoros, a common epithet implying good character.

EDCS-39400556.summary: This inscription records the name Apollonios, son of Epistratos, possibly a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-39400557.translation: Ariston of Olbia, son of Sosipolis.
EDCS-39400557.summary: This inscription records Ariston, a citizen of Olbia, son of Sosipolis, possibly for a public honor or private dedication.

EDCS-39400558.summary: This inscription records the name Herakleon, son of Nikon, possibly a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-39400562.summary: This inscription records the name Theopompos, son of Artemidoros, possibly a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-39400563.translation: Kotytis, wife of Thearidas son of Dionysius.
EDCS-39400563.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying Kotytis by her husband and father.

EDCS-39400565.translation: Tiberius Plautius Silvanus, son of the city, aged 22. Greetings.
EDCS-39400565.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Plautius Silvanus, identifying him by his age and civic status.

EDCS-39400566.translation: Zeiza, daughter of Seilanus, aged [---]. Greetings.
EDCS-39400566.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Zeiza, identifying her by her father and age.

EDCS-39400568.translation: Evandros Phrontonos of Heracleia, aged 31. Greetings.
EDCS-39400568.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Evandros Phrontonos, identifying him by his place of origin and age.

EDCS-39400571.translation: Apphia Julia, while alive, erected this stele for her husband and children as a memorial. Greetings, passerby.
EDCS-39400571.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Apphia Julia for her family.

EDCS-39400572.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Dioskourides son of Maximos, who lived 30 years; Heliosdoros son of Artemidoros; and Polydeukes son of Maximos, on...
EDCS-39400572.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Chthonic Gods by Dioskourides and Polydeukes, mentioning their ages and fathers.

EDCS-39400573.translation: Gaius Philodespotos, who lived sixty years, made this for myself while alive and of sound mind. Greetings, passerby. Do not do to another what you hate.
EDCS-39400573.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument erected by Gaius Philodespotos with a moral admonition.

EDCS-39400574.summary: This inscription is a simple name, likely a funerary marker or a dedication.

EDCS-39400576.translation: Isagorus, aged 5. Herakleon, son of Isagorus, aged five.
EDCS-39400576.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for two individuals, Isagorus and Herakleon, indicating their ages and familial relationship.

EDCS-39400577.translation: Aurelius Hesychis, stone-worker. Lived three years [possibly a misreading or unusual abbreviation]. His wife Basilikè, daughter of Dion, having lived together 9 years, left behind...
EDCS-39400577.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelius Hesychis, a stone-worker, and his wife Basilikè, mentioning their lifespans and familial relationships.

EDCS-39400579.translation: Philippos, son of Stephanos, lived a good life for 63 years. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-39400579.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Philippos, who lived 63 years, dated to the Roman period.

EDCS-39400584.translation: Farewell, friend Dorion. Here I lie, Charkes of Crete, factory worker of the Kalatians. Farewell, travelers.
EDCS-39400584.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Charkes, a factory worker from Crete, dated to the Roman period.

EDCS-39400601.translation: ABC... XYZ. Zeus, O T S.
EDCS-39400601.summary: This inscription consists of the Greek alphabet followed by the letters OTS and 'Zeus', its purpose is unclear, possibly a practice text or a graffito.

EDCS-39400613.translation: Of Zeus the Savior, of Badiskos.
EDCS-39400613.summary: This inscription is a dedication or dedicatory fragment, possibly to Zeus Soter, associated with Badiskos.

EDCS-39400614.translation: I, Naukasamai, and my fellow diners of Timonax, farewell to all who set me up for Naukasamai.
EDCS-39400614.summary: This is a dedication likely from a dining association or related to a communal meal, honoring Naukasamai.

EDCS-39400629.translation: In the time of Baton, Flavius Agathenor made...
EDCS-39400629.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning the consuls Baton and Flavius Agathenor, likely recording a public work or dedication.

EDCS-39400630.translation: To the Hero Dosamenos, the responsive one, Rufus, son of Rufus, dedicated in gratitude for his own [affairs].
EDCS-39400630.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Rufus to the hero Dosamenos for answered prayers.

EDCS-39400631.summary: This is a simple funerary or dedicatory inscription identifying Fuscina, daughter of Meliskos.

EDCS-39400632.translation: Aurelia Flavia. Marcus, priest, having been captured by barbarians, having returned safely...
EDCS-39400632.summary: This inscription records the experience of Marcus, a priest, who was captured by barbarians and returned safely.

EDCS-39400633.translation: ...and to my wife Sabeina, daughter of Peius, and to my children Lucilla and Basia and Procellina and Aquila and Victorinus, for the sake of honor.
EDCS-39400633.summary: This is a funerary inscription listing family members, likely a dedication of a tomb or monument.

EDCS-46700101.translation: Aeschrion, son of Menis, son of Artemidorus, son of Calpurnius, son of Herophilos, son of Saturnion, son of Asclepiades Ponticus; Heli, son of Maior Valerius; Acanthus. Artemidorus.
EDCS-46700101.summary: This inscription lists a series of names, possibly forming a complex genealogy or a list of individuals associated with a monument or event.

EDCS-48001033.translation: Oescius, prefect of the Fifth Macedonian Legion.
EDCS-48001033.summary: This inscription records the name and military position of Oescius, prefect of the Fifth Macedonian Legion.

EDCS-49000001.translation: son of Neikios, the lover of hunting, lived 52 years and [---] and son-in-law, a beneficianus. His wife [---]
EDCS-49000001.summary: This inscription is a funerary text mentioning the deceased's age, relationship to others, and a military or administrative role (beneficiarius).

EDCS-49000064.translation: To the Holy God Hercules, Valerius Victorinus, a beneficiarius of the tribune of the First Italian Gordian Legion, erected this in fulfillment of a vow, when Aviola was consul.
EDCS-49000064.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules by Valerius Victorinus, a beneficiarius of a legionary tribune, dating to the consulship of Aviola.

EDCS-49000070.translation: pinus, beneficiarius of a consular legate, willingly fulfilled his vow as deserved.
EDCS-49000070.summary: This is a votive inscription by a beneficiarius of a consular legate, expressing fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-49000071.translation: To the Holy God Nebulo Poti Reducto, Vivius Maximus, beneficiarius of a consular legate, willingly erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-49000071.summary: This is a votive inscription by Vivius Maximus, a beneficiarius of a consular legate, dedicated to the god Nebulo Poti Reducto.

EDCS-51000072.translation: To good fortune, Helios Mithras, the responsive god. Aurelius Marcus, a fuller, made this stele with its painting at his own expense as a thank-offering.
EDCS-51000072.summary: This is a thank-offering inscription by Aurelius Marcus, a fuller, for the gods Helios Mithras, mentioning the creation of a stele with a painting.

EDCS-51700934.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Vivianus, biarchus, who lived 75 years.
EDCS-51700934.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valerius Vivianus, a biarchus, recording his age and honoring the Spirits of the Departed.

EDCS-52100018.translation: Asclepiades, son of Apellas, son of Demetrius, chief physician and beloved of the people, and priest of the Great God, and gymnasiarch, and the bravest, and his wife Annie, daughter of Nicomedes, rejoice!
EDCS-52100018.summary: This is a greeting inscribed by Asclepiades, a prominent local official and physician, and his wife Annie, listing his numerous titles and public roles.

EDCS-55200001.translation: Uricieius Martialis took care of the setting up.
EDCS-55200001.summary: This inscription records that Uricieius Martialis arranged for a monument to be erected.

EDCS-55300341.summary: This is a label for the Aelian Estate.

EDCS-55300342.summary: This is a label for the Aelian Estate.

EDCS-55300344.translation: The Annian Estate of the Emperors
EDCS-55300344.summary: This is a label for the Annian Estate of the Emperors.

EDCS-55300345.translation: The Annian Estate of the Emperors
EDCS-55300345.summary: This is a label for the Annian Estate of the Emperors.

EDCS-55701578.translation: Claudius Acon, his father, made this for himself while alive, for his most dutiful son, wishing you light earth
EDCS-55701578.summary: This funerary inscription records a father's dedication for his deceased son.

EDCS-55701582.translation: Octavius Probus willingly and deservedly [fulfilled his] vow to the Unconquered Mithras
EDCS-55701582.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mithras by Octavius Probus.

EDCS-55701584.translation: Lucianus [fulfilled his vow] to the Unconquered God Mithras, according to his vow
EDCS-55701584.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mithras by Lucianus.

EDCS-55701588.translation: To Good Fortune, a thank-offering to the Mother of the Gods, Souris
EDCS-55701588.summary: This is a Greek votive inscription dedicated to the Mother of the Gods and Good Fortune.

EDCS-58000074.summary: This is likely an inscription related to Emperor Anastasius, possibly identifying a building or monument.

EDCS-58800108.translation: He took care to have this made for his deserving mother
EDCS-58800108.summary: This inscription records someone commissioning a monument for their mother.

EDCS-59400147.summary: This is a personal name inscription, possibly a dedication or a label, with the name Agrippina repeated three times.

EDCS-59400150.translation: Son of Marcus, from the Treviri.
EDCS-59400150.summary: This inscription identifies an individual as the son of Marcus and belonging to the Treviri tribal group, likely a funerary or identity marker.

EDCS-59400151.translation: Aurelius Flavius Aquila, priest, who lived for [years].
EDCS-59400151.summary: This inscription records the name and priestly office of Aurelius Flavius Aquila, noting his lifespan.

EDCS-59400167.translation: Ater had the dutifully made thing set up.
EDCS-59400167.summary: This inscription states that a person named Ater commissioned the creation of a monument or object.

EDCS-59400214.translation: Philippus and Publius Varius, also [from the] Ubii.
EDCS-59400214.summary: This inscription names Philippus and Publius Varius and identifies them as belonging to the Ubii tribe, likely on a monument.

EDCS-59400220.translation: [They] set up this monument for Opan and [their] father.
EDCS-59400220.summary: This inscription records that individuals erected a monument dedicated to Opan and their father.

EDCS-59600026.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Aurelius Licinianus, of the ducenarian rank, lived 85 years, and his wife Sura lived 55 years. Aurelius Marcus, their son, along with his daughters Marcellina and Marcia, erected this for those well-deserving.
EDCS-59600026.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aurelius Licinianus, his wife Sura, and their daughters, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-59600027.translation: To Apollo, Marcus Ulpius Victor, a veteran, erected this.
EDCS-59600027.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Apollo by Marcus Ulpius Victor, a military veteran.

EDCS-59600028.translation: To the Holy God Apollo, Caius Valerius Crescens, a soldier of the eleventh Claudian legion, willingly and deservedly paid his vow for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-59600028.summary: This is a votive inscription where a soldier of the XI Claudia legion dedicates a fulfillment of a vow to Apollo for the well-being of himself and his family.

EDCS-59600032.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, Flavius Valens, an adjutant of the legate, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-59600032.summary: This inscription records a soldier named Flavius Valens fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Dolichenus, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59600033.summary: A cornicularius (an officer in the Roman army) set up this dedication, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-59600046.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. A veteran signaler of the Fifth Macedonian Legion served for years; he lived for 60 years. His wife, who lived with him, saw to it that this was made for him and for herself, a deserving spouse.
EDCS-59600046.summary: A funerary epitaph for a veteran signaler of Legio V Macedonica, detailing his service and lifespan, and commissioned by his wife.

EDCS-59700171.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ius IV.
EDCS-59700171.summary: A funerary dedication, possibly to the 'Spirits of the Departed,' with a possibly abbreviated or symbolic numerical designation.

EDCS-59800022.summary: A dedication to Emperor Caesar Augustus, likely on a public monument or building.

EDCS-59800023.translation: By the grace of God. Greetings, traveler, rest here. She lived for fifty-one years. May God give her rest with the righteous. Kalliope Souliphera. Amen. I, Entolios, son of Caesareus, born from the chosen of God, built this eternal house.
EDCS-59800023.summary: A funerary inscription with Greek text for Kalliope Souliphera, who lived 51 years, with a dedication for the construction of her 'eternal house' by Entolios.

EDCS-59800032.translation: To Good Fortune. Anthos dedicated this in thanks to the great Lord God Serapis, and Isis of myriad names, and Anubis, and the attendant gods who hear prayers.
EDCS-59800032.summary: A dedication of thanks by Anthos to the gods Serapis, Isis, Anubis, and other associated deities.

EDCS-59800111.translation: To Hygieia, from a vision. Titus Aelius.
EDCS-59800111.summary: A dedication to the goddess Hygieia by Titus Aelius, prompted by a vision.

EDCS-59800113.translation: Quintus, son of Silumi, lived for 55 years. His sons set this up for their most dutiful father, who deserved well. He ordered this to be made while alive. [He] lived for 20 years. CVIN lived for...
EDCS-59800113.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus, son of Silumi, who lived 55 years, commissioned by his sons, with additional, possibly incomplete, information about another person named CVIN.

EDCS-59800114.translation: Fortune envied Blando. It has torn us apart by such a pact. We are grieving three small children, pledges born to us, who might honor your memory. It is not permitted to us to pour forth for the Manes from our hearts, whom it was not allowed to complete their ancestral years, but who were snatched away by a false, unworthy death. Farewell, my most dutiful husband. Ursine always. Victorina possesses the air of the shadows here. Live happily, you for whom fortune is blessed.
EDCS-59800114.summary: A poignant epitaph for Blando and his wife Victorina, mourning the premature death of their young children and lamenting their own fate.

EDCS-59800115.translation: Here I lie, Victorina by name, consigned to the underworld realms by fate's lot. I inhabited the brief light of life, a guest of noble and free lineage. I completed ten and five times ten years of life, nor was it permitted to me beyond what the fates granted. Remember me. b
EDCS-59800115.summary: A funerary epitaph for Victorina, detailing her lineage, lifespan (possibly 60 years), and her acceptance of her fate.

EDCS-59800117.translation: For his own health and that of his son, he dedicated this to the Hero.
EDCS-59800117.summary: This is a dedication to a hero, likely for the health of the dedicator and his son.

EDCS-60400091.translation: Sextus S., soldier of the legion P.
EDCS-60400091.summary: This is a soldier's identification, noting his name, rank, and legion.

EDCS-60400096.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to an unconquered deity.

EDCS-60700013.translation: Titolos Julius, a standard-bearer, set this up where my dear wife, Bonosa, and Titus will rest, so that this monument may be counted for her memory, because when I was in my [XX]th year and ...
EDCS-60700013.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription set up by Julius to honor his wife Bonosa and Titus, indicating his own age and likely continuing the inscription.

EDCS-60900016.summary: This inscription records the title of Augustus as chief priest.

EDCS-61000002.translation: The successors, brothers Timocles, Dionysius, and Democrates, sons of Dionysius, dedicated this as a thank-offering to the Hero Irsaios, for themselves.
EDCS-61000002.summary: This is a thank-offering dedicated to the Hero Irsaios by the sons of Dionysius, who are identified as successors and brothers.

EDCS-61000015.translation: God and Saint George, preserve your servant George, son of Marinus.
EDCS-61000015.summary: This is a prayer for protection addressed to God and Saint George for the servant George.

EDCS-61000047.translation: To the Goddess Minerva, mother of the Muses, Julius Iucundus, a sacred offering.
EDCS-61000047.summary: This is a sacred offering dedicated to the goddess Minerva by Julius Iucundus.

EDCS-62500007.translation: To the departed spirits, Ulpius Martialis and his son Ulpius Optatus, and Ulpius Longinius, set this up for their well-deserving father and brother.
EDCS-62500007.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Ulpius Martialis and his son for their father and brother.

EDCS-62500009.summary: This is the identification of an individual named Quintus Terentius Terentianus.

EDCS-62500011.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Titus Aelius Apollonius, his father, a former duplicarius, and Aurelia Restuta, his mother, parents, and for Aelia Apollonia, his sister, and Varius, his brother, and Aelius Martialis, his son, and Antonia, his daughter, and Vitalio, his son, and Apollodorus and Restuta, his devoted children, Aelius Apollinaris, and Antonia Sabina, and Aelius Sabinus, and Herculanus, have set this up for their well-deserving relatives.
EDCS-62500011.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Aelius Apollinaris and others for their deceased family members, including parents, siblings, and children.

EDCS-62500013.translation: For his own health and that of his son Heliodorus, aged 17.
EDCS-62500013.summary: This inscription records a vow or dedication for the health of the dedicator and his son, mentioning his son's age.

EDCS-62600942.translation: To Tiberius Victor, son of Tiberius, a beneficiarius consularis, lived 20 years.
EDCS-62600942.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name, title, and age at death of Tiberius Victor, who held a military administrative position.

EDCS-64100191.translation: Under good fortune, under the priest Laverius Maximus, priests of Dionysus Karpophoros: Gaucus son of Zoilus, Dionysius son of Satyrianos, Ninna son of Dionysius, Menecharmos son of Apollonides.
EDCS-64100191.summary: This inscription lists priests of Dionysus Karpophoros, likely associated with a cult or festival, and mentions a priest named Laverius Maximus.

EDCS-64100192.translation: Leaving behind the renowned city of Einachian land, I came to this sacred ground, a child of the city of Tomoi. Not slow to excel nor uninitiated in gaining honors, but indeed I bore garlands upon my temples, which the Muses, goddesses, bestowed upon me throughout all the land. Proclaiming beautifully the noble verse, and singing beautifully, the city, a hospitable stranger, held me here. It gave me a dear wife, it gave me offspring, and I completed my life as long as the Fates spun the threads. And I have a tomb in my own lovely place, in a flowering place where Bacchus's lovely vines grow. I came to Hades, leaving my home for my children.
