So that no citizen, after my death, speaks ill of me. Now, most excellent passerby, you must learn my name: I am Euelpistus, son of Sousos, and all called me a Byzantine.
EDCS-64100192.summary: This is a funerary epigram of Euelpistus, a Byzantine, who recounts his life in the city of Tomoi, his artistic talents, family, and peaceful death.

EDCS-64100193.translation: Flavius Aelis A., Flavius Macedon, Philon, Artemas, Kalleas son of Doryphoros, Apreilis M.
EDCS-64100193.summary: This appears to be a list of names, possibly related to a group, association, or a record of individuals, possibly from Roman Asia Minor.

EDCS-64100194.translation: Under good fortune, to the ancestral gods, Sedatius Apollonius.
EDCS-64100194.summary: This is a dedication to the ancestral gods by Sedatius Apollonius, likely for good fortune.

EDCS-65100074.translation: Aurelius Maximus, to the Genius and Hercules. Aurelius Maximus, to the Genius and Hercules.
EDCS-65100074.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius Maximus to his Genius (guardian spirit) and to Hercules, possibly in a personal capacity or as part of a cult.

EDCS-65100075.translation: Valerius Valerinus Constans, brother, offers Secundus to Hermes. ... Valerius Valerinus, of the First Jovian Legion, erected this monument for Valeria Diocletiana, an ossuary. He will distribute it among the heirs...
EDCS-65100075.summary: This inscription records the erection of a tomb monument by Valerius Valerinus for Valeria Diocletiana, and mentions his military affiliation and the distribution of the inheritance.

EDCS-65100080.translation: Aurelius Bassus, Gaius. Aurelius Gaius, Proclus. Aurelius Gaius.
EDCS-65100080.summary: This is a list of three names, all beginning with 'Aurelius Gaius', possibly indicating family members or members of a collegium.

EDCS-65600128.translation: To the gods above and to the illustrious city of Novesium and the first Italic legion, giving thanks. For the sake of memory, and for this, we have erected this most excellent statue of a soldier in full armour. Flavius Bassus, primipilaris, citizen of the colony of Alexandria, and Flavius [---], primipilaris, citizen of [---], worshipping the heavenly god, praying to the one who saved us. [---] near the Hellespont.
EDCS-65600128.summary: This inscription, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, records the erection of a statue by two primipilares of the Legio I Italica and Legio II Flavia Constantia, dedicated to the city of Novesium and the legion, expressing gratitude and piety.

EDCS-67100102.translation: To my lord Trifolius, greetings. If you are well, may your foot always tread upon your slaves. Cuna, the old servant, farewell.
EDCS-67100102.summary: This is a personal note from an owner to a slave named Trifolius, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period, wishing him well and asserting ownership.

EDCS-67200416.translation: Pautalos, son of Cornutus, priest of Bacchus of the Asiana, erected this column at his own expense.
EDCS-67200416.summary: This inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records the erection of a column by Pautalos, a priest of Bacchus, at his own expense.

EDCS-67200417.translation: Claudius Rufus to the Hero Suregethes.
EDCS-67200417.summary: A simple dedication of an offering by Claudius Rufus to the hero cult of Suregethes, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67200420.translation: To the chthonic gods. Valeria Firmina [---] a gift [---].
EDCS-67200420.summary: This inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, is a dedication to the chthonic gods by Valeria Firmina, possibly related to a funerary context.

EDCS-67200497.translation: Marcus Ulpius Valerianus Pistos, son of Glykon, Gaius Valerius Proclus, son of Germanus, Romanos, Quintus [---], son of Menekrates, Kallikles [---]. Having conquered, under the agonothete [---], with Marcus Ulpius Artemidorus, first pontarch and son of the city, and Marcus Ulpius Kallistratos, Marcus Ulpius Sabinianus, Marcus Ulpius Praisentus, Marcus Ulpius Germanicus, Titus Cominius Eugenides the younger, pontarch, Marcus Ulpius Demetrius, Diogenes, Karpos, Artemidorus, Karpos, Kalpurnius Karpos, Herodoros Karpos, Kallistratos Karpos, Aelius Artemidorus, Dioskourides, Publius Aelius Phaidrios, Karpos Apollodoros, Dionysius, Moirodoros, Chairemon, Papius Pollion, Memnon, Theocles, while Publius Aelius Hekataios was high priest and Marcus Aelius Valerianus was mesochoros, erected this sacred competition, in the names of those who sacrificed, competed, and generously contributed.
EDCS-67200497.summary: This complex inscription, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, lists numerous officials and contributors involved in a victorious athletic or religious festival, dedicated by Marcus Ulpius Valerianus Pistos and others.

EDCS-67200578.translation: Good luck! For victory for our masters. Flavii Aphrodisios and Artemisius, primipilares, erected this statue for the Italic legion, of the province of Hellespont, in the 11th indiction.
EDCS-67200578.summary: This inscription from the 11th indiction (likely 6th century CE) records the erection of a statue by two primipilares honouring the Legio I Italica and dedicated to the province of Hellespont.

EDCS-67300110.translation: To the chthonic gods, farewell. Drakon, may the earth lie lightly upon you always. Bury Drakontes, Nuneches, and Pakuros, the brothers, along with Hermione and Mamata, their sisters. Sabinianos was the father, and Korinthie the mother. A sorrowful stele, an eternal memorial for mortals. If affection still exists among the living, may they remain there enjoying excellent peace; for it is enough to love them there, and to hate those here, leaving the ones here still to be hated by their fathers.
EDCS-67300110.summary: This funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial or late antique period, is addressed to the chthonic gods and commemorates Drakon and other family members, with an unusual expression of lasting hatred for those remaining alive.

EDCS-67300112.summary: A brief, positive dedication or greeting to Sergeia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67300113.translation: Priscinus Bales, tessellarius of the first Italic legion, dedicated this vow to the god Jupiter for [my] salvation.
EDCS-67300113.summary: This inscription from the Roman Imperial period records a vow made by Priscinus Bales, a tessellarius of Legio I Italica, to Jupiter for his salvation.

EDCS-67300117.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Kelerina, a deaconess, likely on a funerary monument or as a personal dedication.

EDCS-67300119.translation: To the god Okkonnos, Asynkritos and Evagelos son of Evagelos, with their families, set up the altar, fulfilling a vow.
EDCS-67300119.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Asynkritos and Evagelos to the god Okkonnos, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67300124.translation: To the god Mithras, Aurelius Marcus set this up.
EDCS-67300124.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the god Mithras by Aurelius Marcus, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67400500.translation: Orfius Sabinus, an evocatus of the Legio V Macedonica, willingly paid his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-67400500.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Orfius Sabinus, a soldier of Legio V Macedonica, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67400503.translation: Good luck! To Jupiter Ombrios, for the safety and victory of the emperors Maximinus Augustus and Maximus Caesar, Protogenes the magistrate dedicated this sacred precinct at his own expense.
EDCS-67400503.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Ombrios by Protogenes, a magistrate, for the safety and victory of Emperors Maximinus and Maximus, dating to their reign (235-238 AD).

EDCS-67400505.translation: To the holy Heron, Caius Punius Crescens made this for the safety of himself and his children, aged 50.
EDCS-67400505.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the holy Heron by Caius Punius Crescens for his family's well-being, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67400513.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Martiales lived for 9 years, to whom his father Eutyches and mother Victoria rightfully erected this tombstone.
EDCS-67400513.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Martiales, who died at the age of 9, erected by his parents Eutyches and Victoria, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67400527.summary: This inscription is a simple name, possibly a funerary epitaph or a dedication by an individual named Valerius Valens, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67400722.summary: This inscription simply names Emperor Anastasius, likely indicating a monument or location associated with him, possibly from his reign (491-518 AD).

EDCS-67400727.translation: Castus, a duovir, set up this vow for himself and his family, in the consulship of [---].
EDCS-67400727.summary: This is a votive inscription by Castus, a local official (duovir), dedicated to the Eternal God.

EDCS-67400730.translation: A veteran, [---], set [this] up for his most pious wife.
EDCS-67400730.summary: This inscription records a memorial set up by a veteran for his wife.

EDCS-67500007.translation: The people honored the lifelong priest of the emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, and high priest of the goddess Roma, and brother of the people, Apollonius, son of Apollonius, of the family of Promathion, with golden eternal wreaths and bronze painted statues, for his diligent and beneficial service to the fatherland, for his virtue and his goodwill towards them.
EDCS-67500007.summary: This is a Greek inscription from a city, honoring Apollonius for his service to the state and the emperor, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-68900044.translation: Seleucus, a scribe from the register of Roman citizens, dedicated this to Diana Regina for himself and his family in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-68900044.summary: This is a Latin inscription from an uncertain location, recording a votive dedication to Diana Regina by a scribe named Seleucus.

EDCS-68900047.translation: Tiberius Claudius Vitulus and his sons Claudius Constans, Celsus, and Apollinarius gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to Diana.
EDCS-68900047.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely from Roman Britain or a similar frontier province, recording a votive fulfillment to Diana by Tiberius Claudius Vitulus and his sons.

EDCS-68900052.translation: Titus Flavius Miles dedicated this to the Holy Apollo for his services.
EDCS-68900052.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely from a Roman province, recording a dedication to Apollo by Titus Flavius Miles.

EDCS-68900053.translation: Pudentinus, a centurion of the I Italica legion, erected this altar with a statue, fulfilling a vow with his family and their property, without any inscription from anyone else, to the lady of the Montanenses, Diana and Apollo.
EDCS-68900053.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely from a frontier region, recording a centurion's votive dedication of an altar and statue to Diana and Apollo.

EDCS-68900055.translation: Muco Eptenis and T. Zimius Bithi dedicated this to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-68900055.summary: This is a Latin inscription, possibly from Pannonia or Moesia, recording a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by two individuals.

EDCS-68900056.translation: Sanctus, a veteran and resident of the Montanenses, dedicated this to the Eternal God Maximus for the well-being of the Montanenses and himself, gladly and deservedly fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-68900056.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely from a frontier province, recording a veteran's votive dedication to an eternal deity for the community's well-being.

EDCS-68900061.translation: Publius Aelius Antoninus, tribune of a cohort, restored this shrine to Hercules the Savior, of the divine house and the place.
EDCS-68900061.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely from a Roman military context, recording the restoration of a sanctuary to Hercules by a military officer.

EDCS-68900064.translation: Silvanensis dedicated this to the Holy Silvanus in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-68900064.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely from a rural or frontier area, recording a votive dedication to Silvanus.

EDCS-68900065.translation: Aelius Severus, a centurion of the XI Claudia legion, [dedicated this] to the Holy Silvanus.
EDCS-68900065.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely from a military context, recording a dedication to Silvanus by a centurion of the XI Claudia legion.

EDCS-68900074.translation: Marcia lived 13 years. Caius Crepereius M. placed this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-68900074.summary: A funerary monument erected by Caius Crepereius M. for his wife Marcia, who died at age 13.

EDCS-68900075.translation: Aurelius Anicetus, freedman and heir, saw to it that this was made for his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-68900075.summary: A funerary monument established by Aurelius Anicetus for his patron, with a mention of Anicetus's age (65).

EDCS-68900078.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelius and [others?] placed this for his well-deserving wife, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-68900078.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Aurelius's wife, who died at age 24, placed by Aurelius and possibly others.

EDCS-68900082.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelius Maximinus placed this for his well-deserving wife, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-68900082.summary: A funerary monument erected by Aurelius Maximinus for his wife, who died at age 60.

EDCS-68900089.summary: A very fragmentary inscription indicating a scribe named Aelius S. made a dedication.

EDCS-69000069.translation: Romulianus, prefect of the cavalry of the Dalmatians of Beroea and those stationed in the province, and Flavia Maxima, his chaste wife, for their most beloved daughter Romula, citizen of Aquisenses, originating from the province of Dacia, where her parents lived. Avius Apat Romulus, a great man, who was made tribune by the aforementioned prefect and later became a companion. Romula herself lived 7 years. We have made this memorial out of great gratitude for her. Romula, your daughter, has fixed great sorrow in our hearts.
EDCS-69000069.summary: A funerary monument for Romula, daughter of Romulianus (a military officer) and Flavia Maxima, who died at age 7, originating from Dacia and citizens of Aquisenses.

EDCS-71200475.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Flavius Antonius, decurion of the Montanenses, lived 45 years and 28 days. Opellia Me...
EDCS-71200475.summary: A funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Antonius, a decurion of the Montanenses, who died at age 45 years and 28 days, likely with a second person mentioned.

EDCS-72500220.summary: A funerary epitaph for Varinia Dalmata.

EDCS-72500221.summary: A funerary epitaph for Varinia, indicating the dedicants may be 'Dalmatae' (perhaps referring to a group or her family).

EDCS-72500226.summary: A dedication by Zonus, identifying him as being from Dacia Ripensis.

EDCS-72800175.translation: Ulp(ius) Caius Valerius Castus, Aelius Respectus, Valerius Saturninus (?), Fonteius Julius (?)
EDCS-72800175.summary: This inscription lists the names of several individuals, likely part of a funerary monument or a collegium.

EDCS-72900278.translation: Aurelius Quadru, a vow of money.
EDCS-72900278.summary: This inscription records a vow made by Aurelius Quadru, likely dedicating money for a religious purpose.

EDCS-73700300.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus established the boundary between Thrace and Moesia.
EDCS-73700300.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary by Marcus Antius Rufinus under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-73700301.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus established the boundary between Thrace and Moesia.
EDCS-73700301.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary by Marcus Antius Rufinus under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-73700302.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time. Marcus Antius Rufinus established the boundary between Thrace and Moesia.
EDCS-73700302.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a boundary by Marcus Antius Rufinus under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-73700303.translation: Ulpia Restuta, for the honor of the flamonic office, was granted this site from the public treasury by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-73700303.summary: This inscription records the granting of land by decurions to Ulpia Restuta for her honorary position as a flaminica, likely in a municipal context.

EDCS-73700304.translation: To Nemesis Regina and the Immortal Gods, Publius Vaterculus Theodorus, a beneficiary (official) of the legate of the IV Flavia legion, gives thanks from his post at Ratiaria, while our lord Gordian Augustus II and Pompeianus were consuls.
EDCS-73700304.summary: This inscription is a dedication of thanks by an official of the IV Flavia legion to the gods Nemesis Regina and the Immortal Gods, dating to the consulship of Gordian II and Pompeianus (215 AD).

EDCS-74700040.translation: Aurelius Phoebus, centurion of the cohort.
EDCS-74700040.summary: This inscription likely records the name and rank of Aurelius Phoebus, a centurion in a Roman legion.

EDCS-74700042.translation: while Aelius Carinus and [---] were in charge.
EDCS-74700042.summary: This fragmented inscription indicates that Aelius Carinus was in charge of some matter or office.

EDCS-74700053.translation: To the Divine Shades. Terentius Iunior, tribune of soldiers of the V Macedonica legion, placed this for Euticus, who was also Ofellus, his freedman.
EDCS-74700053.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Terentius Iunior, a military tribune, for his freedman Euticus (also known as Ofellus).

EDCS-75000361.summary: This inscription appears to record the name of Geronius, possibly a 'probator' (assayer or inspector), on an unspecified monument.

EDCS-75300273.translation: Flavius... placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-75300273.summary: This text indicates that Flavius dedicated something in fulfillment of a vow, likely to a holy deity, on an unspecified monument.

EDCS-75300274.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Publius Aelius Gaianus, curator of the district.
EDCS-75300274.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Publius Aelius Gaianus, the curator of a district, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-75700102.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Iulius Ingenuus, decurion of the colony.
EDCS-75700102.summary: This is a dedication by Iulius Ingenuus, a decurion of the colony, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, probably on a public or religious monument.

EDCS-76100062.translation: To the Concord of the Augusti.
EDCS-76100062.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Concord (harmony) of the Emperors Augustus, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-76100066.translation: Iulius Renatus placed this in fulfillment of a vow to the god Apollo.
EDCS-76100066.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Iulius Renatus to the god Apollo, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-76100068.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno, Iulius Iulianus, a soldier of the I Italica legion, placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-76100068.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Iulius Iulianus, a soldier of the I Italica legion, to Jupiter and Juno in fulfillment of a vow, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-76100072.summary: This inscription records a vow made by Aurelius Mucianus, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-76100076.translation: ... cornicularius placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-76100076.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by an individual identified as a 'cornicularius' (a type of military officer), likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-76100077.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aelius Metroporus to Hygieia (goddess of health), likely on a religious or votive monument.

EDCS-76900120.translation: Sosicrates, son of Sosicrates, lies here, having lived 20 years. Farewell, passerby.
EDCS-76900120.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sosicrates, dating to antiquity, likely Greek.

EDCS-76900121.translation: Teimocrates, son of Alexandros, of the Nikomedeian tribe of the Romans, who lived honorably in Tomis, being alive and well, for himself and for his wife Olpia Casta and for his son Ulpius Martinus of the tribe of the Romans, a lover of his city, constructed this stele with the enclosed plot, which is common to me with Kaleinikos Strophe. Farewell, passerby.
EDCS-76900121.summary: A funerary monument inscription by Teimocrates for himself, his wife, and son, from the Roman period in Tomis (modern Constanta, Romania).

EDCS-76900152.translation: To Iunia Domitia and Iunia Nica.
EDCS-76900152.summary: A dedication to Iunia Domitia and Iunia Nica, likely funerary or votive, from the Roman period.

EDCS-76900161.translation: This memorial is for Marcia Aurella, former principal wife of Marcus.
EDCS-76900161.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcia Aurella, wife of Marcus, from the Roman period.

EDCS-76900186.summary: A fragment of a Roman inscription, likely a funerary or dedication, providing patronymic information.

EDCS-77000186.translation: Mother, pray for your sons, Holy Mary, pray for your son Herillus Phocas Elias.
EDCS-77000186.summary: A Christian inscription invoking the Virgin Mary to pray for her son, likely from the Byzantine period.

EDCS-77000201.translation: This tomb holds the brother and sister, the young man Mark and Silke. Farewell, passerby. Silke, daughter of Semnos, made this public monument for Silke, as a remembrance for her citizens.
EDCS-77000201.summary: A funerary epitaph for Mark and Silke, set up by Silke (daughter of Semnos) for herself and her brother, likely from the Greek-speaking Roman Empire.

EDCS-77000206.translation: To Mark the Hero, a thank-offering.
EDCS-77000206.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to a hero named Mark, from the Greek-speaking Roman world.

EDCS-77200011.summary: An imperial titulary inscription referring to Emperor Justinian I, from the Byzantine Empire.

EDCS-77200012.summary: An imperial titulary inscription referring to Emperor Justinian I, from the Byzantine Empire.

EDCS-77200016.translation: To our lord Mauricius Tiberius, perpetual Augustus.
EDCS-77200016.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Mauricius Tiberius, likely from the late 6th or early 7th century AD.

EDCS-77200018.translation: To our lords Heraclius and Heraclius Constantine, perpetual Augusti.
EDCS-77200018.summary: This is a dedication to Emperors Heraclius and Heraclius Constantine, likely from the early 7th century AD.

EDCS-77200020.summary: This is a dedication to an individual named Theodosius, identified as 'illustrious', likely of senatorial rank.

EDCS-77200035.summary: This inscription honors Theodorus, who held the office of Silentiarius, a court official, likely in the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-77200037.translation: To Theodorus, Prefect of the Islands.
EDCS-77200037.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Theodorus, who served as the Prefect of the Islands, likely during the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-77200038.translation: To Theodorus, Prefect of the Islands.
EDCS-77200038.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Theodorus, who served as the Prefect of the Islands, likely during the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-77200039.translation: To Theodorus, Prefect of the Islands.
EDCS-77200039.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Theodorus, who served as the Prefect of the Islands, likely during the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-77200045.summary: This is a dedication to Conimundus, who held the military title of Stratilates, likely in the Byzantine period.

EDCS-77200048.translation: To Petrus, former consul and logothete.
EDCS-77200048.summary: This inscription honors Petrus, who previously held the high office of consul and the administrative role of logothete, dating to the Byzantine era.

EDCS-77200069.summary: This inscription, possibly a funerary dedication or memorial, is addressed twice to an individual named Cyrillus.

EDCS-77200073.summary: This is a dedication to George Theodulus.

EDCS-77200074.summary: This is a dedication to George Theodulus.

EDCS-77200134.summary: This inscription refers to an image of an unconquered deity or concept.

EDCS-77200168.summary: A Greek invocation for help to the Theotokos (Mother of God) for Paul.

EDCS-77500095.translation: To good fortune. The most holy council and the most excellent people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube erected this statue of Julia Domna, divine Augusta, mother of the camps of Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Pius, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, high priest, in his 6th year of tribunician power, 11th of imperial acclamation, 8th of consulship, father of the country, wife, and mother of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and of Caesar Lucius Septimius Geta, during the vicegerency of the province by Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, legate of the Augusti, proconsul.
EDCS-77500095.summary: This is a public dedication of a statue to Empress Julia Domna, mother of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, by the council and people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube, dated to the time of Gaius Ovinius Tertullus' proconsulship.

EDCS-77500100.translation: Marcus Junius Lucianus, priest, and Ulpia Agrippeina, priestess, his wife, erected this at their own expense to Julia Domna, divine Augusta, mother of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, and mother of the sacred troops and of the Senate and of the Roman people.
EDCS-77500100.summary: This is a private dedication by a priestly couple, Marcus Junius Lucianus and Ulpia Agrippeina, to Empress Julia Domna.

EDCS-77500103.translation: To good fortune. The most holy council and the most excellent people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube erected this statue of Julia Domna, divine Augusta, mother of the camps of Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Pius, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, high priest, in his 6th year of tribunician power, 11th of imperial acclamation, 8th of consulship, father of the country, wife, and mother of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and of Caesar Lucius Septimius Geta, during the vicegerency of the province by Gaius Ovinius Tertullus, legate of the Augusti, proconsul.
EDCS-77500103.summary: This is a public dedication of a statue to Empress Julia Domna, mother of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, by the council and people of Ulpia Nicopolis on the Danube, dated to the time of Gaius Ovinius Tertullus' proconsulship.

EDCS-78100025.translation: Claudius Valens, council member and priest of Rome, made this for himself and for his father and mother, while alive and in sound mind, out of remembrance.
EDCS-78100025.summary: This is a funerary monument or dedication made by Claudius Valens for himself and his parents.

EDCS-78300419.translation: Flavius Verinus, a protector, born in the province of Pannonia, lived 42 years, served 18 years in the Pannonian legion. Flavius Florinus, biarch, placed this for his most sweet brother.
EDCS-78300419.summary: This is a funerary monument for Flavius Verinus, a soldier from Pannonia, erected by his brother Flavius Florinus.

EDCS-78300724.translation: To good fortune. The council sings the decrees and proclaims to mortals the zeal which he from the beginning showed to all in the year.
EDCS-78300724.summary: This is a laudatory inscription praising a leader named Valentis for his zeal and service, possibly commemorating his election or achievements.

EDCS-78300726.translation: Lu(ppus) made [---] from his own resources for Luppus and Maximus, with good fortune.
EDCS-78300726.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Luppus, possibly a private individual, to Luppus and Maximus.

EDCS-78300780.translation: Statilis Valentos dedicated this to the Hero Paladeineos, a god.
EDCS-78300780.summary: This is a dedication by Statilis Valentos to the heroic deity Paladeineos, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300781.translation: With good fortune, Paulina dedicated this thanksgiving to Ourania, Mother of the Gods, with good fortune.
EDCS-78300781.summary: This is a votive offering of thanksgiving by Paulina to the goddess Ourania, Mother of the Gods.

EDCS-78300782.translation: With good fortune, Aurelius Martinos, son of Dionysius, I dedicated an altar to the god Apollo Avlarkenos, having offered thanks to the powerful god.
EDCS-78300782.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Martinos dedicating an altar to Apollo Avlarkenos out of gratitude.

EDCS-78300783.translation: Hermantes, the priests of Asiaticus, through the supervisors Theodotos, son of Herakons, and Zenius, son of Posidonius, sacrificed to the Hero Manimazos.
EDCS-78300783.summary: This inscription details a sacrifice made by priests of the hero Asiaticus to the hero Manimazos.

EDCS-78700103.summary: This is a short dedication or invocation to a deity or heroic figure named Zbelthiourdos.

EDCS-78700104.translation: Marcus Sikis Sossis erected an altar to Helios Mithras.
EDCS-78700104.summary: This inscription records Marcus Sikis Sossis erecting an altar to the combined deities Helios Mithras.

EDCS-78700105.translation: Straton, son of Straton, dedicated an altar to Helios Mithras.
EDCS-78700105.summary: This inscription records Straton, son of Straton, dedicating an altar to Helios Mithras.

EDCS-78800201.translation: With good fortune, for the most manifest Zeus Keraunios, by the command of a dream, Decmia Epictesis erected this for herself, her husband Decmius Sapius, their children, and her own household, for the sake of a vow.
EDCS-78800201.summary: This is a votive inscription by Decmia Epictesis to Zeus Keraunios, made according to a dream, for her family and household.

EDCS-79000100.translation: Claudius Julianus, a statio-keeper, dedicated this thanksgiving to the Hero Outaspios.
EDCS-79000100.summary: This inscription is a thanksgiving dedication by Claudius Julianus, a soldier, to the heroic deity Outaspios.

EDCS-79000101.translation: To Lord Asclepius and Soldeen Hygieia, Preiscos, son of Julius, phylarch of Pautali, set up this offering of thanks.
EDCS-79000101.summary: This is a thank-offering to Asclepius and Hygieia by a local official from Pautali, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-79000102.translation: To the god Saltobusenus, the listener, Marcus Lucius, a circus performer, his vow.
EDCS-79000102.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Saltobusenus by a circus performer, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-79400002.translation: Abezlelmıs Dorpanas dedicated [this] to the god Eitiossarus.
EDCS-79400002.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to the god Eitiossarus by Abezlelmıs Dorpanas, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-79400003.translation: Loukis, his vow to Lord Heracles.
EDCS-79400003.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Heracles by Loukis, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-79700001.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a dedication by Amianus, possibly to the emperor Augustus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81300045.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning Uricius and Martialis, with an unclear dedication, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81900026.translation: To good fortune, for the greatest and most divine Imperator Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius, his mother the goddess Julia Mamaea, the most distinguished council and the most sacred people of the most brilliant city of Nicopolis on the Istros erected [this] auspiciously, during the consulship of Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Valerianus, with Aurelius Theodotus Nisbenus, archiereutikos, superintending.
EDCS-81900026.summary: This large inscription from Nicopolis on the Istros records the erection of a monument to Julia Mamaea, mother of Emperor Severus Alexander, during the consulship of Decius in 240 CE.

EDCS-82500063.translation: To good fortune, to the god Heracles, Maximos, son of Sios, and Neikon, son of Theodoros, for the guild of Nicomedian stonecutters, [set up] this thank-offering altar.
EDCS-82500063.summary: This is a thank-offering altar dedicated by the guild of stonecutters of Nicomedia to the god Heracles, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-82600022.translation: Beithys, son of Avlux, dedicated this thank-offering to the god Totousura.
EDCS-82600022.summary: This is a thank-offering to the god Totousura by Beithys, son of Avlux, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.
